Retrieves the data protection policy for the specified Amazon SNS topic.

Syntax
aws sns get-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn <value>
Parameters
  • --resource-arn (string): ARN of the SNS topic
Return Values
  • DataProtectionPolicy: JSON string of the data protection policy
Example 1: Get topic data protection policy

Retrieve PII protection settings for a topic.

#!/bin/bash
aws sns get-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-jobpost-alerts
Output: { "DataProtectionPolicy": "{\"Name\":\"JobPostDataProtection\",\"Description\":\"Protect PII in job notifications\",\"Version\":\"2021-06-01\",\"Configuration\":{\"CustomDataIdentifier\":[],\"ManagedDataIdentifier\":{\"Audit\":[{\"DataIdentifier\":[\"arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress\",\"arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/PhoneNumber\"]},{\"Operation\":{\"Deidentify\":{\"MaskConfig\":{\"MaskWithCharacter\":\"*\"}}}}]}}}" }
Django Context: # Data protection for User PII in JobPost alerts # Email and phone numbers protected # GDPR/CCPA compliant notification handling
Example 2: Parse policy JSON

Extract and format policy details.

#!/bin/bash
aws sns get-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-jobpost-alerts \
    --query 'DataProtectionPolicy' --output text | jq .
Output: { "Name": "JobPostDataProtection", "Description": "Protect PII in job notifications", "Version": "2021-06-01", "Configuration": { "CustomDataIdentifier": [], "ManagedDataIdentifier": { "Audit": [ { "DataIdentifier": [ "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress", "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/PhoneNumber" ] } ] } } }
Django Context: # Detailed data protection configuration # User.email and User.phone protected # JobPost notification PII handling rules
Example 3: Check if policy exists

Verify data protection is configured.

#!/bin/bash
TOPIC_ARN="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-jobpost-alerts"

POLICY=$(aws sns get-data-protection-policy --resource-arn $TOPIC_ARN \
    --query 'DataProtectionPolicy' --output text 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$POLICY" ] && [ "$POLICY" != "None" ]; then
    echo "Data protection policy is configured"
    echo "Policy name: $(echo $POLICY | jq -r '.Name')"
else
    echo "No data protection policy configured"
fi
Output: Data protection policy is configured Policy name: JobPostDataProtection
Django Context: # Data protection verification # User PII security confirmed # JobPost notifications comply with privacy policy
Example 4: List protected data identifiers

See what PII types are protected.

#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Protected Data Types ==="
aws sns get-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-jobpost-alerts \
    --query 'DataProtectionPolicy' --output text | jq -r '
    .Configuration.ManagedDataIdentifier.Audit[0].DataIdentifier[] | 
    split("/") | last'
Output: === Protected Data Types === EmailAddress PhoneNumber
Django Context: # Email and phone PII protected # User.email and User.phone fields # JobPost contact info secured
Example 5: Audit data protection across topics

Check policy status for multiple topics.

#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Data Protection Policy Audit ==="

TOPICS=("anihpj-jobpost-alerts" "anihpj-user-notifications" "anihpj-author-updates")

for topic in "${TOPICS[@]}"; do
    ARN="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:$topic"
    POLICY=$(aws sns get-data-protection-policy --resource-arn $ARN \
        --query 'DataProtectionPolicy' --output text 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ -n "$POLICY" ] && [ "$POLICY" != "None" ]; then
        NAME=$(echo $POLICY | jq -r '.Name // "Unnamed"')
        echo "$topic: Protected ($NAME)"
    else
        echo "$topic: NOT PROTECTED"
    fi
done
Output: === Data Protection Policy Audit === anihpj-jobpost-alerts: Protected (JobPostDataProtection) anihpj-user-notifications: Protected (UserDataProtection) anihpj-author-updates: NOT PROTECTED
Django Context: # Multi-topic privacy audit # JobPost and User topics protected # Author topic needs policy configuration

put-data-protection-policy

Adds or updates a data protection policy for the specified Amazon SNS topic.

Syntax
aws sns put-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn <value> \
    --data-protection-policy <value>
Parameters
  • --resource-arn (string): ARN of the SNS topic
  • --data-protection-policy (string): JSON policy document for data protection
Return Values
  • No output on success
Example 1: Create basic PII protection policy

Protect email addresses and phone numbers.

#!/bin/bash
aws sns put-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-jobpost-alerts \
    --data-protection-policy '{
        "Name": "JobPostDataProtection",
        "Description": "Protect PII in JobPost notifications",
        "Version": "2021-06-01",
        "Statement": [{
            "DataDirection": "Inbound",
            "Principal": ["*"],
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/PhoneNumber"
            ],
            "Operation": {
                "Audit": {
                    "SampleRate": "100",
                    "FindingsDestination": {
                        "CloudWatchLogs": {
                            "LogGroup": "/aws/sns/anihpj-data-protection"
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }]
    }'

echo "Data protection policy applied"
Output: Data protection policy applied
Django Context: # PII protection for User contact info # Email and phone audited in JobPost alerts # 100% of messages scanned for PII
Example 2: Policy with PII masking

Mask sensitive data in messages.

#!/bin/bash
aws sns put-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-user-notifications \
    --data-protection-policy '{
        "Name": "UserNotificationProtection",
        "Description": "Mask PII in user notifications",
        "Version": "2021-06-01",
        "Statement": [{
            "DataDirection": "Outbound",
            "Principal": ["*"],
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardNumber",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/SsnUs"
            ],
            "Operation": {
                "Deidentify": {
                    "MaskConfig": {
                        "MaskWithCharacter": "*"
                    }
                }
            }
        }]
    }'

echo "PII masking policy applied"
Output: PII masking policy applied
Django Context: # Sensitive User data masked in outbound messages # Credit cards: ****-****-****-1234 # SSN protected in Author payment notifications
Example 3: Comprehensive protection policy

Full data protection with audit and masking.

#!/bin/bash
cat > /tmp/data-protection-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
    "Name": "ComprehensiveDataProtection",
    "Description": "Full PII protection for anihpj notifications",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "AuditPII",
            "DataDirection": "Inbound",
            "Principal": ["*"],
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/PhoneNumber",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/Address"
            ],
            "Operation": {
                "Audit": {
                    "SampleRate": "100",
                    "FindingsDestination": {
                        "CloudWatchLogs": {
                            "LogGroup": "/aws/sns/data-protection-findings"
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        },
        {
            "Sid": "MaskSensitive",
            "DataDirection": "Outbound",
            "Principal": ["*"],
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/SsnUs"
            ],
            "Operation": {
                "Deidentify": {
                    "MaskConfig": {"MaskWithCharacter": "#"}
                }
            }
        }
    ]
}
EOF

aws sns put-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-jobpost-alerts \
    --data-protection-policy file:///tmp/data-protection-policy.json
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Comprehensive User data protection # Audit: email, phone, address from JobPost.location # Mask: SSN for Author payment processing
Example 4: Update existing policy

Add new data identifiers to existing policy.

#!/bin/bash
TOPIC_ARN="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-jobpost-alerts"

# Get current policy and update
CURRENT=$(aws sns get-data-protection-policy --resource-arn $TOPIC_ARN \
    --query 'DataProtectionPolicy' --output text)

# Add new data identifier
UPDATED=$(echo $CURRENT | jq '.Statement[0].DataIdentifier += ["arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/IpAddress"]')

aws sns put-data-protection-policy \
    --resource-arn $TOPIC_ARN \
    --data-protection-policy "$UPDATED"

echo "Policy updated with IP address protection"
Output: Policy updated with IP address protection
Django Context: # IP address now protected in JobPost notifications # User location privacy enhanced # Author access logs protected
Example 5: Apply policy to multiple topics

Standardize data protection across topics.

#!/bin/bash
POLICY='{
    "Name": "StandardDataProtection",
    "Description": "Standard PII protection for anihpj",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "DataDirection": "Inbound",
        "Principal": ["*"],
        "DataIdentifier": [
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/PhoneNumber"
        ],
        "Operation": {"Audit": {"SampleRate": "100"}}
    }]
}'

TOPICS=("anihpj-jobpost-alerts" "anihpj-user-notifications" "anihpj-skills-updates")

for topic in "${TOPICS[@]}"; do
    ARN="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:$topic"
    aws sns put-data-protection-policy --resource-arn $ARN --data-protection-policy "$POLICY"
    echo "Applied to: $topic"
done
Output: Applied to: anihpj-jobpost-alerts Applied to: anihpj-user-notifications Applied to: anihpj-skills-updates
Django Context: # Consistent User data protection across all topics # JobPost, User, Skills notifications all protected # Standardized compliance configuration

Amazon API Gateway

Build, deploy, and manage APIs at scale for your Django application

📋 API Gateway Overview

Amazon API Gateway is a fully managed service that makes it easy to create, publish, maintain, monitor, and secure APIs at any scale. Use it to expose your anihpj Django REST endpoints, handle authentication, rate limiting, and create serverless API integrations with Lambda.

Core API Gateway Operations

create-rest-api - Create new REST API
get-rest-apis - List all REST APIs
create-resource - Create API resource
put-method - Define HTTP method
put-integration - Set backend integration
create-deployment - Deploy API to stage
create-stage - Create deployment stage
create-api-key - Generate API keys

create-rest-api

Creates a new REST API for exposing your Django jobpost application endpoints.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-rest-api \
    --name <value> \
    --description <value> \
    [--endpoint-configuration types=REGIONAL|EDGE|PRIVATE] \
    [--tags key=value,...]
Parameters
  • --name (string): Name of the REST API
  • --description (string): Description of the API
  • --endpoint-configuration (structure): Endpoint type configuration
  • --tags (map): Tags to apply to the API
Return Values
  • id: The API's unique identifier
  • name: The API name
  • rootResourceId: Root resource ID for creating paths
Example 1: Create JobPost REST API

Create a new REST API for the jobpost application.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-rest-api \
    --name "anihpj-jobpost-api" \
    --description "REST API for Django jobpost app with Skills, Author, Location, JobPost models" \
    --endpoint-configuration types=REGIONAL \
    --tags Environment=production,App=jobpost
Output: { "id": "abc123def4", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-api", "description": "REST API for Django jobpost app with Skills, Author, Location, JobPost models", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T16:00:00Z", "endpointConfiguration": { "types": ["REGIONAL"] }, "tags": { "Environment": "production", "App": "jobpost" } }
Django Context: # REST API created for exposing Django models: # - /skills → Skills.objects.all() # - /authors → Author.objects.all() # - /locations → Location.objects.all() # - /jobposts → JobPost.objects.all() # Base URL: https://abc123def4.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/
Example 2: Create Edge-optimized API

Create API with CloudFront edge caching.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-rest-api \
    --name "anihpj-global-api" \
    --description "Edge-optimized API for global jobpost access" \
    --endpoint-configuration types=EDGE
Output: { "id": "xyz789ghi0", "name": "anihpj-global-api", "endpointConfiguration": { "types": ["EDGE"] } }
Django Context: # Edge-optimized for global User access # CloudFront caches JobPost.objects responses # Faster Location.objects queries worldwide
Example 3: Create Private API

Create VPC-only private API.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-rest-api \
    --name "anihpj-internal-api" \
    --description "Internal API for backend services" \
    --endpoint-configuration types=PRIVATE,vpcEndpointIds=vpce-abc123
Output: { "id": "prv456jkl1", "name": "anihpj-internal-api", "endpointConfiguration": { "types": ["PRIVATE"], "vpcEndpointIds": ["vpce-abc123"] } }
Django Context: # Private API accessible only within VPC # Internal Author.objects processing # Secure Skills.objects bulk operations
Example 4: Create API with binary support

Create API supporting binary media types for file uploads.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-rest-api \
    --name "anihpj-media-api" \
    --description "API supporting media uploads for JobPost images" \
    --binary-media-types "image/png" "image/jpeg" "application/pdf"
Output: { "id": "med789xyz2", "name": "anihpj-media-api", "binaryMediaTypes": ["image/png", "image/jpeg", "application/pdf"] }
Django Context: # Binary support for JobPost media uploads # Author profile images # Resume PDF attachments for job applications
Example 5: Clone existing API

Create new API by cloning existing one.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-rest-api \
    --name "anihpj-jobpost-api-v2" \
    --description "Version 2 of jobpost API" \
    --clone-from abc123def4
Output: { "id": "v2clone345", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-api-v2", "description": "Version 2 of jobpost API" }
Django Context: # V2 API cloned from production # Same Skills, Author, Location, JobPost endpoints # Ready for new feature development

create-resource

Creates a new resource (URL path) in your REST API.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-resource \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --parent-id <value> \
    --path-part <value>
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The API identifier
  • --parent-id (string): Parent resource ID
  • --path-part (string): URL path segment
Example 1: Create /jobposts resource

Create endpoint for JobPost model.

#!/bin/bash
# Get root resource ID first
ROOT_ID=$(aws apigateway get-resources --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --query 'items[?path==`/`].id' --output text)

aws apigateway create-resource \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --parent-id $ROOT_ID \
    --path-part "jobposts"
Output: { "id": "res123job", "parentId": "rootxyz", "pathPart": "jobposts", "path": "/jobposts" }
Django Context: # /jobposts → JobPost.objects.all() # GET: List all job posts # POST: Create new JobPost
Example 2: Create /skills resource

Create endpoint for Skills model.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-resource \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --parent-id rootxyz \
    --path-part "skills"
Output: { "id": "res456skl", "path": "/skills" }
Django Context: # /skills → Skills.objects.all() # GET: List all skills in database # POST: Add new Skill entry
Example 3: Create nested /jobposts/{id}

Create path parameter for specific JobPost.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-resource \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --parent-id res123job \
    --path-part "{jobpost_id}"
Output: { "id": "res789id", "path": "/jobposts/{jobpost_id}" }
Django Context: # /jobposts/{id} → JobPost.objects.get(pk=id) # GET: Retrieve specific job post # PUT: Update job post # DELETE: Remove job post
Example 4: Create /authors resource

Create endpoint for Author model.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-resource \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --parent-id rootxyz \
    --path-part "authors"
Output: { "id": "res321auth", "path": "/authors" }
Django Context: # /authors → Author.objects.all() # GET: List authors with their job posts # Includes Author.user and Author.location
Example 5: Create /locations resource

Create endpoint for Location model.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-resource \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --parent-id rootxyz \
    --path-part "locations"
Output: { "id": "res654loc", "path": "/locations" }
Django Context: # /locations → Location.objects.all() # GET: List all job locations # Filter jobs by Location.city, Location.country

create-api-key

Creates an API key for rate limiting and access control.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-api-key \
    --name <value> \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--enabled | --no-enabled]
Example 1: Create production API key

Generate API key for production access.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-api-key \
    --name "anihpj-prod-key" \
    --description "Production API key for jobpost app" \
    --enabled
Output: { "id": "key123prod", "name": "anihpj-prod-key", "value": "AbCdEfGhIjKlMnOpQrStUvWxYz123456", "enabled": true }
Django Context: # API key for production Django app # Rate limits applied to JobPost queries # Track usage per client application
Example 2: Create partner API key

Generate key for external partner access.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-api-key \
    --name "partner-acme-corp" \
    --description "API key for ACME Corp integration" \
    --enabled \
    --tags Partner=ACME,Tier=Premium
Output: { "id": "key456ptnr", "name": "partner-acme-corp", "value": "PaRtNeRkEy789012345678901234567", "tags": { "Partner": "ACME", "Tier": "Premium" } }
Django Context: # Partner integration for JobPost syndication # Read-only access to Location.objects # Limited Skills.objects queries
Example 3: Create disabled API key

Create key in disabled state for later activation.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-api-key \
    --name "anihpj-staging-key" \
    --description "Staging API key - activate before testing" \
    --no-enabled
Output: { "id": "key789stg", "name": "anihpj-staging-key", "value": "StAgInGkEy123456789012345678901", "enabled": false }
Django Context: # Disabled key for staging environment # Enable when testing JobPost endpoints # Safe pre-production setup
Example 4: Create key with custom value

Create API key with specified value.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-api-key \
    --name "anihpj-migration-key" \
    --description "Migration key with known value" \
    --value "MyCustomApiKeyValue123456789012" \
    --enabled
Output: { "id": "keymig456", "name": "anihpj-migration-key", "value": "MyCustomApiKeyValue123456789012", "enabled": true }
Django Context: # Custom key for migration scenarios # Maintain User.objects API access during transition # Consistent key across environments
Example 5: Create key and associate with usage plan

Create key and immediately link to usage plan.

#!/bin/bash
# Create the API key
KEY_ID=$(aws apigateway create-api-key \
    --name "anihpj-premium-client" \
    --description "Premium tier client access" \
    --enabled \
    --query 'id' --output text)

# Associate with usage plan
aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
    --usage-plan-id up123premium \
    --key-id $KEY_ID \
    --key-type API_KEY

echo "Created and associated key: $KEY_ID"
Output: { "id": "keyprem789", "name": "anihpj-premium-client", "value": "PrEmIuMkEy987654321098765432109", "enabled": true } { "id": "keyprem789", "type": "API_KEY", "name": "anihpj-premium-client" } Created and associated key: keyprem789
Django Context: # Premium key with usage plan # Higher rate limits for Author.objects queries # Skills.objects bulk access enabled

create-authorizer

Creates an authorizer for securing API endpoints with JWT, Lambda, or Cognito.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --name <value> \
    --type TOKEN|REQUEST|COGNITO_USER_POOLS \
    [--authorizer-uri <value>] \
    [--identity-source <value>]
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The REST API identifier
  • --name (string): Name of the authorizer
  • --type (string): Authorizer type (TOKEN, REQUEST, COGNITO_USER_POOLS)
  • --authorizer-uri (string): Lambda function ARN for custom authorizer
  • --identity-source (string): Identity source for authorization
Return Values
  • id: Authorizer identifier
  • name: Authorizer name
  • type: Authorizer type
Example 1: Create Cognito authorizer

Secure API with Cognito User Pool authentication.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "anihpj-cognito-auth" \
    --type COGNITO_USER_POOLS \
    --provider-arns arn:aws:cognito-idp:us-east-1:123456789012:userpool/us-east-1_ABC123 \
    --identity-source "method.request.header.Authorization"
Output: { "id": "auth123", "name": "anihpj-cognito-auth", "type": "COGNITO_USER_POOLS", "providerARNs": ["arn:aws:cognito-idp:us-east-1:123456789012:userpool/us-east-1_ABC123"] }
Django Context: # Cognito auth for User.objects access control # JWT tokens validate JobPost ownership # Author.objects protected by user pool
Example 2: Create Lambda authorizer

Custom authorization with Lambda function.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "anihpj-lambda-auth" \
    --type TOKEN \
    --authorizer-uri "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-authorizer/invocations" \
    --identity-source "method.request.header.Authorization" \
    --authorizer-result-ttl-in-seconds 300
Output: { "id": "auth456", "name": "anihpj-lambda-auth", "type": "TOKEN", "authorizerResultTtlInSeconds": 300 }
Django Context: # Custom auth logic for Skills.objects access # Lambda validates Django session tokens # 5-minute cache for JobPost permissions
Example 3: Create request-based authorizer

Authorize based on request parameters.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "anihpj-request-auth" \
    --type REQUEST \
    --authorizer-uri "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:request-authorizer/invocations" \
    --identity-source "method.request.header.x-api-key,method.request.querystring.token"
Output: { "id": "auth789", "name": "anihpj-request-auth", "type": "REQUEST" }
Django Context: # Multi-source authorization for Location queries # API key + token validation for Author updates # Flexible auth for JobPost batch operations
Example 4: Create authorizer with validation

Add identity validation expression.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "anihpj-jwt-auth" \
    --type TOKEN \
    --authorizer-uri "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jwt-validator/invocations" \
    --identity-source "method.request.header.Authorization" \
    --identity-validation-expression "^Bearer [-0-9a-zA-Z._]*$"
Output: { "id": "authjwt1", "name": "anihpj-jwt-auth", "identityValidationExpression": "^Bearer [-0-9a-zA-Z._]*$" }
Django Context: # JWT format validation before Lambda invoke # Protects User.objects modification endpoints # Bearer token required for all JobPost writes
Example 5: Create authorizer with credentials

Specify IAM role for Lambda invocation.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "anihpj-secure-auth" \
    --type TOKEN \
    --authorizer-uri "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:secure-authorizer/invocations" \
    --authorizer-credentials "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/api-gateway-authorizer-role" \
    --identity-source "method.request.header.Authorization"
Output: { "id": "authsec2", "name": "anihpj-secure-auth", "authorizerCredentials": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/api-gateway-authorizer-role" }
Django Context: # IAM role controls authorizer execution # Cross-account Skills.objects access # Secure Author.objects admin operations

create-base-path-mapping

Creates a base path mapping for a custom domain name to an API stage.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name <value> \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    [--base-path <value>] \
    [--stage <value>]
Parameters
  • --domain-name (string): Custom domain name
  • --rest-api-id (string): REST API identifier
  • --base-path (string): Base path for mapping (empty for root)
  • --stage (string): API stage name
Return Values
  • basePath: The base path
  • restApiId: API identifier
  • stage: Stage name
Example 1: Map root path to production

Map custom domain root to production stage.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name api.anihpj.com \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage prod
Output: { "basePath": "(none)", "restApiId": "abc123def4", "stage": "prod" }
Django Context: # https://api.anihpj.com/ → Production API # JobPost.objects.filter(status='published') at root # Clean URLs for User-facing endpoints
Example 2: Map versioned path

Create v1 API path mapping.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name api.anihpj.com \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --base-path v1 \
    --stage prod
Output: { "basePath": "v1", "restApiId": "abc123def4", "stage": "prod" }
Django Context: # https://api.anihpj.com/v1/ → V1 API # Versioned Skills.objects endpoints # Backward compatibility for Author clients
Example 3: Map staging environment

Create staging path for testing.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name api.anihpj.com \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --base-path staging \
    --stage dev
Output: { "basePath": "staging", "restApiId": "abc123def4", "stage": "dev" }
Django Context: # https://api.anihpj.com/staging/ → Dev stage # Test JobPost.objects CRUD operations # QA environment for Location updates
Example 4: Map internal API path

Create internal services path.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name internal.anihpj.com \
    --rest-api-id prv456jkl1 \
    --base-path admin \
    --stage prod
Output: { "basePath": "admin", "restApiId": "prv456jkl1", "stage": "prod" }
Django Context: # https://internal.anihpj.com/admin/ → Admin API # Bulk User.objects operations # Admin-only Skills management
Example 5: Map partner API

Create partner integration path.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name partners.anihpj.com \
    --rest-api-id prt789abc2 \
    --base-path acme \
    --stage prod
Output: { "basePath": "acme", "restApiId": "prt789abc2", "stage": "prod" }
Django Context: # https://partners.anihpj.com/acme/ → Partner API # ACME Corp JobPost syndication # Isolated Author.objects for partner

create-deployment

Creates a deployment for the specified REST API stage.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-deployment \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    [--stage-name <value>] \
    [--stage-description <value>] \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--cache-cluster-enabled] \
    [--cache-cluster-size <value>]
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The REST API identifier
  • --stage-name (string): Name of the stage to deploy to
  • --stage-description (string): Description for the stage
  • --description (string): Description for the deployment
  • --cache-cluster-enabled (boolean): Enable API caching
  • --cache-cluster-size (string): Cache cluster size
Return Values
  • id: Deployment identifier
  • description: Deployment description
  • createdDate: Deployment timestamp
Example 1: Deploy to production

Create production deployment with caching.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-deployment \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name prod \
    --stage-description "Production environment" \
    --description "Release v2.1.0 - JobPost filtering improvements" \
    --cache-cluster-enabled \
    --cache-cluster-size 0.5
Output: { "id": "dep123abc", "description": "Release v2.1.0 - JobPost filtering improvements", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T18:00:00Z" }
Django Context: # Production deployment with API caching # JobPost.objects.filter() responses cached # 0.5GB cache for Skills.objects queries
Example 2: Deploy to staging

Deploy for testing without cache.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-deployment \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name staging \
    --description "Testing new Author endpoints"
Output: { "id": "dep456stg", "description": "Testing new Author endpoints", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T17:30:00Z" }
Django Context: # Staging deployment for Author.objects tests # No caching for real-time testing # Preview Location.objects changes
Example 3: Deploy with variables

Deploy with stage variables for configuration.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-deployment \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name dev \
    --variables djangoEnv=development,dbHost=dev-db.anihpj.com
Output: { "id": "dep789dev", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T16:00:00Z" }
Django Context: # Stage variables for Django settings # DEV database for JobPost.objects # Isolated User.objects test data
Example 4: Canary deployment

Create canary deployment for gradual rollout.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-deployment \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name prod \
    --canary-settings percentTraffic=10,useStageCache=false
Output: { "id": "depcan123", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T19:00:00Z" }
Django Context: # 10% traffic to new Skills.objects endpoint # Gradual JobPost.objects feature rollout # Safe Author.objects API changes
Example 5: Deploy with tracing

Enable X-Ray tracing on deployment.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-deployment \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name prod \
    --tracing-enabled
Output: { "id": "deptrc456", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T20:00:00Z" }
Django Context: # X-Ray tracing for Location.objects latency # Debug JobPost.objects slow queries # Monitor User.objects API performance

create-documentation-part

Creates a documentation part for an API resource or method.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-documentation-part \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --location type=API|AUTHORIZER|MODEL|RESOURCE|METHOD|PATH_PARAMETER|QUERY_PARAMETER|REQUEST_HEADER|REQUEST_BODY|RESPONSE|RESPONSE_HEADER|RESPONSE_BODY \
    --properties <value>
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The REST API identifier
  • --location (structure): Location of the documentation part
  • --properties (string): JSON documentation content
Return Values
  • id: Documentation part identifier
  • location: Documentation location
  • properties: Documentation content
Example 1: Document API overview

Add API-level documentation.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-part \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --location type=API \
    --properties '{"info":{"title":"Anihpj JobPost API","description":"REST API for job posting platform with Skills, Author, Location, JobPost models","version":"2.0"}}'
Output: { "id": "doc123api", "location": {"type": "API"}, "properties": "{\"info\":{\"title\":\"Anihpj JobPost API\"}}" }
Django Context: # API overview documentation # Describes JobPost.objects endpoints # OpenAPI spec for Skills, Author, Location
Example 2: Document resource

Add documentation for /jobposts resource.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-part \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --location type=RESOURCE,path=/jobposts \
    --properties '{"description":"Job posting resources for creating, reading, updating job listings"}'
Output: { "id": "doc456res", "location": {"type": "RESOURCE", "path": "/jobposts"}, "properties": "{\"description\":\"Job posting resources...\"}" }
Django Context: # /jobposts resource documentation # Maps to JobPost.objects CRUD # Endpoint description for developers
Example 3: Document method

Add GET method documentation.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-part \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --location type=METHOD,path=/jobposts,method=GET \
    --properties '{"summary":"List all job posts","description":"Returns paginated list of published JobPost objects"}'
Output: { "id": "doc789mth", "location": {"type": "METHOD", "path": "/jobposts", "method": "GET"}, "properties": "{\"summary\":\"List all job posts\"}" }
Django Context: # GET /jobposts documentation # JobPost.objects.filter(status='published') # Swagger/OpenAPI method spec
Example 4: Document query parameter

Document filtering parameter.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-part \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --location type=QUERY_PARAMETER,path=/jobposts,method=GET,name=location_id \
    --properties '{"description":"Filter jobs by Location ID from Location.objects"}'
Output: { "id": "docqry123", "location": {"type": "QUERY_PARAMETER", "name": "location_id"}, "properties": "{\"description\":\"Filter jobs by Location ID\"}" }
Django Context: # ?location_id parameter docs # Maps to JobPost.objects.filter(location_id=x) # Foreign key to Location.objects
Example 5: Document response body

Document API response structure.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-part \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --location type=RESPONSE_BODY,path=/jobposts,method=GET,statusCode=200 \
    --properties '{"description":"Array of JobPost objects with nested Author and Location data"}'
Output: { "id": "docrsp456", "location": {"type": "RESPONSE_BODY", "statusCode": "200"}, "properties": "{\"description\":\"Array of JobPost objects...\"}" }
Django Context: # 200 response body documentation # JobPost serializer output format # Includes Author, Location, Skills relations

create-documentation-version

Creates a documentation version snapshot for publishing.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-documentation-version \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --documentation-version <value> \
    [--stage-name <value>] \
    [--description <value>]
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The REST API identifier
  • --documentation-version (string): Version identifier
  • --stage-name (string): Stage to associate with
  • --description (string): Version description
Return Values
  • version: Version identifier
  • createdDate: Creation timestamp
  • description: Version description
Example 1: Create version 1.0

Snapshot initial documentation.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-version \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --documentation-version "1.0" \
    --description "Initial API documentation release"
Output: { "version": "1.0", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T10:00:00Z", "description": "Initial API documentation release" }
Django Context: # V1.0 docs for JobPost API # Snapshot of all endpoint documentation # First public API reference
Example 2: Create and associate with stage

Create version linked to production.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-version \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --documentation-version "2.0" \
    --stage-name prod \
    --description "V2 with new Skills endpoints"
Output: { "version": "2.0", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T14:00:00Z", "description": "V2 with new Skills endpoints" }
Django Context: # V2.0 docs linked to production # New Skills.objects endpoints documented # Production API reference update
Example 3: Create patch version

Create minor version update.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-version \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --documentation-version "2.1" \
    --description "Added Author filtering documentation"
Output: { "version": "2.1", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T16:00:00Z", "description": "Added Author filtering documentation" }
Django Context: # V2.1 patch documentation # Author.objects filter docs added # Minor API reference update
Example 4: Create beta documentation

Create beta version for preview.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-version \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --documentation-version "3.0-beta" \
    --stage-name dev \
    --description "Beta docs for new Location features"
Output: { "version": "3.0-beta", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T12:00:00Z", "description": "Beta docs for new Location features" }
Django Context: # Beta documentation for testing # Preview Location.objects new endpoints # Dev stage API reference
Example 5: Create dated version

Create version with date identifier.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-documentation-version \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --documentation-version "2025-01-11" \
    --description "Daily documentation snapshot"
Output: { "version": "2025-01-11", "createdDate": "2025-01-11T23:59:00Z", "description": "Daily documentation snapshot" }
Django Context: # Daily snapshot of API docs # Historical JobPost API reference # Compliance documentation archive

create-domain-name

Creates a custom domain name for your API.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-domain-name \
    --domain-name <value> \
    [--certificate-arn <value>] \
    [--regional-certificate-arn <value>] \
    [--endpoint-configuration types=REGIONAL|EDGE] \
    [--security-policy TLS_1_0|TLS_1_2]
Parameters
  • --domain-name (string): Custom domain name
  • --certificate-arn (string): ACM certificate ARN for edge
  • --regional-certificate-arn (string): ACM certificate ARN for regional
  • --endpoint-configuration (structure): Endpoint type
  • --security-policy (string): TLS version policy
Return Values
  • domainName: The custom domain name
  • distributionDomainName: CloudFront distribution domain
  • regionalDomainName: Regional endpoint domain
Example 1: Create regional domain

Create regional custom domain with TLS 1.2.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name \
    --domain-name api.anihpj.com \
    --regional-certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc123 \
    --endpoint-configuration types=REGIONAL \
    --security-policy TLS_1_2
Output: { "domainName": "api.anihpj.com", "regionalDomainName": "d-abc123.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com", "regionalHostedZoneId": "Z1UJRXOUMOOFQ8", "endpointConfiguration": {"types": ["REGIONAL"]}, "securityPolicy": "TLS_1_2" }
Django Context: # https://api.anihpj.com custom domain # TLS 1.2 for secure JobPost.objects access # Regional endpoint for User.objects API
Example 2: Create edge domain

Create CloudFront-backed domain.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name \
    --domain-name global.anihpj.com \
    --certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/xyz789 \
    --endpoint-configuration types=EDGE
Output: { "domainName": "global.anihpj.com", "distributionDomainName": "d111111abcdef8.cloudfront.net", "distributionHostedZoneId": "Z2FDTNDATAQYW2", "endpointConfiguration": {"types": ["EDGE"]} }
Django Context: # CloudFront edge distribution # Global Skills.objects caching # Low latency JobPost queries worldwide
Example 3: Create staging domain

Create domain for staging environment.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name \
    --domain-name staging-api.anihpj.com \
    --regional-certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/stg456 \
    --endpoint-configuration types=REGIONAL \
    --security-policy TLS_1_2
Output: { "domainName": "staging-api.anihpj.com", "regionalDomainName": "d-stg456.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com", "securityPolicy": "TLS_1_2" }
Django Context: # Staging domain for QA testing # Test Author.objects endpoints # Pre-production Location.objects validation
Example 4: Create partner domain

Create domain for partner integrations.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name \
    --domain-name partners.anihpj.com \
    --regional-certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/prt789 \
    --endpoint-configuration types=REGIONAL \
    --mutual-tls-authentication truststoreUri=s3://anihpj-certs/truststore.pem
Output: { "domainName": "partners.anihpj.com", "regionalDomainName": "d-prt789.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com", "mutualTlsAuthentication": { "truststoreUri": "s3://anihpj-certs/truststore.pem" } }
Django Context: # Partner domain with mTLS # Secure JobPost syndication # Client certificate verification
Example 5: Create internal domain

Create domain for internal services.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name \
    --domain-name internal.anihpj.com \
    --regional-certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/int123 \
    --endpoint-configuration types=REGIONAL \
    --security-policy TLS_1_2 \
    --tags Environment=internal,Team=backend
Output: { "domainName": "internal.anihpj.com", "regionalDomainName": "d-int123.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com", "securityPolicy": "TLS_1_2", "tags": {"Environment": "internal", "Team": "backend"} }
Django Context: # Internal API domain for backend # Bulk User.objects operations # Admin Skills.objects management

create-domain-name-access-association

Creates a domain name access association for private API access.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-domain-name-access-association \
    --domain-name-arn <value> \
    --access-association-source-type VPCE \
    --access-association-source <value> \
    [--tags <value>]
Parameters
  • --domain-name-arn (string): Domain name ARN
  • --access-association-source-type (string): Source type (VPCE)
  • --access-association-source (string): VPC endpoint ID
  • --tags (map): Resource tags
Return Values
  • domainNameAccessAssociationArn: Association ARN
  • accessAssociationSource: VPC endpoint source
Example 1: Associate VPC endpoint

Create access association for private domain.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name-access-association \
    --domain-name-arn arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1::/domainnames/internal.anihpj.com \
    --access-association-source-type VPCE \
    --access-association-source vpce-abc123456
Output: { "domainNameAccessAssociationArn": "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:123456789012:domainnames/internal.anihpj.com/accessassociations/vpce-abc123456", "accessAssociationSource": "vpce-abc123456", "accessAssociationSourceType": "VPCE" }
Django Context: # VPC endpoint access for internal API # Secure JobPost.objects backend access # Private network User.objects operations
Example 2: Associate with tags

Create association with resource tags.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name-access-association \
    --domain-name-arn arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1::/domainnames/private.anihpj.com \
    --access-association-source-type VPCE \
    --access-association-source vpce-xyz789012 \
    --tags Environment=production,Team=backend
Output: { "domainNameAccessAssociationArn": "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:123456789012:domainnames/private.anihpj.com/accessassociations/vpce-xyz789012", "tags": {"Environment": "production", "Team": "backend"} }
Django Context: # Tagged VPC endpoint association # Production backend Skills.objects access # Team ownership tracking
Example 3: Multi-VPC access

Create associations for multiple VPCs.

#!/bin/bash
for VPCE in vpce-prod1 vpce-prod2 vpce-staging; do
    aws apigateway create-domain-name-access-association \
        --domain-name-arn arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1::/domainnames/api.anihpj.com \
        --access-association-source-type VPCE \
        --access-association-source $VPCE
    echo "Associated: $VPCE"
done
Output: Associated: vpce-prod1 Associated: vpce-prod2 Associated: vpce-staging
Django Context: # Multiple VPC access to API # Production and staging JobPost.objects # Cross-VPC Author.objects synchronization
Example 4: Partner VPC access

Grant partner VPC endpoint access.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name-access-association \
    --domain-name-arn arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1::/domainnames/partners.anihpj.com \
    --access-association-source-type VPCE \
    --access-association-source vpce-partner123 \
    --tags Partner=ACME,AccessLevel=readonly
Output: { "domainNameAccessAssociationArn": "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:123456789012:domainnames/partners.anihpj.com/accessassociations/vpce-partner123", "tags": {"Partner": "ACME", "AccessLevel": "readonly"} }
Django Context: # Partner VPC endpoint access # Read-only JobPost.objects for syndication # Controlled Location.objects queries
Example 5: Development VPC access

Create dev environment access.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-domain-name-access-association \
    --domain-name-arn arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1::/domainnames/dev.anihpj.com \
    --access-association-source-type VPCE \
    --access-association-source vpce-dev456 \
    --tags Environment=development
Output: { "domainNameAccessAssociationArn": "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:123456789012:domainnames/dev.anihpj.com/accessassociations/vpce-dev456" }
Django Context: # Dev VPC access for testing # Development Skills.objects experiments # Isolated User.objects test environment

create-model

Creates a model for request/response validation in API Gateway.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-model \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --name <value> \
    --content-type <value> \
    [--schema <value>] \
    [--description <value>]
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The REST API identifier
  • --name (string): Model name
  • --content-type (string): Content type (application/json)
  • --schema (string): JSON Schema definition
  • --description (string): Model description
Return Values
  • id: Model identifier
  • name: Model name
  • schema: JSON Schema
Example 1: Create JobPost model

Define JobPost request/response schema.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-model \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name JobPost \
    --content-type "application/json" \
    --description "JobPost model for anihpj Django app" \
    --schema '{
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "title": {"type": "string", "maxLength": 200},
            "description": {"type": "string"},
            "author_id": {"type": "integer"},
            "location_id": {"type": "integer"},
            "skills": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "integer"}},
            "status": {"type": "string", "enum": ["draft", "published", "archived"]}
        },
        "required": ["title", "author_id"]
    }'
Output: { "id": "mdl123job", "name": "JobPost", "contentType": "application/json", "description": "JobPost model for anihpj Django app" }
Django Context: # Maps to JobPost Django model # Validates title, author_id, location_id # Skills array for many-to-many relation
Example 2: Create Author model

Define Author schema for validation.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-model \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name Author \
    --content-type "application/json" \
    --schema '{
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "name": {"type": "string", "maxLength": 100},
            "email": {"type": "string", "format": "email"},
            "bio": {"type": "string"},
            "user_id": {"type": "integer"}
        },
        "required": ["name", "email"]
    }'
Output: { "id": "mdl456aut", "name": "Author", "contentType": "application/json" }
Django Context: # Maps to Author Django model # Email format validation # Links to User.objects via user_id
Example 3: Create Skills model

Define Skills schema.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-model \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name Skill \
    --content-type "application/json" \
    --schema '{
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "name": {"type": "string", "maxLength": 50},
            "category": {"type": "string"},
            "level": {"type": "string", "enum": ["beginner", "intermediate", "expert"]}
        },
        "required": ["name"]
    }'
Output: { "id": "mdl789skl", "name": "Skill", "contentType": "application/json" }
Django Context: # Maps to Skills Django model # Skill level validation # Category for Skills.objects.filter()
Example 4: Create Location model

Define Location schema with geo data.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-model \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name Location \
    --content-type "application/json" \
    --schema '{
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "city": {"type": "string"},
            "state": {"type": "string"},
            "country": {"type": "string"},
            "postal_code": {"type": "string"},
            "remote": {"type": "boolean"}
        },
        "required": ["city", "country"]
    }'
Output: { "id": "mdlloc123", "name": "Location", "contentType": "application/json" }
Django Context: # Maps to Location Django model # Geo filtering validation # Remote work flag for JobPost.objects
Example 5: Create Error model

Define error response schema.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-model \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name Error \
    --content-type "application/json" \
    --description "Standard error response" \
    --schema '{
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "code": {"type": "integer"},
            "message": {"type": "string"},
            "details": {"type": "object"}
        },
        "required": ["code", "message"]
    }'
Output: { "id": "mdlerr456", "name": "Error", "contentType": "application/json" }
Django Context: # Standard Django REST error format # Consistent error handling # Maps to DRF exception responses

create-request-validator

Creates a request validator for validating incoming API requests.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-request-validator \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --name <value> \
    [--validate-request-body] \
    [--validate-request-parameters]
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The REST API identifier
  • --name (string): Validator name
  • --validate-request-body (boolean): Validate request body
  • --validate-request-parameters (boolean): Validate parameters
Return Values
  • id: Validator identifier
  • name: Validator name
  • validateRequestBody: Body validation flag
  • validateRequestParameters: Parameter validation flag
Example 1: Create full validator

Validate both body and parameters.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-request-validator \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "full-validator" \
    --validate-request-body \
    --validate-request-parameters
Output: { "id": "val123full", "name": "full-validator", "validateRequestBody": true, "validateRequestParameters": true }
Django Context: # Full validation for JobPost.objects POST # Body schema + required parameters # Rejects invalid Author.objects requests
Example 2: Body-only validator

Validate request body only.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-request-validator \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "body-validator" \
    --validate-request-body \
    --no-validate-request-parameters
Output: { "id": "val456body", "name": "body-validator", "validateRequestBody": true, "validateRequestParameters": false }
Django Context: # Body validation for Skills.objects creation # JSON schema enforcement # Flexible query parameters allowed
Example 3: Parameter-only validator

Validate parameters only.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-request-validator \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "params-validator" \
    --no-validate-request-body \
    --validate-request-parameters
Output: { "id": "val789par", "name": "params-validator", "validateRequestBody": false, "validateRequestParameters": true }
Django Context: # Parameter validation for GET endpoints # Required headers and query params # Location.objects filter validation
Example 4: Minimal validator

Create validator with no validation (passthrough).

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-request-validator \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "passthrough" \
    --no-validate-request-body \
    --no-validate-request-parameters
Output: { "id": "valpass12", "name": "passthrough", "validateRequestBody": false, "validateRequestParameters": false }
Django Context: # No API Gateway validation # Django handles all validation # Flexible JobPost.objects endpoints
Example 5: Strict validator for writes

Strict validation for mutation endpoints.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-request-validator \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "strict-write-validator" \
    --validate-request-body \
    --validate-request-parameters
Output: { "id": "valstrict", "name": "strict-write-validator", "validateRequestBody": true, "validateRequestParameters": true }
Django Context: # Strict validation for POST/PUT/PATCH # Enforces User.objects create constraints # Required fields for Author.objects updates

create-stage

Creates a new stage for a deployment.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-stage \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --stage-name <value> \
    --deployment-id <value> \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--cache-cluster-enabled] \
    [--cache-cluster-size <value>] \
    [--variables <value>]
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): The REST API identifier
  • --stage-name (string): Stage name
  • --deployment-id (string): Deployment ID to associate
  • --description (string): Stage description
  • --cache-cluster-enabled (boolean): Enable caching
  • --cache-cluster-size (string): Cache size
  • --variables (map): Stage variables
Return Values
  • stageName: Stage name
  • deploymentId: Associated deployment
  • cacheClusterEnabled: Cache status
Example 1: Create production stage

Create production stage with caching.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-stage \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name prod \
    --deployment-id dep123abc \
    --description "Production environment for anihpj" \
    --cache-cluster-enabled \
    --cache-cluster-size 0.5
Output: { "stageName": "prod", "deploymentId": "dep123abc", "description": "Production environment for anihpj", "cacheClusterEnabled": true, "cacheClusterSize": "0.5", "cacheClusterStatus": "CREATE_IN_PROGRESS" }
Django Context: # Production stage for JobPost API # 0.5GB cache for Skills.objects queries # Live User.objects endpoints
Example 2: Create staging with variables

Create staging with environment variables.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-stage \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name staging \
    --deployment-id dep456stg \
    --variables djangoSettings=anihpj.settings.staging,dbHost=staging-db.anihpj.com
Output: { "stageName": "staging", "deploymentId": "dep456stg", "variables": { "djangoSettings": "anihpj.settings.staging", "dbHost": "staging-db.anihpj.com" } }
Django Context: # Staging with Django settings override # Separate database for Author.objects tests # Pre-production Location.objects validation
Example 3: Create dev stage

Create development stage without cache.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-stage \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name dev \
    --deployment-id dep789dev \
    --description "Development environment" \
    --no-cache-cluster-enabled
Output: { "stageName": "dev", "deploymentId": "dep789dev", "description": "Development environment", "cacheClusterEnabled": false }
Django Context: # Development stage, no caching # Real-time JobPost.objects changes # Debug Skills.objects operations
Example 4: Create canary stage

Create stage with canary settings.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-stage \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name canary \
    --deployment-id depcan123 \
    --canary-settings percentTraffic=10,deploymentId=depnew456
Output: { "stageName": "canary", "deploymentId": "depcan123", "canarySettings": { "percentTraffic": 10.0, "deploymentId": "depnew456" } }
Django Context: # Canary release for new features # 10% traffic to new User.objects endpoints # Safe rollout for Author.objects changes
Example 5: Create stage with tracing

Create stage with X-Ray tracing enabled.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-stage \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --stage-name monitored \
    --deployment-id deptrc456 \
    --tracing-enabled \
    --tags Environment=production,Monitoring=enabled
Output: { "stageName": "monitored", "deploymentId": "deptrc456", "tracingEnabled": true, "tags": {"Environment": "production", "Monitoring": "enabled"} }
Django Context: # X-Ray tracing for performance # Monitor JobPost.objects latency # Debug Location.objects slow queries

create-usage-plan

Creates a usage plan for API throttling and quota management.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-usage-plan \
    --name <value> \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--api-stages <value>] \
    [--throttle <value>] \
    [--quota <value>]
Parameters
  • --name (string): Usage plan name
  • --description (string): Plan description
  • --api-stages (list): API stages to include
  • --throttle (structure): Throttle settings (burstLimit, rateLimit)
  • --quota (structure): Quota settings (limit, period, offset)
Return Values
  • id: Usage plan identifier
  • name: Plan name
  • throttle: Throttle configuration
  • quota: Quota configuration
Example 1: Create basic usage plan

Create plan with throttling and quota.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan \
    --name "anihpj-standard" \
    --description "Standard tier for jobpost API" \
    --api-stages apiId=abc123def4,stage=prod \
    --throttle burstLimit=100,rateLimit=50 \
    --quota limit=10000,period=MONTH
Output: { "id": "usp123std", "name": "anihpj-standard", "throttle": {"burstLimit": 100, "rateLimit": 50.0}, "quota": {"limit": 10000, "period": "MONTH"} }
Django Context: # Standard tier: 10K requests/month # 50 req/sec for JobPost.objects queries # Burst to 100 for Skills.objects bulk
Example 2: Create premium plan

High-volume usage plan for partners.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan \
    --name "anihpj-premium" \
    --description "Premium tier for high-volume partners" \
    --api-stages apiId=abc123def4,stage=prod \
    --throttle burstLimit=500,rateLimit=200 \
    --quota limit=100000,period=MONTH
Output: { "id": "usp456prm", "name": "anihpj-premium", "throttle": {"burstLimit": 500, "rateLimit": 200.0}, "quota": {"limit": 100000, "period": "MONTH"} }
Django Context: # Premium: 100K requests/month # High-throughput Author.objects sync # Partner JobPost.objects integration
Example 3: Create free tier plan

Limited plan for free users.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan \
    --name "anihpj-free" \
    --description "Free tier with limited access" \
    --api-stages apiId=abc123def4,stage=prod \
    --throttle burstLimit=10,rateLimit=5 \
    --quota limit=1000,period=MONTH
Output: { "id": "usp789fre", "name": "anihpj-free", "throttle": {"burstLimit": 10, "rateLimit": 5.0}, "quota": {"limit": 1000, "period": "MONTH"} }
Django Context: # Free tier: 1K requests/month # Limited User.objects read access # Basic Location.objects queries
Example 4: Create daily quota plan

Plan with daily request limits.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan \
    --name "anihpj-daily" \
    --api-stages apiId=abc123def4,stage=prod \
    --throttle burstLimit=50,rateLimit=20 \
    --quota limit=5000,period=DAY
Output: { "id": "uspdaily1", "name": "anihpj-daily", "quota": {"limit": 5000, "period": "DAY"} }
Django Context: # Daily quota: 5K requests/day # Prevents JobPost.objects abuse # Fair access to Skills.objects
Example 5: Create multi-stage plan

Plan covering multiple API stages.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan \
    --name "anihpj-enterprise" \
    --api-stages apiId=abc123def4,stage=prod apiId=abc123def4,stage=staging \
    --throttle burstLimit=1000,rateLimit=500 \
    --quota limit=1000000,period=MONTH
Output: { "id": "uspent123", "name": "anihpj-enterprise", "apiStages": [ {"apiId": "abc123def4", "stage": "prod"}, {"apiId": "abc123def4", "stage": "staging"} ] }
Django Context: # Enterprise: 1M requests/month # Access to prod + staging environments # Full JobPost.objects CRUD operations

create-usage-plan-key

Associates an API key with a usage plan.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
    --usage-plan-id <value> \
    --key-id <value> \
    --key-type API_KEY
Parameters
  • --usage-plan-id (string): Usage plan identifier
  • --key-id (string): API key identifier
  • --key-type (string): Key type (API_KEY)
Return Values
  • id: Key identifier
  • type: Key type
  • name: Key name
Example 1: Associate key with standard plan

Add API key to standard usage plan.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
    --usage-plan-id usp123std \
    --key-id key123abc \
    --key-type API_KEY
Output: { "id": "key123abc", "type": "API_KEY", "name": "anihpj-production-key" }
Django Context: # Production key linked to standard tier # 10K monthly JobPost.objects requests # Skills.objects access enabled
Example 2: Associate partner key with premium

Link partner key to premium plan.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
    --usage-plan-id usp456prm \
    --key-id key456ptnr \
    --key-type API_KEY
Output: { "id": "key456ptnr", "type": "API_KEY", "name": "partner-acme-corp" }
Django Context: # ACME Corp partner key upgraded # 100K monthly Author.objects sync # Premium JobPost syndication
Example 3: Associate multiple keys

Add multiple keys to same plan.

#!/bin/bash
for KEY_ID in key001 key002 key003; do
    aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
        --usage-plan-id usp123std \
        --key-id $KEY_ID \
        --key-type API_KEY
    echo "Associated: $KEY_ID"
done
Output: Associated: key001 Associated: key002 Associated: key003
Django Context: # Multiple keys share standard quota # Team access to Location.objects # Shared User.objects limits
Example 4: Upgrade key to enterprise

Move key from standard to enterprise plan.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove from standard
aws apigateway delete-usage-plan-key \
    --usage-plan-id usp123std \
    --key-id key789upgrade

# Add to enterprise
aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
    --usage-plan-id uspent123 \
    --key-id key789upgrade \
    --key-type API_KEY
Output: { "id": "key789upgrade", "type": "API_KEY", "name": "enterprise-client" }
Django Context: # Key upgraded to enterprise tier # 1M monthly JobPost.objects requests # Full API access for Author.objects
Example 5: Associate development key

Link dev key to free tier.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
    --usage-plan-id usp789fre \
    --key-id keydev123 \
    --key-type API_KEY
Output: { "id": "keydev123", "type": "API_KEY", "name": "development-testing" }
Django Context: # Dev key on free tier limits # 1K monthly Skills.objects tests # Development Location.objects queries

create-vpc-link

Creates a VPC link for private integrations with Network Load Balancer.

Syntax
aws apigateway create-vpc-link \
    --name <value> \
    --target-arns <value> \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--tags <value>]
Parameters
  • --name (string): VPC link name
  • --target-arns (list): Network Load Balancer ARNs
  • --description (string): VPC link description
  • --tags (map): Resource tags
Return Values
  • id: VPC link identifier
  • name: VPC link name
  • status: VPC link status
  • targetArns: Target NLB ARNs
Example 1: Create VPC link for Django backend

Link API Gateway to private Django servers.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-vpc-link \
    --name "anihpj-django-vpc-link" \
    --target-arns arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/anihpj-django-nlb/abc123 \
    --description "VPC link to Django jobpost backend"
Output: { "id": "vpc123lnk", "name": "anihpj-django-vpc-link", "status": "PENDING", "targetArns": ["arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/anihpj-django-nlb/abc123"] }
Django Context: # VPC link to private Django servers # Secure JobPost.objects backend access # NLB routes to Django EC2 instances
Example 2: Create multi-target VPC link

Link to multiple NLBs for HA.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-vpc-link \
    --name "anihpj-ha-vpc-link" \
    --target-arns arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/nlb-primary/abc \
                  arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/nlb-secondary/xyz \
    --description "HA VPC link with failover"
Output: { "id": "vpchalink", "name": "anihpj-ha-vpc-link", "status": "PENDING", "targetArns": [ "arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/nlb-primary/abc", "arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/nlb-secondary/xyz" ] }
Django Context: # HA configuration for User.objects API # Failover between Django clusters # Resilient Skills.objects endpoints
Example 3: Create tagged VPC link

Create with resource tags for management.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-vpc-link \
    --name "anihpj-prod-vpc-link" \
    --target-arns arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/prod-nlb/def456 \
    --tags Environment=production,Team=backend,App=jobpost
Output: { "id": "vpcprod12", "name": "anihpj-prod-vpc-link", "status": "PENDING", "tags": { "Environment": "production", "Team": "backend", "App": "jobpost" } }
Django Context: # Production VPC link tagged # Backend team ownership # Jobpost app identification
Example 4: Create staging VPC link

Separate VPC link for staging environment.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-vpc-link \
    --name "anihpj-staging-vpc-link" \
    --target-arns arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/staging-nlb/stg789 \
    --description "Staging environment VPC link" \
    --tags Environment=staging
Output: { "id": "vpcstg456", "name": "anihpj-staging-vpc-link", "status": "PENDING" }
Django Context: # Staging VPC link for testing # Pre-production Author.objects validation # Test Location.objects integrations
Example 5: Create internal services VPC link

Link for internal microservices.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway create-vpc-link \
    --name "anihpj-internal-services" \
    --target-arns arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/net/internal-nlb/int123 \
    --description "Internal microservices VPC link"
Output: { "id": "vpcint789", "name": "anihpj-internal-services", "status": "PENDING" }
Django Context: # Internal services API access # Bulk JobPost.objects processing # Admin Skills.objects management

delete-api-key

Deletes an API key from API Gateway.

Syntax
aws apigateway delete-api-key \
    --api-key <value>
Parameters
  • --api-key (string): API key identifier to delete
Return Values
  • No return value on success
Example 1: Delete expired API key

Remove key that's no longer needed.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-api-key \
    --api-key key123expired
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove expired client key # Revoke JobPost.objects access # Client no longer using API
Example 2: Delete compromised key

Immediately revoke compromised credentials.

#!/bin/bash
# Emergency revocation
aws apigateway delete-api-key \
    --api-key key456compromised

echo "Key revoked - issue new key to client"
Output: Key revoked - issue new key to client
Django Context: # Security incident response # Protect User.objects data # Prevent unauthorized access
Example 3: Delete multiple unused keys

Cleanup old API keys in batch.

#!/bin/bash
UNUSED_KEYS=("key001old" "key002old" "key003old")

for KEY in "${UNUSED_KEYS[@]}"; do
    aws apigateway delete-api-key --api-key $KEY
    echo "Deleted: $KEY"
done
Output: Deleted: key001old Deleted: key002old Deleted: key003old
Django Context: # Cleanup inactive client keys # Reduce Skills.objects abuse risk # Maintain key hygiene
Example 4: Delete test environment key

Remove test key after testing complete.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-api-key \
    --api-key keytest789

echo "Test key removed from environment"
Output: Test key removed from environment
Django Context: # Remove test API access # Clean Author.objects test data # Prepare for production
Example 5: Delete with verification

Verify key exists before deletion.

#!/bin/bash
KEY_ID="keytodelete"

if aws apigateway get-api-key --api-key $KEY_ID 2>/dev/null; then
    aws apigateway delete-api-key --api-key $KEY_ID
    echo "Key $KEY_ID deleted successfully"
else
    echo "Key $KEY_ID not found"
fi
Output: Key keytodelete deleted successfully
Django Context: # Safe deletion with check # Prevent Location.objects errors # Idempotent cleanup script

delete-authorizer

Deletes an authorizer from a REST API.

Syntax
aws apigateway delete-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --authorizer-id <value>
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): REST API identifier
  • --authorizer-id (string): Authorizer identifier to delete
Return Values
  • No return value on success
Example 1: Delete deprecated authorizer

Remove old Lambda authorizer.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --authorizer-id auth789old
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove legacy authorizer # Migrate to Cognito for User.objects # Simplify auth architecture
Example 2: Delete test authorizer

Remove authorizer used for testing.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --authorizer-id authtest123

echo "Test authorizer removed"
Output: Test authorizer removed
Django Context: # Clean up test auth # JobPost.objects auth testing complete # Ready for production auth
Example 3: Replace authorizer

Delete old authorizer after creating replacement.

#!/bin/bash
# Create new authorizer first
NEW_AUTH=$(aws apigateway create-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --name "new-cognito-auth" \
    --type COGNITO_USER_POOLS \
    --provider-arns arn:aws:cognito-idp:us-east-1:123456789012:userpool/us-east-1_NewPool \
    --query 'id' --output text)

# Delete old authorizer
aws apigateway delete-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --authorizer-id auth456old

echo "Migrated to new authorizer: $NEW_AUTH"
Output: Migrated to new authorizer: auth789new
Django Context: # Migrate User.objects authentication # New Cognito pool integration # Skills.objects secure access
Example 4: Delete Lambda authorizer

Remove Lambda-based custom authorizer.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-authorizer \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --authorizer-id lambdaauth123
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove custom Lambda auth # Switch to Cognito for Author.objects # Reduce Lambda costs
Example 5: Batch delete authorizers

Remove multiple unused authorizers.

#!/bin/bash
API_ID="abc123def4"

# List and delete unused authorizers
aws apigateway get-authorizers --rest-api-id $API_ID \
    --query 'items[?contains(name, `deprecated`)].id' \
    --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read AUTH_ID; do
    aws apigateway delete-authorizer \
        --rest-api-id $API_ID \
        --authorizer-id $AUTH_ID
    echo "Deleted authorizer: $AUTH_ID"
done
Output: Deleted authorizer: auth001dep Deleted authorizer: auth002dep
Django Context: # Cleanup deprecated authorizers # Streamline Location.objects auth # Reduce API complexity

delete-base-path-mapping

Deletes a base path mapping from a custom domain.

Syntax
aws apigateway delete-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name <value> \
    --base-path <value>
Parameters
  • --domain-name (string): Custom domain name
  • --base-path (string): Base path to delete (use "(none)" for root)
Return Values
  • No return value on success
Example 1: Delete base path mapping

Remove v1 API path mapping.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name api.anihpj.com \
    --base-path v1
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove deprecated v1 API # JobPost.objects migrated to v2 # v1 endpoints decommissioned
Example 2: Delete root mapping

Remove root path mapping.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name api.anihpj.com \
    --base-path "(none)"
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove root API mapping # Skills.objects requires versioned path # Enforce API versioning
Example 3: Migrate to new API version

Delete old mapping when migrating versions.

#!/bin/bash
DOMAIN="api.anihpj.com"

# Create new v3 mapping
aws apigateway create-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name $DOMAIN \
    --base-path v3 \
    --rest-api-id newapi456 \
    --stage prod

# Remove deprecated v2
aws apigateway delete-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name $DOMAIN \
    --base-path v2

echo "Migrated from v2 to v3"
Output: Migrated from v2 to v3
Django Context: # Version migration complete # Author.objects uses v3 endpoints # v2 User.objects deprecated
Example 4: Delete staging mapping

Remove staging environment mapping.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-base-path-mapping \
    --domain-name staging-api.anihpj.com \
    --base-path test

echo "Staging test path removed"
Output: Staging test path removed
Django Context: # Remove test endpoint # Location.objects testing complete # Cleanup staging environment
Example 5: Delete all deprecated mappings

Cleanup all old version mappings.

#!/bin/bash
DOMAIN="api.anihpj.com"
DEPRECATED=("v1" "legacy" "beta")

for PATH in "${DEPRECATED[@]}"; do
    aws apigateway delete-base-path-mapping \
        --domain-name $DOMAIN \
        --base-path $PATH 2>/dev/null && \
    echo "Removed: $PATH" || echo "Not found: $PATH"
done
Output: Removed: v1 Removed: legacy Not found: beta
Django Context: # Cleanup deprecated API versions # All JobPost.objects on current API # Simplified routing structure

delete-client-certificate

Deletes a client certificate used for mutual TLS authentication.

Syntax
aws apigateway delete-client-certificate \
    --client-certificate-id <value>
Parameters
  • --client-certificate-id (string): Client certificate identifier
Return Values
  • No return value on success
Example 1: Delete expired certificate

Remove certificate that has expired.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-client-certificate \
    --client-certificate-id cert123exp
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove expired mTLS cert # JobPost.objects secure endpoint # Renew certificate before deletion
Example 2: Delete rotated certificate

Remove old cert after rotation.

#!/bin/bash
# After deploying new certificate
aws apigateway delete-client-certificate \
    --client-certificate-id cert456old

echo "Old certificate removed after rotation"
Output: Old certificate removed after rotation
Django Context: # Certificate rotation complete # User.objects mTLS updated # Security best practice
Example 3: Cleanup unused certificates

Remove certificates not in use.

#!/bin/bash
# List certificates not attached to any stage
aws apigateway get-client-certificates \
    --query 'items[?expirationDate<`2024-01-01`].clientCertificateId' \
    --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read CERT_ID; do
    aws apigateway delete-client-certificate \
        --client-certificate-id $CERT_ID
    echo "Deleted expired cert: $CERT_ID"
done
Output: Deleted expired cert: cert001 Deleted expired cert: cert002
Django Context: # Cleanup expired certificates # Maintain Skills.objects security # Regular security maintenance
Example 4: Delete test certificate

Remove certificate used in testing.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-client-certificate \
    --client-certificate-id certtest123
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove test mTLS cert # Author.objects testing complete # Clean test environment
Example 5: Delete with verification

Check certificate before deletion.

#!/bin/bash
CERT_ID="certtodelete"

# Verify not in use
STAGES=$(aws apigateway get-stages --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --query "item[?clientCertificateId=='$CERT_ID'].stageName" \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$STAGES" ]; then
    aws apigateway delete-client-certificate \
        --client-certificate-id $CERT_ID
    echo "Certificate deleted safely"
else
    echo "Certificate in use by stages: $STAGES"
fi
Output: Certificate deleted safely
Django Context: # Safe certificate deletion # Verify Location.objects not affected # Prevent service disruption

delete-deployment

Deletes a deployment from a REST API.

Syntax
aws apigateway delete-deployment \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --deployment-id <value>
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): REST API identifier
  • --deployment-id (string): Deployment identifier to delete
Return Values
  • No return value on success
Example 1: Delete old deployment

Remove deployment no longer needed.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-deployment \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --deployment-id dep789old
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Remove old deployment version # JobPost.objects API cleanup # Keep recent deployments only
Example 2: Delete failed deployment

Remove deployment that had issues.

#!/bin/bash
aws apigateway delete-deployment \
    --rest-api-id abc123def4 \
    --deployment-id depfailed123

echo "Failed deployment removed"
Output: Failed deployment removed
Django Context: # Remove broken deployment # User.objects API restored # Rollback complete
Example 3: Cleanup old deployments

Keep only recent deployments.

#!/bin/bash
API_ID="abc123def4"

# Get deployments older than 30 days
aws apigateway get-deployments --rest-api-id $API_ID \
    --query 'items[?createdDate<`2024-01-01`].id' \
    --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read DEP_ID; do
    aws apigateway delete-deployment \
        --rest-api-id $API_ID \
        --deployment-id $DEP_ID
    echo "Deleted old deployment: $DEP_ID"
done
Output: Deleted old deployment: dep001 Deleted old deployment: dep002 Deleted old deployment: dep003
Django Context: # Cleanup deployment history # Skills.objects API maintenance # Keep 5 recent deployments
Example 4: Delete unused deployment

Remove deployment not attached to any stage.

#!/bin/bash
API_ID="abc123def4"
DEP_ID="depunused"

# Verify not in use
IN_USE=$(aws apigateway get-stages --rest-api-id $API_ID \
    --query "item[?deploymentId=='$DEP_ID']" --output text)

if [ -z "$IN_USE" ]; then
    aws apigateway delete-deployment \
        --rest-api-id $API_ID \
        --deployment-id $DEP_ID
    echo "Unused deployment deleted"
fi
Output: Unused deployment deleted
Django Context: # Safe deployment deletion # Author.objects API unaffected # Clean deployment list
Example 5: Delete test deployments

Remove all test environment deployments.

#!/bin/bash
API_ID="abc123def4"

# Delete deployments with test description
aws apigateway get-deployments --rest-api-id $API_ID \
    --query 'items[?contains(description, `test`)].id' \
    --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read DEP_ID; do
    aws apigateway delete-deployment \
        --rest-api-id $API_ID \
        --deployment-id $DEP_ID
done

echo "Test deployments cleaned up"
Output: Test deployments cleaned up
Django Context: # Remove test deployments # Location.objects prod clean # Separate test from production

delete-documentation-part

Deletes a documentation part from a REST API.

Syntax
aws apigateway delete-documentation-part \
    --rest-api-id <value> \
    --documentation-part-id <value>
Parameters
  • --rest-api-id (string): REST API identifier
  • --documentation-part-id (string): Documentation part identifier
Return Values
  • No return value on success
Example 1: Delete outdated documentation

Remove documentation for removed endpoint.

#!/bin/bash
