Django Context: # Traffic distribution: # JobPost.objects: 1523 requests (most popular) # Skills.objects: 892 requests # Location.objects: 456 requests # Author.objects: 234 requests

create-log-group

Creates a new log group for organizing Django application logs. Log groups are containers for log streams and allow you to specify retention settings, access policies, and metric filters for your anihpj application logs.

Syntax
aws logs create-log-group \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    [--kms-key-id <value>] \
    [--tags <value>] \
    [--log-group-class STANDARD|INFREQUENT_ACCESS]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group to create. Use naming convention /aws/django/anihpj-{component} for your Django application
  • --kms-key-id (string): The Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the KMS key to use for encrypting log data
  • --tags (map): Key-value pairs to associate with the log group for cost allocation and organization
  • --log-group-class (string): STANDARD for frequently accessed logs, INFREQUENT_ACCESS for cost savings on rarely queried logs
Return Value
  • No output on success. Use describe-log-groups to verify creation.
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Create Django Application Log Group

Create a log group for the anihpj jobpost application with tags for environment tracking and cost allocation. This log group will store logs from JobPost, Skills, Author, Location, and User model operations.

#!/bin/bash
aws logs create-log-group \
    --log-group-name "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost" \
    --tags Environment=production,App=jobpost,Framework=Django,Team=backend
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: # Log group for Django logging configuration: # LOGGING = { # 'handlers': { # 'cloudwatch': { # 'class': 'watchtower.CloudWatchLogHandler', # 'log_group': '/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost', # } # } # } # Captures logs for: # - JobPost.objects CRUD operations # - Skills.objects validation and changes # - Author.objects activity tracking # - Location.objects geographic queries
Example 2: Create Model-Specific Log Groups

Organize logs by Django model for better organization and targeted querying. Each model gets its own log group with appropriate tags.

#!/bin/bash
# Create log groups for each Django model
for model in jobpost skills author location user; do
    aws logs create-log-group \
        --log-group-name "/aws/django/anihpj/$model" \
        --tags Model=$model,App=jobpost,Framework=Django
    echo "Created log group for $model"
done
Output: Created log group for jobpost Created log group for skills Created log group for author Created log group for location Created log group for user
Django Context: # Separate log groups per model: # /aws/django/anihpj/jobpost → JobPost.objects logs # /aws/django/anihpj/skills → Skills.objects logs # /aws/django/anihpj/author → Author.objects logs # /aws/django/anihpj/location → Location.objects logs # /aws/django/anihpj/user → User.objects logs # Django logger configuration per model: # logger = logging.getLogger('jobpost.models.JobPost')

create-log-stream

Creates a log stream within a log group in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Log streams represent a sequence of log events from the same source, such as a specific Django application instance, container, or microservice component. Each stream contains chronologically ordered events from the anihpj jobpost application, tracking operations on Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models.

Syntax
aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    --log-stream-name <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>] \
    [--generate-cli-skeleton <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group where the stream will be created. Must exist before creating streams. For Django apps, use naming like "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost" or "/aws/django/anihpj/models"
  • --log-stream-name (string, required): The name for the new log stream. Best practice is to include instance ID, date, or model name. Examples: "web-server-01/2025/01/17", "i-0abc123/jobpost", "docker-container-xyz/skills"
  • --cli-input-json (string): Reads arguments from a JSON string or file. Useful for automation scripts deploying multiple Django instances
  • --generate-cli-skeleton (string): Prints JSON skeleton without making API call. Values: input | output
Return Value
  • None: This command does not return any output on success
  • ResourceAlreadyExistsException: Returned if stream name already exists in the log group
  • ResourceNotFoundException: Returned if the specified log group does not exist
  • InvalidParameterException: Returned if stream name contains invalid characters
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Create Daily Log Stream for Django Web Server Instance

Creates a date-partitioned log stream for a specific Django web server instance running the anihpj jobpost application. This organization allows easy filtering of logs by date and server for debugging JobPost and Skills model operations.

#!/bin/bash
# Create daily log stream for Django web server
# Used for tracking JobPost CRUD operations

aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost" \
    --log-stream-name "web-server-01/2025/01/18"

echo "Log stream created successfully for web-server-01"

# Verify the stream was created
aws logs describe-log-streams \
    --log-group-name "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost" \
    --log-stream-name-prefix "web-server-01/2025/01/18"
Output: Log stream created successfully for web-server-01 { "logStreams": [ { "logStreamName": "web-server-01/2025/01/18", "creationTime": 1737187200000, "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost:log-stream:web-server-01/2025/01/18", "storedBytes": 0 } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Configure logger for this specific stream logger = logging.getLogger('django.jobpost') # Sample operations that will be logged to this stream: # Creating a new job post job = JobPost.objects.create( title="Senior Python Developer", company="Tech Corp", description="Django expertise required" ) logger.info(f"Created JobPost: {job.id} - {job.title}") # Query all job posts all_jobs = JobPost.objects.all() print(f"Total JobPosts: {all_jobs.count()}") # Output: Total JobPosts: 1 # Stream will capture these model operations: # - JobPost.objects.create() calls # - JobPost.objects.filter() queries # - JobPost.objects.update() modifications # - Related Skills.objects operations
Example 2: Create Instance-Specific Log Stream with Auto-Discovery

Automatically creates a log stream using the EC2 instance ID and current date. This is useful for auto-scaling environments where multiple Django instances serve the anihpj application and each needs its own log stream for User and Author model tracking.

#!/bin/bash
# Auto-discover instance ID and create stream
# Perfect for auto-scaling Django deployments

# Get EC2 instance metadata
INSTANCE_ID=$(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/instance-id)
INSTANCE_TYPE=$(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/instance-type)
AZ=$(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/placement/availability-zone)
DATE=$(date +%Y/%m/%d)
HOUR=$(date +%H)

echo "Creating log stream for instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Instance Type: $INSTANCE_TYPE"
echo "Availability Zone: $AZ"

# Create stream with instance metadata
aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name "/aws/django/anihpj/users" \
    --log-stream-name "$INSTANCE_ID/$AZ/$DATE/$HOUR"

# Create parallel stream for Author model
aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name "/aws/django/anihpj/authors" \
    --log-stream-name "$INSTANCE_ID/$AZ/$DATE/$HOUR"

echo "Created streams for User and Author models on $INSTANCE_ID"
Output: Creating log stream for instance: i-0abc123def456789 Instance Type: t3.medium Availability Zone: us-east-1a Created streams for User and Author models on i-0abc123def456789 # Verification query shows: { "logStreams": [ { "logStreamName": "i-0abc123def456789/us-east-1a/2025/01/18/14", "creationTime": 1737208800000, "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj/users:log-stream:i-0abc123def456789/us-east-1a/2025/01/18/14" } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Instance-specific logging configuration logger_users = logging.getLogger('django.users') logger_authors = logging.getLogger('django.authors') # User model operations logged to /aws/django/anihpj/users stream new_user = User.objects.create( username="john_developer", email="john@techcorp.com", first_name="John", last_name="Developer" ) logger_users.info(f"Created User: {new_user.id} - {new_user.username}") # Author model operations logged to /aws/django/anihpj/authors stream author = Author.objects.create( user=new_user, bio="Senior Python developer with 10 years experience", website="https://johndev.com" ) logger_authors.info(f"Created Author: {author.id} linked to User: {new_user.id}") # Queryset verification print(User.objects.filter(username="john_developer").values()) # Output: print(Author.objects.select_related('user').all()) # Output: ]>
Example 3: Create Model-Specific Streams for All Django Models

Creates separate log streams for each Django model in the anihpj jobpost application. This separation allows granular log analysis per model - tracking Skills changes separately from Location updates for better debugging and monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
# Create model-specific log streams for Django application
# Enables granular monitoring per model type

LOG_GROUP_PREFIX="/aws/django/anihpj"
INSTANCE_ID="i-0abc123def456789"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M)

# Array of Django models
declare -a MODELS=("skills" "user" "author" "location" "jobpost")

echo "Creating log streams for all Django models..."
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Timestamp: $TIMESTAMP"
echo "========================================="

for model in "${MODELS[@]}"; do
    echo "Creating stream for model: $model"
    
    aws logs create-log-stream \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP_PREFIX/$model" \
        --log-stream-name "$INSTANCE_ID/$TIMESTAMP"
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "  ✓ Successfully created: $LOG_GROUP_PREFIX/$model/$INSTANCE_ID/$TIMESTAMP"
    else
        echo "  ✗ Failed to create stream for $model"
    fi
done

echo "========================================="
echo "All model streams created successfully!"
Output: Creating log streams for all Django models... Instance: i-0abc123def456789 Timestamp: 2025-01-18_14-30 ========================================= Creating stream for model: skills ✓ Successfully created: /aws/django/anihpj/skills/i-0abc123def456789/2025-01-18_14-30 Creating stream for model: user ✓ Successfully created: /aws/django/anihpj/user/i-0abc123def456789/2025-01-18_14-30 Creating stream for model: author ✓ Successfully created: /aws/django/anihpj/author/i-0abc123def456789/2025-01-18_14-30 Creating stream for model: location ✓ Successfully created: /aws/django/anihpj/location/i-0abc123def456789/2025-01-18_14-30 Creating stream for model: jobpost ✓ Successfully created: /aws/django/anihpj/jobpost/i-0abc123def456789/2025-01-18_14-30 ========================================= All model streams created successfully!
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Configure separate loggers for each model loggers = { 'skills': logging.getLogger('django.models.skills'), 'user': logging.getLogger('django.models.user'), 'author': logging.getLogger('django.models.author'), 'location': logging.getLogger('django.models.location'), 'jobpost': logging.getLogger('django.models.jobpost') } # Skills model operations skill1 = Skills.objects.create(name="Python", category="Programming") skill2 = Skills.objects.create(name="Django", category="Framework") skill3 = Skills.objects.create(name="PostgreSQL", category="Database") loggers['skills'].info(f"Created {Skills.objects.count()} skills") print(Skills.objects.all().values_list('name', flat=True)) # Output: # Location model operations location = Location.objects.create( city="San Francisco", state="California", country="USA", remote_allowed=True ) loggers['location'].info(f"Created Location: {location.city}, {location.state}") print(Location.objects.filter(remote_allowed=True)) # Output: ]> # Each model's operations are logged to separate streams for easy filtering
Example 4: Create Log Stream for Docker Container Running Django

Creates a log stream specifically for Docker containers running the Django anihpj application. Uses container ID and task definition for identification, enabling correlation between container logs and CloudWatch for JobPost model debugging.

#!/bin/bash
# Create log stream for Docker container running Django
# Used with ECS Fargate or EC2 container instances

# Get container metadata (ECS)
CONTAINER_ID=$(cat /proc/self/cgroup | grep docker | head -1 | sed 's/.*\///' | cut -c1-12)
TASK_ARN=$(curl -s ${ECS_CONTAINER_METADATA_URI_V4}/task | jq -r '.TaskARN' | awk -F'/' '{print $NF}')
CLUSTER=$(curl -s ${ECS_CONTAINER_METADATA_URI_V4}/task | jq -r '.Cluster' | awk -F'/' '{print $NF}')

# Fallback for local Docker
if [ -z "$CONTAINER_ID" ]; then
    CONTAINER_ID=$(hostname)
fi

TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)

echo "Container ID: $CONTAINER_ID"
echo "Task ARN: $TASK_ARN"
echo "Cluster: $CLUSTER"

# Create stream for container
aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name "/aws/ecs/anihpj-django-cluster" \
    --log-stream-name "container/$CONTAINER_ID/$TASK_ARN/$TIMESTAMP"

# Create additional stream for Django model operations
aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost" \
    --log-stream-name "ecs/$CLUSTER/$CONTAINER_ID/$TIMESTAMP"

echo "Docker container streams created successfully"
Output: Container ID: abc123def456 Task ARN: task-789xyz Cluster: anihpj-production Docker container streams created successfully # Stream verification: { "logStreams": [ { "logStreamName": "container/abc123def456/task-789xyz/2025-01-18", "creationTime": 1737208800000, "storedBytes": 0 }, { "logStreamName": "ecs/anihpj-production/abc123def456/2025-01-18", "creationTime": 1737208801000, "storedBytes": 0 } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging import os # Get container context for logging container_id = os.environ.get('HOSTNAME', 'local') logger = logging.getLogger(f'django.container.{container_id}') # JobPost operations in containerized environment job1 = JobPost.objects.create( title="DevOps Engineer", company="Cloud Solutions Inc", description="Kubernetes and Docker expertise", location=Location.objects.get(city="San Francisco"), salary_min=120000, salary_max=180000 ) logger.info(f"[Container:{container_id}] Created JobPost: {job1.id}") # Add skills to job post python_skill = Skills.objects.get(name="Python") docker_skill = Skills.objects.create(name="Docker", category="DevOps") job1.required_skills.add(python_skill, docker_skill) logger.info(f"[Container:{container_id}] Added {job1.required_skills.count()} skills to JobPost") # Query with container context print(JobPost.objects.filter(title__icontains="DevOps").select_related('location')) # Output: ]> print(job1.required_skills.all().values('name', 'category')) # Output:
Example 5: Create Hourly Rotating Log Streams with Cron Automation

Sets up automated hourly log stream creation using cron for high-traffic Django applications. This rotation strategy prevents individual streams from becoming too large while maintaining organized logs for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

#!/bin/bash
# create_hourly_stream.sh - Called by cron every hour
# Creates rotating log streams for Django anihpj application

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-production"
INSTANCE_ID=$(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/instance-id 2>/dev/null || echo "local-dev")
YEAR=$(date +%Y)
MONTH=$(date +%m)
DAY=$(date +%d)
HOUR=$(date +%H)

STREAM_NAME="$INSTANCE_ID/$YEAR/$MONTH/$DAY/$HOUR"

echo "[$(date)] Creating hourly log stream: $STREAM_NAME"

# Create main application stream
aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --log-stream-name "$STREAM_NAME" \
    2>/dev/null

# Create model-specific hourly streams
for model in jobpost skills user author location; do
    aws logs create-log-stream \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP/models" \
        --log-stream-name "$model/$YEAR/$MONTH/$DAY/$HOUR" \
        2>/dev/null
    echo "  Created stream for $model model"
done

# Log rotation - delete streams older than 7 days
CUTOFF_DATE=$(date -d "7 days ago" +%Y/%m/%d)
echo "Cleaning up streams older than $CUTOFF_DATE"

# Add to crontab:
# 0 * * * * /opt/scripts/create_hourly_stream.sh >> /var/log/stream-rotation.log 2>&1
Output: [2025-01-18 14:00:01] Creating hourly log stream: i-0abc123def456789/2025/01/18/14 Created stream for jobpost model Created stream for skills model Created stream for user model Created stream for author model Created stream for location model Cleaning up streams older than 2025/01/11 # Verification of created streams: { "logStreams": [ {"logStreamName": "i-0abc123def456789/2025/01/18/14", "creationTime": 1737208801000}, {"logStreamName": "jobpost/2025/01/18/14", "creationTime": 1737208802000}, {"logStreamName": "skills/2025/01/18/14", "creationTime": 1737208803000}, {"logStreamName": "user/2025/01/18/14", "creationTime": 1737208804000}, {"logStreamName": "author/2025/01/18/14", "creationTime": 1737208805000}, {"logStreamName": "location/2025/01/18/14", "creationTime": 1737208806000} ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime import logging # Hourly logger configuration current_hour = datetime.now().strftime('%Y/%m/%d/%H') logger = logging.getLogger(f'django.hourly.{current_hour}') # High-traffic operations logged to hourly streams # Create multiple job posts jobs_data = [ {"title": "Python Developer", "company": "StartupABC", "salary_min": 80000}, {"title": "Django Specialist", "company": "WebTech", "salary_min": 95000}, {"title": "Full Stack Engineer", "company": "Innovation Labs", "salary_min": 110000}, ] created_jobs = [] for data in jobs_data: job = JobPost.objects.create(**data) created_jobs.append(job) logger.info(f"[Hour:{current_hour}] Created: {job.title}") # Bulk skill creation skills_list = ["Python", "Django", "REST API", "PostgreSQL", "Redis"] skills_objs = [Skills(name=s, category="Technical") for s in skills_list] Skills.objects.bulk_create(skills_objs, ignore_conflicts=True) logger.info(f"[Hour:{current_hour}] Bulk created {len(skills_list)} skills") # Hourly summary queries print(f"Jobs created this session: {len(created_jobs)}") # Output: Jobs created this session: 3 print(JobPost.objects.filter(salary_min__gte=90000).values('title', 'company')) # Output: print(Skills.objects.filter(category="Technical").count()) # Output: 5 # All operations logged to hourly rotating streams for easy time-based queries

delete-log-group

Deletes a log group and all its associated log streams, archived log events, and metric filters in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. This is a destructive operation that permanently removes all logs for the specified group. Use with caution when managing Django application logs for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models as the data cannot be recovered.

Syntax
aws logs delete-log-group \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>] \
    [--generate-cli-skeleton <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group to delete. All streams and events within this group will be permanently deleted. Example: "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test"
  • --cli-input-json (string): Reads arguments from a JSON string or file path. Useful for automated cleanup scripts
  • --generate-cli-skeleton (string): Prints JSON skeleton for input without making API call. Values: input | output
Return Value
  • None: This command does not return any output on success
  • ResourceNotFoundException: Returned if the specified log group does not exist
  • OperationAbortedException: Returned if multiple requests to update same resource are made simultaneously
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete Test Environment Log Group After Django Testing

Deletes a test environment log group after completing pytest test runs for the anihpj jobpost application. This cleanup prevents accumulation of test logs and reduces CloudWatch costs while preserving production logs for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean up test environment log group after pytest completion
# Run this after CI/CD pipeline test phase

TEST_LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test"

echo "Cleaning up test log group: $TEST_LOG_GROUP"

# First, check if log group exists
aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "$TEST_LOG_GROUP" \
    --query 'logGroups[?logGroupName==`'$TEST_LOG_GROUP'`].logGroupName' \
    --output text

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    # Get storage info before deletion
    STORED_BYTES=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
        --log-group-name-prefix "$TEST_LOG_GROUP" \
        --query 'logGroups[0].storedBytes' \
        --output text)
    
    echo "Log group contains $STORED_BYTES bytes of test logs"
    
    # Delete the test log group
    aws logs delete-log-group \
        --log-group-name "$TEST_LOG_GROUP"
    
    echo "✓ Successfully deleted test log group"
else
    echo "Log group does not exist, nothing to delete"
fi
Output: Cleaning up test log group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test Log group contains 15728640 bytes of test logs ✓ Successfully deleted test log group # Verification that group is gone: aws logs describe-log-groups --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test" { "logGroups": [] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Test environment cleanup context # These test objects were logged to the deleted log group # Test data that was created during pytest: test_skills = Skills.objects.filter(name__startswith="test_") print(f"Test skills count: {test_skills.count()}") # Output: Test skills count: 25 test_users = User.objects.filter(username__startswith="test_user_") print(f"Test users count: {test_users.count()}") # Output: Test users count: 10 # Clean up test data from database too test_skills.delete() test_users.delete() # Verify production log group still exists # /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost (production) - NOT deleted prod_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True) print(f"Production JobPosts still tracked: {prod_jobs.count()}") # Output: Production JobPosts still tracked: 150
Example 2: Delete Old Development Log Groups with Date Pattern

Deletes development log groups that are older than a specified date threshold. This automated cleanup removes outdated Django model logs while keeping recent development activity for debugging JobPost and Skills operations.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete old development log groups by date pattern
# Keeps last 30 days of development logs

ENVIRONMENT="development"
CUTOFF_EPOCH=$(date -d "30 days ago" +%s)000  # Convert to milliseconds

echo "Deleting development log groups older than 30 days"
echo "Cutoff timestamp: $CUTOFF_EPOCH"

# List all development log groups
LOG_GROUPS=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django/anihpj-dev" \
    --query 'logGroups[*].[logGroupName,creationTime]' \
    --output text)

# Process each log group
while IFS=$'\t' read -r log_group creation_time; do
    if [ -n "$log_group" ] && [ "$creation_time" -lt "$CUTOFF_EPOCH" ]; then
        echo "Deleting old log group: $log_group (created: $creation_time)"
        
        aws logs delete-log-group \
            --log-group-name "$log_group"
        
        echo "  ✓ Deleted: $log_group"
    else
        echo "Keeping recent log group: $log_group"
    fi
done <<< "$LOG_GROUPS"

echo "Development log cleanup completed"
Output: Deleting development log groups older than 30 days Cutoff timestamp: 1734566400000 Deleting old log group: /aws/django/anihpj-dev-20241201 (created: 1733097600000) ✓ Deleted: /aws/django/anihpj-dev-20241201 Deleting old log group: /aws/django/anihpj-dev-20241210 (created: 1733875200000) ✓ Deleted: /aws/django/anihpj-dev-20241210 Keeping recent log group: /aws/django/anihpj-dev-20250115 Keeping recent log group: /aws/django/anihpj-dev-20250118 Development log cleanup completed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime, timedelta import logging logger = logging.getLogger('django.cleanup') # Development data context - old logs being deleted cutoff_date = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=30) # Development JobPosts created before cutoff (logs now deleted) old_dev_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__lt=cutoff_date, environment='development' ) print(f"Old development jobs (logs deleted): {old_dev_jobs.count()}") # Output: Old development jobs (logs deleted): 45 # Recent development activity (logs preserved) recent_dev_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__gte=cutoff_date, environment='development' ) print(f"Recent development jobs (logs kept): {recent_dev_jobs.count()}") # Output: Recent development jobs (logs kept): 23 # Skills used in development dev_skills = Skills.objects.filter( jobpost__environment='development' ).distinct() print(dev_skills.values_list('name', flat=True)) # Output:
Example 3: Delete Multiple Model-Specific Log Groups During Migration

Deletes multiple model-specific log groups when migrating from separate logging to consolidated logging strategy. This is useful when restructuring Django application logging for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models into a unified log group.

#!/bin/bash
# Migration script: Delete old model-specific log groups
# Consolidating to single log group with stream-based separation

OLD_PREFIX="/aws/django/anihpj/models"
MODELS=("skills" "user" "author" "location" "jobpost")
BACKUP_BUCKET="s3://anihpj-log-backups"

echo "=== Log Group Migration: Separate → Consolidated ==="
echo "Old structure: $OLD_PREFIX/{model}"
echo "New structure: /aws/django/anihpj-unified/models/{model}"

# Export logs to S3 before deletion (backup)
for model in "${MODELS[@]}"; do
    OLD_GROUP="$OLD_PREFIX/$model"
    
    echo ""
    echo "Processing: $OLD_GROUP"
    
    # Check if group exists
    EXISTS=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
        --log-group-name-prefix "$OLD_GROUP" \
        --query 'logGroups[?logGroupName==`'$OLD_GROUP'`].logGroupName' \
        --output text)
    
    if [ -n "$EXISTS" ]; then
        # Create export task to S3 (backup)
        echo "  Creating backup export to S3..."
        aws logs create-export-task \
            --log-group-name "$OLD_GROUP" \
            --from 0 \
            --to $(date +%s)000 \
            --destination "$BACKUP_BUCKET" \
            --destination-prefix "migration-backup/$model" \
            2>/dev/null
        
        # Delete the old log group
        echo "  Deleting old log group..."
        aws logs delete-log-group \
            --log-group-name "$OLD_GROUP"
        
        echo "  ✓ Deleted: $OLD_GROUP"
    else
        echo "  ⊘ Log group does not exist: $OLD_GROUP"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "=== Migration Complete ==="
echo "Old model-specific log groups have been deleted"
echo "Backups exported to: $BACKUP_BUCKET/migration-backup/"
Output: === Log Group Migration: Separate → Consolidated === Old structure: /aws/django/anihpj/models/{model} New structure: /aws/django/anihpj-unified/models/{model} Processing: /aws/django/anihpj/models/skills Creating backup export to S3... Deleting old log group... ✓ Deleted: /aws/django/anihpj/models/skills Processing: /aws/django/anihpj/models/user Creating backup export to S3... Deleting old log group... ✓ Deleted: /aws/django/anihpj/models/user Processing: /aws/django/anihpj/models/author Creating backup export to S3... Deleting old log group... ✓ Deleted: /aws/django/anihpj/models/author Processing: /aws/django/anihpj/models/location Creating backup export to S3... Deleting old log group... ✓ Deleted: /aws/django/anihpj/models/location Processing: /aws/django/anihpj/models/jobpost Creating backup export to S3... Deleting old log group... ✓ Deleted: /aws/django/anihpj/models/jobpost === Migration Complete === Old model-specific log groups have been deleted Backups exported to: s3://anihpj-log-backups/migration-backup/
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Migration context - all models now log to unified group # Old logging configuration (deleted): # - /aws/django/anihpj/models/skills → Skills.objects operations # - /aws/django/anihpj/models/user → User.objects operations # - /aws/django/anihpj/models/author → Author.objects operations # - /aws/django/anihpj/models/location → Location.objects operations # - /aws/django/anihpj/models/jobpost → JobPost.objects operations # New unified logging configuration: unified_logger = logging.getLogger('django.models.unified') # All models now use single log group with stream separation # Model operation counts at migration time: print(f"Skills: {Skills.objects.count()}") # Output: Skills: 45 print(f"Users: {User.objects.count()}") # Output: Users: 120 print(f"Authors: {Author.objects.count()}") # Output: Authors: 85 print(f"Locations: {Location.objects.count()}") # Output: Locations: 30 print(f"JobPosts: {JobPost.objects.count()}") # Output: JobPosts: 250 # Example unified logging job = JobPost.objects.first() unified_logger.info(f"[jobpost] Accessed: {job.title}") # Now logs to: /aws/django/anihpj-unified/models/jobpost stream
Example 4: Delete Staging Environment Log Group with Confirmation

Safely deletes a staging environment log group with user confirmation and detailed logging of the deletion process. Includes validation checks to prevent accidental deletion of production Django application logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe deletion of staging log group with confirmation
# Includes production protection safeguards

LOG_GROUP_TO_DELETE="/aws/django/anihpj-staging"
PROTECTED_PATTERNS=("production" "prod" "live")

echo "╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  CloudWatch Log Group Deletion - Staging Cleanup       ║"
echo "╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo ""

# Check for protected patterns (production safety)
for pattern in "${PROTECTED_PATTERNS[@]}"; do
    if [[ "$LOG_GROUP_TO_DELETE" == *"$pattern"* ]]; then
        echo "❌ ERROR: Cannot delete log group containing '$pattern'"
        echo "   This appears to be a production log group!"
        exit 1
    fi
done

# Get log group details before deletion
echo "📋 Log Group Details:"
LOG_GROUP_INFO=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "$LOG_GROUP_TO_DELETE" \
    --query 'logGroups[0]' \
    --output json)

echo "$LOG_GROUP_INFO" | jq '.'

STORED_MB=$(echo "$LOG_GROUP_INFO" | jq '.storedBytes / 1048576 | floor')
STREAM_COUNT=$(aws logs describe-log-streams \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP_TO_DELETE" \
    --query 'length(logStreams)' \
    --output text)

echo ""
echo "📊 Summary:"
echo "   Log Group: $LOG_GROUP_TO_DELETE"
echo "   Stored Data: ${STORED_MB} MB"
echo "   Log Streams: $STREAM_COUNT"
echo ""

# Confirmation prompt
read -p "⚠️  Are you sure you want to delete this log group? (type 'DELETE' to confirm): " CONFIRM

if [ "$CONFIRM" == "DELETE" ]; then
    echo ""
    echo "🗑️  Deleting log group..."
    
    aws logs delete-log-group \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP_TO_DELETE"
    
    echo "✅ Log group deleted successfully: $LOG_GROUP_TO_DELETE"
else
    echo ""
    echo "❌ Deletion cancelled. Log group preserved."
    exit 0
fi
Output: ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ CloudWatch Log Group Deletion - Staging Cleanup ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ 📋 Log Group Details: { "logGroupName": "/aws/django/anihpj-staging", "creationTime": 1735689600000, "retentionInDays": 14, "metricFilterCount": 2, "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-staging:*", "storedBytes": 52428800 } 📊 Summary: Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-staging Stored Data: 50 MB Log Streams: 15 ⚠️ Are you sure you want to delete this log group? (type 'DELETE' to confirm): DELETE 🗑️ Deleting log group... ✅ Log group deleted successfully: /aws/django/anihpj-staging
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Staging environment context - logs now deleted # Staging database still contains test data: staging_jobs = JobPost.objects.using('staging').all() print(f"Staging JobPosts (DB): {staging_jobs.count()}") # Output: Staging JobPosts (DB): 75 # Staging model distribution print(JobPost.objects.using('staging').values('status').annotate( count=models.Count('id') )) # Output: # Production logs remain intact prod_logger = logging.getLogger('django.production') prod_jobs = JobPost.objects.using('default').filter(is_active=True) print(f"Production JobPosts (logs preserved): {prod_jobs.count()}") # Output: Production JobPosts (logs preserved): 250 # Staging Skills that were logged (logs now gone) staging_skills = Skills.objects.using('staging').all() print(staging_skills.values_list('name', 'category')) # Output:
Example 5: Batch Delete Empty Log Groups Across All Django Applications

Identifies and deletes empty log groups (0 stored bytes) across all Django application namespaces. This cleanup removes unused log groups created during development while preserving groups with actual JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location model logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch delete empty log groups to reduce clutter
# Only deletes groups with 0 stored bytes

PREFIX="/aws/django"
DELETED_COUNT=0
PRESERVED_COUNT=0

echo "╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  Empty Log Group Cleanup - Django Applications            ║"
echo "╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo ""
echo "Scanning for empty log groups under: $PREFIX"
echo ""

# Get all log groups with the prefix
LOG_GROUPS=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --query 'logGroups[*].[logGroupName,storedBytes]' \
    --output text)

# Process each log group
while IFS=$'\t' read -r log_group stored_bytes; do
    if [ -n "$log_group" ]; then
        if [ "$stored_bytes" == "0" ] || [ -z "$stored_bytes" ]; then
            echo "🗑️  Empty log group found: $log_group"
            
            aws logs delete-log-group \
                --log-group-name "$log_group"
            
            echo "   ✓ Deleted"
            ((DELETED_COUNT++))
        else
            STORED_MB=$((stored_bytes / 1048576))
            echo "📦 Preserving: $log_group (${STORED_MB} MB)"
            ((PRESERVED_COUNT++))
        fi
    fi
done <<< "$LOG_GROUPS"

echo ""
echo "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
echo "📊 Cleanup Summary:"
echo "   Deleted (empty): $DELETED_COUNT log groups"
echo "   Preserved (with data): $PRESERVED_COUNT log groups"
echo "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
Output: ╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ Empty Log Group Cleanup - Django Applications ║ ╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ Scanning for empty log groups under: /aws/django 🗑️ Empty log group found: /aws/django/anihpj-feature-branch-123 ✓ Deleted 🗑️ Empty log group found: /aws/django/anihpj-test-20241201 ✓ Deleted 🗑️ Empty log group found: /aws/django/anihpj-debug-temp ✓ Deleted 📦 Preserving: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost (50 MB) 📦 Preserving: /aws/django/anihpj-production (125 MB) 📦 Preserving: /aws/django/anihpj/skills (10 MB) 📦 Preserving: /aws/django/anihpj/users (25 MB) 🗑️ Empty log group found: /aws/django/anihpj-pr-456 ✓ Deleted ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ 📊 Cleanup Summary: Deleted (empty): 4 log groups Preserved (with data): 4 log groups ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Cleanup context - empty log groups were from abandoned features/PRs # Preserved log groups contain active model data: # /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost → JobPost operations print(f"JobPost records: {JobPost.objects.count()}") # Output: JobPost records: 250 # /aws/django/anihpj-production → All production models print(f"Active jobs: {JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count()}") # Output: Active jobs: 180 # /aws/django/anihpj/skills → Skills operations skills_with_jobs = Skills.objects.filter(jobpost__isnull=False).distinct() print(f"Skills linked to jobs: {skills_with_jobs.count()}") # Output: Skills linked to jobs: 35 # /aws/django/anihpj/users → User operations active_authors = Author.objects.filter( user__is_active=True ).select_related('user') print(f"Active authors: {active_authors.count()}") # Output: Active authors: 85 # Deleted empty groups were from: # - Feature branches never merged # - PR preview environments # - Debug/test sessions that created no logs # - Abandoned development experiments

delete-log-stream

Deletes a log stream and permanently removes all archived log events associated with it from Amazon CloudWatch Logs. This operation is irreversible and should be used carefully when managing Django application logs. Each stream typically contains chronological events from specific instances or time periods tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs delete-log-stream \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    --log-stream-name <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>] \
    [--generate-cli-skeleton <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group containing the stream to delete. Example: "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
  • --log-stream-name (string, required): The name of the log stream to delete. Example: "web-server-01/2024/12/01" or "i-0abc123def/2025/01/15"
  • --cli-input-json (string): Reads arguments from JSON string or file for batch operations
  • --generate-cli-skeleton (string): Prints JSON skeleton without API call. Values: input | output
Return Value
  • None: This command does not return any output on success
  • ResourceNotFoundException: Returned if log group or stream does not exist
  • OperationAbortedException: Returned if concurrent operations conflict
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete Old Daily Log Stream from Django Application

Deletes an old daily log stream from the Django anihpj jobpost application. This is useful for removing outdated logs from specific dates while preserving recent streams that contain current JobPost and Skills model operation logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete old daily log stream
# Cleans up logs from December 2024

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
OLD_STREAM="web-server-01/2024/12/01"

echo "Deleting old log stream from December 2024"
echo "Log Group: $LOG_GROUP"
echo "Stream: $OLD_STREAM"

# Check stream exists and get info
STREAM_INFO=$(aws logs describe-log-streams \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --log-stream-name-prefix "$OLD_STREAM" \
    --query 'logStreams[0]' \
    --output json 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$STREAM_INFO" ] && [ "$STREAM_INFO" != "null" ]; then
    STORED_BYTES=$(echo "$STREAM_INFO" | jq '.storedBytes')
    LAST_EVENT=$(echo "$STREAM_INFO" | jq '.lastEventTimestamp')
    
    echo "Stream found:"
    echo "  Stored bytes: $STORED_BYTES"
    echo "  Last event: $LAST_EVENT"
    
    # Delete the stream
    aws logs delete-log-stream \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
        --log-stream-name "$OLD_STREAM"
    
    echo "✓ Successfully deleted stream: $OLD_STREAM"
else
    echo "Stream not found: $OLD_STREAM"
fi
Output: Deleting old log stream from December 2024 Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Stream: web-server-01/2024/12/01 Stream found: Stored bytes: 1048576 Last event: 1733097599000 ✓ Successfully deleted stream: web-server-01/2024/12/01
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import date import logging logger = logging.getLogger('django.cleanup') # Context: December 2024 logs being deleted # These JobPost operations were logged in that stream: dec_2024_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__year=2024, created_at__month=12, created_at__day=1 ) print(f"JobPosts from Dec 1, 2024: {dec_2024_jobs.count()}") # Output: JobPosts from Dec 1, 2024: 12 # Those jobs still exist in database, just logs removed for job in dec_2024_jobs[:3]: print(f" - {job.title} at {job.company}") # Output: # - Python Developer at TechCorp # - Django Engineer at StartupXYZ # - Backend Developer at DataInc # Current streams still have recent logs recent_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(created_at__gte=date(2025, 1, 1)) print(f"Recent JobPosts (logs preserved): {recent_jobs.count()}") # Output: Recent JobPosts (logs preserved): 45
Example 2: Batch Delete Multiple Old Streams by Date Pattern

Deletes multiple log streams matching a date pattern from the Django application logs. Removes all streams older than 30 days to manage storage costs while maintaining recent User, Author, and Location model operation logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch delete streams older than 30 days
# Iterates through and removes outdated streams

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
CUTOFF_DATE=$(date -d "30 days ago" +%Y/%m/%d)
DELETED_COUNT=0

echo "╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  Log Stream Cleanup - Delete Streams > 30 Days    ║"
echo "╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo "Log Group: $LOG_GROUP"
echo "Cutoff Date: $CUTOFF_DATE"
echo ""

# Get all streams sorted by last event time
STREAMS=$(aws logs describe-log-streams \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --order-by LastEventTime \
    --query 'logStreams[*].[logStreamName,lastEventTimestamp]' \
    --output text)

# Calculate cutoff timestamp (30 days ago in milliseconds)
CUTOFF_EPOCH=$(date -d "30 days ago" +%s)000

while IFS=$'\t' read -r stream_name last_event; do
    if [ -n "$stream_name" ] && [ "$last_event" -lt "$CUTOFF_EPOCH" ]; then
        echo "Deleting old stream: $stream_name"
        echo "  Last event: $(date -d @$((last_event/1000)) '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')"
        
        aws logs delete-log-stream \
            --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
            --log-stream-name "$stream_name"
        
        ((DELETED_COUNT++))
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    fi
done <<< "$STREAMS"

echo ""
echo "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════"
echo "Cleanup complete: $DELETED_COUNT streams deleted"
echo "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════"
Output: ╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ Log Stream Cleanup - Delete Streams > 30 Days ║ ╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Cutoff Date: 2024/12/19 Deleting old stream: web-server-01/2024/12/01 Last event: 2024-12-01 23:59:45 ✓ Deleted Deleting old stream: web-server-01/2024/12/10 Last event: 2024-12-10 18:30:22 ✓ Deleted Deleting old stream: web-server-02/2024/12/15 Last event: 2024-12-15 14:45:10 ✓ Deleted Deleting old stream: i-0abc123/2024/12/18 Last event: 2024-12-18 09:15:33 ✓ Deleted ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════ Cleanup complete: 4 streams deleted ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime, timedelta import logging # 30-day cleanup context cutoff = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=30) # Model operations logged in deleted streams: old_users = User.objects.filter(date_joined__lt=cutoff) print(f"Users from deleted period: {old_users.count()}") # Output: Users from deleted period: 25 old_authors = Author.objects.filter(created_at__lt=cutoff) print(f"Authors from deleted period: {old_authors.count()}") # Output: Authors from deleted period: 18 old_locations = Location.objects.filter(created_at__lt=cutoff) print(f"Locations from deleted period: {old_locations.count()}") # Output: Locations from deleted period: 8 # Recent data still has logs in preserved streams recent_activity = JobPost.objects.filter( updated_at__gte=cutoff ).select_related('location') print(f"Recent JobPosts (logs preserved): {recent_activity.count()}") # Output: Recent JobPosts (logs preserved): 120 # Sample recent job with location job = recent_activity.first() print(f"Latest: {job.title} in {job.location.city}") # Output: Latest: Senior Python Dev in San Francisco
Example 3: Delete Instance-Specific Streams After EC2 Termination

Removes log streams associated with terminated EC2 instances running Django. When an instance is terminated, its log streams become orphaned and should be cleaned up to maintain organized logging for the anihpj jobpost application.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean up log streams from terminated EC2 instances
# Run after instance termination to remove orphaned streams

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-production"
TERMINATED_INSTANCE="i-0dead123instance"

echo "Cleaning up streams for terminated instance: $TERMINATED_INSTANCE"
echo "Log Group: $LOG_GROUP"
echo ""

# Find all streams for this instance
INSTANCE_STREAMS=$(aws logs describe-log-streams \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --log-stream-name-prefix "$TERMINATED_INSTANCE" \
    --query 'logStreams[*].logStreamName' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$INSTANCE_STREAMS" ]; then
    echo "No streams found for instance: $TERMINATED_INSTANCE"
    exit 0
fi

# Count streams
STREAM_COUNT=$(echo "$INSTANCE_STREAMS" | wc -w)
echo "Found $STREAM_COUNT streams to delete"
echo ""

# Delete each stream
for stream in $INSTANCE_STREAMS; do
    echo "Deleting: $stream"
    
    aws logs delete-log-stream \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
        --log-stream-name "$stream"
    
    echo "  ✓ Deleted"
done

echo ""
echo "Cleanup complete for terminated instance: $TERMINATED_INSTANCE"
echo "Deleted $STREAM_COUNT orphaned log streams"
Output: Cleaning up streams for terminated instance: i-0dead123instance Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-production Found 5 streams to delete Deleting: i-0dead123instance/2025/01/10 ✓ Deleted Deleting: i-0dead123instance/2025/01/11 ✓ Deleted Deleting: i-0dead123instance/2025/01/12 ✓ Deleted Deleting: i-0dead123instance/2025/01/13 ✓ Deleted Deleting: i-0dead123instance/2025/01/14 ✓ Deleted Cleanup complete for terminated instance: i-0dead123instance Deleted 5 orphaned log streams
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Instance termination context # Terminated instance handled these model operations: # The instance processed job applications before termination terminated_instance_id = "i-0dead123instance" # JobPosts created/updated by this instance (data preserved in DB) jobs_from_instance = JobPost.objects.filter( metadata__contains={'instance': terminated_instance_id} ) print(f"JobPosts processed by terminated instance: {jobs_from_instance.count()}") # Output: JobPosts processed by terminated instance: 45 # Skills associated with those jobs related_skills = Skills.objects.filter( jobpost__in=jobs_from_instance ).distinct() print(f"Skills used: {related_skills.count()}") # Output: Skills used: 23 print(related_skills.values_list('name', flat=True)[:5]) # Output: # Active instances still logging # Their streams are preserved active_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).exclude( metadata__contains={'instance': terminated_instance_id} ) print(f"Jobs on active instances: {active_jobs.count()}") # Output: Jobs on active instances: 205
Example 4: Delete Model-Specific Streams During Schema Migration

Removes old log streams for a specific Django model before migrating to a new logging schema. This prepares for clean logging after model changes to Skills, User, Author, Location, or JobPost are deployed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete old model streams before schema migration
# Prepares for clean logging with new model structure

MODEL_NAME="jobpost"
LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj/models"
MIGRATION_MARKER="pre-migration-v2"

echo "╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  Pre-Migration Cleanup: $MODEL_NAME Model Streams          ║"
echo "╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo ""
echo "Preparing for JobPost model migration to v2 schema"
echo "Removing old streams with deprecated log format"
echo ""

# Get all streams for this model
MODEL_STREAMS=$(aws logs describe-log-streams \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --log-stream-name-prefix "$MODEL_NAME/" \
    --query 'logStreams[*].[logStreamName,storedBytes]' \
    --output text)

TOTAL_BYTES=0
STREAM_COUNT=0

while IFS=$'\t' read -r stream_name stored_bytes; do
    if [ -n "$stream_name" ]; then
        echo "Processing: $stream_name"
        echo "  Size: $((stored_bytes / 1024)) KB"
        
        # Export to S3 before deletion (optional backup)
        # aws logs create-export-task ...
        
        # Delete the stream
        aws logs delete-log-stream \
            --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
            --log-stream-name "$stream_name"
        
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
        
        TOTAL_BYTES=$((TOTAL_BYTES + stored_bytes))
        ((STREAM_COUNT++))
    fi
done <<< "$MODEL_STREAMS"

echo ""
echo "════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
echo "Pre-migration cleanup complete"
echo "  Streams deleted: $STREAM_COUNT"
echo "  Storage freed: $((TOTAL_BYTES / 1048576)) MB"
echo ""
echo "Ready to deploy JobPost model v2 migration"
echo "════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
Output: ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ Pre-Migration Cleanup: jobpost Model Streams ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ Preparing for JobPost model migration to v2 schema Removing old streams with deprecated log format Processing: jobpost/i-0abc123/2025/01/10 Size: 512 KB ✓ Deleted Processing: jobpost/i-0abc123/2025/01/11 Size: 480 KB ✓ Deleted Processing: jobpost/i-0def456/2025/01/12 Size: 520 KB ✓ Deleted Processing: jobpost/i-0def456/2025/01/13 Size: 495 KB ✓ Deleted ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Pre-migration cleanup complete Streams deleted: 4 Storage freed: 2 MB Ready to deploy JobPost model v2 migration ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Migration context - preparing for JobPost v2 schema logger = logging.getLogger('django.migrations') # Current JobPost model (v1) - logs deleted, data preserved all_jobs = JobPost.objects.all() print(f"Total JobPosts in database: {all_jobs.count()}") # Output: Total JobPosts in database: 250 # JobPost v2 will add new fields: # - remote_type (choices: full, hybrid, onsite) # - experience_level (choices: entry, mid, senior, lead) # - application_deadline (DateField) # Related models unaffected by migration: print(f"Skills: {Skills.objects.count()}") # Output: Skills: 45 print(f"Users: {User.objects.count()}") # Output: Users: 120 print(f"Authors: {Author.objects.count()}") # Output: Authors: 85 print(f"Locations: {Location.objects.count()}") # Output: Locations: 30 # After migration, new logging streams will use v2 format: # - Includes new fields in log entries # - Structured JSON logging for better querying # - Instance-aware stream naming convention
Example 5: Automated Hourly Stream Rotation and Cleanup

Implements automated hourly log stream rotation by deleting streams older than 24 hours and creating new ones. This maintains efficient logging for high-traffic Django applications handling Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost operations.

#!/bin/bash
# rotate_streams.sh - Hourly stream rotation script
# Delete old hourly streams and create new ones
# Cron: 0 * * * * /opt/scripts/rotate_streams.sh

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-high-traffic"
INSTANCE_ID=$(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/instance-id 2>/dev/null || echo "local")
CURRENT_HOUR=$(date +%Y/%m/%d/%H)
RETENTION_HOURS=24

echo "[$(date)] Starting hourly stream rotation"
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Current hour: $CURRENT_HOUR"

# 1. Delete streams older than retention period
echo ""
echo "Step 1: Cleaning old streams (>$RETENTION_HOURS hours)"

CUTOFF_EPOCH=$(date -d "$RETENTION_HOURS hours ago" +%s)000

OLD_STREAMS=$(aws logs describe-log-streams \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --log-stream-name-prefix "$INSTANCE_ID/" \
    --query "logStreams[?lastEventTimestamp<\`$CUTOFF_EPOCH\`].logStreamName" \
    --output text)

DELETED=0
for stream in $OLD_STREAMS; do
    echo "  Deleting: $stream"
    aws logs delete-log-stream \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
        --log-stream-name "$stream"
    ((DELETED++))
done

echo "  Deleted $DELETED old streams"

# 2. Create new hourly stream
echo ""
echo "Step 2: Creating new hourly stream"

NEW_STREAM="$INSTANCE_ID/$CURRENT_HOUR"
aws logs create-log-stream \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --log-stream-name "$NEW_STREAM" \
    2>/dev/null

echo "  Created: $NEW_STREAM"

# 3. Summary
echo ""
echo "[$(date)] Rotation complete"
echo "  Old streams deleted: $DELETED"
echo "  New stream: $NEW_STREAM"
Output: [2025-01-18 15:00:01] Starting hourly stream rotation Instance: i-0abc123def456789 Current hour: 2025/01/18/15 Step 1: Cleaning old streams (>24 hours) Deleting: i-0abc123def456789/2025/01/17/14 Deleting: i-0abc123def456789/2025/01/17/13 Deleting: i-0abc123def456789/2025/01/17/12 Deleted 3 old streams Step 2: Creating new hourly stream Created: i-0abc123def456789/2025/01/18/15 [2025-01-18 15:00:03] Rotation complete Old streams deleted: 3 New stream: i-0abc123def456789/2025/01/18/15
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime, timedelta import logging # Hourly rotation logging context current_hour = datetime.now().strftime('%H:00') logger = logging.getLogger(f'django.hourly') # High-traffic operations during this hour window hourly_stats = { 'jobs_created': JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__hour=datetime.now().hour ).count(), 'skills_added': Skills.objects.filter( created_at__hour=datetime.now().hour ).count(), 'users_registered': User.objects.filter( date_joined__hour=datetime.now().hour ).count() } print(f"Hourly stats at {current_hour}:") print(f" JobPosts created: {hourly_stats['jobs_created']}") # Output: JobPosts created: 15 print(f" Skills added: {hourly_stats['skills_added']}") # Output: Skills added: 5 print(f" Users registered: {hourly_stats['users_registered']}") # Output: Users registered: 8 # Top job categories this hour top_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__gte=datetime.now() - timedelta(hours=1) ).values('category').annotate( count=models.Count('id') ).order_by('-count')[:3] print(f"Top categories this hour: {list(top_jobs)}") # Output: Top categories this hour: [{'category': 'Engineering', 'count': 8}, {'category': 'Design', 'count': 4}, {'category': 'Marketing', 'count': 3}] # Each hour's logs in separate stream for easy analysis # 24-hour retention keeps recent debugging capability

describe-log-groups

Lists and describes the log groups in your AWS account within Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Returns comprehensive information about each log group including creation time, retention settings, stored bytes, metric filter count, and KMS encryption details. Essential for monitoring Django application logging infrastructure for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs describe-log-groups \
    [--account-identifiers <value>] \
    [--log-group-name-prefix <value>] \
    [--log-group-name-pattern <value>] \
    [--include-linked-accounts] \
    [--log-group-class <value>] \
    [--limit <value>] \
    [--next-token <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name-prefix (string): Filter results to log groups with names starting with this prefix. Example: "/aws/django/anihpj" returns all anihpj application log groups
  • --log-group-name-pattern (string): Pattern match using wildcard (*) for flexible filtering. Example: "*jobpost*" matches any group containing "jobpost"
  • --limit (integer): Maximum number of log groups to return (1-50). Default: 50. Use with --next-token for pagination
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token from previous response for retrieving next set of results
  • --include-linked-accounts: Include log groups from linked accounts in AWS Organizations
  • --log-group-class (string): Filter by log class: STANDARD | INFREQUENT_ACCESS. Standard for active Django apps, IA for archived logs
  • --account-identifiers (list): List of account IDs to include when querying cross-account
Return Value
  • logGroups (Array): List of log group objects matching the query criteria
  • logGroupName (string): Name of the log group
  • creationTime (long): Creation timestamp in milliseconds since epoch
  • retentionInDays (integer): Number of days logs are retained (null = never expire)
  • metricFilterCount (integer): Number of metric filters associated with the group
  • arn (string): Amazon Resource Name for the log group
  • storedBytes (long): Total bytes of log data stored
  • kmsKeyId (string): KMS key ID if encryption is enabled
  • dataProtectionStatus (string): Data protection policy status
  • nextToken (string): Token for pagination if more results exist
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List All Django Application Log Groups with Full Details

Retrieves comprehensive information about all log groups for the Django anihpj jobpost application. Shows storage usage, retention policies, and metric filters for groups tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

#!/bin/bash
# List all Django application log groups with full details
# Useful for infrastructure monitoring and cost analysis

echo "╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  Django Application Log Groups - Full Inventory               ║"
echo "╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo ""

# Get all Django log groups with full details
aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django/anihpj" \
    --output json | jq '
    .logGroups[] | {
        name: .logGroupName,
        created: (.creationTime/1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")),
        retention: (if .retentionInDays then "\(.retentionInDays) days" else "Never expires" end),
        storage_mb: ((.storedBytes // 0) / 1048576 | floor),
        metric_filters: (.metricFilterCount // 0),
        encrypted: (if .kmsKeyId then "Yes" else "No" end)
    }'

echo ""
echo "════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"

# Calculate totals
TOTALS=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django/anihpj" \
    --query '[length(logGroups), sum(logGroups[].storedBytes)]' \
    --output text)

GROUP_COUNT=$(echo $TOTALS | awk '{print $1}')
TOTAL_BYTES=$(echo $TOTALS | awk '{print $2}')
TOTAL_MB=$((TOTAL_BYTES / 1048576))

echo "Summary:"
echo "  Total log groups: $GROUP_COUNT"
echo "  Total storage: ${TOTAL_MB} MB"
echo "════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
Output: ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ Django Application Log Groups - Full Inventory ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ { "name": "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost", "created": "2025-01-01 00:00:00", "retention": "30 days", "storage_mb": 125, "metric_filters": 5, "encrypted": "Yes" } { "name": "/aws/django/anihpj-production", "created": "2025-01-01 00:00:00", "retention": "90 days", "storage_mb": 250, "metric_filters": 10, "encrypted": "Yes" } { "name": "/aws/django/anihpj/skills", "created": "2025-01-05 10:30:00", "retention": "14 days", "storage_mb": 25, "metric_filters": 2, "encrypted": "No" } { "name": "/aws/django/anihpj/users", "created": "2025-01-05 10:30:00", "retention": "30 days", "storage_mb": 50, "metric_filters": 3, "encrypted": "Yes" } { "name": "/aws/django/anihpj/authors", "created": "2025-01-05 10:30:00", "retention": "14 days", "storage_mb": 15, "metric_filters": 1, "encrypted": "No" } ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Summary: Total log groups: 5 Total storage: 465 MB ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import logging # Log group inventory corresponds to these model operations: # /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost (125 MB, 30 days retention) # Primary JobPost operations log group jobpost_count = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"JobPost model - Records: {jobpost_count}") # Output: JobPost model - Records: 250 # /aws/django/anihpj-production (250 MB, 90 days retention) # All production model operations print(f"Production totals:") print(f" JobPosts: {JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count()}") print(f" Users: {User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count()}") # Output: Production totals: # JobPosts: 180 # Users: 120 # /aws/django/anihpj/skills (25 MB, 14 days retention) skills_count = Skills.objects.count() print(f"Skills model - Records: {skills_count}") # Output: Skills model - Records: 45 # /aws/django/anihpj/users (50 MB, 30 days retention) user_count = User.objects.count() print(f"User model - Records: {user_count}") # Output: User model - Records: 120 # /aws/django/anihpj/authors (15 MB, 14 days retention) author_count = Author.objects.count() print(f"Author model - Records: {author_count}") # Output: Author model - Records: 85 # Storage correlates with model activity levels
Example 2: Find Log Groups by Pattern with Storage Analysis

Uses pattern matching to find log groups containing specific terms and analyzes storage distribution. Helps identify which Django models generate the most log data for cost optimization.

#!/bin/bash
# Find log groups by pattern and analyze storage
# Useful for identifying high-volume logging areas

PATTERN="*jobpost*"

echo "Searching for log groups matching pattern: $PATTERN"
echo ""

# Use pattern matching to find relevant groups
aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-pattern "$PATTERN" \
    --query 'logGroups[*].{
        Name: logGroupName,
        StorageMB: `storedBytes`,
        Retention: retentionInDays,
        Created: creationTime
    }' \
    --output table

echo ""
echo "Detailed storage breakdown:"

# Get detailed info with JSON output
aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-pattern "$PATTERN" \
    --output json | jq -r '
    .logGroups | sort_by(-.storedBytes) | .[] |
    "\(.logGroupName): \((.storedBytes/1048576 | floor)) MB (\(.retentionInDays // "∞") day retention)"'

# Calculate storage statistics
echo ""
echo "Storage Statistics:"
aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-pattern "$PATTERN" \
    --query '{
        TotalGroups: length(logGroups),
        TotalStorageBytes: sum(logGroups[].storedBytes),
        AvgStorageBytes: avg(logGroups[].storedBytes),
        MaxStorageBytes: max(logGroups[].storedBytes)
    }' \
    --output json | jq '
    {
        total_groups: .TotalGroups,
        total_storage_mb: ((.TotalStorageBytes // 0) / 1048576 | floor),
        avg_storage_mb: ((.AvgStorageBytes // 0) / 1048576 | floor),
        max_storage_mb: ((.MaxStorageBytes // 0) / 1048576 | floor)
    }'
Output: Searching for log groups matching pattern: *jobpost* --------------------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeLogGroups | +---------------------------------+-----------+-----------+----------+ | Name | StorageMB | Retention | Created | +---------------------------------+-----------+-----------+----------+ | /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost | 125 | 30 | 173... | | /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test | 15 | 7 | 173... | | /aws/ecs/anihpj-jobpost-cluster | 80 | 14 | 173... | | /aws/lambda/anihpj-jobpost-api | 45 | 30 | 173... | +---------------------------------+-----------+-----------+----------+ Detailed storage breakdown: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost: 125 MB (30 day retention) /aws/ecs/anihpj-jobpost-cluster: 80 MB (14 day retention) /aws/lambda/anihpj-jobpost-api: 45 MB (30 day retention) /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test: 15 MB (7 day retention) Storage Statistics: { "total_groups": 4, "total_storage_mb": 265, "avg_storage_mb": 66, "max_storage_mb": 125 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count, Avg import logging # Storage analysis correlates with JobPost model activity # JobPost query complexity affects log volume: # Simple queries = less logs simple_query = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"Simple filter (less logging): {simple_query}") # Output: Simple filter (less logging): 180 # Complex queries = more logs complex_query = JobPost.objects.select_related( 'location', 'author__user' ).prefetch_related( 'required_skills' ).annotate( skill_count=Count('required_skills') ).filter( salary_min__gte=80000, location__remote_allowed=True ).order_by('-created_at')[:10] print(f"Complex query (more logging): {len(complex_query)}") # Output: Complex query (more logging): 10 # Log group storage by operation type: # /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost (125 MB) # - JobPost CRUD operations # - Related Skills associations # - Location lookups # /aws/ecs/anihpj-jobpost-cluster (80 MB) # - Container-level Django logs # - Health checks and deployments # /aws/lambda/anihpj-jobpost-api (45 MB) # - API Gateway integration logs # - JobPost API endpoint calls print(f"Total JobPost records: {JobPost.objects.count()}") # Output: Total JobPost records: 250
Example 3: Monitor Log Group Retention Policies Across Environments

Audits retention policies across all Django application log groups to ensure compliance with data retention requirements. Identifies groups with missing or non-compliant retention for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit log group retention policies
# Ensure compliance with data retention requirements

echo "╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  Log Group Retention Policy Audit - Django Applications       ║"
echo "╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo ""

# Define retention requirements by environment
PROD_MIN_RETENTION=30
DEV_MAX_RETENTION=14
COMPLIANCE_RETENTION=90

# Get all Django log groups
LOG_GROUPS=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django" \
    --query 'logGroups[*].[logGroupName,retentionInDays]' \
    --output text)

echo "Retention Policy Analysis:"
echo "=========================="
echo ""

# Categorize and check compliance
echo "📋 Production Groups (require >= $PROD_MIN_RETENTION days):"
echo "$LOG_GROUPS" | grep -i "production\|prod" | while IFS=$'\t' read -r name retention; do
    if [ -z "$retention" ] || [ "$retention" == "None" ]; then
        echo "  ⚠️  $name: No retention set (REVIEW)"
    elif [ "$retention" -lt "$PROD_MIN_RETENTION" ]; then
        echo "  ❌ $name: $retention days (NON-COMPLIANT)"
    else
        echo "  ✅ $name: $retention days"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "📋 Development Groups (should be <= $DEV_MAX_RETENTION days):"
echo "$LOG_GROUPS" | grep -i "dev\|test\|staging" | while IFS=$'\t' read -r name retention; do
    if [ -z "$retention" ] || [ "$retention" == "None" ]; then
        echo "  ⚠️  $name: No retention set (COST CONCERN)"
    elif [ "$retention" -gt "$DEV_MAX_RETENTION" ]; then
        echo "  ⚠️  $name: $retention days (REVIEW - may be excessive)"
    else
        echo "  ✅ $name: $retention days"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "📋 Model-Specific Groups:"
echo "$LOG_GROUPS" | grep -E "skills|users|authors|locations|jobpost" | while IFS=$'\t' read -r name retention; do
    if [ -z "$retention" ] || [ "$retention" == "None" ]; then
        echo "  ⚠️  $name: No retention set"
    else
        echo "  ℹ️  $name: $retention days"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
Output: ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ Log Group Retention Policy Audit - Django Applications ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ Retention Policy Analysis: ========================== 📋 Production Groups (require >= 30 days): ✅ /aws/django/anihpj-production: 90 days ✅ /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost: 30 days ❌ /aws/django/anihpj-prod-api: 14 days (NON-COMPLIANT) 📋 Development Groups (should be <= 14 days): ✅ /aws/django/anihpj-dev: 7 days ✅ /aws/django/anihpj-test: 7 days ⚠️ /aws/django/anihpj-staging: 30 days (REVIEW - may be excessive) 📋 Model-Specific Groups: ℹ️ /aws/django/anihpj/skills: 14 days ℹ️ /aws/django/anihpj/users: 30 days ℹ️ /aws/django/anihpj/authors: 14 days ℹ️ /aws/django/anihpj/locations: 7 days ℹ️ /aws/django/anihpj/jobpost: 30 days ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime, timedelta import logging # Retention policy context for Django models: # Production (90 days retention): # - Critical for debugging production issues prod_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter( environment='production', created_at__gte=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=90) ) print(f"Production jobs in retention window: {prod_jobs.count()}") # Output: Production jobs in retention window: 250 # Model-specific retention analysis: # Users (30 days) - longer retention for user activity tracking users_recent = User.objects.filter( last_login__gte=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=30) ) print(f"Active users (30 days): {users_recent.count()}") # Output: Active users (30 days): 95 # Skills (14 days) - shorter retention, less critical skills_recent = Skills.objects.filter( created_at__gte=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=14) ) print(f"New skills (14 days): {skills_recent.count()}") # Output: New skills (14 days): 8 # Locations (7 days) - minimal retention, low change rate locations = Location.objects.all() print(f"Total locations: {locations.count()}") # Output: Total locations: 30 # Authors (14 days) - moderate retention authors = Author.objects.select_related('user').all() print(f"Total authors: {authors.count()}") # Output: Total authors: 85 # Compliance note: Production logs must be retained 30+ days # for incident investigation and audit purposes
Example 4: Generate Cost Report for Log Group Storage

Creates a detailed cost analysis report for CloudWatch Logs storage across all Django application log groups. Helps identify opportunities to reduce costs through retention optimization and infrequent access class usage.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate CloudWatch Logs cost analysis report
# Calculates estimated monthly costs by log group

# CloudWatch Logs pricing (us-east-1)
INGESTION_COST=0.50  # per GB ingested
STORAGE_COST=0.03    # per GB per month (standard)
IA_STORAGE_COST=0.01 # per GB per month (infrequent access)

echo "╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  CloudWatch Logs Cost Analysis - Django Applications            ║"
echo "╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo ""

# Get all log groups with storage info
LOG_DATA=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django" \
    --query 'logGroups[*].[logGroupName,storedBytes,logGroupClass]' \
    --output json)

echo "Storage Cost Breakdown by Log Group:"
echo "====================================="
echo ""
printf "%-45s %12s %12s\n" "Log Group" "Storage (GB)" "Monthly Cost"
printf "%-45s %12s %12s\n" "---------" "------------" "------------"

TOTAL_COST=0
TOTAL_GB=0

echo "$LOG_DATA" | jq -r '.[] | @tsv' | while IFS=$'\t' read -r name bytes class; do
    GB=$(echo "scale=2; $bytes / 1073741824" | bc)
    
    if [ "$class" == "INFREQUENT_ACCESS" ]; then
        COST=$(echo "scale=2; $GB * $IA_STORAGE_COST" | bc)
        CLASS_NOTE=" (IA)"
    else
        COST=$(echo "scale=2; $GB * $STORAGE_COST" | bc)
        CLASS_NOTE=""
    fi
    
    printf "%-45s %12s %12s\n" "$name$CLASS_NOTE" "${GB} GB" "\$${COST}"
done

echo ""
echo "====================================="

# Calculate totals
TOTALS=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django" \
    --query 'sum(logGroups[].storedBytes)' \
    --output text)

TOTAL_GB=$(echo "scale=2; $TOTALS / 1073741824" | bc)
TOTAL_COST=$(echo "scale=2; $TOTAL_GB * $STORAGE_COST" | bc)

echo "Total Storage: ${TOTAL_GB} GB"
echo "Estimated Monthly Storage Cost: \$${TOTAL_COST}"
echo ""
echo "Cost Optimization Recommendations:"
echo "  1. Move inactive groups to Infrequent Access class (saves 66%)"
echo "  2. Reduce retention on dev/test groups"
echo "  3. Delete empty or obsolete log groups"
echo "====================================="
Output: ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ CloudWatch Logs Cost Analysis - Django Applications ║ ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ Storage Cost Breakdown by Log Group: ===================================== Log Group Storage (GB) Monthly Cost --------- ------------ ------------ /aws/django/anihpj-production 0.24 GB $0.01 /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost 0.12 GB $0.00 /aws/django/anihpj/users 0.05 GB $0.00 /aws/django/anihpj/skills 0.02 GB $0.00 /aws/django/anihpj/authors 0.01 GB $0.00 /aws/django/anihpj/locations 0.01 GB $0.00 /aws/django/anihpj-archive (IA) 1.50 GB $0.02 /aws/django/anihpj-dev 0.01 GB $0.00 /aws/django/anihpj-test 0.01 GB $0.00 ===================================== Total Storage: 1.97 GB Estimated Monthly Storage Cost: $0.06 Cost Optimization Recommendations: 1. Move inactive groups to Infrequent Access class (saves 66%) 2. Reduce retention on dev/test groups 3. Delete empty or obsolete log groups =====================================
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Sum, Count import logging # Cost correlation with model activity: # High-volume models (more logs, higher cost): job_operations = JobPost.objects.aggregate( total=Count('id'), active=Count('id', filter=models.Q(is_active=True)) ) print(f"JobPost operations: {job_operations}") # Output: JobPost operations: {'total': 250, 'active': 180} user_activity = User.objects.filter( is_active=True ).aggregate( total=Count('id'), with_jobs=Count('author__jobpost', distinct=True) ) print(f"User activity: {user_activity}") # Output: User activity: {'total': 120, 'with_jobs': 85} # Low-volume models (less logs, lower cost): skills_stats = Skills.objects.aggregate( total=Count('id'), used=Count('jobpost', distinct=True) ) print(f"Skills stats: {skills_stats}") # Output: Skills stats: {'total': 45, 'used': 35} location_stats = Location.objects.aggregate( total=Count('id'), with_jobs=Count('jobpost') ) print(f"Location stats: {location_stats}") # Output: Location stats: {'total': 30, 'with_jobs': 250} # Archive group (Infrequent Access) contains historical data: # - Old JobPost records # - Deactivated User logs # - Historical Author activity print("Archive data available via IA log group for compliance queries")
Example 5: Monitor Log Groups with Metric Filters for Alerting

Lists log groups that have metric filters configured for CloudWatch alarms. Shows which Django model operations are being monitored for errors, latency, and other critical metrics affecting Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor log groups with metric filters
# Identify monitored vs unmonitored Django components

echo "╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗"
echo "║  Log Group Metric Filter Inventory - Django Monitoring          ║"
echo "╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝"
echo ""

# Get log groups with metric filter counts
aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django/anihpj" \
    --query 'logGroups[*].{
        Name: logGroupName,
        MetricFilters: metricFilterCount,
        StorageMB: storedBytes
    }' \
    --output json | jq -r '
    sort_by(-.MetricFilters) | .[] |
    if .MetricFilters > 0 then
        "✅ \(.Name)\n   Metric Filters: \(.MetricFilters) | Storage: \((.StorageMB/1048576 | floor)) MB"
    else
        "⚠️  \(.Name)\n   Metric Filters: 0 (UNMONITORED) | Storage: \((.StorageMB/1048576 | floor)) MB"
    end'

echo ""
echo "════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
echo ""

# Show details for groups with metrics
echo "Detailed Metric Filter Information:"
echo ""

for group in $(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django/anihpj" \
    --query 'logGroups[?metricFilterCount>`0`].logGroupName' \
    --output text); do
    
    echo "📊 $group:"
    aws logs describe-metric-filters \
        --log-group-name "$group" \
        --query 'metricFilters[*].{
            Name: filterName,
            Pattern: filterPattern,
            Metric: metricTransformations[0].metricName
        }' \
        --output table
    echo ""
done

echo "════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════"
echo "Recommendation: Add metric filters to unmonitored groups for alerting"
