describe-query-definitions

Lists all saved CloudWatch Logs Insights query definitions. This command retrieves reusable queries stored in your account, helping manage and organize queries for analyzing Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    [--query-definition-name-prefix <value>] \
    [--max-results <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --query-definition-name-prefix (string, optional): Filter results to queries with names starting with this prefix. Example: "Django-"
  • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum number of query definitions to return. Range: 1-1000
Return Value
  • queryDefinitions (list): Array of query definition objects containing:
    • queryDefinitionId: Unique identifier for the query
    • name: Name of the saved query
    • queryString: The Logs Insights query
    • logGroupNames: Associated log groups
    • lastModified: Last modification timestamp
  • nextToken (string): Token for pagination
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List All Saved Query Definitions

Retrieves all saved Logs Insights queries to see what reusable queries are available for Django log analysis.

#!/bin/bash
# List all saved query definitions
# View available queries

echo "All Saved Query Definitions:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --output json | jq -r '.queryDefinitions[] | 
        "Name: \(.name)\n" +
        "  ID: \(.queryDefinitionId)\n" +
        "  Log Groups: \(.logGroupNames // ["Not specified"] | join(", "))\n" +
        "  Query: \(.queryString | .[0:60])...\n"'
Output: All Saved Query Definitions: Name: Django-Model-Errors ID: qd-abc123def456-error-analysis Log Groups: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Query: fields @timestamp, @message, level, model | filter level... Name: Django-Slow-Operations ID: qd-slow-ops-789ghi Log Groups: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost, /aws/django/anihpj-production Query: fields @timestamp, @message, duration_ms | filter durat... Name: Django-User-Activity ID: qd-user-activity-dashboard-abc Log Groups: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Query: fields @timestamp, user_id, action | stats count(*) by ...
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Available saved queries: print(f"Saved Queries for Django Models:") print(f"") print(f"1. Django-Model-Errors") print(f" Analyzes: JobPost, Skills, User errors") print(f"") print(f"2. Django-Slow-Operations") print(f" Monitors: Slow database queries") print(f"") print(f"3. Django-User-Activity") print(f" Tracks: User actions on models")
Example 2: List Queries with Specific Prefix

Retrieves only queries that start with a specific prefix to filter Django-related queries.

#!/bin/bash
# List queries with prefix
# Filter Django queries

PREFIX="Django-"

echo "Queries starting with: $PREFIX"
echo ""

COUNT=$(aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --query-definition-name-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --query 'queryDefinitions | length' \
    --output text)

echo "Found: $COUNT queries"
echo ""

aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --query-definition-name-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --query 'queryDefinitions[].{Name: name, ID: queryDefinitionId}' \
    --output table
Output: Queries starting with: Django- Found: 5 queries ------------------------------------------------------------ | QueryDefinitions | +----------------------------------+-----------------------+ | Name | ID | +----------------------------------+-----------------------+ | Django-Model-Errors | qd-abc123def456 | | Django-Slow-Operations | qd-slow-ops-789ghi | | Django-User-Activity | qd-user-activity-abc | | Django-JobPost-Operations | qd-jobpost-ops-111 | | Django-Skills-Analysis | qd-skills-222 | +----------------------------------+-----------------------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django-prefixed queries: print(f"Django Query Definitions:") print(f"") print(f" Django-Model-Errors: All model errors") print(f" Django-Slow-Operations: Performance") print(f" Django-User-Activity: User tracking") print(f" Django-JobPost-Operations: JobPost CRUD") print(f" Django-Skills-Analysis: Skills data")
Example 3: Get Full Query Details

Retrieves complete details of all saved queries including the full query strings and associated log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Get full query details
# Complete query information

echo "Full Query Definition Details:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --output json | jq -r '.queryDefinitions[] | 
        "=====================================\n" +
        "Name: \(.name)\n" +
        "ID: \(.queryDefinitionId)\n" +
        "Last Modified: \(.lastModified // "Unknown")\n" +
        "Log Groups: \(.logGroupNames // ["Any"] | join(", "))\n" +
        "Query:\n\(.queryString)\n"'
Output: Full Query Definition Details: ===================================== Name: Django-Model-Errors ID: qd-abc123def456-error-analysis Last Modified: 1705593600000 Log Groups: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Query: fields @timestamp, @message, level, model | filter level = "ERROR" | parse @message "ERROR: * - Model: *" as error_type, model_name | stats count(*) as error_count by model_name, error_type | sort error_count desc ===================================== Name: Django-Slow-Operations ID: qd-slow-ops-789ghi Last Modified: 1705507200000 Log Groups: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost, /aws/django/anihpj-production Query: fields @timestamp, @message, duration_ms | filter duration_ms > 500 | stats avg(duration_ms) as avg_duration by model, operation | sort avg_duration desc
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Full query details: print(f"Query: Django-Model-Errors") print(f" Parses error logs for models:") print(f" - JobPost.DoesNotExist") print(f" - Skills.ValidationError") print(f" - User.IntegrityError") print(f"") print(f"Query: Django-Slow-Operations") print(f" Finds slow operations:") print(f" - JobPost.objects.select_related()") print(f" - Skills.objects.prefetch_related()")
Example 4: Export Query Definitions to File

Exports all saved query definitions to a JSON file for backup or migration purposes.

#!/bin/bash
# Export query definitions
# Backup saved queries

OUTPUT_FILE="query-definitions-backup.json"

echo "Exporting query definitions..."

aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --output json > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

# Show summary
COUNT=$(jq '.queryDefinitions | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")
echo "Exported $COUNT query definitions to $OUTPUT_FILE"
echo ""

# List exported queries
echo "Exported queries:"
jq -r '.queryDefinitions[].name' "$OUTPUT_FILE" | while read NAME; do
    echo "  - $NAME"
done
Output: Exporting query definitions... Exported 5 query definitions to query-definitions-backup.json Exported queries: - Django-Model-Errors - Django-Slow-Operations - Django-User-Activity - Django-JobPost-Operations - Django-Skills-Analysis
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backup context: print(f"Query Definitions Backup:") print(f" File: query-definitions-backup.json") print(f" Queries: 5") print(f"") print(f"Models covered:") print(f" - JobPost operations") print(f" - Skills analysis") print(f" - User activity") print(f" - Author profiles") print(f" - Location data")
Example 5: Compare Query Definitions Across Accounts

Lists queries and generates a comparison report to ensure consistency across environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare query definitions
# Consistency check

echo "Query Definitions Summary:"
echo ""

# Count by prefix
PREFIXES=("Django-" "Error-" "Performance-" "Security-")

for PREFIX in "${PREFIXES[@]}"; do
    COUNT=$(aws logs describe-query-definitions \
        --query-definition-name-prefix "$PREFIX" \
        --query 'queryDefinitions | length' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
    
    printf "  %-15s : %s queries\n" "$PREFIX*" "$COUNT"
done

echo ""
echo "Total queries:"
TOTAL=$(aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --query 'queryDefinitions | length' \
    --output text)
echo "  $TOTAL"
Output: Query Definitions Summary: Django-* : 5 queries Error-* : 2 queries Performance-* : 3 queries Security-* : 1 queries Total queries: 11
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Query organization: print(f"Query Categories:") print(f" Django-* (5): Model-specific queries") print(f" - JobPost, Skills, User, Author, Location") print(f"") print(f" Error-* (2): Error analysis") print(f" Performance-* (3): Slow query detection") print(f" Security-* (1): Security auditing")

delete-query-definition

Deletes a saved CloudWatch Logs Insights query definition. This command removes unused or obsolete saved queries to keep your query library organized and manageable for analyzing Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs delete-query-definition \
    --query-definition-id <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --query-definition-id (string, required): The unique identifier of the query definition to delete. Obtained from describe-query-definitions command
Return Value
  • success (boolean): True if the query definition was successfully deleted, False otherwise
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete a Single Query Definition

Removes an obsolete or unused saved query from the CloudWatch console.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete single query definition
# Remove obsolete query

QUERY_ID="qd-old-query-abc123"

echo "Deleting query definition: $QUERY_ID"

aws logs delete-query-definition \
    --query-definition-id "$QUERY_ID"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "Query definition deleted successfully"
else
    echo "Failed to delete query definition"
fi
Output: Deleting query definition: qd-old-query-abc123 { "success": true } Query definition deleted successfully
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Deleted obsolete query: print(f"Query Deleted:") print(f" ID: qd-old-query-abc123") print(f" Was analyzing deprecated JobPost fields") print(f" No longer needed after model update")
Example 2: Delete Queries by Prefix

Removes all saved queries with a specific prefix, useful for cleaning up test queries.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete queries by prefix
# Clean up test queries

PREFIX="Test-"

echo "Deleting queries starting with: $PREFIX"
echo ""

# Get query IDs with prefix
aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --query-definition-name-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --query 'queryDefinitions[].{id: queryDefinitionId, name: name}' \
    --output json | jq -r '.[] | .id + "|" + .name' | while IFS='|' read ID NAME; do
    
    echo "Deleting: $NAME"
    aws logs delete-query-definition --query-definition-id "$ID"
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    else
        echo "  ✗ Failed"
    fi
done
Output: Deleting queries starting with: Test- Deleting: Test-JobPost-Query ✓ Deleted Deleting: Test-Skills-Analysis ✓ Deleted Deleting: Test-User-Activity ✓ Deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Test queries removed: print(f"Cleaned Up Test Queries:") print(f" Test-JobPost-Query: Deleted") print(f" Test-Skills-Analysis: Deleted") print(f" Test-User-Activity: Deleted") print(f"") print(f"Production queries preserved")
Example 3: Delete with Confirmation

Prompts for confirmation before deleting a query definition to prevent accidental deletion.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with confirmation
# Safe deletion workflow

QUERY_ID="qd-django-errors-456"

# Get query details first
QUERY_NAME=$(aws logs describe-query-definitions \
    --query "queryDefinitions[?queryDefinitionId=='$QUERY_ID'].name | [0]" \
    --output text)

if [ "$QUERY_NAME" == "None" ]; then
    echo "Query definition not found: $QUERY_ID"
    exit 1
fi

echo "Query to delete:"
echo "  ID: $QUERY_ID"
echo "  Name: $QUERY_NAME"
echo ""
read -p "Are you sure you want to delete this query? (y/n): " CONFIRM

if [ "$CONFIRM" == "y" ]; then
    aws logs delete-query-definition --query-definition-id "$QUERY_ID"
    echo "Query deleted successfully"
else
    echo "Deletion cancelled"
fi
Output: Query to delete: ID: qd-django-errors-456 Name: Django-Old-Error-Query Are you sure you want to delete this query? (y/n): y { "success": true } Query deleted successfully
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Confirmed deletion: print(f"Query Deleted with Confirmation:") print(f" Name: Django-Old-Error-Query") print(f" Was used for: Legacy error tracking") print(f"") print(f"Remaining Django queries:") print(f" - Django-Model-Errors (current)") print(f" - Django-Slow-Operations")
Example 4: Cleanup Old Queries

Removes query definitions that haven't been modified recently, keeping only active queries.

#!/bin/bash
# Cleanup old queries
# Remove stale definitions

DAYS_OLD=90
THRESHOLD=$(($(date +%s) - ($DAYS_OLD * 86400)))
THRESHOLD_MS=$((THRESHOLD * 1000))

echo "Deleting queries not modified in $DAYS_OLD days..."
echo ""

DELETED=0

aws logs describe-query-definitions --output json | jq -r --argjson threshold "$THRESHOLD_MS" '
    .queryDefinitions[] | 
    select(.lastModified < $threshold) | 
    .queryDefinitionId + "|" + .name' | while IFS='|' read ID NAME; do
    
    echo "Deleting old query: $NAME"
    aws logs delete-query-definition --query-definition-id "$ID" > /dev/null
    ((DELETED++))
done

echo ""
echo "Cleanup complete"
Output: Deleting queries not modified in 90 days... Deleting old query: Legacy-JobPost-Monitor Deleting old query: Old-Skills-Dashboard Deleting old query: Deprecated-User-Tracking Cleanup complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Old queries removed: print(f"Stale Queries Cleaned Up:") print(f" - Legacy-JobPost-Monitor: 120 days old") print(f" - Old-Skills-Dashboard: 95 days old") print(f" - Deprecated-User-Tracking: 110 days old") print(f"") print(f"Active queries preserved for:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, User, Author, Location")
Example 5: Batch Delete Query Definitions

Deletes multiple query definitions from a list, useful for migration or reorganization.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch delete queries
# Migration cleanup

QUERIES_TO_DELETE=(
    "qd-old-jobpost-111"
    "qd-deprecated-skills-222"
    "qd-legacy-user-333"
)

echo "Batch Deleting Query Definitions"
echo ""

SUCCESS=0
FAILED=0

for QUERY_ID in "${QUERIES_TO_DELETE[@]}"; do
    echo "Deleting: $QUERY_ID"
    
    RESULT=$(aws logs delete-query-definition \
        --query-definition-id "$QUERY_ID" \
        --query 'success' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ "$RESULT" == "True" ]; then
        ((SUCCESS++))
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    else
        ((FAILED++))
        echo "  ✗ Failed (may not exist)"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Summary: $SUCCESS deleted, $FAILED failed"
Output: Batch Deleting Query Definitions Deleting: qd-old-jobpost-111 ✓ Deleted Deleting: qd-deprecated-skills-222 ✓ Deleted Deleting: qd-legacy-user-333 ✓ Deleted Summary: 3 deleted, 0 failed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch deletion complete: print(f"Batch Delete Summary:") print(f" Deleted: 3 queries") print(f" Failed: 0") print(f"") print(f"Removed queries for deprecated:") print(f" - Old JobPost structure") print(f" - Legacy Skills tracking") print(f" - Previous User monitoring")

create-export-task

Creates an export task to export log data from a CloudWatch log group to an Amazon S3 bucket. This command archives historical logs for compliance, long-term analysis, or cost optimization when analyzing Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs create-export-task \
    --task-name <value> \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    --from <value> \
    --to <value> \
    --destination <value> \
    [--destination-prefix <value>] \
    [--log-stream-name-prefix <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --task-name (string, optional): A name for the export task to identify it in the console
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group to export. Example: "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
  • --from (long, required): Start of the time range in epoch milliseconds. Use $(date +%s)000 for current time
  • --to (long, required): End of the time range in epoch milliseconds
  • --destination (string, required): Name of the S3 bucket for exported logs
  • --destination-prefix (string, optional): S3 key prefix for exported files
  • --log-stream-name-prefix (string, optional): Filter to export only streams matching this prefix
Return Value
  • taskId (string): Unique identifier for the export task, used to monitor progress
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Export Last 7 Days of Django Logs to S3

Creates an export task to archive the last week of Django application logs for compliance and long-term storage.

#!/bin/bash
# Export 7 days of logs to S3
# Compliance archiving

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-logs-archive"
PREFIX="exports/django/$(date +%Y-%m)"

# Calculate time range (7 days ago to now)
FROM=$(($(date -d '7 days ago' +%s) * 1000))
TO=$(($(date +%s) * 1000))

echo "Exporting logs from $LOG_GROUP"
echo "Time range: 7 days"
echo "Destination: s3://$S3_BUCKET/$PREFIX"
echo ""

aws logs create-export-task \
    --task-name "Django-Weekly-Export-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --from $FROM \
    --to $TO \
    --destination "$S3_BUCKET" \
    --destination-prefix "$PREFIX"
Output: Exporting logs from /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Time range: 7 days Destination: s3://anihpj-logs-archive/exports/django/2024-01 { "taskId": "export-task-abc123def456-weekly" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Export context: print(f"Django Logs Export Started:") print(f" Task ID: export-task-abc123def456-weekly") print(f" Period: Last 7 days") print(f"") print(f"Logs exported for:") print(f" - JobPost CRUD operations") print(f" - Skills associations") print(f" - User activities") print(f" - Author profile updates") print(f" - Location validations")
Example 2: Export Specific Log Stream

Exports only logs from a specific stream, useful for isolating production or error logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Export specific log stream
# Production logs only

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
STREAM_PREFIX="production/"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-logs-archive"

# Last 24 hours
FROM=$(($(date -d '1 day ago' +%s) * 1000))
TO=$(($(date +%s) * 1000))

echo "Exporting production logs only..."

aws logs create-export-task \
    --task-name "Production-Export-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --log-stream-name-prefix "$STREAM_PREFIX" \
    --from $FROM \
    --to $TO \
    --destination "$S3_BUCKET" \
    --destination-prefix "exports/production/$(date +%Y/%m/%d)"
Output: Exporting production logs only... { "taskId": "export-task-prod-789ghi-daily" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production stream export: print(f"Production Logs Export:") print(f" Stream Prefix: production/") print(f" Period: Last 24 hours") print(f"") print(f"Contains operations for:") print(f" JobPost.objects.create()") print(f" Skills.objects.filter()") print(f" User authentication events")
Example 3: Monthly Archive Export

Creates a monthly archive of all Django logs for long-term compliance and audit requirements.

#!/bin/bash
# Monthly archive export
# Compliance storage

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-compliance-archive"

# Previous month
YEAR=$(date -d 'last month' +%Y)
MONTH=$(date -d 'last month' +%m)

# First and last second of previous month
FROM=$(date -d "$YEAR-$MONTH-01" +%s)000
TO=$(date -d "$YEAR-$MONTH-01 +1 month -1 second" +%s)000

echo "Monthly Archive Export"
echo "Month: $YEAR-$MONTH"
echo ""

aws logs create-export-task \
    --task-name "Monthly-Archive-$YEAR-$MONTH" \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --from $FROM \
    --to $TO \
    --destination "$S3_BUCKET" \
    --destination-prefix "archives/$YEAR/$MONTH"
Output: Monthly Archive Export Month: 2024-12 { "taskId": "export-task-monthly-2024-12-archive" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Monthly archive: print(f"Monthly Compliance Archive:") print(f" Month: December 2024") print(f" S3 Path: archives/2024/12/") print(f"") print(f"Archived Django model operations:") print(f" - All JobPost transactions") print(f" - User creation/deletion") print(f" - Skills modifications") print(f" - Author profile changes") print(f" - Location updates")
Example 4: Export Multiple Log Groups

Creates export tasks for multiple Django application log groups to consolidate archives.

#!/bin/bash
# Export multiple log groups
# Consolidated archive

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-production"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-api"
)

S3_BUCKET="anihpj-logs-archive"

# Last 30 days
FROM=$(($(date -d '30 days ago' +%s) * 1000))
TO=$(($(date +%s) * 1000))

echo "Exporting multiple log groups..."
echo ""

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    # Extract short name
    SHORT_NAME=$(basename "$LOG_GROUP")
    
    echo "Exporting: $SHORT_NAME"
    
    TASK_ID=$(aws logs create-export-task \
        --task-name "Export-$SHORT_NAME-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
        --from $FROM \
        --to $TO \
        --destination "$S3_BUCKET" \
        --destination-prefix "exports/$SHORT_NAME/$(date +%Y/%m)" \
        --query 'taskId' \
        --output text)
    
    echo "  Task ID: $TASK_ID"
done

echo ""
echo "All exports started"
Output: Exporting multiple log groups... Exporting: anihpj-jobpost Task ID: export-task-jobpost-111aaa Exporting: anihpj-production Task ID: export-task-production-222bbb Exporting: anihpj-api Task ID: export-task-api-333ccc All exports started
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Multiple log group exports: print(f"Consolidated Export:") print(f"") print(f"anihpj-jobpost:") print(f" - JobPost, Skills, Location") print(f"") print(f"anihpj-production:") print(f" - User, Author") print(f"") print(f"anihpj-api:") print(f" - API access for all models")
Example 5: Export with Progress Monitoring

Creates an export task and monitors its progress until completion.

#!/bin/bash
# Export with progress monitoring
# Wait for completion

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-logs-archive"

FROM=$(($(date -d '3 days ago' +%s) * 1000))
TO=$(($(date +%s) * 1000))

echo "Starting export task..."

TASK_ID=$(aws logs create-export-task \
    --task-name "Monitored-Export-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)" \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --from $FROM \
    --to $TO \
    --destination "$S3_BUCKET" \
    --destination-prefix "exports/monitored" \
    --query 'taskId' \
    --output text)

echo "Task ID: $TASK_ID"
echo ""
echo "Monitoring progress..."

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
        --task-id "$TASK_ID" \
        --query 'exportTasks[0].status.code' \
        --output text)
    
    echo "Status: $STATUS"
    
    if [ "$STATUS" == "COMPLETED" ] || [ "$STATUS" == "FAILED" ] || [ "$STATUS" == "CANCELLED" ]; then
        break
    fi
    
    sleep 10
done

echo ""
echo "Export finished with status: $STATUS"
Output: Starting export task... Task ID: export-task-monitored-456def Monitoring progress... Status: PENDING Status: RUNNING Status: RUNNING Status: COMPLETED Export finished with status: COMPLETED
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Export completion: print(f"Export Completed Successfully:") print(f" Task ID: export-task-monitored-456def") print(f" Duration: ~30 seconds") print(f" Status: COMPLETED") print(f"") print(f"Exported logs for:") print(f" JobPost: {JobPost.objects.count()} records") print(f" Skills: {Skills.objects.count()} records") print(f" User: {User.objects.count()} records")

describe-export-tasks

Lists CloudWatch Logs export tasks and their status. This command monitors the progress of log exports to S3, helping track archival operations for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    [--task-id <value>] \
    [--status-code CANCELLED|COMPLETED|FAILED|PENDING|PENDING_CANCEL|RUNNING] \
    [--max-items <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --task-id (string, optional): Filter to a specific export task by its unique identifier
  • --status-code (string, optional): Filter by task status. Values: CANCELLED, COMPLETED, FAILED, PENDING, PENDING_CANCEL, RUNNING
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum number of tasks to return
Return Value
  • exportTasks (list): Array of export task objects containing:
    • taskId: Unique identifier for the task
    • taskName: Name assigned to the task
    • logGroupName: Source log group
    • status: Current status (code and message)
    • destination: Target S3 bucket
    • executionInfo: Completion time and error info
  • nextToken (string): Token for pagination
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Check Specific Export Task Status

Monitors a specific export task to track its progress and completion status.

#!/bin/bash
# Check specific export task
# Monitor progress

TASK_ID="export-task-abc123def456"

echo "Checking export task: $TASK_ID"
echo ""

aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --task-id "$TASK_ID" \
    --output json | jq -r '.exportTasks[] | 
        "Task ID: \(.taskId)\n" +
        "Name: \(.taskName // "Unnamed")\n" +
        "Status: \(.status.code)\n" +
        "Log Group: \(.logGroupName)\n" +
        "Destination: s3://\(.destination)/\(.destinationPrefix // "")\n" +
        "Created: \(.from | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"))\n" +
        "To: \(.to | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"))"'
Output: Checking export task: export-task-abc123def456 Task ID: export-task-abc123def456 Name: Django-Weekly-Export-20240120 Status: COMPLETED Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Destination: s3://anihpj-logs-archive/exports/django/2024-01 Created: 2024-01-13 00:00:00 To: 2024-01-20 23:59:59
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Export task status: print(f"Export Task Completed:") print(f" Status: COMPLETED") print(f" Duration: 7 days of logs") print(f"") print(f"Exported Django models:") print(f" - JobPost operations") print(f" - Skills changes") print(f" - User activities")
Example 2: List All Running Export Tasks

Retrieves all currently running export tasks to monitor active archival operations.

#!/bin/bash
# List running exports
# Monitor active tasks

echo "Currently Running Export Tasks:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code RUNNING \
    --output json | jq -r '.exportTasks[] | 
        "Task: \(.taskName // .taskId)\n" +
        "  Log Group: \(.logGroupName)\n" +
        "  Destination: \(.destination)\n" +
        "  Status: \(.status.code)\n"'

# Show count
COUNT=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code RUNNING \
    --query 'exportTasks | length' \
    --output text)

echo "Total running: $COUNT"
Output: Currently Running Export Tasks: Task: Django-Weekly-Export-20240120 Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Destination: anihpj-logs-archive Status: RUNNING Task: Production-Export-20240120 Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-production Destination: anihpj-logs-archive Status: RUNNING Total running: 2
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Running exports: print(f"Active Export Tasks: 2") print(f"") print(f"Task 1: Django Weekly") print(f" Exporting: JobPost, Skills logs") print(f"") print(f"Task 2: Production") print(f" Exporting: User, Author logs")
Example 3: View Export History

Lists all completed export tasks to review archival history and verify successful backups.

#!/bin/bash
# View export history
# Completed tasks

echo "Completed Export Tasks:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code COMPLETED \
    --query 'exportTasks[*].{
        Name: taskName,
        LogGroup: logGroupName,
        Bucket: destination,
        Completed: executionInfo.completionTime
    }' \
    --output table
Output: Completed Export Tasks: --------------------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeExportTasks | +---------------+---------------------------+------------------+----+ | Bucket | LogGroup | Name | | +---------------+---------------------------+------------------+----+ | logs-archive | /aws/django/anihpj-job.. | Weekly-Jan-W2 | | | logs-archive | /aws/django/anihpj-job.. | Weekly-Jan-W1 | | | logs-archive | /aws/django/anihpj-pro.. | Monthly-Dec | | +---------------+---------------------------+------------------+----+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Export history: print(f"Completed Exports:") print(f" Weekly-Jan-W2: JobPost logs") print(f" Weekly-Jan-W1: JobPost logs") print(f" Monthly-Dec: Production logs")
Example 4: Check for Failed Exports

Identifies failed export tasks to diagnose issues and retry if necessary.

#!/bin/bash
# Check failed exports
# Diagnose issues

echo "Failed Export Tasks:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code FAILED \
    --output json | jq -r '.exportTasks[] | 
        "Task: \(.taskName // .taskId)\n" +
        "  Log Group: \(.logGroupName)\n" +
        "  Error: \(.status.message // "Unknown error")\n" +
        "  Destination: \(.destination)\n"'

FAILED_COUNT=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code FAILED \
    --query 'exportTasks | length' \
    --output text)

if [ "$FAILED_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "WARNING: $FAILED_COUNT failed exports need attention"
else
    echo "No failed exports"
fi
Output: Failed Export Tasks: Task: Large-Export-Attempt Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-production Error: Access Denied to S3 bucket Destination: restricted-bucket WARNING: 1 failed exports need attention
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Failed export analysis: print(f"Failed Export:") print(f" Task: Large-Export-Attempt") print(f" Error: S3 access denied") print(f"") print(f"Resolution:") print(f" - Check bucket policy") print(f" - Verify CloudWatch Logs permissions") print(f" - Retry after fixing permissions")
Example 5: Export Summary Report

Generates a comprehensive summary of all export tasks by status for reporting.

#!/bin/bash
# Export summary report
# Status overview

echo "Export Tasks Summary Report"
echo "==========================="
echo ""

for STATUS in PENDING RUNNING COMPLETED FAILED CANCELLED; do
    COUNT=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
        --status-code "$STATUS" \
        --query 'exportTasks | length' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
    
    printf "  %-15s : %s\n" "$STATUS" "$COUNT"
done

echo ""
echo "Recent Completed Exports:"
aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code COMPLETED \
    --max-items 3 \
    --query 'exportTasks[].{Name: taskName, LogGroup: logGroupName}' \
    --output table
Output: Export Tasks Summary Report =========================== PENDING : 1 RUNNING : 2 COMPLETED : 15 FAILED : 1 CANCELLED : 0 Recent Completed Exports: ----------------------------------------- | DescribeExportTasks | +---------------------+-----------------+ | LogGroup | Name | +---------------------+-----------------+ | /aws/django/ani.. | Weekly-W3 | | /aws/django/ani.. | Weekly-W2 | | /aws/django/ani.. | Monthly-Jan | +---------------------+-----------------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Summary report: print(f"Export Status Summary:") print(f" PENDING: 1") print(f" RUNNING: 2") print(f" COMPLETED: 15") print(f" FAILED: 1") print(f"") print(f"Django logs archived regularly")

cancel-export-task

Cancels a running CloudWatch Logs export task. This command stops an in-progress export to S3 to prevent unwanted data transfer or storage costs when archiving Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs cancel-export-task \
    --task-id <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --task-id (string, required): The unique identifier of the export task to cancel. Task must be in PENDING or RUNNING status
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Any data already exported remains in S3
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Cancel a Running Export Task

Stops an active export task that was started accidentally or is taking too long.

#!/bin/bash
# Cancel running export
# Stop unwanted transfer

TASK_ID="export-task-abc123def456"

echo "Cancelling export task: $TASK_ID"

# Check current status first
STATUS=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --task-id "$TASK_ID" \
    --query 'exportTasks[0].status.code' \
    --output text)

echo "Current status: $STATUS"

if [ "$STATUS" == "RUNNING" ] || [ "$STATUS" == "PENDING" ]; then
    aws logs cancel-export-task --task-id "$TASK_ID"
    echo "Cancel request sent"
    
    # Verify cancellation
    sleep 2
    NEW_STATUS=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
        --task-id "$TASK_ID" \
        --query 'exportTasks[0].status.code' \
        --output text)
    echo "New status: $NEW_STATUS"
else
    echo "Task cannot be cancelled (status: $STATUS)"
fi
Output: Cancelling export task: export-task-abc123def456 Current status: RUNNING Cancel request sent New status: CANCELLED
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Export cancelled: print(f"Export Task Cancelled:") print(f" Task ID: export-task-abc123def456") print(f" Previous Status: RUNNING") print(f" New Status: CANCELLED") print(f"") print(f"Django logs export stopped:") print(f" JobPost data transfer halted") print(f" S3 storage costs avoided")
Example 2: Cancel All Running Exports

Emergency cancellation of all running export tasks to immediately stop data transfer.

#!/bin/bash
# Cancel all running exports
# Emergency stop

echo "Cancelling all running export tasks..."
echo ""

TASK_IDS=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code RUNNING \
    --query 'exportTasks[].taskId' \
    --output text)

CANCELLED=0
for TASK_ID in $TASK_IDS; do
    echo "Cancelling: $TASK_ID"
    aws logs cancel-export-task --task-id "$TASK_ID" 2>/dev/null
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "  ✓ Cancelled"
        ((CANCELLED++))
    else
        echo "  ✗ Failed"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Cancelled $CANCELLED export tasks"
Output: Cancelling all running export tasks... Cancelling: export-task-abc123 ✓ Cancelled Cancelling: export-task-def456 ✓ Cancelled Cancelling: export-task-ghi789 ✓ Cancelled Cancelled 3 export tasks
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # All exports cancelled: print(f"Emergency Stop Complete:") print(f" Cancelled: 3 tasks") print(f"") print(f"Stopped exports for:") print(f" - JobPost weekly export") print(f" - Skills monthly archive") print(f" - User activity export")
Example 3: Cancel Export for Specific Log Group

Cancels export tasks targeting a specific Django application log group.

#!/bin/bash
# Cancel exports for specific log group
# Targeted cancellation

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Cancelling exports for: $LOG_GROUP"
echo ""

aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --status-code RUNNING \
    --output json | jq -r --arg lg "$LOG_GROUP" '
    .exportTasks[] | 
    select(.logGroupName == $lg) | 
    .taskId' | while read TASK_ID; do
    
    if [ -n "$TASK_ID" ]; then
        echo "Cancelling: $TASK_ID"
        aws logs cancel-export-task --task-id "$TASK_ID"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "All exports for $LOG_GROUP cancelled"
Output: Cancelling exports for: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Cancelling: export-task-jobpost-111 All exports for /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost cancelled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Log group specific cancel: print(f"Cancelled exports for anihpj-jobpost:") print(f" Task: export-task-jobpost-111") print(f"") print(f"Affected models:") print(f" - JobPost") print(f" - Skills") print(f" - Location")
Example 4: Cancel with Cleanup Verification

Cancels an export task and verifies partial data in S3 for cleanup decisions.

#!/bin/bash
# Cancel with S3 verification
# Check partial data

TASK_ID="export-task-abc123"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-logs-archive"
S3_PREFIX="exports/django/2024-01"

echo "Cancelling export and checking partial data..."
echo ""

# Get task details before cancel
TASK_INFO=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --task-id "$TASK_ID" \
    --query 'exportTasks[0]' \
    --output json)

echo "Task Info:"
echo "$TASK_INFO" | jq '{taskId, logGroupName, destination, status}'
echo ""

# Cancel the task
aws logs cancel-export-task --task-id "$TASK_ID"
echo "Task cancelled"
echo ""

# Check for partial data in S3
echo "Partial data in S3:"
aws s3 ls "s3://$S3_BUCKET/$S3_PREFIX/" --recursive --summarize | tail -5
Output: Cancelling export and checking partial data... Task Info: { "taskId": "export-task-abc123", "logGroupName": "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost", "destination": "anihpj-logs-archive", "status": { "code": "RUNNING" } } Task cancelled Partial data in S3: 2024-01-20 10:15:23 125432 000000.gz 2024-01-20 10:15:45 134521 000001.gz Total Objects: 2 Total Size: 259953
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Partial export data: print(f"Export Cancelled - Partial Data:") print(f" Files exported: 2") print(f" Total size: ~254 KB") print(f"") print(f"Decision needed:") print(f" - Delete partial data?") print(f" - Or restart export?")
Example 5: Graceful Export Cancellation Script

Provides a user-friendly script for cancelling exports with confirmation and status tracking.

#!/bin/bash
# Graceful export cancellation
# User-friendly workflow

TASK_ID="$1"

if [ -z "$TASK_ID" ]; then
    echo "Usage: cancel-export.sh <task-id>"
    echo ""
    echo "Running exports:"
    aws logs describe-export-tasks \
        --status-code RUNNING \
        --query 'exportTasks[].{ID: taskId, Name: taskName, LogGroup: logGroupName}' \
        --output table
    exit 1
fi

# Get task info
TASK_NAME=$(aws logs describe-export-tasks \
    --task-id "$TASK_ID" \
    --query 'exportTasks[0].taskName' \
    --output text)

echo "Export Task: $TASK_NAME ($TASK_ID)"
echo ""
read -p "Are you sure you want to cancel this export? (y/n): " CONFIRM

if [ "$CONFIRM" == "y" ]; then
    aws logs cancel-export-task --task-id "$TASK_ID"
    echo "Export cancelled successfully"
else
    echo "Cancellation aborted"
fi
Output: Export Task: Django-Weekly-Export (export-task-weekly-123) Are you sure you want to cancel this export? (y/n): y Export cancelled successfully
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Graceful cancellation: print(f"Export Cancellation Complete:") print(f" Task: Django-Weekly-Export") print(f" Confirmed by user") print(f"") print(f"Action items:") print(f" - Review partial data") print(f" - Reschedule if needed")

associate-kms-key

Associates an AWS KMS customer master key (CMK) with a CloudWatch log group for encryption at rest. This command enables server-side encryption of log data to meet compliance requirements when storing Django application logs containing sensitive data from Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs associate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    --kms-key-id <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group to encrypt. Example: "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
  • --kms-key-id (string, required): The ARN of the KMS key to use for encryption. Must have appropriate key policy permissions
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. New log events will be encrypted with the specified key
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Encrypt Django Application Log Group

Associates a KMS key with the Django application log group to encrypt all log data at rest.

#!/bin/bash
# Encrypt Django log group
# Compliance requirement

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
KMS_KEY_ARN="arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abc123-def456-ghi789"

echo "Associating KMS key with log group..."
echo "Log Group: $LOG_GROUP"
echo "KMS Key: $KMS_KEY_ARN"
echo ""

aws logs associate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --kms-key-id "$KMS_KEY_ARN"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ Log group encryption enabled"
else
    echo "✗ Failed to enable encryption"
fi
Output: Associating KMS key with log group... Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost KMS Key: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abc123-def456-ghi789 ✓ Log group encryption enabled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Encryption enabled for: print(f"Log Group Encrypted:") print(f" /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost") print(f"") print(f"Protected data:") print(f" - User PII (email, name)") print(f" - Author profiles") print(f" - JobPost details") print(f" - Skills data")
Example 2: Encrypt Multiple Log Groups

Associates the same KMS key with multiple Django application log groups for consistent encryption.

#!/bin/bash
# Encrypt multiple log groups
# Same KMS key for all

KMS_KEY_ARN="arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abc123-def456-ghi789"

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-production"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-api"
)

echo "Encrypting log groups with KMS..."
echo ""

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    echo "Processing: $LOG_GROUP"
    aws logs associate-kms-key \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
        --kms-key-id "$KMS_KEY_ARN" 2>/dev/null
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "  ✓ Encrypted"
    else
        echo "  ✗ Failed"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Encryption setup complete"
Output: Encrypting log groups with KMS... Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost ✓ Encrypted Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-production ✓ Encrypted Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-api ✓ Encrypted Encryption setup complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # All log groups encrypted: print(f"Encrypted Log Groups:") print(f" anihpj-jobpost: JobPost, Skills, Location") print(f" anihpj-production: User, Author") print(f" anihpj-api: API access logs")
Example 3: Create KMS Key and Associate

Creates a new KMS key specifically for CloudWatch Logs and associates it with the log group.

#!/bin/bash
# Create KMS key and associate
# New key for logs

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating KMS key for CloudWatch Logs..."

# Create KMS key
KEY_ID=$(aws kms create-key \
    --description "CloudWatch Logs encryption for Django app" \
    --key-usage ENCRYPT_DECRYPT \
    --query 'KeyMetadata.KeyId' \
    --output text)

echo "Created KMS Key: $KEY_ID"

# Create alias
aws kms create-alias \
    --alias-name "alias/cloudwatch-django-logs" \
    --target-key-id "$KEY_ID"

echo "Created alias: alias/cloudwatch-django-logs"

# Get key ARN
KEY_ARN=$(aws kms describe-key \
    --key-id "$KEY_ID" \
    --query 'KeyMetadata.Arn' \
    --output text)

# Associate with log group
echo "Associating with log group..."
aws logs associate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --kms-key-id "$KEY_ARN"

echo "✓ Log group now encrypted with new KMS key"
Output: Creating KMS key for CloudWatch Logs... Created KMS Key: abc123-def456-ghi789-jkl012 Created alias: alias/cloudwatch-django-logs Associating with log group... ✓ Log group now encrypted with new KMS key
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # New KMS key created: print(f"KMS Key Configuration:") print(f" Key ID: abc123-def456-ghi789-jkl012") print(f" Alias: cloudwatch-django-logs") print(f"") print(f"Protects Django logs:") print(f" - JobPost transactions") print(f" - User credentials") print(f" - Author data")
Example 4: Verify Encryption Status

Associates KMS key and verifies the encryption is properly configured for the log group.

#!/bin/bash
# Associate and verify encryption
# Confirm configuration

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
KMS_KEY_ARN="arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abc123-def456"

echo "Setting up encryption..."

# Associate KMS key
aws logs associate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --kms-key-id "$KMS_KEY_ARN"

# Verify encryption
echo ""
echo "Verifying encryption status..."

KMS_KEY=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --query "logGroups[?logGroupName=='$LOG_GROUP'].kmsKeyId | [0]" \
    --output text)

if [ "$KMS_KEY" != "None" ] && [ -n "$KMS_KEY" ]; then
    echo "✓ Encryption verified"
    echo "  KMS Key ARN: $KMS_KEY"
else
    echo "✗ Encryption not found"
fi
Output: Setting up encryption... Verifying encryption status... ✓ Encryption verified KMS Key ARN: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abc123-def456
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Encryption verified: print(f"Log Group Encryption Status:") print(f" Status: ENCRYPTED") print(f" Key Type: Customer Managed") print(f"") print(f"Data protected:") print(f" - All JobPost operations") print(f" - User activity logs") print(f" - Skills modifications")
Example 5: Encryption with Key Policy Update

Associates KMS key after ensuring the key policy allows CloudWatch Logs service.

#!/bin/bash
# Associate with key policy check
# Ensure proper permissions

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
KMS_KEY_ID="abc123-def456-ghi789"
REGION="us-east-1"
ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"

echo "Checking KMS key policy..."

# Key policy for CloudWatch Logs
KEY_POLICY='{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "Enable CloudWatch Logs",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "Service": "logs.'$REGION'.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": [
                "kms:Encrypt*",
                "kms:Decrypt*",
                "kms:ReEncrypt*",
                "kms:GenerateDataKey*",
                "kms:Describe*"
            ],
            "Resource": "*",
            "Condition": {
                "ArnLike": {
                    "kms:EncryptionContext:aws:logs:arn": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':*"
                }
            }
        }
    ]
}'

# Note: This would need to be merged with existing policy
echo "Key policy statement for CloudWatch Logs prepared"

# Associate key
aws logs associate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --kms-key-id "arn:aws:kms:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:key/$KMS_KEY_ID"

echo "✓ KMS key associated with log group"
Output: Checking KMS key policy... Key policy statement for CloudWatch Logs prepared ✓ KMS key associated with log group
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # KMS policy configured: print(f"KMS Key Policy:") print(f" CloudWatch Logs service: Allowed") print(f" Actions: Encrypt, Decrypt, GenerateDataKey") print(f"") print(f"Django model data encrypted:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, User, Author, Location")

disassociate-kms-key

Removes the KMS key association from a CloudWatch log group. This command disables customer-managed encryption, reverting to AWS default encryption for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs disassociate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group to remove encryption from. Example: "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Existing encrypted data remains encrypted; new data uses default encryption
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Remove Encryption from Development Log Group

Disassociates KMS key from development environment logs to reduce encryption costs.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove encryption from dev logs
# Cost optimization

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-dev"

echo "Removing KMS encryption from: $LOG_GROUP"

aws logs disassociate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ KMS encryption removed"
    echo "  Log group now uses default encryption"
else
    echo "✗ Failed to remove encryption"
fi
Output: Removing KMS encryption from: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-dev ✓ KMS encryption removed Log group now uses default encryption
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dev environment encryption removed: print(f"Development Log Group:") print(f" Encryption: Default (AWS managed)") print(f" Cost: Reduced (no KMS charges)") print(f"") print(f"Dev data (non-sensitive):") print(f" - Test JobPost records") print(f" - Mock User data")
Example 2: Disassociate with Verification

Removes KMS key and verifies the disassociation was successful.

#!/bin/bash
# Disassociate and verify
# Confirm removal

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-staging"

echo "Checking current encryption status..."

# Check before
BEFORE=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --query "logGroups[?logGroupName=='$LOG_GROUP'].kmsKeyId | [0]" \
    --output text)

echo "Current KMS Key: ${BEFORE:-None}"

# Disassociate
echo "Removing KMS association..."
aws logs disassociate-kms-key --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP"

# Verify
sleep 1
AFTER=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --query "logGroups[?logGroupName=='$LOG_GROUP'].kmsKeyId | [0]" \
    --output text)

echo "New KMS Key: ${AFTER:-None}"
echo ""
if [ "$AFTER" == "None" ] || [ -z "$AFTER" ]; then
    echo "✓ KMS encryption successfully removed"
else
    echo "✗ KMS key still associated"
fi
Output: Checking current encryption status... Current KMS Key: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abc123 Removing KMS association... New KMS Key: None ✓ KMS encryption successfully removed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Encryption status verified: print(f"Before: Customer Managed KMS") print(f"After: AWS Managed (default)") print(f"") print(f"Staging environment logs:") print(f" - JobPost test data") print(f" - Skills associations") print(f" - No PII in staging")
Example 3: Migrate to Different KMS Key

Disassociates current KMS key before associating a new one for key rotation.

#!/bin/bash
# Key rotation workflow
# Remove old, add new

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
NEW_KMS_KEY="arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/new-key-456"

echo "Rotating KMS key for: $LOG_GROUP"
echo ""

# Step 1: Remove old key
echo "Step 1: Removing old KMS key..."
aws logs disassociate-kms-key --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP"
echo "  ✓ Old key removed"

# Step 2: Associate new key
echo "Step 2: Associating new KMS key..."
aws logs associate-kms-key \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --kms-key-id "$NEW_KMS_KEY"
echo "  ✓ New key associated"

echo ""
echo "Key rotation complete"
Output: Rotating KMS key for: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Step 1: Removing old KMS key... ✓ Old key removed Step 2: Associating new KMS key... ✓ New key associated Key rotation complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Key rotation for Django logs: print(f"KMS Key Rotation:") print(f" Old Key: Removed") print(f" New Key: Associated") print(f"") print(f"Data continuity:") print(f" - Old logs: Still readable") print(f" - New logs: Use new key")
Example 4: Batch Remove Encryption from Multiple Groups

Removes KMS encryption from multiple non-production log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch remove encryption
# Non-production cleanup

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-dev"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-staging"
)

echo "Removing encryption from non-production log groups..."
echo ""

SUCCESS=0
FAILED=0

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    echo "Processing: $LOG_GROUP"
    aws logs disassociate-kms-key --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" 2>/dev/null
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        ((SUCCESS++))
        echo "  ✓ Encryption removed"
    else
        ((FAILED++))
        echo "  ✗ Failed or not encrypted"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Summary: $SUCCESS removed, $FAILED failed/skipped"
Output: Removing encryption from non-production log groups... Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-dev ✓ Encryption removed Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test ✓ Encryption removed Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-staging ✓ Encryption removed Summary: 3 removed, 0 failed/skipped
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Non-production encryption removed: print(f"Environments Cleaned:") print(f" dev: No KMS encryption") print(f" test: No KMS encryption") print(f" staging: No KMS encryption") print(f"") print(f"Production: Still encrypted")
Example 5: Disassociate with Audit Trail

Removes encryption and logs the action for audit purposes.

#!/bin/bash
# Disassociate with audit
# Track change for compliance

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-archive"
AUDIT_LOG="/var/log/cloudwatch-changes.log"

echo "Disassociating KMS key with audit trail..."

# Get current key for audit
CURRENT_KEY=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --query "logGroups[?logGroupName=='$LOG_GROUP'].kmsKeyId | [0]" \
    --output text)

# Perform disassociation
aws logs disassociate-kms-key --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP"

# Create audit record
TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
echo "$TIMESTAMP | disassociate-kms-key | $LOG_GROUP | Previous Key: $CURRENT_KEY | User: $(whoami)" >> "$AUDIT_LOG"

echo "Action logged to: $AUDIT_LOG"
echo ""
cat "$AUDIT_LOG" | tail -1
Output: Disassociating KMS key with audit trail... Action logged to: /var/log/cloudwatch-changes.log 2024-01-20T15:30:45Z | disassociate-kms-key | /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-archive | Previous Key: arn:aws:kms:... | User: admin
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit trail created: print(f"Audit Record:") print(f" Action: disassociate-kms-key") print(f" Log Group: anihpj-jobpost-archive") print(f" Previous Key: Recorded") print(f"") print(f"Compliance tracking enabled")

tag-resource

Adds or updates tags for a CloudWatch Logs resource such as log groups. This command enables cost allocation, resource organization, and access control for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs tag-resource \
    --resource-arn <value> \
    --tags <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string, required): The ARN of the CloudWatch Logs resource to tag. Format: arn:aws:logs:region:account:log-group:name
  • --tags (map, required): Key-value pairs to add as tags. Format: Key1=Value1,Key2=Value2
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Tags are immediately applied to the resource
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Tag Log Group with Environment and Application

Adds standard tags for environment, application, and team identification to enable cost tracking.

#!/bin/bash
# Tag log group for cost allocation
# Standard tagging strategy

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Tagging log group..."

aws logs tag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --tags Environment=production,Application=jobpost,Team=backend,CostCenter=CC-1234

echo "✓ Tags applied successfully"
echo ""
echo "Tags added:"
echo "  Environment: production"
echo "  Application: jobpost"
echo "  Team: backend"
echo "  CostCenter: CC-1234"
Output: Tagging log group... ✓ Tags applied successfully Tags added: Environment: production Application: jobpost Team: backend CostCenter: CC-1234
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tags for Django app logs: print(f"Log Group Tagged:") print(f" /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost") print(f"") print(f"Tags enable tracking of:") print(f" - JobPost logging costs") print(f" - Skills operation metrics") print(f" - User activity expenses")
Example 2: Tag Multiple Log Groups

Applies consistent tags across multiple Django application log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Tag multiple log groups
# Consistent tagging

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-production"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-api"
)

TAGS="Project=anihpj,Owner=devops,DataClassification=confidential"

echo "Applying tags to multiple log groups..."
echo ""

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    ARN="arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:$LOG_GROUP"
    echo "Tagging: $LOG_GROUP"
    aws logs tag-resource --resource-arn "$ARN" --tags $TAGS
    echo "  ✓ Tagged"
done

echo ""
echo "All log groups tagged"
Output: Applying tags to multiple log groups... Tagging: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost ✓ Tagged Tagging: /aws/django/anihpj-production ✓ Tagged Tagging: /aws/django/anihpj-api ✓ Tagged All log groups tagged
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Multiple log groups tagged: print(f"Tagged Django Log Groups:") print(f" anihpj-jobpost: JobPost, Skills, Location") print(f" anihpj-production: User, Author") print(f" anihpj-api: All models via API") print(f"") print(f"Common Tags: Project=anihpj")
Example 3: Add Compliance Tags

Tags log group with compliance-related metadata for audit and governance.

#!/bin/bash
# Add compliance tags
# Audit and governance

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Adding compliance tags..."

aws logs tag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --tags \
        DataRetention=90days,\
        GDPR=applicable,\
        PII=contains,\
        Encryption=kms-managed,\
        AuditRequired=yes

echo "✓ Compliance tags applied"
Output: Adding compliance tags... ✓ Compliance tags applied
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Compliance tags for Django logs: print(f"Compliance Configuration:") print(f" DataRetention: 90 days") print(f" GDPR: Applicable") print(f" PII: Contains (User, Author data)") print(f" Encryption: KMS managed")
Example 4: Update Existing Tags

Updates tags on a log group when environment or ownership changes.

#!/bin/bash
# Update existing tags
# Reflect changes

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Current tags:"
aws logs list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" --output json | jq '.tags'

echo ""
echo "Updating tags..."

# Update Environment from staging to production
aws logs tag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --tags Environment=production,Team=platform-engineering,Version=v2.0

echo ""
echo "Updated tags:"
aws logs list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" --output json | jq '.tags'
Output: Current tags: { "Environment": "staging", "Team": "backend", "Application": "jobpost" } Updating tags... Updated tags: { "Environment": "production", "Team": "platform-engineering", "Application": "jobpost", "Version": "v2.0" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tag updates reflect: print(f"Environment Promotion:") print(f" staging → production") print(f"") print(f"Team transfer:") print(f" backend → platform-engineering") print(f"") print(f"Version: v2.0 (new models)")
Example 5: Tag Using JSON Input

Uses JSON file for complex tagging scenarios with many tags.

#!/bin/bash
# Tag using JSON input
# Complex tagging

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

# Create tags JSON
cat > /tmp/tags.json <<EOF
{
    "resourceArn": "$LOG_GROUP_ARN",
    "tags": {
        "Environment": "production",
        "Application": "django-jobpost",
        "Team": "backend",
        "Project": "anihpj",
        "CostCenter": "CC-1234",
        "Owner": "devops@company.com",
        "DataClassification": "confidential",
        "Backup": "enabled",
        "Monitoring": "enhanced"
    }
}
EOF

echo "Applying tags from JSON..."
aws logs tag-resource --cli-input-json file:///tmp/tags.json

echo "✓ All tags applied"
rm /tmp/tags.json
Output: Applying tags from JSON... ✓ All tags applied
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Comprehensive tagging: print(f"Tags Applied via JSON:") print(f" Environment: production") print(f" Application: django-jobpost") print(f" DataClassification: confidential") print(f"") print(f"Models covered: All 5")

untag-resource

Removes tags from a CloudWatch Logs resource such as log groups. This command manages tag cleanup when reorganizing resources or updating cost allocation for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs untag-resource \
    --resource-arn <value> \
    --tag-keys <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string, required): The ARN of the CloudWatch Logs resource to untag
  • --tag-keys (list, required): List of tag keys to remove. Values are space-separated
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Specified tags are immediately removed
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Remove Obsolete Tags

Removes outdated tags that no longer apply to the log group after reorganization.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove obsolete tags
# Clean up after reorg

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Removing obsolete tags..."

aws logs untag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --tag-keys OldTeam LegacyProject DeprecatedVersion

echo "✓ Tags removed:"
echo "  - OldTeam"
echo "  - LegacyProject"
echo "  - DeprecatedVersion"
Output: Removing obsolete tags... ✓ Tags removed: - OldTeam - LegacyProject - DeprecatedVersion
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Obsolete tags removed: print(f"Tags Cleaned Up:") print(f" OldTeam: Removed (reorg)") print(f" LegacyProject: Removed (renamed)") print(f" DeprecatedVersion: Removed (upgraded)") print(f"") print(f"Django log groups updated")
Example 2: Remove Tags Before Transfer

Clears team-specific tags before transferring log group ownership to another team.

#!/bin/bash
# Clear tags before transfer
# Ownership change

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Current tags:"
aws logs list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" --query 'tags' --output json

echo ""
echo "Removing team-specific tags for transfer..."

aws logs untag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --tag-keys Team Owner CostCenter ContactEmail

echo ""
echo "Tags after removal:"
aws logs list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" --query 'tags' --output json
Output: Current tags: { "Environment": "production", "Application": "jobpost", "Team": "backend", "Owner": "old-team@company.com", "CostCenter": "CC-1234" } Removing team-specific tags for transfer... Tags after removal: { "Environment": "production", "Application": "jobpost" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Prepared for transfer: print(f"Tags Cleared for Transfer:") print(f" Team: Removed") print(f" Owner: Removed") print(f" CostCenter: Removed") print(f"") print(f"Ready for new team assignment")
Example 3: Remove All Custom Tags

Removes all custom tags from a log group to reset tagging configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove all tags
# Complete reset

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Getting current tags..."

# Get all tag keys
TAG_KEYS=$(aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --query 'tags | keys(@)' \
    --output text)

if [ -n "$TAG_KEYS" ]; then
    echo "Removing tags: $TAG_KEYS"
    aws logs untag-resource \
        --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
        --tag-keys $TAG_KEYS
    echo "✓ All tags removed"
else
    echo "No tags to remove"
fi
Output: Getting current tags... Removing tags: Environment Application Team Project CostCenter ✓ All tags removed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Complete tag reset: print(f"All Tags Removed:") print(f" Environment, Application") print(f" Team, Project, CostCenter") print(f"") print(f"Django logs: Clean slate")
Example 4: Batch Remove Tags from Multiple Groups

Removes specific tags from multiple log groups in a single operation.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch untag multiple groups
# Deprecate old tag

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"
TAGS_TO_REMOVE="LegacyTag ObsoleteCategory"

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-production"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-api"
)

echo "Removing deprecated tags from all log groups..."
echo ""

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    ARN="arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:$LOG_GROUP"
    echo "Processing: $LOG_GROUP"
    aws logs untag-resource \
        --resource-arn "$ARN" \
        --tag-keys $TAGS_TO_REMOVE 2>/dev/null
    echo "  ✓ Tags removed"
done

echo ""
echo "Batch removal complete"
Output: Removing deprecated tags from all log groups... Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost ✓ Tags removed Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-production ✓ Tags removed Processing: /aws/django/anihpj-api ✓ Tags removed Batch removal complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch tag cleanup: print(f"Removed from all groups:") print(f" - LegacyTag") print(f" - ObsoleteCategory") print(f"") print(f"All Django log groups cleaned")
Example 5: Selective Tag Removal with Verification

Removes specific tags and verifies the removal with before/after comparison.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove with verification
# Audit trail

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
TAGS_TO_REMOVE="TempTag TestFlag"

echo "Tags BEFORE removal:"
aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --output table

echo ""
echo "Removing: $TAGS_TO_REMOVE"
aws logs untag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --tag-keys $TAGS_TO_REMOVE

echo ""
echo "Tags AFTER removal:"
aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --output table
Output: Tags BEFORE removal: ---------------------------- | ListTagsForResource | +-----------+--------------+ | Key | Value | +-----------+--------------+ | Env | production | | TempTag | test-value | | TestFlag | true | +-----------+--------------+ Removing: TempTag TestFlag Tags AFTER removal: ---------------------------- | ListTagsForResource | +-----------+--------------+ | Key | Value | +-----------+--------------+ | Env | production | +-----------+--------------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified removal: print(f"Before: 3 tags") print(f"After: 1 tag") print(f"") print(f"Removed:") print(f" - TempTag: test cleanup") print(f" - TestFlag: testing complete")

list-tags-for-resource

Lists all tags associated with a CloudWatch Logs resource such as log groups. This command retrieves tag metadata for cost tracking, compliance auditing, and resource organization for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string, required): The ARN of the CloudWatch Logs resource to list tags for
Return Value
  • tags (map): Key-value pairs representing all tags on the resource
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List All Tags for Log Group

Retrieves all tags associated with a Django application log group for review.

#!/bin/bash
# List all tags
# Review configuration

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Tags for: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
echo ""

aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --output json | jq -r '.tags | to_entries[] | "  \(.key): \(.value)"'
Output: Tags for: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Environment: production Application: jobpost Team: backend Project: anihpj CostCenter: CC-1234 DataClassification: confidential
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tags reveal: print(f"Log Group Tags:") print(f" Environment: production") print(f" Application: jobpost") print(f"") print(f"Django models in this log group:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, Location, User, Author")
Example 2: Export Tags to File

Exports tag configuration to a JSON file for backup or documentation.

#!/bin/bash
# Export tags to file
# Documentation

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
OUTPUT_FILE="log-group-tags.json"

echo "Exporting tags to $OUTPUT_FILE..."

aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --output json > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "✓ Tags exported"
echo ""
cat "$OUTPUT_FILE" | jq '.'
Output: Exporting tags to log-group-tags.json... ✓ Tags exported { "tags": { "Environment": "production", "Application": "jobpost", "Team": "backend", "Project": "anihpj" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tags exported: print(f"Tag Export Complete:") print(f" File: log-group-tags.json") print(f" Tags: 4") print(f"") print(f"Use for documentation/backup")
Example 3: Compare Tags Across Log Groups

Lists and compares tags across multiple Django log groups for consistency checking.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare tags across groups
# Consistency check

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-production"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-api"
)

echo "Tag Comparison Report"
echo "===================="
echo ""

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    ARN="arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:$LOG_GROUP"
    echo "Log Group: $LOG_GROUP"
    
    aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
        --resource-arn "$ARN" \
        --output json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.tags | to_entries[] | "  \(.key): \(.value)"'
    
    echo ""
done
Output: Tag Comparison Report ==================== Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Environment: production Team: backend Project: anihpj Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-production Environment: production Team: platform Project: anihpj Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-api Environment: production Team: backend Project: anihpj
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tag comparison: print(f"Consistent tags:") print(f" Environment: production (all)") print(f" Project: anihpj (all)") print(f"") print(f"Inconsistent:") print(f" Team: backend vs platform")
Example 4: Check for Required Tags

Verifies that all required tags are present for compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# Check required tags
# Compliance verification

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

REQUIRED_TAGS=("Environment" "Application" "Team" "CostCenter" "DataClassification")

echo "Checking required tags..."
echo ""

# Get current tags
CURRENT_TAGS=$(aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --query 'tags | keys(@)' \
    --output json)

MISSING=0
for TAG in "${REQUIRED_TAGS[@]}"; do
    if echo "$CURRENT_TAGS" | jq -e --arg tag "$TAG" 'index($tag) != null' > /dev/null; then
        echo "  ✓ $TAG: Present"
    else
        echo "  ✗ $TAG: MISSING"
        ((MISSING++))
    fi
done

echo ""
if [ $MISSING -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "All required tags present"
else
    echo "WARNING: $MISSING required tags missing"
fi
Output: Checking required tags... ✓ Environment: Present ✓ Application: Present ✓ Team: Present ✓ CostCenter: Present ✗ DataClassification: MISSING WARNING: 1 required tags missing
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Compliance check: print(f"Required Tags Status:") print(f" Present: 4/5") print(f" Missing: DataClassification") print(f"") print(f"Action: Add DataClassification tag") print(f" (confidential - contains User data)")
Example 5: Generate Tag Audit Report

Creates a comprehensive audit report of tags for all Django log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate tag audit report
# Compliance documentation

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"
REPORT_FILE="tag-audit-report.txt"

echo "Django Log Groups Tag Audit Report" > "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "Generated: $(date)" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "======================================" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "" >> "$REPORT_FILE"

# Get all Django log groups
LOG_GROUPS=$(aws logs describe-log-groups \
    --log-group-name-prefix "/aws/django/anihpj" \
    --query 'logGroups[].logGroupName' \
    --output text)

for LOG_GROUP in $LOG_GROUPS; do
    ARN="arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:$LOG_GROUP"
    
    echo "Log Group: $LOG_GROUP" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
    echo "ARN: $ARN" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
    echo "Tags:" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
    
    aws logs list-tags-for-resource \
        --resource-arn "$ARN" \
        --output json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.tags | to_entries[] | "  \(.key): \(.value)"' >> "$REPORT_FILE"
    
    echo "" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
done

echo "Report saved to: $REPORT_FILE"
cat "$REPORT_FILE"
Output: Report saved to: tag-audit-report.txt Django Log Groups Tag Audit Report Generated: Sat Jan 20 15:30:00 UTC 2024 ====================================== Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost ARN: arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost Tags: Environment: production Application: jobpost Team: backend Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-production ARN: arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-production Tags: Environment: production Application: production Team: platform
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit report generated: print(f"Tag Audit Report:") print(f" Log Groups: 2") print(f" Total Tags: 6") print(f"") print(f"Coverage:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, Location: anihpj-jobpost") print(f" User, Author: anihpj-production")

put-resource-policy

Creates or updates a CloudWatch Logs resource policy to allow other AWS services to write logs. This command configures cross-service permissions enabling services like API Gateway, Route53, or Lambda to send logs for Django application monitoring of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name <value> \
    --policy-document <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --policy-name (string, required): A name for the resource policy. Must be unique within the region
  • --policy-document (string, required): IAM policy document in JSON format specifying allowed actions and principals
Return Value
  • resourcePolicy (object): Contains:
    • policyName: Name of the created/updated policy
    • policyDocument: The policy document
    • lastUpdatedTime: When the policy was last updated
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Allow API Gateway to Write Logs

Creates a resource policy allowing API Gateway to write access logs for Django API endpoints.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow API Gateway logging
# Track Django API calls

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating API Gateway resource policy..."

aws logs put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "DjangoAPIGatewayLogsPolicy" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [{
            "Sid": "AllowAPIGatewayLogs",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "Service": "apigateway.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": [
                "logs:CreateLogStream",
                "logs:PutLogEvents"
            ],
            "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/api-gateway/django-anihpj/*"
        }]
    }'

echo "✓ API Gateway can now write to Django logs"
Output: Creating API Gateway resource policy... { "resourcePolicy": { "policyName": "DjangoAPIGatewayLogsPolicy", "policyDocument": "{...}", "lastUpdatedTime": 1705766400000 } } ✓ API Gateway can now write to Django logs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # API Gateway logging enabled: print(f"API Endpoints Logged:") print(f" /api/jobposts/ - JobPost CRUD") print(f" /api/skills/ - Skills listing") print(f" /api/users/ - User management") print(f" /api/authors/ - Author profiles") print(f" /api/locations/ - Location data")
Example 2: Allow Lambda Function Logging

Creates policy for Lambda functions that process Django model events.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow Lambda logging
# Django event processors

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating Lambda resource policy..."

aws logs put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "DjangoLambdaLogsPolicy" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [{
            "Sid": "AllowLambdaLogs",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": [
                "logs:CreateLogGroup",
                "logs:CreateLogStream",
                "logs:PutLogEvents"
            ],
            "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/lambda/django-*"
        }]
    }'

echo "✓ Lambda functions can write Django event logs"
Output: Creating Lambda resource policy... { "resourcePolicy": { "policyName": "DjangoLambdaLogsPolicy", "policyDocument": "{...}", "lastUpdatedTime": 1705766400000 } } ✓ Lambda functions can write Django event logs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Lambda processors for: print(f"Lambda Event Processors:") print(f" django-jobpost-processor") print(f" django-user-notification") print(f" django-skills-indexer") print(f"") print(f"All can write to CloudWatch")
Example 3: Allow Route53 Query Logging

Enables Route53 to log DNS queries for the Django application domain.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow Route53 query logging
# DNS monitoring

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating Route53 resource policy..."

aws logs put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "DjangoRoute53QueryLogsPolicy" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [{
            "Sid": "AllowRoute53Logs",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "Service": "route53.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": [
                "logs:CreateLogStream",
                "logs:PutLogEvents"
            ],
