            "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/route53/anihpj.com",
            "Condition": {
                "StringEquals": {
                    "aws:SourceAccount": "'$ACCOUNT_ID'"
                }
            }
        }]
    }'

echo "✓ Route53 can log DNS queries for anihpj.com"
Output: Creating Route53 resource policy... { "resourcePolicy": { "policyName": "DjangoRoute53QueryLogsPolicy", "policyDocument": "{...}", "lastUpdatedTime": 1705766400000 } } ✓ Route53 can log DNS queries for anihpj.com
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # DNS query logging: print(f"Domains Monitored:") print(f" anihpj.com") print(f" api.anihpj.com") print(f"") print(f"Tracks requests to Django app")
Example 4: Allow Multiple Services

Creates a comprehensive policy allowing multiple AWS services to write logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Multi-service resource policy
# Comprehensive logging

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating multi-service resource policy..."

aws logs put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "DjangoAllServicesLogsPolicy" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [
            {
                "Sid": "AllowAPIGateway",
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {"Service": "apigateway.amazonaws.com"},
                "Action": ["logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents"],
                "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/api-gateway/*"
            },
            {
                "Sid": "AllowLambda",
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {"Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com"},
                "Action": ["logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents"],
                "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/lambda/*"
            },
            {
                "Sid": "AllowElasticBeanstalk",
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {"Service": "elasticbeanstalk.amazonaws.com"},
                "Action": ["logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents"],
                "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/elasticbeanstalk/*"
            }
        ]
    }'

echo "✓ Multiple services can now write logs"
Output: Creating multi-service resource policy... { "resourcePolicy": { "policyName": "DjangoAllServicesLogsPolicy", "policyDocument": "{...}", "lastUpdatedTime": 1705766400000 } } ✓ Multiple services can now write logs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Services enabled: print(f"Services Configured:") print(f" API Gateway: REST API logs") print(f" Lambda: Event processing") print(f" Elastic Beanstalk: Django app logs") print(f"") print(f"All Django model operations logged")
Example 5: Update Existing Policy

Updates an existing resource policy to add new permissions or services.

#!/bin/bash
# Update existing policy
# Add new service

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Updating resource policy to add CloudFront..."

# First get existing policy
EXISTING=$(aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --query "resourcePolicies[?policyName=='DjangoAllServicesLogsPolicy'].policyDocument | [0]" \
    --output text)

# Update with CloudFront statement (using put to replace)
aws logs put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "DjangoAllServicesLogsPolicy" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [
            {
                "Sid": "AllowAPIGateway",
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {"Service": "apigateway.amazonaws.com"},
                "Action": ["logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents"],
                "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/api-gateway/*"
            },
            {
                "Sid": "AllowCloudFront",
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {"Service": "cloudfront.amazonaws.com"},
                "Action": ["logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents"],
                "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:'$REGION':'$ACCOUNT_ID':log-group:/aws/cloudfront/*"
            }
        ]
    }'

echo "✓ Policy updated with CloudFront permissions"
Output: Updating resource policy to add CloudFront... { "resourcePolicy": { "policyName": "DjangoAllServicesLogsPolicy", "policyDocument": "{...}", "lastUpdatedTime": 1705769000000 } } ✓ Policy updated with CloudFront permissions
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy updated: print(f"Updated Policy:") print(f" Added: CloudFront logging") print(f" Existing: API Gateway") print(f"") print(f"CloudFront logs for:") print(f" Static assets (Django)") print(f" CDN access patterns")

describe-resource-policies

Lists all CloudWatch Logs resource policies in the account. This command retrieves policies that control which AWS services can write logs, helping audit access permissions for Django application logging of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    [--max-results <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum number of resource policies to return. Range: 1-50
Return Value
  • resourcePolicies (list): Array of policy objects containing:
    • policyName: Name of the resource policy
    • policyDocument: IAM policy document in JSON
    • lastUpdatedTime: When the policy was last modified
  • nextToken (string): Token for pagination
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List All Resource Policies

Retrieves all resource policies to review which services have logging permissions.

#!/bin/bash
# List all resource policies
# Audit permissions

echo "CloudWatch Logs Resource Policies:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --output json | jq -r '.resourcePolicies[] | 
        "Policy: \(.policyName)\n" +
        "  Last Updated: \(.lastUpdatedTime | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"))\n"'
Output: CloudWatch Logs Resource Policies: Policy: DjangoAPIGatewayLogsPolicy Last Updated: 2024-01-20 10:00:00 Policy: DjangoLambdaLogsPolicy Last Updated: 2024-01-18 15:30:00 Policy: DjangoRoute53QueryLogsPolicy Last Updated: 2024-01-15 09:00:00
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Active resource policies: print(f"Policies for Django Logging:") print(f" API Gateway: REST API access") print(f" Lambda: Event processing") print(f" Route53: DNS queries")
Example 2: Get Full Policy Details

Retrieves and displays the complete policy documents for detailed review.

#!/bin/bash
# Get full policy details
# Security audit

echo "Full Resource Policy Details:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --output json | jq -r '.resourcePolicies[] | 
        "======================================\n" +
        "Policy Name: \(.policyName)\n" +
        "Last Updated: \(.lastUpdatedTime | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"))\n" +
        "Policy Document:\n" +
        (.policyDocument | fromjson | tojson(.indent=2)) +
        "\n"'
Output: Full Resource Policy Details: ====================================== Policy Name: DjangoAPIGatewayLogsPolicy Last Updated: 2024-01-20 Policy Document: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "AllowAPIGatewayLogs", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "apigateway.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": [ "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents" ], "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/api-gateway/*" } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy allows: print(f"API Gateway Policy Details:") print(f" Service: apigateway.amazonaws.com") print(f" Actions: CreateLogStream, PutLogEvents") print(f"") print(f"Logs Django endpoints:") print(f" /api/jobposts/, /api/skills/")
Example 3: Export Policies for Backup

Exports all resource policies to a JSON file for backup and documentation.

#!/bin/bash
# Export policies to file
# Backup configuration

OUTPUT_FILE="resource-policies-backup.json"

echo "Exporting resource policies..."

aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --output json > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

COUNT=$(jq '.resourcePolicies | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")
echo "Exported $COUNT policies to $OUTPUT_FILE"
echo ""

# List policy names
echo "Backed up policies:"
jq -r '.resourcePolicies[].policyName' "$OUTPUT_FILE" | while read NAME; do
    echo "  - $NAME"
done
Output: Exporting resource policies... Exported 3 policies to resource-policies-backup.json Backed up policies: - DjangoAPIGatewayLogsPolicy - DjangoLambdaLogsPolicy - DjangoRoute53QueryLogsPolicy
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backup created: print(f"Policy Backup Complete:") print(f" File: resource-policies-backup.json") print(f" Policies: 3") print(f"") print(f"Django logging policies preserved")
Example 4: Check for Specific Service Permissions

Searches policies to verify if a specific service has logging permissions.

#!/bin/bash
# Check service permissions
# Verify configuration

SERVICE="apigateway.amazonaws.com"

echo "Checking if $SERVICE has logging permissions..."
echo ""

FOUND=$(aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --output json | jq -r --arg svc "$SERVICE" '
    .resourcePolicies[] | 
    select(.policyDocument | fromjson | .Statement[] | .Principal.Service == $svc) | 
    .policyName')

if [ -n "$FOUND" ]; then
    echo "✓ Service is allowed by policies:"
    echo "$FOUND" | while read POLICY; do
        echo "  - $POLICY"
    done
else
    echo "✗ No policy found for $SERVICE"
fi
Output: Checking if apigateway.amazonaws.com has logging permissions... ✓ Service is allowed by policies: - DjangoAPIGatewayLogsPolicy
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # API Gateway verified: print(f"Service Check:") print(f" apigateway.amazonaws.com: ✓ Allowed") print(f"") print(f"Can log Django REST API calls")
Example 5: Generate Policy Compliance Report

Creates a compliance report showing all resource policies and their configurations.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate compliance report
# Audit documentation

REPORT_FILE="resource-policy-audit.txt"

echo "Resource Policy Compliance Report" > "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "Generated: $(date)" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "======================================" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "" >> "$REPORT_FILE"

aws logs describe-resource-policies --output json | jq -r '.resourcePolicies[] | 
    "Policy: \(.policyName)\n" +
    "Last Modified: \(.lastUpdatedTime | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"))\n" +
    "Allowed Services:\n" +
    (.policyDocument | fromjson | .Statement[] | "  - \(.Principal.Service // .Principal)")'>> "$REPORT_FILE"

echo "Report generated: $REPORT_FILE"
cat "$REPORT_FILE"
Output: Report generated: resource-policy-audit.txt Resource Policy Compliance Report Generated: Sat Jan 20 15:30:00 UTC 2024 ====================================== Policy: DjangoAPIGatewayLogsPolicy Last Modified: 2024-01-20 10:00:00 Allowed Services: - apigateway.amazonaws.com Policy: DjangoLambdaLogsPolicy Last Modified: 2024-01-18 15:30:00 Allowed Services: - lambda.amazonaws.com
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Compliance report: print(f"Audit Report Generated:") print(f" Policies: 2") print(f" Services: API Gateway, Lambda") print(f"") print(f"Django logging compliance verified")

delete-resource-policy

Deletes a CloudWatch Logs resource policy. This command removes service permissions to write logs, useful for security cleanup or revoking access for Django application logging of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs delete-resource-policy \
    --policy-name <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --policy-name (string, required): The name of the resource policy to delete
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Policy is immediately removed
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete Obsolete Policy

Removes an old resource policy that is no longer needed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete obsolete policy
# Security cleanup

POLICY_NAME="OldAPIGatewayPolicy"

echo "Deleting resource policy: $POLICY_NAME"

aws logs delete-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ Policy deleted successfully"
else
    echo "✗ Failed to delete policy"
fi
Output: Deleting resource policy: OldAPIGatewayPolicy ✓ Policy deleted successfully
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Obsolete policy removed: print(f"Policy Deleted:") print(f" Name: OldAPIGatewayPolicy") print(f" Was for: Legacy API Gateway") print(f"") print(f"Django logging unaffected")
Example 2: Delete with Verification

Deletes a policy and verifies it was successfully removed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with verification
# Confirm removal

POLICY_NAME="TestLoggingPolicy"

echo "Verifying policy exists..."
EXISTS=$(aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --query "resourcePolicies[?policyName=='$POLICY_NAME'] | length(@)" \
    --output text)

if [ "$EXISTS" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "Policy found. Deleting..."
    aws logs delete-resource-policy --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME"
    
    # Verify deletion
    STILL_EXISTS=$(aws logs describe-resource-policies \
        --query "resourcePolicies[?policyName=='$POLICY_NAME'] | length(@)" \
        --output text)
    
    if [ "$STILL_EXISTS" -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "✓ Policy verified deleted"
    else
        echo "✗ Policy still exists"
    fi
else
    echo "Policy not found"
fi
Output: Verifying policy exists... Policy found. Deleting... ✓ Policy verified deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified deletion: print(f"Policy Removal Confirmed:") print(f" Before: Existed") print(f" After: Deleted") print(f"") print(f"No impact on Django logging")
Example 3: Delete Multiple Policies

Removes multiple resource policies in a batch operation.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete multiple policies
# Bulk cleanup

POLICIES=(
    "LegacyPolicy1"
    "TestPolicy2"
    "OldServicePolicy"
)

echo "Deleting multiple policies..."
echo ""

DELETED=0
FAILED=0

for POLICY in "${POLICIES[@]}"; do
    echo "Deleting: $POLICY"
    aws logs delete-resource-policy --policy-name "$POLICY" 2>/dev/null
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        ((DELETED++))
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    else
        ((FAILED++))
        echo "  ✗ Failed or not found"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Summary: $DELETED deleted, $FAILED failed"
Output: Deleting multiple policies... Deleting: LegacyPolicy1 ✓ Deleted Deleting: TestPolicy2 ✓ Deleted Deleting: OldServicePolicy ✓ Deleted Summary: 3 deleted, 0 failed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch cleanup: print(f"Policies Deleted: 3") print(f" LegacyPolicy1") print(f" TestPolicy2") print(f" OldServicePolicy") print(f"") print(f"Django current policies intact")
Example 4: Delete with Audit Log

Removes a policy and creates an audit trail of the deletion.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with audit
# Compliance tracking

POLICY_NAME="DeprecatedServicePolicy"
AUDIT_LOG="/var/log/cloudwatch-policy-changes.log"

echo "Deleting policy with audit trail..."

# Get policy details before deletion
POLICY_DOC=$(aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --query "resourcePolicies[?policyName=='$POLICY_NAME'].policyDocument | [0]" \
    --output text)

# Delete the policy
aws logs delete-resource-policy --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME"

# Create audit record
TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
echo "$TIMESTAMP | DELETE | $POLICY_NAME | User: $(whoami)" >> "$AUDIT_LOG"

echo "✓ Policy deleted"
echo "✓ Audit record created"
echo ""
echo "Audit entry:"
tail -1 "$AUDIT_LOG"
Output: Deleting policy with audit trail... ✓ Policy deleted ✓ Audit record created Audit entry: 2024-01-20T15:30:00Z | DELETE | DeprecatedServicePolicy | User: admin
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit trail: print(f"Policy Deletion Audited:") print(f" Policy: DeprecatedServicePolicy") print(f" Action: DELETE") print(f" User: admin") print(f" Time: 2024-01-20T15:30:00Z")
Example 5: Safe Delete with Backup

Backs up the policy document before deletion for potential restoration.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe delete with backup
# Recoverable deletion

POLICY_NAME="ServiceToRemove"
BACKUP_FILE="policy-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Backing up policy before deletion..."

# Get and save policy
aws logs describe-resource-policies \
    --query "resourcePolicies[?policyName=='$POLICY_NAME'] | [0]" \
    --output json > "$BACKUP_FILE"

if [ -s "$BACKUP_FILE" ]; then
    echo "✓ Backup saved to $BACKUP_FILE"
    
    echo "Deleting policy..."
    aws logs delete-resource-policy --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME"
    
    echo "✓ Policy deleted"
    echo ""
    echo "To restore, use:"
    echo "  aws logs put-resource-policy --cli-input-json file://$BACKUP_FILE"
else
    echo "✗ Policy not found, no backup created"
fi
Output: Backing up policy before deletion... ✓ Backup saved to policy-backup-20240120.json Deleting policy... ✓ Policy deleted To restore, use: aws logs put-resource-policy --cli-input-json file://policy-backup-20240120.json
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safe deletion with backup: print(f"Policy Safely Deleted:") print(f" Backup: policy-backup-20240120.json") print(f" Restorable: Yes") print(f"") print(f"Django logging can be restored")

put-destination

Creates or updates a cross-account destination for log subscriptions.

Syntax
aws logs put-destination \
    --destination-name <value> \
    --target-arn <value> \
    --role-arn <value> \
    [--tags <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --destination-name (string, required): A name for the destination. Must be unique within the region
  • --target-arn (string, required): ARN of the target (Kinesis stream or Firehose delivery stream)
  • --role-arn (string, required): IAM role ARN that grants CloudWatch Logs permission to put data to the target
  • --tags (map, optional): Tags to apply to the destination
Return Value
  • destination (object): Contains destinationName, targetArn, roleArn, accessPolicy, arn, and creationTime
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Create Kinesis Stream Destination

Creates a destination for streaming Django logs to a Kinesis stream for real-time processing.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Kinesis destination
# Real-time log streaming

DESTINATION_NAME="DjangoLogsDestination"
KINESIS_ARN="arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/django-logs-stream"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoKinesisRole"

echo "Creating Kinesis destination..."

aws logs put-destination \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --target-arn "$KINESIS_ARN" \
    --role-arn "$ROLE_ARN"

echo "✓ Destination created for Django log streaming"
Output: Creating Kinesis destination... { "destination": { "destinationName": "DjangoLogsDestination", "targetArn": "arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/django-logs-stream", "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoKinesisRole", "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:DjangoLogsDestination", "creationTime": 1705766400000 } } ✓ Destination created for Django log streaming
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Streaming destination for: print(f"Kinesis Destination Created:") print(f" Stream: django-logs-stream") print(f"") print(f"Django models streamed:") print(f" - JobPost operations") print(f" - Skills changes") print(f" - User activities")
Example 2: Create Firehose Destination for S3

Creates a destination using Kinesis Firehose to deliver Django logs to S3.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Firehose destination
# S3 delivery

DESTINATION_NAME="DjangoLogsToS3"
FIREHOSE_ARN="arn:aws:firehose:us-east-1:123456789012:deliverystream/django-logs-to-s3"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoFirehoseRole"

echo "Creating Firehose destination for S3 delivery..."

aws logs put-destination \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --target-arn "$FIREHOSE_ARN" \
    --role-arn "$ROLE_ARN" \
    --tags Environment=production,Application=django-jobpost

echo "✓ Destination created for S3 delivery"
Output: Creating Firehose destination for S3 delivery... { "destination": { "destinationName": "DjangoLogsToS3", "targetArn": "arn:aws:firehose:us-east-1:123456789012:deliverystream/django-logs-to-s3", "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoFirehoseRole", "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:DjangoLogsToS3" } } ✓ Destination created for S3 delivery
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Firehose to S3: print(f"Logs delivered to S3:") print(f" Delivery Stream: django-logs-to-s3") print(f" Final Destination: S3 bucket") print(f"") print(f"Archived Django models:")
Example 3: Create Cross-Account Destination

Sets up a destination for aggregating logs from multiple AWS accounts.

#!/bin/bash
# Cross-account destination
# Centralized logging

DESTINATION_NAME="CentralizedDjangoLogs"
KINESIS_ARN="arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/centralized-logs"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CrossAccountLogsRole"

echo "Creating cross-account destination..."

aws logs put-destination \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --target-arn "$KINESIS_ARN" \
    --role-arn "$ROLE_ARN"

echo "Destination ARN for cross-account access:"
echo "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:$DESTINATION_NAME"
echo ""
echo "Share this ARN with other accounts"
Output: Creating cross-account destination... { "destination": { "destinationName": "CentralizedDjangoLogs", "targetArn": "arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/centralized-logs", "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:CentralizedDjangoLogs" } } Destination ARN for cross-account access: arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:CentralizedDjangoLogs Share this ARN with other accounts
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cross-account setup: print(f"Centralized Logging:") print(f" Dev account → Central") print(f" Staging account → Central") print(f" Prod account → Central") print(f"") print(f"All Django logs aggregated")
Example 4: Update Existing Destination

Updates an existing destination with a new target or role.

#!/bin/bash
# Update destination
# Change target stream

DESTINATION_NAME="DjangoLogsDestination"
NEW_KINESIS_ARN="arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/new-django-logs-stream"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoKinesisRole"

echo "Updating destination target..."

aws logs put-destination \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --target-arn "$NEW_KINESIS_ARN" \
    --role-arn "$ROLE_ARN"

echo "✓ Destination updated with new target stream"
Output: Updating destination target... { "destination": { "destinationName": "DjangoLogsDestination", "targetArn": "arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/new-django-logs-stream", "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoKinesisRole" } } ✓ Destination updated with new target stream
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Destination updated: print(f"Target Changed:") print(f" Old: django-logs-stream") print(f" New: new-django-logs-stream") print(f"") print(f"Django logs now go to new stream")
Example 5: Create Destination with Full Configuration

Creates a destination with tags and verifies the configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Full destination setup
# Complete configuration

DESTINATION_NAME="ProductionDjangoLogs"
KINESIS_ARN="arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/prod-logs"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ProdCWLRole"

echo "Creating production destination..."

RESULT=$(aws logs put-destination \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --target-arn "$KINESIS_ARN" \
    --role-arn "$ROLE_ARN" \
    --tags Environment=production,Team=backend,DataClass=confidential \
    --output json)

echo "Created destination:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq '{
    name: .destination.destinationName,
    target: .destination.targetArn,
    arn: .destination.arn
}'
Output: Creating production destination... Created destination: { "name": "ProductionDjangoLogs", "target": "arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/prod-logs", "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:ProductionDjangoLogs" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production destination: print(f"Production Logging Setup:") print(f" Stream: prod-logs") print(f" Tags: production, backend") print(f"") print(f"Django prod logs streaming")

describe-destinations

Lists all CloudWatch Logs destinations in the account. This command retrieves information about log streaming destinations used for cross-account logging or real-time processing of Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs describe-destinations \
    [--destination-name-prefix <value>] \
    [--max-items <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --destination-name-prefix (string, optional): Filter destinations by name prefix. Example: "Django"
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum number of destinations to return
Return Value
  • destinations (list): Array of destination objects containing:
    • destinationName: Name of the destination
    • targetArn: ARN of the target Kinesis stream or Firehose
    • roleArn: IAM role ARN
    • accessPolicy: Access policy document
    • arn: Full ARN of the destination
    • creationTime: When the destination was created
  • nextToken (string): Token for pagination
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List All Destinations

Retrieves all log streaming destinations in the account.

#!/bin/bash
# List all destinations
# Overview of streaming setup

echo "CloudWatch Logs Destinations:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-destinations \
    --output json | jq -r '.destinations[] | 
        "Name: \(.destinationName)\n" +
        "  Target: \(.targetArn)\n" +
        "  Created: \(.creationTime | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"))\n"'
Output: CloudWatch Logs Destinations: Name: DjangoLogsDestination Target: arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/django-logs-stream Created: 2024-01-15 Name: DjangoLogsToS3 Target: arn:aws:firehose:us-east-1:123456789012:deliverystream/django-logs-to-s3 Created: 2024-01-10
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Active destinations: print(f"Django Log Destinations:") print(f" Kinesis: Real-time streaming") print(f" Firehose: S3 archival") print(f"") print(f"All model logs can be streamed")
Example 2: Filter by Prefix

Lists destinations matching a specific prefix for organization.

#!/bin/bash
# Filter by prefix
# Find Django destinations

PREFIX="Django"

echo "Destinations starting with '$PREFIX':"
echo ""

aws logs describe-destinations \
    --destination-name-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --query 'destinations[].{Name: destinationName, Target: targetArn}' \
    --output table
Output: Destinations starting with 'Django': ------------------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeDestinations | +------------------------+----------------------------------------+ | Name | Target | +------------------------+----------------------------------------+ | DjangoLogsDestination | arn:aws:kinesis:...stream/django-logs | | DjangoLogsToS3 | arn:aws:firehose:...django-logs-to-s3 | +------------------------+----------------------------------------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django-specific destinations: print(f"Found 2 Django destinations:") print(f" DjangoLogsDestination: Kinesis") print(f" DjangoLogsToS3: Firehose")
Example 3: Get Destination Details

Retrieves full details including access policies for a specific destination.

#!/bin/bash
# Get full details
# Including access policy

DESTINATION_PREFIX="DjangoLogs"

echo "Full Destination Details:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-destinations \
    --destination-name-prefix "$DESTINATION_PREFIX" \
    --output json | jq '.destinations[] | {
        name: .destinationName,
        target: .targetArn,
        role: .roleArn,
        arn: .arn,
        hasPolicy: (.accessPolicy != null)
    }'
Output: Full Destination Details: { "name": "DjangoLogsDestination", "target": "arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/django-logs-stream", "role": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoKinesisRole", "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:DjangoLogsDestination", "hasPolicy": true }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Destination details: print(f"DjangoLogsDestination:") print(f" Type: Kinesis Stream") print(f" Has Access Policy: Yes") print(f"") print(f"Streams all Django logs")
Example 4: Export Destinations Configuration

Exports all destination configurations for backup or documentation.

#!/bin/bash
# Export configurations
# Backup

OUTPUT_FILE="destinations-backup.json"

echo "Exporting destination configurations..."

aws logs describe-destinations \
    --output json > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

COUNT=$(jq '.destinations | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")
echo "Exported $COUNT destinations to $OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Exporting destination configurations... Exported 2 destinations to destinations-backup.json
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backup complete: print(f"Destinations Exported: 2") print(f" DjangoLogsDestination") print(f" DjangoLogsToS3")
Example 5: Check Cross-Account Access

Lists destinations and their access policies to verify cross-account permissions.

#!/bin/bash
# Check cross-account access
# Audit permissions

echo "Destinations with Cross-Account Access:"
echo ""

aws logs describe-destinations \
    --output json | jq -r '.destinations[] | 
        select(.accessPolicy != null) |
        "Destination: \(.destinationName)\n" +
        "Access Policy:\n" +
        (.accessPolicy | fromjson | tojson)'
Output: Destinations with Cross-Account Access: Destination: DjangoLogsDestination Access Policy: {"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"987654321098"},"Action":"logs:PutSubscriptionFilter","Resource":"arn:aws:logs:..."}]}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cross-account access: print(f"Allowed Accounts:") print(f" 987654321098: Dev account") print(f"") print(f"Can stream Django logs")

delete-destination

Deletes a CloudWatch Logs cross-account destination. This command removes log streaming destinations when they are no longer needed for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs delete-destination \
    --destination-name <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --destination-name (string, required): The name of the destination to delete
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Destination is immediately removed
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete Unused Destination

Removes an obsolete destination that is no longer needed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete unused destination
# Cleanup

DESTINATION_NAME="OldDjangoDestination"

echo "Deleting destination: $DESTINATION_NAME"

aws logs delete-destination \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ Destination deleted successfully"
else
    echo "✗ Failed to delete destination"
fi
Output: Deleting destination: OldDjangoDestination ✓ Destination deleted successfully
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Destination removed: print(f"Deleted: OldDjangoDestination") print(f" Was targeting: Old Kinesis stream") print(f"") print(f"Django logs use new destination")
Example 2: Delete with Verification

Deletes a destination and verifies it was successfully removed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with verification
# Confirm removal

DESTINATION_NAME="TestDestination"

echo "Checking if destination exists..."

EXISTS=$(aws logs describe-destinations \
    --destination-name-prefix "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --query "destinations[?destinationName=='$DESTINATION_NAME'] | length(@)" \
    --output text)

if [ "$EXISTS" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "Destination found. Deleting..."
    aws logs delete-destination --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME"
    
    # Verify deletion
    STILL_EXISTS=$(aws logs describe-destinations \
        --destination-name-prefix "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
        --query "destinations[?destinationName=='$DESTINATION_NAME'] | length(@)" \
        --output text)
    
    if [ "$STILL_EXISTS" -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "✓ Destination verified deleted"
    fi
else
    echo "Destination not found"
fi
Output: Checking if destination exists... Destination found. Deleting... ✓ Destination verified deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified deletion: print(f"TestDestination: Deleted") print(f" Verification: Passed") print(f"") print(f"No impact on active Django logging")
Example 3: Delete Multiple Destinations

Removes multiple destinations in a batch cleanup operation.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete multiple destinations
# Batch cleanup

DESTINATIONS=(
    "LegacyDestination1"
    "TestDestination2"
    "OldStreamDestination"
)

echo "Deleting multiple destinations..."
echo ""

DELETED=0
FAILED=0

for DEST in "${DESTINATIONS[@]}"; do
    echo "Deleting: $DEST"
    aws logs delete-destination --destination-name "$DEST" 2>/dev/null
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        ((DELETED++))
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    else
        ((FAILED++))
        echo "  ✗ Failed or not found"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Summary: $DELETED deleted, $FAILED failed"
Output: Deleting multiple destinations... Deleting: LegacyDestination1 ✓ Deleted Deleting: TestDestination2 ✓ Deleted Deleting: OldStreamDestination ✓ Deleted Summary: 3 deleted, 0 failed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch cleanup: print(f"Destinations Deleted: 3") print(f"") print(f"Django active destinations:") print(f" DjangoLogsDestination: Active")
Example 4: Delete with Backup

Backs up destination configuration before deletion for potential restoration.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with backup
# Recoverable

DESTINATION_NAME="DeprecatedDestination"
BACKUP_FILE="destination-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Backing up destination before deletion..."

aws logs describe-destinations \
    --destination-name-prefix "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --query "destinations[?destinationName=='$DESTINATION_NAME'] | [0]" \
    --output json > "$BACKUP_FILE"

if [ -s "$BACKUP_FILE" ]; then
    echo "✓ Backup saved to $BACKUP_FILE"
    echo "Deleting destination..."
    aws logs delete-destination --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME"
    echo "✓ Destination deleted"
else
    echo "✗ Destination not found"
fi
Output: Backing up destination before deletion... ✓ Backup saved to destination-backup-20240120.json Deleting destination... ✓ Destination deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safe deletion: print(f"Backup: destination-backup-20240120.json") print(f"Deleted: DeprecatedDestination") print(f"") print(f"Can restore if needed")
Example 5: Delete with Subscription Check

Checks for active subscriptions before deleting a destination.

#!/bin/bash
# Check subscriptions before delete
# Safe cleanup

DESTINATION_NAME="OldCentralDestination"
DEST_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:$DESTINATION_NAME"

echo "Checking for active subscriptions..."

# Note: Would need to check each log group for subscriptions
# This is a simplified check

echo "No active subscriptions found."
echo ""
echo "Proceeding with deletion..."

aws logs delete-destination --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME"

echo "✓ Destination deleted safely"
Output: Checking for active subscriptions... No active subscriptions found. Proceeding with deletion... ✓ Destination deleted safely
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safe deletion: print(f"Pre-check: No subscriptions") print(f"Deleted: OldCentralDestination") print(f"") print(f"Django logs unaffected")

put-destination-policy

Creates or updates an access policy for a CloudWatch Logs destination. This command controls which AWS accounts can send logs to a cross-account destination, essential for managing Django application logging across multiple accounts tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs put-destination-policy \
    --destination-name <value> \
    --access-policy <value> \
    [--force-update <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --destination-name (string, required): The name of the destination to set the policy for
  • --access-policy (string, required): The IAM policy document in JSON format allowing PutSubscriptionFilter
  • --force-update (boolean, optional): Force update even if current policy is different
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Policy is immediately effective
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Allow Single Account Access

Grants a specific AWS account permission to send logs to the destination.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow single account
# Cross-account logging

DESTINATION_NAME="DjangoLogsDestination"
SOURCE_ACCOUNT="987654321098"
DEST_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:$DESTINATION_NAME"

echo "Setting destination policy..."

POLICY=$(cat <<EOF
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "AllowDevAccount",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": "$SOURCE_ACCOUNT"
            },
            "Action": "logs:PutSubscriptionFilter",
            "Resource": "$DEST_ARN"
        }
    ]
}
EOF
)

aws logs put-destination-policy \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --access-policy "$POLICY"

echo "✓ Policy set - Account $SOURCE_ACCOUNT can now send logs"
Output: Setting destination policy... ✓ Policy set - Account 987654321098 can now send logs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cross-account access: print(f"Destination: DjangoLogsDestination") print(f"Allowed Account: 987654321098") print(f"") print(f"Dev Django logs → Central account")
Example 2: Allow Multiple Accounts

Grants multiple AWS accounts permission to send logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow multiple accounts
# Multi-environment setup

DESTINATION_NAME="CentralDjangoDestination"
DEST_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:$DESTINATION_NAME"

echo "Setting multi-account policy..."

POLICY=$(cat <<EOF
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "AllowDevelopmentAccounts",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": [
                    "111111111111",
                    "222222222222",
                    "333333333333"
                ]
            },
            "Action": "logs:PutSubscriptionFilter",
            "Resource": "$DEST_ARN"
        }
    ]
}
EOF
)

aws logs put-destination-policy \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --access-policy "$POLICY"

echo "✓ Policy allows 3 accounts"
Output: Setting multi-account policy... ✓ Policy allows 3 accounts
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Multi-account logging: print(f"Central destination configured") print(f"Allowed accounts:") print(f" Dev: 111111111111") print(f" Staging: 222222222222") print(f" QA: 333333333333")
Example 3: Allow Organization Access

Allows all accounts in an AWS Organization to send logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Organization-wide access
# All accounts in org

DESTINATION_NAME="OrgWideLogsDestination"
ORG_ID="o-exampleorgid"
DEST_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:$DESTINATION_NAME"

echo "Setting organization-wide policy..."

POLICY=$(cat <<EOF
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "AllowOrganization",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": "*",
            "Action": "logs:PutSubscriptionFilter",
            "Resource": "$DEST_ARN",
            "Condition": {
                "StringEquals": {
                    "aws:PrincipalOrgID": "$ORG_ID"
                }
            }
        }
    ]
}
EOF
)

aws logs put-destination-policy \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --access-policy "$POLICY"

echo "✓ All org accounts can send logs"
Output: Setting organization-wide policy... ✓ All org accounts can send logs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Organization access: print(f"Org ID: o-exampleorgid") print(f"") print(f"All Django deployments can log:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, User, Author, Location")
Example 4: Conditional Access with Source Log Group

Restricts access to specific source log groups only.

#!/bin/bash
# Conditional access
# Specific log groups only

DESTINATION_NAME="RestrictedDestination"
DEST_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:$DESTINATION_NAME"
SOURCE_ACCOUNT="987654321098"

echo "Setting conditional policy..."

POLICY=$(cat <<EOF
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "AllowSpecificLogGroups",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": "$SOURCE_ACCOUNT"
            },
            "Action": "logs:PutSubscriptionFilter",
            "Resource": "$DEST_ARN",
            "Condition": {
                "StringLike": {
                    "logs:SourceLogGroupArn": "arn:aws:logs:*:$SOURCE_ACCOUNT:log-group:/aws/django/*"
                }
            }
        }
    ]
}
EOF
)

aws logs put-destination-policy \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --access-policy "$POLICY"

echo "✓ Policy allows only /aws/django/* log groups"
Output: Setting conditional policy... ✓ Policy allows only /aws/django/* log groups
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Restricted access: print(f"Only /aws/django/* logs allowed") print(f"") print(f"Accepted log groups:") print(f" /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost") print(f" /aws/django/skills-api")
Example 5: Update Policy with Force

Forces policy update even when there's a potential race condition.

#!/bin/bash
# Force policy update
# Override existing

DESTINATION_NAME="SharedDestination"
DEST_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:destination:$DESTINATION_NAME"

echo "Force updating destination policy..."

POLICY=$(cat <<EOF
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "UpdatedPolicy",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": [
                    "111111111111",
                    "222222222222",
                    "444444444444"
                ]
            },
            "Action": "logs:PutSubscriptionFilter",
            "Resource": "$DEST_ARN"
        }
    ]
}
EOF
)

aws logs put-destination-policy \
    --destination-name "$DESTINATION_NAME" \
    --access-policy "$POLICY" \
    --force-update

echo "✓ Policy force updated"
echo "Allowed accounts: 3"
Output: Force updating destination policy... ✓ Policy force updated Allowed accounts: 3
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy updated: print(f"Force update applied") print(f"New allowed accounts:") print(f" 111111111111, 222222222222, 444444444444") print(f"") print(f"Django logs can flow from all")

get-log-group-fields

Returns a list of fields that are included in log events in the log group. This command analyzes log structure to discover available fields for querying Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs get-log-group-fields \
    --log-group-name <value> \
    [--time <value>] \
    [--log-group-identifier <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-name (string, required): The name of the log group to analyze
  • --time (long, optional): The time to analyze fields for (epoch milliseconds). Default is recent logs
  • --log-group-identifier (string, optional): Log group identifier (name or ARN)
Return Value
  • logGroupFields (list): Array of field objects containing:
    • name: The name of the field (e.g., @timestamp, level, model)
    • percent: Percentage of log events containing this field (0-100)
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Discover Django Log Fields

Retrieves all available fields in Django application logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Discover log fields
# Schema exploration

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Analyzing fields in: $LOG_GROUP"
echo ""

aws logs get-log-group-fields \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --output json | jq -r '.logGroupFields | sort_by(-.percent) | .[] | 
        "\(.name): \(.percent)%"'
Output: Analyzing fields in: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost @timestamp: 100% @message: 100% @logStream: 100% level: 95% model: 85% action: 80% user_id: 75% jobpost_id: 65% skills_count: 45% author_id: 40% location_id: 35%
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Discovered fields match models: print(f"Model fields found:") print(f" user_id: User.objects") print(f" jobpost_id: JobPost.objects") print(f" skills_count: Skills.objects") print(f" author_id: Author.objects") print(f" location_id: Location.objects")
Example 2: Analyze Fields at Specific Time

Discovers fields from logs at a specific point in time.

#!/bin/bash
# Fields at specific time
# Historical analysis

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
# One week ago
TIME_MS=$(($(date +%s) * 1000 - 604800000))

echo "Analyzing fields from one week ago..."
echo ""

aws logs get-log-group-fields \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --time "$TIME_MS" \
    --output table \
    --query 'logGroupFields[*].[name, percent]'
Output: Analyzing fields from one week ago... ----------------------------- | GetLogGroupFields | +-------------+-------------+ | @timestamp | 100 | | @message | 100 | | level | 92 | | model | 78 | | user_id | 70 | | action | 68 | +-------------+-------------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Historical field analysis: print(f"Week-old log structure:") print(f" Core fields: 100% coverage") print(f" Model fields: 78% coverage") print(f" User tracking: 70% coverage")
Example 3: Compare Fields Across Log Groups

Compares field availability across multiple Django log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare fields across log groups
# Schema consistency check

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-skills"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-users"
)

echo "Comparing fields across log groups..."
echo ""

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    echo "=== ${LOG_GROUP##*/} ==="
    aws logs get-log-group-fields \
        --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
        --query 'logGroupFields[?percent > `50`].[name, percent]' \
        --output text
    echo ""
done
Output: Comparing fields across log groups... === anihpj-jobpost === @timestamp 100 @message 100 level 95 jobpost_id 65 user_id 60 === anihpj-skills === @timestamp 100 @message 100 level 90 skill_id 75 skill_name 70 === anihpj-users === @timestamp 100 @message 100 level 92 user_id 85 email 55
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Field comparison: print(f"Common fields: @timestamp, @message, level") print(f"") print(f"Model-specific:") print(f" JobPost: jobpost_id, user_id") print(f" Skills: skill_id, skill_name") print(f" User: user_id, email")
Example 4: Generate Query Template from Fields

Uses discovered fields to generate a CloudWatch Logs Insights query template.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate query from fields
# Dynamic query building

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Generating query template from discovered fields..."
echo ""

FIELDS=$(aws logs get-log-group-fields \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --query 'logGroupFields[?percent > `40`].name' \
    --output text | tr '\t' ', ')

echo "Available fields: $FIELDS"
echo ""
echo "Suggested query:"
echo "fields $FIELDS"
echo "| filter level = 'ERROR'"
echo "| sort @timestamp desc"
echo "| limit 100"
Output: Generating query template from discovered fields... Available fields: @timestamp, @message, level, model, action, user_id, jobpost_id, skills_count Suggested query: fields @timestamp, @message, level, model, action, user_id, jobpost_id, skills_count | filter level = 'ERROR' | sort @timestamp desc | limit 100
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Auto-generated query: print(f"Query uses Django model fields:") print(f" user_id → User FK") print(f" jobpost_id → JobPost PK") print(f" skills_count → Skills M2M count")
Example 5: Field Coverage Analysis Report

Creates a comprehensive report of field coverage for optimization.

#!/bin/bash
# Field coverage analysis
# Logging quality report

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Field Coverage Analysis Report"
echo "=============================="
echo "Log Group: $LOG_GROUP"
echo ""

RESULT=$(aws logs get-log-group-fields \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --output json)

echo "High Coverage (>80%):"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.logGroupFields[] | select(.percent > 80) | "  \(.name): \(.percent)%"'

echo ""
echo "Medium Coverage (40-80%):"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.logGroupFields[] | select(.percent >= 40 and .percent <= 80) | "  \(.name): \(.percent)%"'

echo ""
echo "Low Coverage (<40%):"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.logGroupFields[] | select(.percent < 40) | "  \(.name): \(.percent)%"'

echo ""
TOTAL=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq '.logGroupFields | length')
echo "Total Fields Discovered: $TOTAL"
Output: Field Coverage Analysis Report ============================== Log Group: /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost High Coverage (>80%): @timestamp: 100% @message: 100% @logStream: 100% level: 95% model: 85% Medium Coverage (40-80%): action: 80% user_id: 75% jobpost_id: 65% skills_count: 45% Low Coverage (<40%): author_id: 38% location_id: 32% error_code: 15% Total Fields Discovered: 12
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Coverage insights: print(f"Well-logged: level, model, action") print(f"") print(f"Consider improving:") print(f" Author: {Author.objects.count()} records, 38% coverage") print(f" Location: {Location.objects.count()} records, 32% coverage")

get-log-record

Retrieves all fields and values of a single log event by its pointer. This command fetches complete log record details from CloudWatch Logs Insights query results for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs get-log-record \
    --log-record-pointer <value> \
    [--unmask <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-record-pointer (string, required): The pointer obtained from CloudWatch Logs Insights query results (@ptr field)
  • --unmask (boolean, optional): Whether to display masked sensitive data in clear text
Return Value
  • logRecord (map): Key-value pairs containing all fields in the log event:
    • @timestamp: When the event occurred
    • @message: The log message content
    • @logStream: The log stream name
    • Additional custom fields from the log event
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Get Full Log Record from Query

Retrieves complete details of a log event using its pointer from a Logs Insights query.

#!/bin/bash
# Get full log record
# From query result

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Running query to get log pointer..."

# First, run a query to get the pointer
QUERY_ID=$(aws logs start-query \
    --log-group-name "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --start-time $(($(date +%s) - 3600)) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --query-string 'fields @timestamp, @message, @ptr | filter level = "ERROR" | limit 1' \
    --query 'queryId' --output text)

sleep 5

# Get the pointer from results
POINTER=$(aws logs get-query-results \
    --query-id "$QUERY_ID" \
    --query 'results[0][?field==`@ptr`].value' \
    --output text)

echo "Getting full record..."
aws logs get-log-record \
    --log-record-pointer "$POINTER" \
    --output json | jq '.logRecord'
Output: Running query to get log pointer... Getting full record... { "@timestamp": "2025-01-17T14:30:00.000Z", "@message": "JobPost creation failed: validation error", "@logStream": "django-app/production/abc123", "level": "ERROR", "model": "JobPost", "action": "create", "user_id": "123", "error_type": "ValidationError", "error_message": "title is required", "request_id": "req-456789" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Full error record retrieved: print(f"Error in JobPost creation") print(f" User: User.objects.get(pk=123)") print(f" Error: ValidationError - title required") print(f" Request ID: req-456789")
Example 2: Retrieve Record with All Fields

Gets a complete log record showing all available fields.

#!/bin/bash
# Get record with all fields
# Complete event data

LOG_POINTER="CmQKJgokcHJvZC1sb2dzLWdyb3VwEAAYABC7ATgBQICA..."

echo "Retrieving complete log record..."
echo ""

RECORD=$(aws logs get-log-record \
    --log-record-pointer "$LOG_POINTER" \
    --output json)

echo "Log Record Fields:"
echo "$RECORD" | jq -r '.logRecord | to_entries[] | "\(.key): \(.value)"'
Output: Retrieving complete log record... Log Record Fields: @timestamp: 2025-01-17T10:15:30.456Z @message: JobPost updated successfully @logStream: django-app/production/xyz789 @ingestionTime: 1705489531000 level: INFO model: JobPost action: update jobpost_id: 456 user_id: 123 author_id: 45 skills_count: 5 location_id: 12
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Complete record shows: jobpost = JobPost.objects.get(pk=456) print(f"JobPost: {jobpost.title}") print(f"User: {User.objects.get(pk=123)}") print(f"Author: {Author.objects.get(pk=45)}") print(f"Skills: {jobpost.skills.count()} skills") print(f"Location: {Location.objects.get(pk=12)}")
Example 3: Get Masked Record Unmasked

Retrieves a log record with sensitive data displayed in clear text.

#!/bin/bash
# Get unmasked record
# Show sensitive data

LOG_POINTER="CmQKJgokcHJvZC1sb2dzLWdyb3VwEAAYABD..."

echo "Retrieving unmasked log record..."

aws logs get-log-record \
    --log-record-pointer "$LOG_POINTER" \
    --unmask \
    --output json | jq '.logRecord'
Output: Retrieving unmasked log record... { "@timestamp": "2025-01-17T09:00:00.000Z", "@message": "User login attempt", "@logStream": "django-app/auth/def456", "level": "INFO", "action": "login", "user_id": "123", "email": "user@example.com", "ip_address": "192.168.1.100", "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0..." }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Unmasked sensitive data: user = User.objects.get(pk=123) print(f"User: {user.email}") print(f"IP Address: 192.168.1.100 (unmasked)") print(f"") print(f"Use --unmask only when necessary")
Example 4: Compare Multiple Log Records

Retrieves and compares multiple related log records.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare multiple records
# Related events

POINTERS=(
    "CmQKJgokcHJvZC1sb2dzLWdyb3VwEAAYABDAAA..."
    "CmQKJgokcHJvZC1sb2dzLWdyb3VwEAAYABDBBB..."
    "CmQKJgokcHJvZC1sb2dzLWdyb3VwEAAYABDCCC..."
)

echo "Comparing related log records..."
echo ""

for i in "${!POINTERS[@]}"; do
    echo "=== Record $((i+1)) ==="
    aws logs get-log-record \
        --log-record-pointer "${POINTERS[$i]}" \
        --query 'logRecord.{timestamp: "@timestamp", action: action, model: model, level: level}' \
        --output table
    echo ""
done
Output: Comparing related log records... === Record 1 === --------------------------------------------------- | GetLogRecord | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ | timestamp | action | model | level | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ | ...T09:00 | create | JobPost | INFO | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ === Record 2 === --------------------------------------------------- | GetLogRecord | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ | timestamp | action | model | level | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ | ...T09:01 | add | Skills | INFO | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ === Record 3 === --------------------------------------------------- | GetLogRecord | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ | timestamp | action | model | level | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+ | ...T09:02 | assign | Author | INFO | +------------+--------+----------+----------------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Related operations sequence: print(f"1. JobPost created") print(f"2. Skills added to JobPost") print(f"3. Author assigned to JobPost") print(f"") print(f"Full workflow captured")
Example 5: Detailed Error Investigation

Retrieves full details of an error log for debugging.

#!/bin/bash
# Error investigation
# Full debug details

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
ERROR_POINTER="CmQKJgokcHJvZC1sb2dzLWdyb3VwEAAYABDError..."

echo "Investigating error log..."
echo ""

RECORD=$(aws logs get-log-record \
    --log-record-pointer "$ERROR_POINTER" \
    --output json)

echo "Error Summary:"
echo "$RECORD" | jq -r '.logRecord | "Timestamp: \(.["@timestamp"])\nLevel: \(.level)\nMessage: \(.["@message"])"'

echo ""
echo "Context:"
echo "$RECORD" | jq -r '.logRecord | "Model: \(.model)\nAction: \(.action)\nUser: \(.user_id)"'

echo ""
echo "Error Details:"
echo "$RECORD" | jq -r '.logRecord | "Type: \(.error_type // "N/A")\nMessage: \(.error_message // "N/A")\nStack: \(.stack_trace // "N/A" | split("\n")[0:3] | join("\n"))"'
Output: Investigating error log... Error Summary: Timestamp: 2025-01-17T08:45:22.789Z Level: ERROR Message: Failed to create JobPost with Skills Context: Model: JobPost Action: create_with_skills User: 123 Error Details: Type: IntegrityError Message: duplicate key value violates unique constraint Stack: Traceback (most recent call last): File "/app/jobpost/views.py", line 45, in create_jobpost File "/app/jobpost/models.py", line 123, in save
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Full error context: print(f"Error: IntegrityError") print(f" User: User.objects.get(pk=123)") print(f" Action: create_with_skills") print(f" File: views.py line 45") print(f"") print(f"Duplicate JobPost detected")

create-log-anomaly-detector

Creates an anomaly detector that monitors log groups for unusual patterns using machine learning. This command sets up automatic anomaly detection for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs create-log-anomaly-detector \
    --log-group-arn-list <value> \
    [--detector-name <value>] \
    [--evaluation-frequency <value>] \
    [--filter-pattern <value>] \
    [--kms-key-id <value>] \
    [--anomaly-visibility-time <value>] \
    [--tags <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-arn-list (list, required): List of log group ARNs to monitor for anomalies
  • --detector-name (string, optional): A human-readable name for the detector
  • --evaluation-frequency (string, optional): How often to evaluate. Values: ONE_MIN, FIVE_MIN, TEN_MIN, FIFTEEN_MIN, THIRTY_MIN, ONE_HOUR
  • --filter-pattern (string, optional): Filter pattern to select which log events to analyze
  • --kms-key-id (string, optional): KMS key ARN for encryption
  • --anomaly-visibility-time (long, optional): How long anomalies remain visible (days)
Return Value
  • anomalyDetectorArn (string): The ARN of the created anomaly detector
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Create Basic Anomaly Detector

Creates an anomaly detector for Django application logs with default settings.

#!/bin/bash
# Create basic anomaly detector
# Django log monitoring

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
DETECTOR_NAME="DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Creating anomaly detector..."

RESULT=$(aws logs create-log-anomaly-detector \
    --log-group-arn-list "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --detector-name "$DETECTOR_NAME" \
    --evaluation-frequency FIVE_MIN \
    --output json)

echo "Detector created:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq '{arn: .anomalyDetectorArn}'

echo ""
echo "✓ Monitoring Django JobPost logs for anomalies"
Output: Creating anomaly detector... Detector created: { "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector" } ✓ Monitoring Django JobPost logs for anomalies
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Anomaly detection enabled: print(f"Detector: DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector") print(f"Monitoring: JobPost, Skills, User logs") print(f"Frequency: Every 5 minutes") print(f"") print(f"ML will learn normal patterns")
Example 2: Create Detector with Filter Pattern

Creates a detector that only monitors error-level logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Filtered anomaly detection
# Errors only

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating error-focused anomaly detector..."

aws logs create-log-anomaly-detector \
    --log-group-arn-list "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --detector-name "DjangoErrorAnomalyDetector" \
    --evaluation-frequency ONE_MIN \
    --filter-pattern '{ $.level = "ERROR" }' \
    --output json | jq '.'

echo ""
echo "✓ Monitoring only ERROR level logs"
Output: Creating error-focused anomaly detector... { "anomalyDetectorArn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoErrorAnomalyDetector" } ✓ Monitoring only ERROR level logs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Error monitoring: print(f"Filter: level = ERROR") print(f"Frequency: Every minute") print(f"") print(f"Detects unusual error patterns in:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, Author operations")
Example 3: Create Detector for Multiple Log Groups

Creates a detector monitoring multiple Django-related log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Multi-log-group detector
# Comprehensive monitoring

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating multi-log-group anomaly detector..."

aws logs create-log-anomaly-detector \
    --log-group-arn-list \
        "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost" \
        "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-skills" \
        "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-users" \
    --detector-name "DjangoAppAnomalyDetector" \
    --evaluation-frequency FIVE_MIN \
    --tags Environment=production,Team=backend \
    --output json | jq '{arn: .anomalyDetectorArn}'

echo "✓ Monitoring 3 Django log groups"
Output: Creating multi-log-group anomaly detector... { "arn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoAppAnomalyDetector" } ✓ Monitoring 3 Django log groups
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Multi-app monitoring: print(f"Monitoring log groups:") print(f" /aws/django/anihpj-jobpost") print(f" /aws/django/anihpj-skills") print(f" /aws/django/anihpj-users") print(f"") print(f"Correlates anomalies across apps")
Example 4: Create Encrypted Detector

Creates an anomaly detector with KMS encryption for sensitive logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Encrypted anomaly detector
# Secure configuration

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
KMS_KEY_ARN="arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012"

echo "Creating encrypted anomaly detector..."

aws logs create-log-anomaly-detector \
    --log-group-arn-list "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --detector-name "SecureDjangoAnomalyDetector" \
    --evaluation-frequency TEN_MIN \
    --kms-key-id "$KMS_KEY_ARN" \
    --anomaly-visibility-time 14 \
    --output json

echo "✓ Encrypted detector created"
echo "  Anomalies visible for 14 days"
Output: Creating encrypted anomaly detector... { "anomalyDetectorArn": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:SecureDjangoAnomalyDetector" } ✓ Encrypted detector created Anomalies visible for 14 days
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Secure anomaly detection: print(f"KMS Encryption: Enabled") print(f"Visibility: 14 days") print(f"") print(f"Protects sensitive Django log data") print(f" User, Author PII protected")
Example 5: Create High-Frequency Detector

Creates a detector with 1-minute evaluation for real-time monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
# Real-time anomaly detection
# High frequency

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating high-frequency anomaly detector..."

RESULT=$(aws logs create-log-anomaly-detector \
    --log-group-arn-list "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --detector-name "RealtimeDjangoAnomalyDetector" \
    --evaluation-frequency ONE_MIN \
    --filter-pattern '{ $.level = "ERROR" || $.level = "WARNING" }' \
    --anomaly-visibility-time 7 \
    --tags Environment=production,AlertPriority=high \
    --output json)

DETECTOR_ARN=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.anomalyDetectorArn')

echo "Detector created:"
echo "  ARN: $DETECTOR_ARN"
echo "  Frequency: 1 minute"
echo "  Filter: ERROR and WARNING only"
echo ""
echo "✓ Real-time anomaly monitoring active"
Output: Creating high-frequency anomaly detector... Detector created: ARN: arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:RealtimeDjangoAnomalyDetector Frequency: 1 minute Filter: ERROR and WARNING only ✓ Real-time anomaly monitoring active
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Real-time detection: print(f"Evaluation: Every 1 minute") print(f"Monitoring: ERROR and WARNING logs") print(f"") print(f"Quick detection of:") print(f" JobPost failures") print(f" Skills validation errors") print(f" User authentication anomalies")

get-log-anomaly-detector

Retrieves information about an anomaly detector including its status, configuration, and monitored log groups. This command provides details about anomaly detection setup for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs get-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --anomaly-detector-arn (string, required): The ARN of the anomaly detector to retrieve information about
Return Value
  • detectorName (string): Name of the anomaly detector
  • anomalyDetectorStatus (string): Current status (INITIALIZING, TRAINING, ANALYZING, FAILED, DELETED, PAUSED)
  • evaluationFrequency (string): How often the detector evaluates logs
  • logGroupArnList (list): Log groups being monitored
  • filterPattern (string): Filter pattern applied to logs
  • kmsKeyId (string): KMS key for encryption
  • creationTimeStamp (long): When the detector was created
  • lastModifiedTimeStamp (long): When last modified
  • anomalyVisibilityTime (long): Days anomalies remain visible
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Get Basic Detector Information

Retrieves basic information about an anomaly detector.

#!/bin/bash
# Get detector details
# Status check

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Getting detector information..."
echo ""

aws logs get-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json | jq '{
        name: .detectorName,
        status: .anomalyDetectorStatus,
        frequency: .evaluationFrequency,
        logGroups: (.logGroupArnList | length)
    }'
Output: Getting detector information... { "name": "DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector", "status": "ANALYZING", "frequency": "FIVE_MIN", "logGroups": 1 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Detector status: print(f"Name: DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector") print(f"Status: ANALYZING (active)") print(f"Monitoring: JobPost, Skills, User logs")
Example 2: Check Detector Training Status

Monitors detector training progress for newly created detectors.

#!/bin/bash
# Check training status
# Wait for readiness

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:NewDjangoDetector"

echo "Checking detector training status..."

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws logs get-log-anomaly-detector \
        --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
        --query 'anomalyDetectorStatus' \
        --output text)
    
    echo "Current status: $STATUS"
    
    if [ "$STATUS" = "ANALYZING" ]; then
        echo "✓ Detector is ready and analyzing!"
        break
    elif [ "$STATUS" = "FAILED" ]; then
        echo "✗ Detector failed"
        break
    fi
    
    sleep 60
done
Output: Checking detector training status... Current status: TRAINING Current status: TRAINING Current status: ANALYZING ✓ Detector is ready and analyzing!
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Training progression: print(f"Status flow:") print(f" INITIALIZING → TRAINING → ANALYZING") print(f"") print(f"Detector learning Django log patterns")
Example 3: Get Full Detector Configuration

Retrieves complete configuration details of an anomaly detector.

#!/bin/bash
# Full configuration
# Audit and review

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Full Detector Configuration"
echo "==========================="
echo ""

RESULT=$(aws logs get-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json)

echo "Name: $(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.detectorName')"
echo "ARN: $(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.anomalyDetectorArn')"
echo "Status: $(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.anomalyDetectorStatus')"
echo "Frequency: $(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.evaluationFrequency')"
echo ""
echo "Monitored Log Groups:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.logGroupArnList[] | "  - " + (. | split(":") | .[-1])'
echo ""
echo "Filter: $(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.filterPattern // "None"')"
echo "Visibility: $(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.anomalyVisibilityTime // 7') days"
Output: Full Detector Configuration =========================== Name: DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector ARN: arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector Status: ANALYZING Frequency: FIVE_MIN Monitored Log Groups: - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-skills - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-users Filter: { $.level = "ERROR" } Visibility: 14 days
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Full configuration: print(f"Monitoring Django logs:") print(f" JobPost operations") print(f" Skills management") print(f" User activities") print(f"") print(f"Filtering: ERROR level only")
Example 4: Check Detector Health

Verifies detector is operating correctly and identifies issues.

#!/bin/bash
# Detector health check
# Operational status

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Detector Health Check"
echo "===================="
echo ""

RESULT=$(aws logs get-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json)

STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.anomalyDetectorStatus')

case $STATUS in
    "ANALYZING")
        echo "✓ Status: HEALTHY (Actively analyzing)"
        ;;
    "TRAINING")
        echo "⏳ Status: TRAINING (Learning patterns)"
        ;;
    "INITIALIZING")
        echo "⏳ Status: INITIALIZING (Starting up)"
        ;;
    "PAUSED")
        echo "⚠ Status: PAUSED (Not monitoring)"
        ;;
    "FAILED")
        echo "✗ Status: FAILED (Needs attention)"
        ;;
    *)
        echo "? Status: $STATUS"
        ;;
esac

echo ""
CREATED=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.creationTimeStamp')
echo "Created: $(date -d @$((CREATED/1000)) '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')"
Output: Detector Health Check ==================== ✓ Status: HEALTHY (Actively analyzing) Created: 2025-01-15 10:30
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Health status: print(f"Detector: HEALTHY") print(f"Actively analyzing:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, User, Author logs")
Example 5: Generate Detector Report

Creates a comprehensive report of detector configuration and status.

#!/bin/bash
# Detector report
# Documentation

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"
REPORT_FILE="detector-report-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Generating detector report..."

RESULT=$(aws logs get-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json)

# Save full report
echo "$RESULT" > "$REPORT_FILE"

echo "Report saved to: $REPORT_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Summary:"
echo "--------"
echo "$RESULT" | jq '{
    detector: .detectorName,
    status: .anomalyDetectorStatus,
    frequency: .evaluationFrequency,
    logGroupCount: (.logGroupArnList | length),
    hasFilter: (.filterPattern != null),
    visibilityDays: (.anomalyVisibilityTime // 7),
    encrypted: (.kmsKeyId != null)
}'
Output: Generating detector report... Report saved to: detector-report-20250120.json Summary: -------- { "detector": "DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector", "status": "ANALYZING", "frequency": "FIVE_MIN", "logGroupCount": 3, "hasFilter": true, "visibilityDays": 14, "encrypted": true }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Detector report: print(f"Report generated for audit") print(f"") print(f"Django monitoring:") print(f" 3 log groups monitored") print(f" Encrypted: Yes") print(f" 14-day anomaly visibility")

list-log-anomaly-detectors

Lists all anomaly detectors in the account. This command retrieves information about all configured anomaly detection systems monitoring Django application logs for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    [--filter-log-group-arn <value>] \
    [--max-items <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --filter-log-group-arn (string, optional): Filter detectors by a specific log group ARN
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum number of detectors to return
Return Value
  • anomalyDetectors (list): Array of detector objects containing:
    • anomalyDetectorArn: The ARN of the detector
    • detectorName: Name of the detector
    • anomalyDetectorStatus: Current status
    • logGroupArnList: Monitored log groups
    • evaluationFrequency: How often evaluation occurs
    • creationTimeStamp: When created
  • nextToken (string): Token for pagination
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List All Anomaly Detectors

Retrieves all anomaly detectors in the account.

#!/bin/bash
# List all detectors
# Account overview

echo "All Anomaly Detectors"
echo "===================="
echo ""

aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    --output json | jq -r '.anomalyDetectors[] | 
        "Name: \(.detectorName)\n  Status: \(.anomalyDetectorStatus)\n  Frequency: \(.evaluationFrequency)\n"'
Output: All Anomaly Detectors ==================== Name: DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector Status: ANALYZING Frequency: FIVE_MIN Name: DjangoErrorDetector Status: ANALYZING Frequency: ONE_MIN Name: UserAuthDetector Status: TRAINING Frequency: TEN_MIN
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Active detectors: print(f"3 anomaly detectors configured") print(f" JobPost: 5-min frequency") print(f" Errors: 1-min frequency") print(f" Auth: 10-min frequency (training)")
Example 2: Filter Detectors by Log Group

Lists detectors monitoring a specific log group.

#!/bin/bash
# Filter by log group
# Specific monitoring

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Detectors for JobPost log group:"
echo ""

aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    --filter-log-group-arn "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --output table \
    --query 'anomalyDetectors[*].[detectorName, anomalyDetectorStatus, evaluationFrequency]'
Output: Detectors for JobPost log group: -------------------------------------------------- | ListLogAnomalyDetectors | +---------------------------+----------+----------+ | DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector | ANALYZING | FIVE_MIN | | DjangoErrorDetector | ANALYZING | ONE_MIN | +---------------------------+----------+----------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # JobPost monitoring: print(f"2 detectors monitor JobPost logs:") print(f" General: 5-min evaluation") print(f" Errors: 1-min evaluation")
Example 3: List Active Detectors Only

Filters to show only actively analyzing detectors.

#!/bin/bash
# Active detectors only
# Operational view

echo "Active Anomaly Detectors"
echo "========================"
echo ""

aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    --output json | jq -r '.anomalyDetectors[] | 
        select(.anomalyDetectorStatus == "ANALYZING") | 
        "\(.detectorName): \(.evaluationFrequency)"'

echo ""
ACTIVE_COUNT=$(aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    --query 'anomalyDetectors[?anomalyDetectorStatus==`ANALYZING`] | length(@)' \
    --output text)

echo "Total active: $ACTIVE_COUNT"
Output: Active Anomaly Detectors ======================== DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector: FIVE_MIN DjangoErrorDetector: ONE_MIN DjangoAppDetector: FIFTEEN_MIN Total active: 3
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Active monitoring: print(f"3 detectors actively analyzing") print(f"") print(f"Django logs monitored for:") print(f" JobPost anomalies") print(f" Error patterns") print(f" Application behavior")
Example 4: Detector Inventory Report

Creates a comprehensive inventory of all detectors.

#!/bin/bash
# Detector inventory
# Full report

echo "Anomaly Detector Inventory"
echo "=========================="
echo ""

RESULT=$(aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors --output json)

# Count by status
echo "By Status:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.anomalyDetectors | group_by(.anomalyDetectorStatus) | .[] | "  \(.[0].anomalyDetectorStatus): \(length)"'

echo ""
echo "By Frequency:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.anomalyDetectors | group_by(.evaluationFrequency) | .[] | "  \(.[0].evaluationFrequency): \(length)"'

echo ""
echo "Total Detectors: $(echo $RESULT | jq '.anomalyDetectors | length')"
Output: Anomaly Detector Inventory ========================== By Status: ANALYZING: 3 TRAINING: 1 By Frequency: ONE_MIN: 1 FIVE_MIN: 2 TEN_MIN: 1 Total Detectors: 4
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Inventory summary: print(f"4 detectors total") print(f" 3 active, 1 training") print(f"") print(f"Django monitoring coverage: Complete")
Example 5: List Detectors with Log Group Details

Shows detectors with their monitored log groups.

#!/bin/bash
