# Detectors with log groups
# Mapping view

echo "Detectors and Monitored Log Groups"
echo "==================================="
echo ""

aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    --output json | jq -r '.anomalyDetectors[] | 
        "Detector: \(.detectorName)
  Status: \(.anomalyDetectorStatus)
  Log Groups:
\(.logGroupArnList | map("    - " + (. | split(":") | .[-1])) | join("\n"))
"'
Output: Detectors and Monitored Log Groups =================================== Detector: DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector Status: ANALYZING Log Groups: - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-skills Detector: DjangoErrorDetector Status: ANALYZING Log Groups: - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-users - log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-authors
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Log group mapping: print(f"Django log groups monitored:") print(f" anihpj-jobpost: 2 detectors") print(f" anihpj-skills: 1 detector") print(f" anihpj-users: 1 detector") print(f" anihpj-authors: 1 detector")

list-anomalies

Lists detected anomalies from an anomaly detector. This command retrieves unusual patterns identified in Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs list-anomalies \
    --anomaly-detector-arn <value> \
    [--suppression-state <value>] \
    [--max-items <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --anomaly-detector-arn (string, required): The ARN of the anomaly detector to list anomalies for
  • --suppression-state (string, optional): Filter by suppression state: SUPPRESSED or UNSUPPRESSED
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum number of anomalies to return
Return Value
  • anomalies (list): Array of anomaly objects containing:
    • anomalyId: Unique identifier for the anomaly
    • patternString: The log pattern detected as anomalous
    • firstSeen: When the anomaly was first detected
    • lastSeen: When the anomaly was last detected
    • priority: Severity level (HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW)
    • state: Current state (ACTIVE, SUPPRESSED)
    • description: Description of the anomaly
    • logGroupArnList: Log groups where anomaly was found
  • nextToken (string): Token for pagination
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List All Detected Anomalies

Retrieves all anomalies found by a detector.

#!/bin/bash
# List all anomalies
# Detection review

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Detected Anomalies"
echo "=================="
echo ""

aws logs list-anomalies \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json | jq -r '.anomalies[] | 
        "ID: \(.anomalyId)
  Pattern: \(.patternString | .[0:60])...
  Priority: \(.priority)
  State: \(.state)
  First Seen: \(.firstSeen | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"))
"'
Output: Detected Anomalies ================== ID: anomaly-abc123 Pattern: ERROR: JobPost.DoesNotExist: JobPost matching query... Priority: HIGH State: ACTIVE First Seen: 2025-01-17 10:30 ID: anomaly-def456 Pattern: WARNING: Skills validation failed for user... Priority: MEDIUM State: ACTIVE First Seen: 2025-01-17 12:15
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Detected anomalies: print(f"HIGH: JobPost.DoesNotExist errors") print(f" Check: JobPost.objects.filter()") print(f"") print(f"MEDIUM: Skills validation failures") print(f" Check: Skills validation logic")
Example 2: List High Priority Anomalies

Filters to show only high priority anomalies requiring immediate attention.

#!/bin/bash
# High priority only
# Urgent issues

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "HIGH Priority Anomalies"
echo "======================="
echo ""

RESULT=$(aws logs list-anomalies \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json)

echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.anomalies[] | select(.priority == "HIGH") | 
    "⚠ \(.anomalyId)
  Pattern: \(.patternString)
  Last Seen: \(.lastSeen | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"))
  Action Required: Investigate immediately
"'

HIGH_COUNT=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq '[.anomalies[] | select(.priority == "HIGH")] | length')
echo "Total HIGH priority: $HIGH_COUNT"
Output: HIGH Priority Anomalies ======================= ⚠ anomaly-abc123 Pattern: ERROR: JobPost.DoesNotExist: JobPost matching query does not exist Last Seen: 2025-01-17 14:22 Action Required: Investigate immediately ⚠ anomaly-ghi789 Pattern: CRITICAL: Database connection failed for User query Last Seen: 2025-01-17 14:18 Action Required: Investigate immediately Total HIGH priority: 2
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # High priority alerts: print(f"2 critical anomalies detected:") print(f"") print(f"1. JobPost.DoesNotExist spike") print(f" JobPost.objects.get() failing") print(f"") print(f"2. Database connection issues") print(f" User.objects queries affected")
Example 3: List Active Unsuppressed Anomalies

Shows anomalies that haven't been suppressed and need attention.

#!/bin/bash
# Unsuppressed anomalies
# Requiring action

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Active Unsuppressed Anomalies"
echo "============================="
echo ""

aws logs list-anomalies \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --suppression-state UNSUPPRESSED \
    --output table \
    --query 'anomalies[*].[anomalyId, priority, state, patternString]'
Output: Active Unsuppressed Anomalies ============================= -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ListAnomalies | +---------------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+ | anomaly-abc123| HIGH | ACTIVE | ERROR: JobPost.DoesNotExist... | | anomaly-def456| MEDIUM | ACTIVE | WARNING: Skills validation... | | anomaly-jkl012| LOW | ACTIVE | INFO: Unusual login pattern... | +---------------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Active anomalies: print(f"3 anomalies need review:") print(f" HIGH: JobPost errors") print(f" MEDIUM: Skills validation") print(f" LOW: Login patterns")
Example 4: Anomaly Timeline Analysis

Analyzes when anomalies were detected over time.

#!/bin/bash
# Anomaly timeline
# Trend analysis

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Anomaly Timeline Analysis"
echo "========================="
echo ""

RESULT=$(aws logs list-anomalies \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json)

echo "By Day First Seen:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.anomalies | group_by(.firstSeen | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d")) | .[] | 
    "  \(.[0].firstSeen | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d")): \(length) anomalies"'

echo ""
echo "Duration (First to Last Seen):"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.anomalies[] | 
    "\(.anomalyId): \(((.lastSeen - .firstSeen) / 3600000 | floor)) hours"'
Output: Anomaly Timeline Analysis ========================= By Day First Seen: 2025-01-15: 1 anomalies 2025-01-16: 2 anomalies 2025-01-17: 3 anomalies Duration (First to Last Seen): anomaly-abc123: 48 hours anomaly-def456: 24 hours anomaly-ghi789: 6 hours
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Timeline insights: print(f"Anomaly trend: Increasing") print(f" Jan 15: 1 anomaly") print(f" Jan 16: 2 anomalies") print(f" Jan 17: 3 anomalies") print(f"") print(f"Investigate Django code changes")
Example 5: Export Anomalies for Investigation

Exports anomaly details for offline analysis and incident tracking.

#!/bin/bash
# Export anomalies
# Incident documentation

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"
EXPORT_FILE="anomalies-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Exporting anomalies for investigation..."

RESULT=$(aws logs list-anomalies \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json)

# Save full export
echo "$RESULT" > "$EXPORT_FILE"

echo "✓ Exported to: $EXPORT_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Summary:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq '{
    totalAnomalies: (.anomalies | length),
    byPriority: (.anomalies | group_by(.priority) | map({(.[0].priority): length}) | add),
    byState: (.anomalies | group_by(.state) | map({(.[0].state): length}) | add),
    oldestAnomaly: (.anomalies | min_by(.firstSeen) | .firstSeen | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d")),
    newestAnomaly: (.anomalies | max_by(.lastSeen) | .lastSeen | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"))
}'
Output: Exporting anomalies for investigation... ✓ Exported to: anomalies-20250117.json Summary: { "totalAnomalies": 6, "byPriority": { "HIGH": 2, "MEDIUM": 3, "LOW": 1 }, "byState": { "ACTIVE": 5, "SUPPRESSED": 1 }, "oldestAnomaly": "2025-01-15", "newestAnomaly": "2025-01-17" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Export summary: print(f"6 anomalies exported") print(f" HIGH: 2 (investigate first)") print(f" MEDIUM: 3") print(f" LOW: 1") print(f"") print(f"Affects Django models:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, User operations")

update-anomaly

Updates the status of an anomaly by suppressing or acknowledging it. This command manages detected anomalies in Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs update-anomaly \
    --anomaly-detector-arn <value> \
    [--anomaly-id <value>] \
    [--pattern-id <value>] \
    [--suppression-type <value>] \
    [--suppression-period <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --anomaly-detector-arn (string, required): The ARN of the anomaly detector containing the anomaly
  • --anomaly-id (string, optional): The unique ID of the anomaly to update
  • --pattern-id (string, optional): The pattern ID to update (alternative to anomaly-id)
  • --suppression-type (string, optional): INFINITE (suppress forever) or LIMITED (suppress for period)
  • --suppression-period (structure, optional): Duration for LIMITED suppression: {value: number, unit: SECONDS|MINUTES|HOURS}
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Anomaly status is immediately updated
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Suppress Anomaly for 24 Hours

Temporarily suppresses a known anomaly during planned maintenance.

#!/bin/bash
# Suppress for maintenance window
# Limited time

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"
ANOMALY_ID="anomaly-abc123"

echo "Suppressing anomaly for 24 hours..."

aws logs update-anomaly \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --anomaly-id "$ANOMALY_ID" \
    --suppression-type LIMITED \
    --suppression-period value=24,unit=HOURS

echo "✓ Anomaly suppressed until maintenance completes"
Output: Suppressing anomaly for 24 hours... ✓ Anomaly suppressed until maintenance completes
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Planned maintenance: print(f"Anomaly: anomaly-abc123 suppressed") print(f"Duration: 24 hours") print(f"Reason: JobPost database migration") print(f"") print(f"Expected errors during migration")
Example 2: Permanently Suppress Known Pattern

Indefinitely suppresses an anomaly that represents expected behavior.

#!/bin/bash
# Permanent suppression
# Expected pattern

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"
ANOMALY_ID="anomaly-def456"

echo "Permanently suppressing known pattern..."

aws logs update-anomaly \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --anomaly-id "$ANOMALY_ID" \
    --suppression-type INFINITE

echo "✓ Anomaly permanently suppressed"
echo "  Will not trigger future alerts"
Output: Permanently suppressing known pattern... ✓ Anomaly permanently suppressed Will not trigger future alerts
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Expected behavior: print(f"Pattern: Skills.DoesNotExist on empty filters") print(f"This is expected for new Users") print(f"") print(f"Skills.objects.filter(user=new_user)") print(f"# Returns empty, logged as warning")
Example 3: Suppress During Deployment

Suppresses anomalies during a deployment window.

#!/bin/bash
# Deployment suppression
# Short window

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"
ANOMALY_ID="anomaly-ghi789"

echo "Starting deployment..."
echo "Suppressing anomaly for 2 hours"

aws logs update-anomaly \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --anomaly-id "$ANOMALY_ID" \
    --suppression-type LIMITED \
    --suppression-period value=2,unit=HOURS

echo "✓ Anomaly suppressed"
echo ""
echo "Deploy Django app now..."
echo "Alerts resume after 2 hours"
Output: Starting deployment... Suppressing anomaly for 2 hours ✓ Anomaly suppressed Deploy Django app now... Alerts resume after 2 hours
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Deployment window: print(f"Deploying Django app") print(f" JobPost model changes") print(f" Skills relationship update") print(f"") print(f"Alerts paused: 2 hours")
Example 4: Suppress Multiple Anomalies

Batch suppresses multiple related anomalies during maintenance.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch suppression
# Multiple anomalies

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

ANOMALIES=(
    "anomaly-abc123"
    "anomaly-def456"
    "anomaly-ghi789"
)

echo "Suppressing multiple anomalies for maintenance..."
echo ""

for ANOMALY_ID in "${ANOMALIES[@]}"; do
    echo "Suppressing: $ANOMALY_ID"
    aws logs update-anomaly \
        --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
        --anomaly-id "$ANOMALY_ID" \
        --suppression-type LIMITED \
        --suppression-period value=4,unit=HOURS
    echo "  ✓ Suppressed for 4 hours"
done

echo ""
echo "All ${#ANOMALIES[@]} anomalies suppressed"
Output: Suppressing multiple anomalies for maintenance... Suppressing: anomaly-abc123 ✓ Suppressed for 4 hours Suppressing: anomaly-def456 ✓ Suppressed for 4 hours Suppressing: anomaly-ghi789 ✓ Suppressed for 4 hours All 3 anomalies suppressed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch maintenance: print(f"3 anomalies suppressed") print(f"") print(f"Affected: JobPost, Skills, User") print(f"Duration: 4 hours") print(f"Reason: Database maintenance")
Example 5: Suppress with Verification

Suppresses an anomaly and verifies the update was applied.

#!/bin/bash
# Suppress with verification
# Confirm status

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"
ANOMALY_ID="anomaly-jkl012"

echo "Suppressing anomaly..."

aws logs update-anomaly \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --anomaly-id "$ANOMALY_ID" \
    --suppression-type LIMITED \
    --suppression-period value=6,unit=HOURS

echo ""
echo "Verifying suppression..."

# Check anomaly status
STATUS=$(aws logs list-anomalies \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --query "anomalies[?anomalyId=='$ANOMALY_ID'].state" \
    --output text)

if [ "$STATUS" = "SUPPRESSED" ]; then
    echo "✓ Verified: Anomaly is SUPPRESSED"
else
    echo "Current status: $STATUS"
fi
Output: Suppressing anomaly... Verifying suppression... ✓ Verified: Anomaly is SUPPRESSED
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified suppression: print(f"Anomaly: anomaly-jkl012") print(f"Status: SUPPRESSED") print(f"Duration: 6 hours") print(f"") print(f"Django log alerts paused")

update-log-anomaly-detector

Updates an anomaly detector's configuration including evaluation frequency and enabled state. This command modifies anomaly detection settings for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn <value> \
    [--evaluation-frequency <value>] \
    [--filter-pattern <value>] \
    [--anomaly-visibility-time <value>] \
    [--enabled | --no-enabled] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --anomaly-detector-arn (string, required): The ARN of the anomaly detector to update
  • --evaluation-frequency (string, optional): New frequency: ONE_MIN, FIVE_MIN, TEN_MIN, FIFTEEN_MIN, THIRTY_MIN, ONE_HOUR
  • --filter-pattern (string, optional): New filter pattern for log event selection
  • --anomaly-visibility-time (long, optional): Days anomalies remain visible
  • --enabled | --no-enabled (boolean, optional): Enable or disable the detector
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Configuration is immediately updated
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Change Evaluation Frequency

Updates the detector to evaluate logs more or less frequently.

#!/bin/bash
# Change frequency
# Performance tuning

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Updating evaluation frequency..."

aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --evaluation-frequency TEN_MIN \
    --enabled

echo "✓ Frequency changed to 10 minutes"
echo "  Reduces evaluation overhead"
Output: Updating evaluation frequency... ✓ Frequency changed to 10 minutes Reduces evaluation overhead
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Frequency update: print(f"Old: 5-minute evaluation") print(f"New: 10-minute evaluation") print(f"") print(f"Less frequent checks, lower cost")
Example 2: Pause Detector During Maintenance

Temporarily disables the detector during system maintenance.

#!/bin/bash
# Pause detector
# Maintenance window

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Pausing detector for maintenance..."

aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --no-enabled

echo "✓ Detector paused"
echo ""
echo "Remember to re-enable after maintenance:"
echo "aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \\"
echo "    --anomaly-detector-arn $DETECTOR_ARN --enabled"
Output: Pausing detector for maintenance... ✓ Detector paused Remember to re-enable after maintenance: aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \ --anomaly-detector-arn arn:aws:logs:... --enabled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Maintenance mode: print(f"Detector: PAUSED") print(f"Reason: Database migration") print(f"") print(f"No JobPost anomaly alerts")
Example 3: Update Filter Pattern

Changes the filter to focus on specific log types.

#!/bin/bash
# Update filter
# Focus detection

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Updating filter pattern..."

aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --filter-pattern '{ $.level = "ERROR" || $.level = "CRITICAL" }' \
    --enabled

echo "✓ Filter updated to ERROR and CRITICAL only"
Output: Updating filter pattern... ✓ Filter updated to ERROR and CRITICAL only
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Focused detection: print(f"Now monitoring only:") print(f" ERROR level logs") print(f" CRITICAL level logs") print(f"") print(f"Ignores INFO and WARNING")
Example 4: Extend Anomaly Visibility

Updates how long detected anomalies remain visible for review.

#!/bin/bash
# Extend visibility
# Longer review period

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Extending anomaly visibility..."

aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --anomaly-visibility-time 30 \
    --enabled

echo "✓ Anomalies visible for 30 days"
echo "  Allows longer investigation window"
Output: Extending anomaly visibility... ✓ Anomalies visible for 30 days Allows longer investigation window
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Extended visibility: print(f"Old: 7 days") print(f"New: 30 days") print(f"") print(f"More time to investigate:") print(f" JobPost anomalies") print(f" Skills pattern changes")
Example 5: Re-enable with Updated Settings

Re-enables a paused detector with new configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Re-enable with updates
# Post-maintenance

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"

echo "Re-enabling detector with updated settings..."

aws logs update-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --evaluation-frequency FIVE_MIN \
    --filter-pattern '{ $.level = "ERROR" }' \
    --anomaly-visibility-time 14 \
    --enabled

echo "✓ Detector re-enabled"
echo ""
echo "Configuration:"
echo "  Frequency: 5 minutes"
echo "  Filter: ERROR only"
echo "  Visibility: 14 days"
Output: Re-enabling detector with updated settings... ✓ Detector re-enabled Configuration: Frequency: 5 minutes Filter: ERROR only Visibility: 14 days
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Detector resumed: print(f"Status: ACTIVE") print(f"Monitoring: ERROR logs") print(f"") print(f"Django anomaly detection active:") print(f" JobPost, Skills, User, Author")

delete-log-anomaly-detector

Deletes an anomaly detector and stops anomaly detection. This command removes monitoring configuration for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs delete-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --anomaly-detector-arn (string, required): The ARN of the anomaly detector to delete
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Detector is immediately removed
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete Unused Detector

Removes an obsolete anomaly detector that is no longer needed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete unused detector
# Cleanup

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:OldDjangoDetector"

echo "Deleting anomaly detector..."

aws logs delete-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN"

echo "✓ Detector deleted successfully"
echo "  Anomaly detection stopped"
Output: Deleting anomaly detector... ✓ Detector deleted successfully Anomaly detection stopped
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Detector removed: print(f"Deleted: OldDjangoDetector") print(f"Was monitoring: Legacy log group") print(f"") print(f"Other detectors still active")
Example 2: Delete with Verification

Deletes a detector and verifies it was successfully removed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with verification
# Confirm removal

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:TestDetector"
DETECTOR_NAME="TestDetector"

echo "Deleting detector: $DETECTOR_NAME"

aws logs delete-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN"

# Verify deletion
EXISTS=$(aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    --query "anomalyDetectors[?contains(anomalyDetectorArn, '$DETECTOR_NAME')] | length(@)" \
    --output text)

if [ "$EXISTS" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ Verified: Detector deleted"
else
    echo "⚠ Detector may still exist"
fi
Output: Deleting detector: TestDetector ✓ Verified: Detector deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified deletion: print(f"TestDetector: Deleted") print(f"Verification: Passed") print(f"") print(f"Django logging unaffected")
Example 3: Delete with Backup

Backs up detector configuration before deletion for potential recreation.

#!/bin/bash
# Backup before delete
# Recoverable

DETECTOR_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:anomaly-detector:DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector"
BACKUP_FILE="detector-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Backing up detector configuration..."

aws logs get-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" \
    --output json > "$BACKUP_FILE"

echo "✓ Backup saved: $BACKUP_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Deleting detector..."

aws logs delete-log-anomaly-detector \
    --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN"

echo "✓ Detector deleted"
echo "  Configuration backed up for recreation"
Output: Backing up detector configuration... ✓ Backup saved: detector-backup-20250120.json Deleting detector... ✓ Detector deleted Configuration backed up for recreation
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safe deletion: print(f"Backup: detector-backup-20250120.json") print(f"Contains: frequency, filter, log groups") print(f"") print(f"Can recreate Django detector if needed")
Example 4: Cleanup Multiple Detectors

Deletes multiple obsolete detectors in a cleanup operation.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch cleanup
# Multiple detectors

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

DETECTORS=(
    "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:anomaly-detector:OldDetector1"
    "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:anomaly-detector:TestDetector2"
    "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:anomaly-detector:DeprecatedDetector"
)

echo "Cleaning up obsolete detectors..."
echo ""

DELETED=0
for DETECTOR_ARN in "${DETECTORS[@]}"; do
    NAME=$(echo "$DETECTOR_ARN" | awk -F: '{print $NF}')
    echo "Deleting: $NAME"
    
    aws logs delete-log-anomaly-detector \
        --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN" 2>/dev/null
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        ((DELETED++))
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    else
        echo "  ✗ Failed or not found"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Summary: $DELETED detectors deleted"
Output: Cleaning up obsolete detectors... Deleting: OldDetector1 ✓ Deleted Deleting: TestDetector2 ✓ Deleted Deleting: DeprecatedDetector ✓ Deleted Summary: 3 detectors deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch cleanup: print(f"3 detectors removed") print(f"") print(f"Active detectors remain:") print(f" DjangoJobPostAnomalyDetector") print(f" DjangoErrorDetector")
Example 5: Delete Failed Detector

Removes a detector that failed to initialize properly.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete failed detector
# Cleanup errors

echo "Finding failed detectors..."

FAILED_DETECTORS=$(aws logs list-log-anomaly-detectors \
    --query 'anomalyDetectors[?anomalyDetectorStatus==`FAILED`].anomalyDetectorArn' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$FAILED_DETECTORS" ]; then
    echo "No failed detectors found"
else
    echo "Found failed detectors. Deleting..."
    
    for DETECTOR_ARN in $FAILED_DETECTORS; do
        echo "Deleting: ${DETECTOR_ARN##*:}"
        aws logs delete-log-anomaly-detector \
            --anomaly-detector-arn "$DETECTOR_ARN"
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    done
    
    echo ""
    echo "✓ All failed detectors cleaned up"
fi
Output: Finding failed detectors... Found failed detectors. Deleting... Deleting: FailedDjangoDetector ✓ Deleted ✓ All failed detectors cleaned up
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Failed detector cleanup: print(f"Removed: FailedDjangoDetector") print(f"Reason: Initialization failure") print(f"") print(f"Can recreate with correct config")

start-live-tail

Starts a live tail session to stream log events in real-time. This command provides real-time visibility into Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations as they happen.

Syntax
aws logs start-live-tail \
    --log-group-identifiers <value> \
    [--log-stream-names <value>] \
    [--log-stream-name-prefixes <value>] \
    [--log-event-filter-pattern <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-identifiers (list, required): List of log group ARNs or names to tail (max 10)
  • --log-stream-names (list, optional): Specific log stream names to include
  • --log-stream-name-prefixes (list, optional): Log stream name prefixes to include
  • --log-event-filter-pattern (string, optional): Filter pattern to match log events
Return Value
  • sessionMetadata: Information about the live tail session including sampled property
  • sessionResults: Stream of log events as they arrive in real-time
  • sessionStart/sessionUpdate: Session lifecycle events
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Live Tail All Django Logs

Streams all log events from Django application logs in real-time.

#!/bin/bash
# Live tail all logs
# Real-time monitoring

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Starting live tail session..."
echo "Press Ctrl+C to stop"
echo ""

aws logs start-live-tail \
    --log-group-identifiers "$LOG_GROUP_ARN"
Output: Starting live tail session... Press Ctrl+C to stop 2025-01-17T14:30:01.123Z INFO: JobPost.objects.create(title='Senior Developer') 2025-01-17T14:30:01.456Z INFO: Skills added: Python, Django, AWS 2025-01-17T14:30:02.789Z INFO: Author.objects.get(user_id=123) assigned 2025-01-17T14:30:03.012Z INFO: Location.objects.filter(city='NYC') matched 2025-01-17T14:30:04.345Z INFO: User.objects.get(pk=45) retrieved ...
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Real-time log streaming: print(f"Watching Django operations live:") print(f" JobPost.objects.create()") print(f" Skills.objects.filter()") print(f" User authentication events")
Example 2: Live Tail Errors Only

Streams only ERROR level log events for debugging.

#!/bin/bash
# Stream errors only
# Debug mode

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Live tailing ERROR logs..."
echo ""

aws logs start-live-tail \
    --log-group-identifiers "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --log-event-filter-pattern '{ $.level = "ERROR" }'
Output: Live tailing ERROR logs... 2025-01-17T14:35:10.123Z ERROR: JobPost.DoesNotExist: JobPost matching query does not exist (pk=999) 2025-01-17T14:35:45.456Z ERROR: Skills validation failed: skill_name cannot be empty 2025-01-17T14:36:20.789Z ERROR: User authentication failed for user_id=456 ...
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Error debugging: print(f"Streaming errors:") print(f" JobPost.DoesNotExist exceptions") print(f" Skills validation failures") print(f" User authentication issues")
Example 3: Tail Multiple Log Groups

Streams logs from multiple Django application log groups simultaneously.

#!/bin/bash
# Multiple log groups
# Unified view

ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Live tailing multiple Django log groups..."
echo ""

aws logs start-live-tail \
    --log-group-identifiers \
        "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost" \
        "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-skills" \
        "arn:aws:logs:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-users"
Output: Live tailing multiple Django log groups... [jobpost] 2025-01-17T14:40:01Z INFO: JobPost created id=789 [skills] 2025-01-17T14:40:01Z INFO: Skill 'Python' added [users] 2025-01-17T14:40:02Z INFO: User login user_id=123 [jobpost] 2025-01-17T14:40:03Z INFO: Author assigned to JobPost ...
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Multi-app view: print(f"Unified streaming from:") print(f" anihpj-jobpost: JobPost, Author") print(f" anihpj-skills: Skills operations") print(f" anihpj-users: User, auth events")
Example 4: Tail Specific Log Streams

Streams logs from specific production log streams only.

#!/bin/bash
# Specific streams
# Production only

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Live tailing production streams..."
echo ""

aws logs start-live-tail \
    --log-group-identifiers "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --log-stream-name-prefixes "production/" "prod-"
Output: Live tailing production streams... [production/web-1] 2025-01-17T14:45:01Z INFO: JobPost view accessed [prod-api] 2025-01-17T14:45:02Z INFO: Skills API called [production/web-2] 2025-01-17T14:45:03Z INFO: User authenticated ...
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production only: print(f"Streaming production streams:") print(f" production/* prefixed") print(f" prod-* prefixed") print(f"") print(f"Excludes: staging, dev, test")
Example 5: Tail with Complex Filter

Streams logs matching specific model operations.

#!/bin/bash
# Complex filter
# Model-specific

LOG_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Live tailing JobPost and Skills operations..."
echo ""

aws logs start-live-tail \
    --log-group-identifiers "$LOG_GROUP_ARN" \
    --log-event-filter-pattern '{ ($.model = "JobPost") || ($.model = "Skills") }'
Output: Live tailing JobPost and Skills operations... 2025-01-17T14:50:01Z {"model": "JobPost", "action": "create", "id": 123} 2025-01-17T14:50:02Z {"model": "Skills", "action": "add", "jobpost_id": 123} 2025-01-17T14:50:03Z {"model": "JobPost", "action": "update", "id": 123} 2025-01-17T14:50:05Z {"model": "Skills", "action": "filter", "count": 5} ...
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Model-specific streaming: print(f"Watching only:") print(f" JobPost.objects operations") print(f" Skills.objects operations") print(f"") print(f"Filtering: User, Author, Location")

test-metric-filter

Tests a filter pattern against sample log event messages before deploying. This command validates that metric filters will correctly match Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs test-metric-filter \
    --filter-pattern <value> \
    --log-event-messages <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --filter-pattern (string, required): The filter pattern to test
  • --log-event-messages (list, required): Array of sample log messages to test against (max 50)
Return Value
  • matches (list): Array of matched log events containing:
    • eventNumber: 1-based index of matched message
    • eventMessage: The matched log message
    • extractedValues: Map of extracted values from pattern
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Test Simple Text Filter

Tests a basic text filter against sample log messages.

#!/bin/bash
# Test simple filter
# Basic pattern matching

echo "Testing ERROR filter pattern..."

aws logs test-metric-filter \
    --filter-pattern "ERROR" \
    --log-event-messages \
        "INFO: JobPost created successfully" \
        "ERROR: Skills validation failed - name required" \
        "DEBUG: Query executed in 50ms" \
        "ERROR: User.DoesNotExist: pk=999" \
        "WARNING: Slow query detected" \
    --output json | jq '{
        totalMessages: 5,
        matchedCount: (.matches | length),
        matches: [.matches[].eventMessage]
    }'
Output: Testing ERROR filter pattern... { "totalMessages": 5, "matchedCount": 2, "matches": [ "ERROR: Skills validation failed - name required", "ERROR: User.DoesNotExist: pk=999" ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Filter test results: print(f"Pattern: ERROR") print(f"Matched: 2 of 5 messages") print(f"") print(f"Would capture:") print(f" Skills validation errors") print(f" User.DoesNotExist exceptions")
Example 2: Test JSON Pattern Filter

Tests a JSON filter pattern for structured log matching.

#!/bin/bash
# Test JSON filter
# Structured logs

echo "Testing JSON filter pattern..."

aws logs test-metric-filter \
    --filter-pattern '{ $.level = "ERROR" }' \
    --log-event-messages \
        '{"level": "INFO", "model": "JobPost", "action": "create"}' \
        '{"level": "ERROR", "model": "Skills", "error": "validation failed"}' \
        '{"level": "DEBUG", "model": "User", "action": "query"}' \
        '{"level": "ERROR", "model": "Author", "error": "not found"}' \
    --output json | jq '.matches[] | {eventNumber, message: .eventMessage | fromjson}'
Output: Testing JSON filter pattern... { "eventNumber": 2, "message": { "level": "ERROR", "model": "Skills", "error": "validation failed" } } { "eventNumber": 4, "message": { "level": "ERROR", "model": "Author", "error": "not found" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # JSON filter results: print(f"Pattern matches JSON level=ERROR") print(f"") print(f"Matched Django errors:") print(f" Skills: validation failed") print(f" Author: not found")
Example 3: Test Pattern Value Extraction

Tests a filter pattern that extracts values from log messages.

#!/bin/bash
# Test value extraction
# Metric values

echo "Testing extraction pattern..."

aws logs test-metric-filter \
    --filter-pattern '[timestamp, level, model, action, duration_ms]' \
    --log-event-messages \
        "2025-01-17T10:00:00Z INFO JobPost create 150" \
        "2025-01-17T10:00:01Z ERROR Skills validate 50" \
        "2025-01-17T10:00:02Z INFO User query 30" \
    --output json | jq '.matches[] | {
        eventNumber,
        extracted: .extractedValues
    }'
Output: Testing extraction pattern... { "eventNumber": 1, "extracted": { "$timestamp": "2025-01-17T10:00:00Z", "$level": "INFO", "$model": "JobPost", "$action": "create", "$duration_ms": "150" } } { "eventNumber": 2, "extracted": { "$timestamp": "2025-01-17T10:00:01Z", "$level": "ERROR", "$model": "Skills", "$action": "validate", "$duration_ms": "50" } } { "eventNumber": 3, "extracted": { "$timestamp": "2025-01-17T10:00:02Z", "$level": "INFO", "$model": "User", "$action": "query", "$duration_ms": "30" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Extracted values: print(f"Pattern extracts 5 fields") print(f"") print(f"duration_ms can be metric value:") print(f" JobPost create: 150ms") print(f" Skills validate: 50ms") print(f" User query: 30ms")
Example 4: Test Complex JSON Filter

Tests a complex JSON filter with multiple conditions.

#!/bin/bash
# Complex JSON filter
# Multi-condition

echo "Testing complex JSON filter..."

aws logs test-metric-filter \
    --filter-pattern '{ ($.level = "ERROR") && ($.model = "JobPost" || $.model = "Skills") }' \
    --log-event-messages \
        '{"level": "ERROR", "model": "JobPost", "error": "validation"}' \
        '{"level": "ERROR", "model": "User", "error": "auth failed"}' \
        '{"level": "INFO", "model": "Skills", "action": "created"}' \
        '{"level": "ERROR", "model": "Skills", "error": "not found"}' \
    --output json | jq '{
        pattern: "ERROR AND (JobPost OR Skills)",
        matchCount: (.matches | length),
        matchedModels: [.matches[].eventMessage | fromjson | .model]
    }'
Output: Testing complex JSON filter... { "pattern": "ERROR AND (JobPost OR Skills)", "matchCount": 2, "matchedModels": [ "JobPost", "Skills" ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Complex filter results: print(f"Filter: ERROR AND (JobPost OR Skills)") print(f"") print(f"Matched: 2 messages") print(f" JobPost ERROR") print(f" Skills ERROR") print(f"") print(f"Excluded: User ERROR (wrong model)")
Example 5: Test Filter Before Deployment

Validates a filter pattern with real-world log samples before creating the metric filter.

#!/bin/bash
# Pre-deployment test
# Validate filter

FILTER_PATTERN='{ $.response_time > 1000 }'

echo "Testing slow response filter before deployment..."
echo "Filter: $FILTER_PATTERN"
echo ""

# Sample real-world logs
RESULT=$(aws logs test-metric-filter \
    --filter-pattern "$FILTER_PATTERN" \
    --log-event-messages \
        '{"endpoint": "/api/jobpost/", "response_time": 150}' \
        '{"endpoint": "/api/skills/", "response_time": 2500}' \
        '{"endpoint": "/api/users/", "response_time": 800}' \
        '{"endpoint": "/api/author/", "response_time": 1500}' \
        '{"endpoint": "/api/location/", "response_time": 450}' \
    --output json)

MATCH_COUNT=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq '.matches | length')
echo "Test Results:"
echo "  Total samples: 5"
echo "  Matches: $MATCH_COUNT"
echo ""

if [ "$MATCH_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "Slow endpoints detected:"
    echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.matches[].eventMessage | fromjson | "  \(.endpoint): \(.response_time)ms"'
    echo ""
    echo "✓ Filter ready for deployment"
else
    echo "⚠ No matches - review filter pattern"
fi
Output: Testing slow response filter before deployment... Filter: { $.response_time > 1000 } Test Results: Total samples: 5 Matches: 2 Slow endpoints detected: /api/skills/: 2500ms /api/author/: 1500ms ✓ Filter ready for deployment
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Filter validation: print(f"Slow response filter tested") print(f"") print(f"Would alert on:") print(f" Skills API: 2500ms") print(f" Author API: 1500ms") print(f"") print(f"Threshold: 1000ms")

put-data-protection-policy

Creates a data protection policy to automatically mask sensitive data in logs. This command protects PII in Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs put-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier <value> \
    --policy-document <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-identifier (string, required): The log group name or ARN to apply the policy to
  • --policy-document (string, required): JSON policy document specifying data identifiers and operations
Return Value
  • logGroupIdentifier (string): The log group the policy was applied to
  • policyDocument (string): The policy document
  • lastUpdatedTime (long): When the policy was last updated
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Mask Email Addresses

Creates a policy to mask email addresses in User-related logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Mask email addresses
# User privacy

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating data protection policy for emails..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "EmailProtectionPolicy",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "Sid": "MaskEmails",
        "DataIdentifier": ["arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress"],
        "Operation": {
            "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
            "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
        }
    }]
}'

aws logs put-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --policy-document "$POLICY"

echo "✓ Email addresses will be masked in logs"
Output: Creating data protection policy for emails... ✓ Email addresses will be masked in logs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Email masking: print(f"Before: user@example.com") print(f"After: ***@***.***") print(f"") print(f"Protects User.email, Author.email")
Example 2: Mask Multiple PII Types

Creates a comprehensive policy masking multiple types of sensitive data.

#!/bin/bash
# Mask multiple PII types
# GDPR compliance

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating comprehensive data protection policy..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "ComprehensivePIIPolicy",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "Sid": "MaskPII",
        "DataIdentifier": [
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/Name",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/PhoneNumber",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/Address"
        ],
        "Operation": {
            "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
            "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
        }
    }]
}'

aws logs put-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --policy-document "$POLICY"

echo "✓ Multiple PII types will be masked"
Output: Creating comprehensive data protection policy... ✓ Multiple PII types will be masked
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Comprehensive masking: print(f"Protected data types:") print(f" User.email → masked") print(f" Author.name → masked") print(f" User.phone → masked") print(f" Location.address → masked")
Example 3: Mask with Audit Destination

Creates a policy that masks data and sends audit findings to another log group.

#!/bin/bash
# Mask with audit trail
# Compliance tracking

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
AUDIT_LOG_GROUP="arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/compliance/pii-audit"

echo "Creating policy with audit destination..."

POLICY=$(cat <<EOF
{
    "Name": "PIIAuditPolicy",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "Sid": "MaskAndAudit",
        "DataIdentifier": [
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardNumber"
        ],
        "Operation": {
            "Audit": {
                "FindingsDestination": {
                    "CloudWatchLogs": {
                        "LogGroup": "$AUDIT_LOG_GROUP"
                    }
                }
            },
            "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
        }
    }]
}
EOF
)

aws logs put-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --policy-document "$POLICY"

echo "✓ PII masked with audit trail"
Output: Creating policy with audit destination... ✓ PII masked with audit trail
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit tracking: print(f"PII found → logged to audit group") print(f"") print(f"Tracks User, Author data exposure")
Example 4: Mask Financial Data

Creates a policy to protect financial information in logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Mask financial data
# PCI compliance

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating financial data protection policy..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "FinancialDataPolicy",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "Sid": "MaskFinancialData",
        "DataIdentifier": [
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardNumber",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardExpiration",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/BankAccountNumber"
        ],
        "Operation": {
            "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
            "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
        }
    }]
}'

aws logs put-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --policy-document "$POLICY"

echo "✓ Financial data protection enabled"
Output: Creating financial data protection policy... ✓ Financial data protection enabled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Financial protection: print(f"Protected:") print(f" Credit card: 4111-****-****-****") print(f" Bank account: ********") print(f"") print(f"PCI DSS compliant logging")
Example 5: Multiple Statement Policy

Creates a policy with multiple statements for different data categories.

#!/bin/bash
# Multiple statement policy
# Categorized protection

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating multi-statement protection policy..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "CategorizedProtectionPolicy",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "ProtectUserIdentity",
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/Name"
            ],
            "Operation": {
                "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
                "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
            }
        },
        {
            "Sid": "ProtectLocationData",
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/Address",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/IpAddress"
            ],
            "Operation": {
                "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
                "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
            }
        }
    ]
}'

aws logs put-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --policy-document "$POLICY"

echo "✓ Multi-category protection enabled"
echo "  User identity: Email, Name masked"
echo "  Location data: Address, IP masked"
Output: Creating multi-statement protection policy... ✓ Multi-category protection enabled User identity: Email, Name masked Location data: Address, IP masked
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Categorized protection: print(f"User Identity:") print(f" User.email, Author.name masked") print(f"") print(f"Location Data:") print(f" Location.address, client IP masked")

get-data-protection-policy

Retrieves the data protection policy for a log group. This command shows what sensitive data is being masked in Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-identifier (string, required): The log group name or ARN to retrieve the policy for
Return Value
  • logGroupIdentifier (string): The log group name or ARN
  • policyDocument (string): JSON policy document with data protection configuration
  • lastUpdatedTime (long): Epoch timestamp of last policy update
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Get Current Data Protection Policy

Retrieves and displays the current data protection policy.

#!/bin/bash
# Get current policy
# Review configuration

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Retrieving data protection policy..."
echo ""

aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --output json | jq '{
        logGroup: .logGroupIdentifier,
        lastUpdated: (.lastUpdatedTime | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")),
        policy: (.policyDocument | fromjson)
    }'
Output: Retrieving data protection policy... { "logGroup": "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost", "lastUpdated": "2025-01-15 10:30", "policy": { "Name": "EmailProtectionPolicy", "Version": "2021-06-01", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "MaskEmails", "DataIdentifier": ["arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress"], "Operation": { "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}}, "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}} } } ] } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Current protection: print(f"Policy: EmailProtectionPolicy") print(f"Masking: Email addresses") print(f"") print(f"Affects: User.email, Author.email")
Example 2: List Protected Data Types

Extracts and displays all data identifiers being protected.

#!/bin/bash
# List protected data types
# Security review

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Protected Data Types:"
echo "====================="
echo ""

aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --query 'policyDocument' \
    --output text | jq -r '.Statement[].DataIdentifier[] | 
        "  - " + (. | split("/") | .[-1])'
Output: Protected Data Types: ===================== - EmailAddress - Name - PhoneNumber - Address
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Protected data in Django: print(f"User: email, name, phone") print(f"Author: name, email") print(f"Location: address")
Example 3: Check Policy Exists

Verifies whether a data protection policy is configured.

#!/bin/bash
# Check if policy exists
# Compliance audit

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Checking data protection policy..."

if aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "✓ Data protection policy EXISTS"
    
    POLICY_NAME=$(aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
        --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
        --query 'policyDocument' \
        --output text | jq -r '.Name')
    
    echo "  Policy Name: $POLICY_NAME"
else
    echo "✗ No data protection policy found"
    echo "  Consider adding one for PII compliance"
fi
Output: Checking data protection policy... ✓ Data protection policy EXISTS Policy Name: ComprehensivePIIPolicy
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Compliance check: print(f"Policy: ComprehensivePIIPolicy") print(f"Status: Active") print(f"") print(f"Django User/Author data protected")
Example 4: Compare Policies Across Log Groups

Compares data protection policies across multiple Django log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare policies
# Consistency check

LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-users"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-skills"
)

echo "Data Protection Policy Comparison"
echo "================================="
echo ""

for LOG_GROUP in "${LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    NAME=$(basename "$LOG_GROUP")
    echo "=== $NAME ==="
    
    POLICY=$(aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
        --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ -n "$POLICY" ]; then
        echo "$POLICY" | jq -r '.policyDocument | fromjson | 
            "Policy: \(.Name)\nIdentifiers: \([.Statement[].DataIdentifier[]] | length)"'
    else
        echo "No policy configured"
    fi
    echo ""
done
Output: Data Protection Policy Comparison ================================= === anihpj-jobpost === Policy: ComprehensivePIIPolicy Identifiers: 4 === anihpj-users === Policy: UserDataPolicy Identifiers: 5 === anihpj-skills === No policy configured
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy comparison: print(f"jobpost: 4 identifiers") print(f"users: 5 identifiers") print(f"skills: NO POLICY") print(f"") print(f"⚠ skills log group needs protection")
Example 5: Generate Policy Report

Creates a comprehensive report of data protection configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate policy report
# Documentation

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
REPORT_FILE="data-protection-report-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Generating data protection report..."

RESULT=$(aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --output json)

# Save full report
echo "$RESULT" > "$REPORT_FILE"

echo "Report saved: $REPORT_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Summary:"
POLICY=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq '.policyDocument | fromjson')

echo "$POLICY" | jq '{
    name: .Name,
    version: .Version,
    statementCount: (.Statement | length),
    dataTypesProtected: [.Statement[].DataIdentifier[]] | unique | length,
    lastUpdate: ('"$(echo "$RESULT" | jq '.lastUpdatedTime')"' | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"))
}'
Output: Generating data protection report... Report saved: data-protection-report-20250120.json Summary: { "name": "ComprehensivePIIPolicy", "version": "2021-06-01", "statementCount": 2, "dataTypesProtected": 6, "lastUpdate": "2025-01-15" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy report: print(f"6 data types protected") print(f"2 policy statements") print(f"") print(f"Django models covered:") print(f" User, Author, Location")

delete-data-protection-policy

Deletes the data protection policy from a log group. This command removes PII masking from Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs delete-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --log-group-identifier (string, required): The log group name or ARN to delete the policy from
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Policy is immediately removed
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete Policy from Dev Environment

Removes data protection from development logs where full data visibility is needed.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove dev policy
# Full visibility

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-dev"

echo "Removing data protection from dev environment..."

aws logs delete-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP"

echo "✓ Data protection policy deleted"
echo "  Dev logs now show unmasked data"
Output: Removing data protection from dev environment... ✓ Data protection policy deleted Dev logs now show unmasked data
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dev environment: print(f"Masking removed from dev logs") print(f"") print(f"Full visibility for debugging:") print(f" User.email visible") print(f" Author.name visible")
Example 2: Delete with Verification

Deletes policy and verifies it was successfully removed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with verification
# Confirm removal

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test"

echo "Deleting data protection policy..."

aws logs delete-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP"

echo ""
echo "Verifying deletion..."

if aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "⚠ Policy still exists"
else
    echo "✓ Verified: Policy deleted"
fi
Output: Deleting data protection policy... Verifying deletion... ✓ Verified: Policy deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified removal: print(f"Policy: Deleted") print(f"Verification: Passed") print(f"") print(f"Test logs now unmasked")
Example 3: Delete with Backup

Backs up policy before deletion for potential restoration.

#!/bin/bash
# Backup before delete
# Recoverable

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"
BACKUP_FILE="data-protection-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Backing up policy before deletion..."

aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --output json > "$BACKUP_FILE"

echo "✓ Backup saved: $BACKUP_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Deleting policy..."

aws logs delete-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP"

echo "✓ Policy deleted"
echo "  Can restore from backup if needed"
Output: Backing up policy before deletion... ✓ Backup saved: data-protection-backup-20250120.json Deleting policy... ✓ Policy deleted Can restore from backup if needed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safe deletion: print(f"Backup: data-protection-backup-20250120.json") print(f"Can restore policy if needed") print(f"") print(f"Django logs temporarily unmasked")
Example 4: Cleanup Test Policies

Removes data protection policies from all test log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Cleanup test policies
# Batch removal

TEST_LOG_GROUPS=(
    "/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost-test"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-skills-test"
    "/aws/django/anihpj-users-test"
)

echo "Cleaning up test environment policies..."
echo ""

DELETED=0
for LOG_GROUP in "${TEST_LOG_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    NAME=$(basename "$LOG_GROUP")
    echo "Deleting policy from: $NAME"
    
    aws logs delete-data-protection-policy \
        --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" 2>/dev/null
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        ((DELETED++))
        echo "  ✓ Deleted"
    else
        echo "  ✗ No policy or failed"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Summary: $DELETED policies deleted"
Output: Cleaning up test environment policies... Deleting policy from: anihpj-jobpost-test ✓ Deleted Deleting policy from: anihpj-skills-test ✓ Deleted Deleting policy from: anihpj-users-test ✗ No policy or failed Summary: 2 policies deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Test cleanup: print(f"2 test policies removed") print(f"") print(f"Test logs unmasked for debugging")
Example 5: Delete Before Policy Update

Removes existing policy to replace with a new configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete for replacement
# Policy update

LOG_GROUP="/aws/django/anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Replacing data protection policy..."
echo ""

# Backup current
echo "1. Backing up current policy..."
aws logs get-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP" \
    --output json > "old-policy.json"
echo "   ✓ Backup saved"

# Delete current
echo "2. Deleting current policy..."
aws logs delete-data-protection-policy \
    --log-group-identifier "$LOG_GROUP"
echo "   ✓ Deleted"

# Apply new (would use put-data-protection-policy)
echo "3. Ready for new policy..."
echo "   Use put-data-protection-policy to apply new configuration"
Output: Replacing data protection policy... 1. Backing up current policy... ✓ Backup saved 2. Deleting current policy... ✓ Deleted 3. Ready for new policy... Use put-data-protection-policy to apply new configuration
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy replacement: print(f"Old policy: Backed up") print(f"Current: Deleted") print(f"") print(f"Ready for new Django data protection")

put-account-policy

Creates an account-level data protection or subscription filter policy that applies to all log groups. This command establishes organization-wide policies for Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs put-account-policy \
    --policy-name <value> \
    --policy-document <value> \
    --policy-type <value> \
    [--scope <value>] \
    [--selection-criteria <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --policy-name (string, required): Name for the account-level policy
  • --policy-document (string, required): JSON policy document
  • --policy-type (string, required): DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY, SUBSCRIPTION_FILTER_POLICY, or TRANSFORMER_POLICY
  • --scope (string, optional): ALL to apply to all log groups
  • --selection-criteria (string, optional): Criteria to select which log groups policy applies to
Return Value
  • accountPolicy: Object containing policy details:
    • policyName: Name of the policy
    • policyDocument: The policy document
    • lastUpdatedTime: When last modified
    • policyType: Type of policy
    • scope: Scope of application
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Create Account-Wide Data Protection

Creates a policy to mask PII across all log groups in the account.

#!/bin/bash
# Account-wide data protection
# All log groups

POLICY_NAME="AccountDataProtection"

echo "Creating account-level data protection policy..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "AccountDataProtection",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "Sid": "MaskPII",
        "DataIdentifier": [
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardNumber"
        ],
        "Operation": {
            "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
            "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
        }
    }]
}'

aws logs put-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --policy-document "$POLICY" \
    --scope ALL

echo "✓ Account-level PII protection enabled"
Output: Creating account-level data protection policy... ✓ Account-level PII protection enabled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Account-wide protection: print(f"All log groups protected:") print(f" User.email: masked") print(f" Credit cards: masked") print(f"") print(f"Applies to ALL Django apps")
Example 2: Create Account Subscription Filter

Creates an account-level subscription filter for centralized logging.

#!/bin/bash
# Account subscription filter
# Central logging

POLICY_NAME="CentralLoggingPolicy"
KINESIS_ARN="arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/central-logs"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CWLtoKinesisRole"

echo "Creating account-level subscription filter..."

POLICY=$(cat <<EOF
{
    "DestinationArn": "$KINESIS_ARN",
    "FilterPattern": "{ $.level = \"ERROR\" }",
    "RoleArn": "$ROLE_ARN"
}
EOF
)

aws logs put-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type SUBSCRIPTION_FILTER_POLICY \
    --policy-document "$POLICY" \
    --scope ALL

echo "✓ All ERROR logs streaming to central Kinesis"
Output: Creating account-level subscription filter... ✓ All ERROR logs streaming to central Kinesis
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Centralized error logging: print(f"All Django ERROR logs →") print(f" Kinesis: central-logs") print(f"") print(f"JobPost, Skills, User errors captured")
Example 3: Create Selective Data Protection

Creates a policy that applies only to specific log groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Selective protection
# Django logs only

POLICY_NAME="DjangoDataProtection"

echo "Creating selective data protection policy..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "DjangoDataProtection",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "Sid": "MaskDjangoPII",
        "DataIdentifier": [
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/Name"
        ],
        "Operation": {
            "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
            "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
        }
    }]
}'

aws logs put-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --policy-document "$POLICY" \
    --selection-criteria 'LogGroupName PREFIX "/aws/django/"'

echo "✓ Protection applies to /aws/django/* log groups"
Output: Creating selective data protection policy... ✓ Protection applies to /aws/django/* log groups
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Selective protection: print(f"Protected: /aws/django/* logs") print(f" anihpj-jobpost") print(f" anihpj-skills") print(f" anihpj-users")
Example 4: Create Comprehensive Account Policy

Creates a comprehensive data protection policy with multiple data types.

#!/bin/bash
# Comprehensive protection
# All PII types

POLICY_NAME="ComprehensiveAccountPolicy"

echo "Creating comprehensive account policy..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "ComprehensiveAccountPolicy",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "MaskIdentity",
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/Name",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/PhoneNumber"
            ],
            "Operation": {"Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}}
        },
        {
            "Sid": "MaskFinancial",
            "DataIdentifier": [
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardNumber",
                "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/BankAccountNumber"
            ],
            "Operation": {"Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}}
        }
    ]
}'

aws logs put-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --policy-document "$POLICY" \
    --scope ALL

echo "✓ Comprehensive protection enabled"
Output: Creating comprehensive account policy... ✓ Comprehensive protection enabled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Comprehensive protection: print(f"Identity: email, name, phone") print(f"Financial: credit cards, bank accounts") print(f"") print(f"All Django models protected")
Example 5: Update Existing Account Policy

Updates an existing account policy with new configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Update account policy
# Add new identifiers

POLICY_NAME="AccountDataProtection"

echo "Updating account policy with additional identifiers..."

POLICY='{
    "Name": "AccountDataProtection",
    "Version": "2021-06-01",
    "Statement": [{
        "Sid": "MaskExtendedPII",
        "DataIdentifier": [
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardNumber",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/SocialSecurityNumber",
            "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/IpAddress"
        ],
        "Operation": {
            "Audit": {"FindingsDestination": {}},
            "Deidentify": {"MaskConfig": {}}
        }
    }]
}'

aws logs put-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --policy-document "$POLICY" \
    --scope ALL

echo "✓ Account policy updated"
echo "  Added: SSN, IP address protection"
Output: Updating account policy with additional identifiers... ✓ Account policy updated Added: SSN, IP address protection
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Extended protection: print(f"New identifiers added:") print(f" Social Security Numbers") print(f" IP Addresses") print(f"") print(f"Enhanced Django log security")

describe-account-policies

Lists account-level policies for data protection or subscription filters. This command retrieves organization-wide policies affecting Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type <value> \
    [--policy-name <value>] \
    [--account-identifiers <value>] \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --policy-type (string, required): DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY, SUBSCRIPTION_FILTER_POLICY, or TRANSFORMER_POLICY
  • --policy-name (string, optional): Filter by specific policy name
  • --account-identifiers (list, optional): List of account IDs to query
Return Value
  • accountPolicies (list): Array of policy objects containing:
    • policyName: Name of the policy
    • policyDocument: JSON policy document
    • lastUpdatedTime: When last modified
    • policyType: Type of policy
    • scope: ALL or specific selection
    • accountId: AWS account ID
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: List Data Protection Policies

Retrieves all account-level data protection policies.

#!/bin/bash
# List data protection policies
# Security audit

echo "Account Data Protection Policies"
echo "================================"
echo ""

aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --output json | jq -r '.accountPolicies[] | 
        "Name: \(.policyName)
  Scope: \(.scope)
  Updated: \(.lastUpdatedTime | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"))
"'
Output: Account Data Protection Policies ================================ Name: AccountDataProtection Scope: ALL Updated: 2025-01-15 Name: DjangoDataProtection Scope: SELECTION_CRITERIA Updated: 2025-01-17
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Account policies: print(f"2 data protection policies") print(f" AccountDataProtection: All logs") print(f" DjangoDataProtection: /aws/django/*")
Example 2: List Subscription Filter Policies

Retrieves all account-level subscription filter policies.

#!/bin/bash
# List subscription policies
# Centralized logging

echo "Account Subscription Filter Policies"
echo "===================================="
echo ""

aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type SUBSCRIPTION_FILTER_POLICY \
    --output json | jq -r '.accountPolicies[] | 
        "Name: \(.policyName)
  Destination: \(.policyDocument | fromjson | .DestinationArn | split(":") | .[-1])
  Filter: \(.policyDocument | fromjson | .FilterPattern // "None")
"'
Output: Account Subscription Filter Policies ==================================== Name: CentralLoggingPolicy Destination: stream/central-logs Filter: { $.level = "ERROR" } Name: AllLogsToS3 Destination: deliverystream/logs-to-s3 Filter: None
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Subscription policies: print(f"Central logging: ERROR logs → Kinesis") print(f"Archive: All logs → S3") print(f"") print(f"Django logs flow to both")
Example 3: Get Specific Policy Details

Retrieves details for a specific named policy.

#!/bin/bash
# Get specific policy
# Detailed view

POLICY_NAME="AccountDataProtection"

echo "Policy Details: $POLICY_NAME"
echo ""

aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --output json | jq '.accountPolicies[0] | {
        name: .policyName,
        scope: .scope,
        lastUpdated: (.lastUpdatedTime | . / 1000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")),
        policy: (.policyDocument | fromjson)
    }'
Output: Policy Details: AccountDataProtection { "name": "AccountDataProtection", "scope": "ALL", "lastUpdated": "2025-01-15 10:30", "policy": { "Name": "AccountDataProtection", "Version": "2021-06-01", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "MaskPII", "DataIdentifier": [ "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/EmailAddress", "arn:aws:dataprotection::aws:data-identifier/CreditCardNumber" ] } ] } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy detail: print(f"Masking: Email, Credit Cards") print(f"Scope: All log groups") print(f"") print(f"Affects all Django applications")
Example 4: Audit Policy Coverage

Audits what data types are protected by account policies.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit policy coverage
# Compliance check

echo "Account Policy Coverage Audit"
echo "============================="
echo ""

POLICIES=$(aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --output json)

echo "Protected Data Types:"
echo "$POLICIES" | jq -r '
    [.accountPolicies[].policyDocument | fromjson | 
    .Statement[].DataIdentifier[]] | 
    flatten | unique | .[] | 
    "  - " + (. | split("/") | .[-1])'

echo ""
POLICY_COUNT=$(echo "$POLICIES" | jq '.accountPolicies | length')
echo "Total Policies: $POLICY_COUNT"
Output: Account Policy Coverage Audit ============================= Protected Data Types: - Address - CreditCardNumber - EmailAddress - Name - PhoneNumber Total Policies: 2
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Coverage audit: print(f"5 data types protected") print(f"") print(f"Django models covered:") print(f" User: email, phone") print(f" Author: name, email") print(f" Location: address")
Example 5: Generate Compliance Report

Generates a comprehensive report of all account policies.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate compliance report
# Documentation

REPORT_FILE="account-policies-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Generating account policies report..."

# Get all policy types
DATA_POLICIES=$(aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY --output json)

SUB_POLICIES=$(aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type SUBSCRIPTION_FILTER_POLICY --output json)

# Combine into report
echo "{
    \"reportDate\": \"$(date -Iseconds)\",
    \"dataProtectionPolicies\": $(echo $DATA_POLICIES | jq '.accountPolicies'),
    \"subscriptionFilterPolicies\": $(echo $SUB_POLICIES | jq '.accountPolicies')
}" | jq '.' > "$REPORT_FILE"

echo "✓ Report saved: $REPORT_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Summary:"
echo "  Data Protection Policies: $(echo $DATA_POLICIES | jq '.accountPolicies | length')"
echo "  Subscription Policies: $(echo $SUB_POLICIES | jq '.accountPolicies | length')"
Output: Generating account policies report... ✓ Report saved: account-policies-20250120.json Summary: Data Protection Policies: 2 Subscription Policies: 2
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Compliance report: print(f"4 total account policies") print(f"") print(f"Django logging governance:") print(f" PII protection: Active") print(f" Central logging: Active")

delete-account-policy

Deletes an account-level policy for data protection or subscription filters. This command removes organization-wide policies affecting Django application logs tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations.

Syntax
aws logs delete-account-policy \
    --policy-name <value> \
    --policy-type <value> \
    [--cli-input-json <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --policy-name (string, required): Name of the account policy to delete
  • --policy-type (string, required): DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY, SUBSCRIPTION_FILTER_POLICY, or TRANSFORMER_POLICY
Return Value
  • (none): Returns empty response on success. Policy is immediately removed
5 Detailed CloudWatch Logs Insights Examples
Example 1: Delete Data Protection Policy

Removes an account-level data protection policy.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete data protection policy
# Policy removal

POLICY_NAME="OldAccountPolicy"

echo "Deleting account policy: $POLICY_NAME"

aws logs delete-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY

echo "✓ Account policy deleted"
Output: Deleting account policy: OldAccountPolicy ✓ Account policy deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy removed: print(f"OldAccountPolicy deleted") print(f"") print(f"PII masking no longer applies")
Example 2: Delete Subscription Filter Policy

Removes an account-level subscription filter policy.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete subscription policy
# Stop central logging

POLICY_NAME="LegacyCentralLogging"

echo "Deleting subscription policy: $POLICY_NAME"

aws logs delete-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type SUBSCRIPTION_FILTER_POLICY

echo "✓ Subscription policy deleted"
echo "  Logs no longer streaming to old destination"
Output: Deleting subscription policy: LegacyCentralLogging ✓ Subscription policy deleted Logs no longer streaming to old destination
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Subscription removed: print(f"Legacy logging stopped") print(f"") print(f"Django logs stay in CloudWatch only")
Example 3: Delete with Backup

Backs up policy before deletion for potential recovery.

#!/bin/bash
# Backup before delete
# Recoverable

POLICY_NAME="AccountDataProtection"
BACKUP_FILE="account-policy-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "Backing up policy before deletion..."

aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --output json > "$BACKUP_FILE"

echo "✓ Backup saved: $BACKUP_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Deleting policy..."

aws logs delete-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY

echo "✓ Policy deleted"
Output: Backing up policy before deletion... ✓ Backup saved: account-policy-backup-20250120.json Deleting policy... ✓ Policy deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safe deletion: print(f"Backup: account-policy-backup-20250120.json") print(f"Can restore if needed") print(f"") print(f"Django PII masking paused")
Example 4: Delete with Verification

Deletes policy and verifies it was removed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with verification
# Confirm removal

POLICY_NAME="TestAccountPolicy"

echo "Deleting account policy..."

aws logs delete-account-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY

echo ""
echo "Verifying deletion..."

EXISTS=$(aws logs describe-account-policies \
    --policy-type DATA_PROTECTION_POLICY \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --query 'accountPolicies | length(@)' \
    --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "0")

if [ "$EXISTS" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ Verified: Policy deleted"
else
    echo "⚠ Policy may still exist"
fi
Output: Deleting account policy... Verifying deletion... ✓ Verified: Policy deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified removal: print(f"TestAccountPolicy: Deleted") print(f"Verification: Passed") print(f"") print(f"Django logs unaffected")
