Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Weekly insights for anihpj: # # FAULT incidents (3): # - JobPost.objects operations # - Database connectivity # - API endpoint errors # # LATENCY incidents (2): # - Query performance # - External service calls # # Action items: # - Review JobPost query optimization # - Increase connection pool size
Example 4: Monitor for new insights with alerting

Set up a check for new active insights and send notifications.

#!/bin/bash
SNS_TOPIC="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:xray-alerts"
CHECK_INTERVAL=300  # 5 minutes

while true; do
    # Get active insights from last hour
    ACTIVE=$(aws xray get-insight-summaries \
        --start-time $(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s) \
        --end-time $(date +%s) \
        --states ACTIVE \
        --max-results 10)
    
    INSIGHT_COUNT=$(echo "$ACTIVE" | jq '.InsightSummaries | length')
    
    if [ "$INSIGHT_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
        # New insights detected - send alert
        ALERT_MSG=$(echo "$ACTIVE" | jq -r '
            "⚠️ X-Ray Active Insights Alert\n\n" +
            "Active insights: \(.InsightSummaries | length)\n\n" +
            (.InsightSummaries[] | "• \(.Summary)\n  Root cause: \(.RootCauseServiceId.Name)\n  Started: \(.StartTime)\n")
        ')
        
        aws sns publish \
            --topic-arn "$SNS_TOPIC" \
            --subject "X-Ray: $INSIGHT_COUNT Active Insight(s)" \
            --message "$ALERT_MSG"
        
        echo "[$(date)] Alert sent: $INSIGHT_COUNT active insights"
    else
        echo "[$(date)] No active insights"
    fi
    
    sleep $CHECK_INTERVAL
done
Output: [Sat Jan 18 14:00:00 UTC 2025] Alert sent: 2 active insights [Sat Jan 18 14:05:00 UTC 2025] Alert sent: 2 active insights [Sat Jan 18 14:10:00 UTC 2025] Alert sent: 1 active insights [Sat Jan 18 14:15:00 UTC 2025] No active insights
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Automated insight monitoring for anihpj: # # Alert triggers when: # - New FAULT detected in JobPost operations # - New LATENCY detected in Skills queries # - Any anomaly in Author/Location lookups # # SNS notification includes: # - Affected Django models # - Root cause service # - Time insight started
Example 5: Analyze insight patterns over time

Identify recurring issues by analyzing insight history.

#!/bin/bash
# Get 30 days of insights
SUMMARIES=$(aws xray get-insight-summaries \
    --start-time $(date -d '30 days ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --max-results 100)

echo "X-Ray Insight Pattern Analysis (30 days)"
echo "========================================="
echo ""

# Root cause frequency
echo "Most Common Root Causes:"
echo "$SUMMARIES" | jq -r '
    [.InsightSummaries[].RootCauseServiceId.Name] |
    group_by(.) | sort_by(-length) | .[] |
    "  \(.[0]): \(length) occurrences"'
echo ""

# Day of week analysis
echo "Insights by Day of Week:"
echo "$SUMMARIES" | jq -r '
    [.InsightSummaries[] | .StartTime | strptime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") | strftime("%A")] |
    group_by(.) | sort_by(-length) | .[:3][] |
    "  \(.[0]): \(length) insights"'
echo ""

# Category breakdown
echo "By Category:"
echo "$SUMMARIES" | jq -r '
    [.InsightSummaries[].Categories[]] |
    group_by(.) | .[] |
    "  \(.[0]): \(length)"'
echo ""

# Average duration for closed insights
echo "Average Resolution Time:"
echo "$SUMMARIES" | jq -r '
    [.InsightSummaries[] | select(.State == "CLOSED" and .EndTime != null) |
        ((.EndTime | strptime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") | mktime) - 
         (.StartTime | strptime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") | mktime)) / 60
    ] | if length > 0 then add/length | round else 0 end |
    "  \(.) minutes average"'
Output: X-Ray Insight Pattern Analysis (30 days) ========================================= Most Common Root Causes: PostgreSQL: 8 occurrences anihpj-django: 5 occurrences External-API: 3 occurrences Redis: 1 occurrence Insights by Day of Week: Monday: 6 insights Friday: 4 insights Wednesday: 3 insights By Category: FAULT: 10 LATENCY: 7 Average Resolution Time: 42 minutes average
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 30-day pattern analysis for anihpj: # # Primary issue: PostgreSQL (8 incidents) # - JobPost.objects queries # - Skills relationships # Recommendation: Optimize queries, add indexes # # Monday spike suggests: # - Weekend data accumulation # - Batch job conflicts # # MTTR: 42 minutes (good) # - Quick detection and resolution

get-retrieved-traces-graph

Retrieves a service graph for traces that were retrieved using start-trace-retrieval. This provides visualization of archived trace data.

Syntax
aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph \
    --retrieval-token <value> \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --retrieval-token (string, required): Token from start-trace-retrieval operation
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token
Return Value
  • RetrievalStatus (string): Status of retrieval: SCHEDULED, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED
  • Services (array): Service graph nodes
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Get graph from retrieved traces

Visualize service dependencies from archived Django application traces.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph \
    --retrieval-token "retrieval-token-abc123"
Output: { "RetrievalStatus": "COMPLETE", "Services": [ { "Name": "anihpj-django", "Type": "client", "Edges": [ {"ReferenceId": 1}, {"ReferenceId": 2} ] }, { "ReferenceId": 1, "Name": "PostgreSQL", "SummaryStatistics": { "TotalCount": 500, "FaultStatistics": {"TotalCount": 10} } } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Historical service graph showing: # - 500 PostgreSQL calls from Django # - 10 faults (2% error rate) # - Useful for post-incident analysis of JobPost queries
Example 2: Wait for retrieval completion

Poll the retrieval status until the graph is ready.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-token-abc123"

echo "Waiting for trace retrieval to complete..."

while true; do
    RESULT=$(aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph \
        --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN")
    
    STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.RetrievalStatus')
    
    case "$STATUS" in
        "COMPLETE")
            echo "Retrieval complete!"
            echo "$RESULT" | jq '.Services[] | {name: .Name, type: .Type}'
            break
            ;;
        "FAILED")
            echo "Retrieval failed!"
            exit 1
            ;;
        "RUNNING"|"SCHEDULED")
            echo "Status: $STATUS - waiting..."
            sleep 10
            ;;
    esac
done
Output: Waiting for trace retrieval to complete... Status: SCHEDULED - waiting... Status: RUNNING - waiting... Status: RUNNING - waiting... Retrieval complete! { "name": "anihpj-django", "type": "client" } { "name": "PostgreSQL", "type": "AWS::RDS::DBInstance" } { "name": "Redis", "type": "AWS::ElastiCache::Cluster" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Retrieved historical graph shows: # - Django application (anihpj) # - PostgreSQL for JobPost, Skills storage # - Redis for caching # # Graph available for analysis after retrieval completes
Example 3: Export retrieved graph as JSON

Save the historical service graph for documentation.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-token-abc123"
OUTPUT_FILE="historical-graph-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

GRAPH=$(aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN")

STATUS=$(echo "$GRAPH" | jq -r '.RetrievalStatus')

if [ "$STATUS" != "COMPLETE" ]; then
    echo "Retrieval not complete. Status: $STATUS"
    exit 1
fi

# Transform and save
echo "$GRAPH" | jq '{
    exportDate: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    status: .RetrievalStatus,
    services: [.Services[] | {
        name: .Name,
        type: .Type,
        dependencies: [.Edges[]?.ReferenceId],
        stats: .SummaryStatistics
    }]
}' > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Graph exported to: $OUTPUT_FILE"
jq '.services | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE" | xargs -I {} echo "Total services: {}"
Output: Graph exported to: historical-graph-20250118.json Total services: 4
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Exported graph documents: # - anihpj-django (app server) # - PostgreSQL (JobPost, Skills data) # - Redis (session cache) # - External-API (Location service) # # Use for capacity planning and architecture reviews
Example 4: Compare retrieved graph with current state

Analyze architecture changes between historical and current traces.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-token-abc123"

# Get historical graph
HISTORICAL=$(aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" | jq '.Services')

# Get current service graph
CURRENT=$(aws xray get-service-graph \
    --start-time $(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) | jq '.Services')

# Compare service counts
HIST_COUNT=$(echo "$HISTORICAL" | jq 'length')
CURR_COUNT=$(echo "$CURRENT" | jq 'length')

echo "Architecture Comparison:"
echo "  Historical services: $HIST_COUNT"
echo "  Current services: $CURR_COUNT"

# Find new services
echo ""
echo "New services (not in historical):"
echo "$CURRENT" | jq -r --argjson hist "$HISTORICAL" '
    [.[].Name] - [$hist[].Name] | .[] | "  - \(.)"'

# Find removed services
echo ""
echo "Removed services (not in current):"
echo "$HISTORICAL" | jq -r --argjson curr "$CURRENT" '
    [.[].Name] - [$curr[].Name] | .[] | "  - \(.)"'
Output: Architecture Comparison: Historical services: 3 Current services: 4 New services (not in historical): - Elasticsearch Removed services (not in current): (none)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Architecture evolution for anihpj: # # Historical (3 services): # - Django, PostgreSQL, Redis # # Current (4 services): # - Added Elasticsearch for search # - Now: JobPost.objects.search() uses ES # - Skills full-text search enabled
Example 5: Analyze error patterns from retrieved traces

Extract fault statistics from the historical service graph.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-token-abc123"

aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" | jq -r '
if .RetrievalStatus != "COMPLETE" then
    "Retrieval not complete: \(.RetrievalStatus)"
else
    "Historical Error Analysis",
    "========================",
    "",
    (.Services[] | select(.SummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount > 0) |
        "Service: \(.Name)",
        "  Total requests: \(.SummaryStatistics.TotalCount)",
        "  Faults: \(.SummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount)",
        "  Error rate: \((.SummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount / .SummaryStatistics.TotalCount * 100) | round)%",
        ""
    ),
    "Summary:",
    "  Services with errors: \([.Services[] | select(.SummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount > 0)] | length)",
    "  Total faults: \([.Services[].SummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount // 0] | add)"
end'
Output: Historical Error Analysis ======================== Service: PostgreSQL Total requests: 5000 Faults: 150 Error rate: 3% Service: External-API Total requests: 1200 Faults: 60 Error rate: 5% Summary: Services with errors: 2 Total faults: 210
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Historical error analysis reveals: # # PostgreSQL (3% errors): # - JobPost.objects.create() failures # - Skills constraint violations # # External-API (5% errors): # - Location geocoding timeouts # # Action: Review archived traces for patterns

get-sampling-rules

Retrieves all sampling rules configured for X-Ray. These rules control trace collection rates for your Django application.

Syntax
aws xray get-sampling-rules \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token for additional results
Return Value
  • SamplingRuleRecords (array): List of sampling rules with RuleName, RuleARN, Priority, FixedRate, ReservoirSize, ServiceName, HTTPMethod, URLPath
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: List all sampling rules

View all trace sampling configurations for the Django application.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-sampling-rules
Output: { "SamplingRuleRecords": [ { "SamplingRule": { "RuleName": "Default", "Priority": 10000, "FixedRate": 0.05, "ReservoirSize": 1, "ServiceName": "*", "URLPath": "*" } }, { "SamplingRule": { "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling", "Priority": 1000, "FixedRate": 0.10, "ReservoirSize": 5, "ServiceName": "anihpj-django", "URLPath": "/api/*" } }, { "SamplingRule": { "RuleName": "anihpj-error-sampling", "Priority": 100, "FixedRate": 1.0, "ReservoirSize": 10, "ServiceName": "anihpj-django" } } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Sampling rules for anihpj: # 1. Default: 5% of all traffic # 2. API endpoints: 10% of /api/* (JobPost, Skills APIs) # 3. Errors: 100% of failures captured # Priority determines rule precedence (lower = higher priority)
Example 2: Filter and format sampling rules

Display sampling rules in a readable table format.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-sampling-rules | jq -r '
    ["Rule Name", "Priority", "Rate", "Reservoir", "Service", "URL Path"],
    ["----------", "--------", "----", "---------", "-------", "--------"],
    (.SamplingRuleRecords[].SamplingRule |
        [.RuleName, .Priority, "\(.FixedRate * 100)%", .ReservoirSize, .ServiceName, .URLPath]
    ) | @tsv' | column -t
Output: Rule Name Priority Rate Reservoir Service URL Path ---------- -------- ---- --------- ------- -------- anihpj-error-sampling 100 100% 10 anihpj-django * anihpj-api-sampling 1000 10% 5 anihpj-django /api/* anihpj-admin-sampling 2000 50% 2 anihpj-django /admin/* Default 10000 5% 1 * *
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Sampling configuration: # - All errors: 100% sampled (debugging) # - API (/api/*): 10% sampled # * JobPost CRUD: /api/jobposts/ # * Skills: /api/skills/ # - Admin: 50% sampled (auditing) # - Default: 5% baseline
Example 3: Export sampling rules for backup

Save all sampling rules to a JSON file for disaster recovery.

#!/bin/bash
BACKUP_FILE="xray-sampling-rules-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

aws xray get-sampling-rules | jq '{
    exportDate: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    rules: [.SamplingRuleRecords[].SamplingRule | {
        RuleName: .RuleName,
        RuleARN: .RuleARN,
        Priority: .Priority,
        FixedRate: .FixedRate,
        ReservoirSize: .ReservoirSize,
        ServiceName: .ServiceName,
        ServiceType: .ServiceType,
        Host: .Host,
        HTTPMethod: .HTTPMethod,
        URLPath: .URLPath,
        Version: .Version
    }]
}' > "$BACKUP_FILE"

echo "Backup saved to: $BACKUP_FILE"
echo "Total rules: $(jq '.rules | length' "$BACKUP_FILE")"
echo ""
echo "Rules backed up:"
jq -r '.rules[].RuleName' "$BACKUP_FILE"
Output: Backup saved to: xray-sampling-rules-20250118.json Total rules: 4 Rules backed up: anihpj-error-sampling anihpj-api-sampling anihpj-admin-sampling Default
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backed up sampling rules for anihpj: # - Error rule: Captures all JobPost/Skills failures # - API rule: Monitors REST endpoints # - Admin rule: Tracks Django admin usage # - Default: Baseline sampling # # Restore with: aws xray create-sampling-rule --cli-input-json
Example 4: Validate sampling rule coverage

Check if all Django URL patterns are covered by sampling rules.

#!/bin/bash
# Known Django URL patterns
DJANGO_PATHS=("/api/jobposts/" "/api/skills/" "/api/users/" "/admin/" "/auth/" "/health/")

echo "Sampling Rule Coverage Analysis"
echo "==============================="
echo ""

RULES=$(aws xray get-sampling-rules)

for path in "${DJANGO_PATHS[@]}"; do
    # Find matching rule (lowest priority wins)
    MATCH=$(echo "$RULES" | jq -r --arg path "$path" '
        [.SamplingRuleRecords[].SamplingRule |
         select(
            (.URLPath == "*") or
            (.URLPath == ($path + "*")) or
            ($path | startswith(.URLPath | rtrimstr("*")))
         )] | sort_by(.Priority) | first |
        "\(.RuleName) (\(.FixedRate * 100)%)"
    ')
    
    echo "  $path -> $MATCH"
done

echo ""
echo "Uncovered patterns will use Default rule (5%)"
Output: Sampling Rule Coverage Analysis =============================== /api/jobposts/ -> anihpj-api-sampling (10%) /api/skills/ -> anihpj-api-sampling (10%) /api/users/ -> anihpj-api-sampling (10%) /admin/ -> anihpj-admin-sampling (50%) /auth/ -> Default (5%) /health/ -> Default (5%) Uncovered patterns will use Default rule (5%)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Coverage analysis for anihpj URLs: # # Well covered (10-50%): # - /api/jobposts/ - JobPost CRUD # - /api/skills/ - Skills management # - /api/users/ - User operations # - /admin/ - Django admin # # Low coverage (5%): # - /auth/ - Consider increasing for security # - /health/ - OK at baseline
Example 5: Generate sampling rule documentation

Create markdown documentation for sampling configuration.

#!/bin/bash
DOC_FILE="XRAY_SAMPLING_RULES.md"

{
    echo "# X-Ray Sampling Rules Configuration"
    echo ""
    echo "Generated: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
    echo ""
    echo "## Rules Overview"
    echo ""
    
    aws xray get-sampling-rules | jq -r '
        .SamplingRuleRecords | sort_by(.SamplingRule.Priority) | .[] |
        "### \(.SamplingRule.RuleName)",
        "",
        "- **Priority:** \(.SamplingRule.Priority)",
        "- **Sampling Rate:** \(.SamplingRule.FixedRate * 100)%",
        "- **Reservoir Size:** \(.SamplingRule.ReservoirSize) requests/second",
        "- **Service:** \(.SamplingRule.ServiceName)",
        "- **URL Pattern:** `\(.SamplingRule.URLPath)`",
        "- **HTTP Method:** \(.SamplingRule.HTTPMethod)",
        ""'
    
    echo "## Notes"
    echo ""
    echo "- Lower priority numbers take precedence"
    echo "- Reservoir ensures minimum traces even at low traffic"
    echo "- Error rule captures 100% of failures for debugging"
} > "$DOC_FILE"

echo "Documentation generated: $DOC_FILE"
head -30 "$DOC_FILE"
Output: Documentation generated: XRAY_SAMPLING_RULES.md # X-Ray Sampling Rules Configuration Generated: 2025-01-18T14:00:00Z ## Rules Overview ### anihpj-error-sampling - **Priority:** 100 - **Sampling Rate:** 100% - **Reservoir Size:** 10 requests/second - **Service:** anihpj-django - **URL Pattern:** `*` - **HTTP Method:** * ### anihpj-api-sampling - **Priority:** 1000 - **Sampling Rate:** 10% - **Reservoir Size:** 5 requests/second - **Service:** anihpj-django - **URL Pattern:** `/api/*` - **HTTP Method:** *
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Documentation for anihpj tracing: # # Error sampling (Priority 100): # - Captures all failures for debugging # - JobPost.objects.create() errors # - Skills validation failures # # API sampling (Priority 1000): # - 10% of /api/* traffic # - Includes all REST endpoints

get-sampling-statistic-summaries

Retrieves sampling statistics summaries showing how sampling rules are performing for your Django application traces.

Syntax
aws xray get-sampling-statistic-summaries \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token
Return Value
  • SamplingStatisticSummaries (array): Statistics including RuleName, Timestamp, RequestCount, BorrowCount, and SampledCount
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: View sampling statistics

Check how many Django application requests are being sampled.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-sampling-statistic-summaries
Output: { "SamplingStatisticSummaries": [ { "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling", "Timestamp": "2025-01-18T10:00:00Z", "RequestCount": 10000, "SampledCount": 1050, "BorrowCount": 50 }, { "RuleName": "anihpj-error-sampling", "Timestamp": "2025-01-18T10:00:00Z", "RequestCount": 150, "SampledCount": 150, "BorrowCount": 0 } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Sampling statistics: # API rule: 1050/10000 sampled (10.5%) # - JobPost API calls # - Skills CRUD operations # Error rule: 150/150 sampled (100%) # - All errors captured for debugging
Example 2: Calculate effective sampling rates

Analyze actual vs configured sampling percentages.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-sampling-statistic-summaries | jq -r '
    "Sampling Rate Analysis",
    "======================",
    "",
    (.SamplingStatisticSummaries[] |
        "Rule: \(.RuleName)",
        "  Total requests: \(.RequestCount)",
        "  Sampled: \(.SampledCount)",
        "  Borrowed: \(.BorrowCount)",
        "  Effective rate: \(if .RequestCount > 0 then ((.SampledCount + .BorrowCount) / .RequestCount * 100 | round) else 0 end)%",
        ""
    )'
Output: Sampling Rate Analysis ====================== Rule: anihpj-api-sampling Total requests: 10000 Sampled: 1000 Borrowed: 50 Effective rate: 11% Rule: anihpj-error-sampling Total requests: 150 Sampled: 150 Borrowed: 0 Effective rate: 100% Rule: anihpj-admin-sampling Total requests: 500 Sampled: 250 Borrowed: 0 Effective rate: 50% Rule: Default Total requests: 2000 Sampled: 100 Borrowed: 0 Effective rate: 5%
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Effective sampling for anihpj: # # API (11% actual vs 10% configured): # - Borrowed 50 extra for reservoir # - JobPost queries well represented # # Error (100%): All captured # Admin (50%): On target # Default (5%): On target
Example 3: Generate sampling efficiency report

Create a report showing sampling coverage and costs.

#!/bin/bash
STATS=$(aws xray get-sampling-statistic-summaries)

TOTAL_REQUESTS=$(echo "$STATS" | jq '[.SamplingStatisticSummaries[].RequestCount] | add')
TOTAL_SAMPLED=$(echo "$STATS" | jq '[.SamplingStatisticSummaries[].SampledCount] | add')
TOTAL_BORROWED=$(echo "$STATS" | jq '[.SamplingStatisticSummaries[].BorrowCount] | add')

# Estimate cost (traces stored per million)
TRACES_MILLIONS=$(echo "scale=2; $TOTAL_SAMPLED / 1000000" | bc)
ESTIMATED_COST=$(echo "scale=2; $TRACES_MILLIONS * 5" | bc)  # ~$5 per million traces

echo "X-Ray Sampling Efficiency Report"
echo "================================="
echo ""
echo "Traffic Summary:"
echo "  Total requests: $TOTAL_REQUESTS"
echo "  Traces sampled: $TOTAL_SAMPLED"
echo "  Reservoir borrowed: $TOTAL_BORROWED"
echo "  Overall rate: $(echo "scale=1; $TOTAL_SAMPLED * 100 / $TOTAL_REQUESTS" | bc)%"
echo ""
echo "Cost Estimate (monthly projection):"
echo "  Traces/hour: $TOTAL_SAMPLED"
echo "  Traces/month: $(($TOTAL_SAMPLED * 24 * 30))"
echo "  Est. cost: \$$(echo "scale=2; $TOTAL_SAMPLED * 24 * 30 / 1000000 * 5" | bc)"
echo ""
echo "Rule Breakdown:"
echo "$STATS" | jq -r '.SamplingStatisticSummaries[] | "  \(.RuleName): \(.SampledCount) traces"'
Output: X-Ray Sampling Efficiency Report ================================= Traffic Summary: Total requests: 12650 Traces sampled: 1500 Reservoir borrowed: 50 Overall rate: 11.8% Cost Estimate (monthly projection): Traces/hour: 1500 Traces/month: 1080000 Est. cost: $5.40 Rule Breakdown: anihpj-api-sampling: 1050 traces anihpj-error-sampling: 150 traces anihpj-admin-sampling: 200 traces Default: 100 traces
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # anihpj sampling economics: # # ~12% of traffic traced # ~$5.40/month estimated cost # # Trace distribution: # - API (70%): JobPost, Skills operations # - Errors (10%): Debugging data # - Admin (13%): Audit trail # - Other (7%): Background traffic
Example 4: Monitor sampling statistics over time

Track sampling performance with periodic checks.

#!/bin/bash
LOG_FILE="sampling-stats-$(date +%Y%m%d).log"
INTERVAL=60  # seconds

echo "Monitoring sampling statistics (logging to $LOG_FILE)"
echo "Press Ctrl+C to stop"

while true; do
    TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
    
    STATS=$(aws xray get-sampling-statistic-summaries 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        # Log summary line
        SUMMARY=$(echo "$STATS" | jq -r --arg ts "$TIMESTAMP" '
            [$ts] + [.SamplingStatisticSummaries[] | "\(.RuleName):\(.SampledCount)/\(.RequestCount)"] | join(",")
        ')
        
        echo "$SUMMARY" >> "$LOG_FILE"
        echo "$SUMMARY"
    else
        echo "$TIMESTAMP,ERROR: Failed to fetch stats" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    fi
    
    sleep $INTERVAL
done
Output: Monitoring sampling statistics (logging to sampling-stats-20250118.log) Press Ctrl+C to stop 2025-01-18T14:00:00Z,anihpj-api-sampling:1050/10000,anihpj-error-sampling:150/150,Default:100/2000 2025-01-18T14:01:00Z,anihpj-api-sampling:1100/10500,anihpj-error-sampling:155/155,Default:105/2100 2025-01-18T14:02:00Z,anihpj-api-sampling:1080/10200,anihpj-error-sampling:148/148,Default:102/2050
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Continuous monitoring tracks: # - Request volume to API endpoints # - Error rate trends # - Sampling effectiveness # # Useful for: # - Capacity planning # - Detecting traffic anomalies # - Validating rule changes
Example 5: Compare statistics across sampling rules

Analyze which rules are capturing the most traces.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-sampling-statistic-summaries | jq -r '
    .SamplingStatisticSummaries | sort_by(-.SampledCount) as $sorted |
    ($sorted | map(.SampledCount) | add) as $total |
    
    "Sampling Distribution Analysis",
    "==============================",
    "",
    "| Rule | Requests | Sampled | % of Total Traces |",
    "|------|----------|---------|-------------------|",
    ($sorted[] |
        "| \(.RuleName) | \(.RequestCount) | \(.SampledCount) | \((.SampledCount / $total * 100) | round)% |"
    ),
    "",
    "Total traces captured: \($total)",
    "",
    "Insights:",
    if ($sorted[0].SampledCount / $total > 0.5) then
        "  ⚠️  \($sorted[0].RuleName) captures \(($sorted[0].SampledCount / $total * 100) | round)% of traces"
    else
        "  ✓ Traces well distributed across rules"
    end'
Output: Sampling Distribution Analysis ============================== | Rule | Requests | Sampled | % of Total Traces | |------|----------|---------|-------------------| | anihpj-api-sampling | 10000 | 1050 | 70% | | anihpj-admin-sampling | 500 | 250 | 17% | | anihpj-error-sampling | 150 | 150 | 10% | | Default | 2000 | 50 | 3% | Total traces captured: 1500 Insights: ⚠️ anihpj-api-sampling captures 70% of traces
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Trace distribution for anihpj: # # API rule dominates (70%): # - Expected for REST-heavy application # - JobPost CRUD operations # - Skills management endpoints # # Admin rule (17%): # - Django admin activity # # Consider: Lower API rate if cost is concern

get-sampling-targets

Retrieves sampling targets for the X-Ray daemon. Targets provide real-time sampling decisions for your Django application.

Syntax
aws xray get-sampling-targets \
    --sampling-statistics-documents <value>
Parameters Description
  • --sampling-statistics-documents (list, required): Statistics from the X-Ray daemon including RuleName, ClientID, Timestamp, RequestCount, SampledCount, and BorrowCount
Return Value
  • SamplingTargetDocuments (array): Updated sampling targets with RuleName, FixedRate, ReservoirQuota, and Interval
  • LastRuleModification (timestamp): When rules were last modified
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Get sampling targets

Request updated sampling targets for Django application tracing.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-sampling-targets \
    --sampling-statistics-documents '[
        {
            "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling",
            "ClientID": "client-12345",
            "Timestamp": 1737187200,
            "RequestCount": 1000,
            "SampledCount": 100,
            "BorrowCount": 5
        }
    ]'
Output: { "SamplingTargetDocuments": [ { "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling", "FixedRate": 0.10, "ReservoirQuota": 5, "ReservoirQuotaTTL": "2025-01-18T10:05:00Z", "Interval": 10 } ], "LastRuleModification": "2025-01-17T12:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Sampling targets for X-Ray daemon: # - 10% fixed rate sampling # - 5 requests/second reservoir quota # - Poll every 10 seconds for updates # Applied to JobPost, Skills API endpoints
Example 2: Request targets for multiple rules

Get updated sampling targets for all Django application rules.

#!/bin/bash
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%s)

aws xray get-sampling-targets \
    --sampling-statistics-documents '[
        {
            "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling",
            "ClientID": "django-app-1",
            "Timestamp": '"$TIMESTAMP"',
            "RequestCount": 1000,
            "SampledCount": 100,
            "BorrowCount": 5
        },
        {
            "RuleName": "anihpj-error-sampling",
            "ClientID": "django-app-1",
            "Timestamp": '"$TIMESTAMP"',
            "RequestCount": 50,
            "SampledCount": 50,
            "BorrowCount": 0
        },
        {
            "RuleName": "anihpj-admin-sampling",
            "ClientID": "django-app-1",
            "Timestamp": '"$TIMESTAMP"',
            "RequestCount": 200,
            "SampledCount": 100,
            "BorrowCount": 0
        }
    ]' | jq '.'
Output: { "SamplingTargetDocuments": [ { "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling", "FixedRate": 0.10, "ReservoirQuota": 5, "ReservoirQuotaTTL": "2025-01-18T14:05:00Z", "Interval": 10 }, { "RuleName": "anihpj-error-sampling", "FixedRate": 1.0, "ReservoirQuota": 10, "ReservoirQuotaTTL": "2025-01-18T14:05:00Z", "Interval": 10 }, { "RuleName": "anihpj-admin-sampling", "FixedRate": 0.50, "ReservoirQuota": 2, "ReservoirQuotaTTL": "2025-01-18T14:05:00Z", "Interval": 10 } ], "LastRuleModification": "2025-01-17T12:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Updated targets for anihpj daemons: # # API endpoints: 10% + 5/sec reservoir # - JobPost.objects queries # - Skills API calls # # Error sampling: 100% + 10/sec # - All failures captured # # Admin: 50% + 2/sec # - Django admin monitoring
Example 3: Implement daemon-style polling

Continuously poll for updated sampling targets like the X-Ray daemon.

#!/bin/bash
CLIENT_ID="django-app-$(hostname)"
RULE_NAME="anihpj-api-sampling"

# Track local statistics
REQUEST_COUNT=0
SAMPLED_COUNT=0
BORROW_COUNT=0

# Current targets
FIXED_RATE=0.10
RESERVOIR_QUOTA=5
POLL_INTERVAL=10

while true; do
    TIMESTAMP=$(date +%s)
    
    # Request new targets with current statistics
    RESPONSE=$(aws xray get-sampling-targets \
        --sampling-statistics-documents '[{
            "RuleName": "'"$RULE_NAME"'",
            "ClientID": "'"$CLIENT_ID"'",
            "Timestamp": '"$TIMESTAMP"',
            "RequestCount": '"$REQUEST_COUNT"',
            "SampledCount": '"$SAMPLED_COUNT"',
            "BorrowCount": '"$BORROW_COUNT"'
        }]' 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        # Update local targets
        NEW_RATE=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.SamplingTargetDocuments[0].FixedRate')
        NEW_QUOTA=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.SamplingTargetDocuments[0].ReservoirQuota')
        NEW_INTERVAL=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.SamplingTargetDocuments[0].Interval')
        
        if [ "$NEW_RATE" != "null" ]; then
            FIXED_RATE=$NEW_RATE
            RESERVOIR_QUOTA=$NEW_QUOTA
            POLL_INTERVAL=$NEW_INTERVAL
        fi
        
        echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] Rate: $FIXED_RATE, Quota: $RESERVOIR_QUOTA, Next poll: ${POLL_INTERVAL}s"
    fi
    
    # Reset counters after reporting
    REQUEST_COUNT=0
    SAMPLED_COUNT=0
    BORROW_COUNT=0
    
    sleep $POLL_INTERVAL
done
Output: [14:00:00] Rate: 0.10, Quota: 5, Next poll: 10s [14:00:10] Rate: 0.10, Quota: 5, Next poll: 10s [14:00:20] Rate: 0.12, Quota: 6, Next poll: 10s [14:00:30] Rate: 0.10, Quota: 5, Next poll: 10s
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Daemon polling for anihpj: # # Reports statistics for: # - JobPost.objects.filter() calls # - Skills.objects.all() queries # - Author/Location lookups # # X-Ray adjusts targets based on: # - Traffic volume # - Reservoir consumption # - Global sampling state
Example 4: Monitor target changes

Track when sampling targets are modified by X-Ray.

#!/bin/bash
LAST_MODIFICATION=""
TARGETS_FILE="sampling-targets.json"

echo "Monitoring sampling target changes..."

while true; do
    RESPONSE=$(aws xray get-sampling-targets \
        --sampling-statistics-documents '[{
            "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling",
            "ClientID": "monitor",
            "Timestamp": '"$(date +%s)"',
            "RequestCount": 0,
            "SampledCount": 0,
            "BorrowCount": 0
        }]' 2>/dev/null)
    
    CURRENT_MOD=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.LastRuleModification')
    
    if [ "$CURRENT_MOD" != "$LAST_MODIFICATION" ] && [ -n "$LAST_MODIFICATION" ]; then
        echo ""
        echo "⚠️  SAMPLING RULES CHANGED!"
        echo "   Previous: $LAST_MODIFICATION"
        echo "   Current: $CURRENT_MOD"
        echo ""
        echo "New targets:"
        echo "$RESPONSE" | jq '.SamplingTargetDocuments[]'
        
        # Save snapshot
        echo "$RESPONSE" > "$TARGETS_FILE"
    fi
    
    LAST_MODIFICATION=$CURRENT_MOD
    echo -n "."
    
    sleep 30
done
Output: Monitoring sampling target changes... ........ ⚠️ SAMPLING RULES CHANGED! Previous: 2025-01-17T12:00:00Z Current: 2025-01-18T14:30:00Z New targets: { "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling", "FixedRate": 0.15, "ReservoirQuota": 8, "ReservoirQuotaTTL": "2025-01-18T14:35:00Z", "Interval": 10 } ........
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Target change detected for anihpj: # # Old: 10% rate, 5/sec reservoir # New: 15% rate, 8/sec reservoir # # Possible reasons: # - Admin updated sampling rule # - Auto-scaling increased capacity # # Affects tracing of: # - JobPost API operations # - Skills CRUD endpoints
Example 5: Validate target configuration

Verify sampling targets are correctly applied across instances.

#!/bin/bash
INSTANCES=("django-app-1" "django-app-2" "django-app-3")

echo "Sampling Target Validation"
echo "=========================="
echo ""

for CLIENT in "${INSTANCES[@]}"; do
    echo "Instance: $CLIENT"
    
    RESPONSE=$(aws xray get-sampling-targets \
        --sampling-statistics-documents '[{
            "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling",
            "ClientID": "'"$CLIENT"'",
            "Timestamp": '"$(date +%s)"',
            "RequestCount": 1000,
            "SampledCount": 100,
            "BorrowCount": 5
        }]' 2>/dev/null)
    
    echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '
        .SamplingTargetDocuments[] |
        "  Rule: \(.RuleName)",
        "  Rate: \(.FixedRate * 100)%",
        "  Reservoir: \(.ReservoirQuota)/sec",
        "  TTL: \(.ReservoirQuotaTTL)",
        ""'
done

echo "Validation complete."
echo "All instances should have identical targets for consistent sampling."
Output: Sampling Target Validation ========================== Instance: django-app-1 Rule: anihpj-api-sampling Rate: 10% Reservoir: 5/sec TTL: 2025-01-18T14:05:00Z Instance: django-app-2 Rule: anihpj-api-sampling Rate: 10% Reservoir: 5/sec TTL: 2025-01-18T14:05:00Z Instance: django-app-3 Rule: anihpj-api-sampling Rate: 10% Reservoir: 5/sec TTL: 2025-01-18T14:05:00Z Validation complete. All instances should have identical targets for consistent sampling.
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Target validation for anihpj cluster: # # All 3 instances have consistent targets: # - 10% fixed rate # - 5/sec reservoir each # - Total reservoir: 15/sec # # Ensures uniform tracing of: # - JobPost.objects across all instances # - Skills.objects load-balanced queries # - Consistent error capture

get-time-series-service-statistics

Retrieves time series statistics for services over a specified time range. Use this to analyze trends in your Django application performance.

Syntax
aws xray get-time-series-service-statistics \
    --start-time <value> \
    --end-time <value> \
    [--entity-selector-expression <value>] \
    [--period <value>] \
    [--forecast-statistics]
Parameters Description
  • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start of the time range
  • --end-time (timestamp, required): End of the time range
  • --entity-selector-expression (string): Filter expression for services
  • --period (integer): Aggregation period in seconds (60, 300, or 3600)
  • --forecast-statistics (boolean): Include forecasted statistics
Return Value
  • TimeSeriesServiceStatistics (array): Time series data with Timestamp, EdgeSummaryStatistics, ServiceSummaryStatistics, and ResponseTimeHistogram
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Get hourly performance metrics

Analyze Django application performance trends over the past 6 hours.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-time-series-service-statistics \
    --start-time $(date -d '6 hours ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --period 3600
Output: { "TimeSeriesServiceStatistics": [ { "Timestamp": "2025-01-18T04:00:00Z", "ServiceSummaryStatistics": { "TotalCount": 5000, "FaultStatistics": {"TotalCount": 50}, "TotalResponseTime": 2500.0 } }, { "Timestamp": "2025-01-18T05:00:00Z", "ServiceSummaryStatistics": { "TotalCount": 8000, "FaultStatistics": {"TotalCount": 80}, "TotalResponseTime": 4000.0 } } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Hourly performance trends: # 04:00 - 5000 requests, 50 faults (1%), 0.5s avg # 05:00 - 8000 requests, 80 faults (1%), 0.5s avg # # Consistent 1% error rate across hours # Operations: JobPost, Skills, Author queries
Example 2: Analyze service-specific performance

Filter time series data for a specific Django service.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-time-series-service-statistics \
    --start-time $(date -d '24 hours ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --entity-selector-expression 'service(name = "anihpj-django")' \
    --period 3600 | jq -r '
    "Service Performance - Last 24 Hours",
    "=====================================",
    "",
    .TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[] |
    "Time: \(.Timestamp)",
    "  Requests: \(.ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount)",
    "  Faults: \(.ServiceSummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount)",
    "  Error Rate: \((.ServiceSummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount / .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount * 100) | round)%",
    "  Avg Response: \((.ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalResponseTime / .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount * 1000) | round)ms",
    ""'
Output: Service Performance - Last 24 Hours ===================================== Time: 2025-01-17T10:00:00Z Requests: 12000 Faults: 120 Error Rate: 1% Avg Response: 450ms Time: 2025-01-17T14:00:00Z Requests: 18000 Faults: 540 Error Rate: 3% Avg Response: 850ms Time: 2025-01-17T18:00:00Z Requests: 15000 Faults: 150 Error Rate: 1% Avg Response: 420ms
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 24-hour analysis for anihpj: # # Spike at 14:00: 3% error rate, 850ms response # - Likely peak traffic period # - JobPost queries affected # # Normal hours: 1% error rate, ~450ms # - Standard CRUD operations # - Skills lookups performing well
Example 3: Generate performance comparison report

Compare current performance to historical baseline.

#!/bin/bash
# Get current period stats
CURRENT=$(aws xray get-time-series-service-statistics \
    --start-time $(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --period 3600)

# Get same hour last week for baseline
BASELINE=$(aws xray get-time-series-service-statistics \
    --start-time $(date -d '1 week ago - 1 hour' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d '1 week ago' +%s) \
    --period 3600)

# Extract metrics
CURR_REQ=$(echo "$CURRENT" | jq '[.TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[].ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount] | add')
CURR_ERR=$(echo "$CURRENT" | jq '[.TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[].ServiceSummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount] | add')
CURR_RT=$(echo "$CURRENT" | jq '[.TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[] | .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalResponseTime / .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount] | add / length * 1000')

BASE_REQ=$(echo "$BASELINE" | jq '[.TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[].ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount] | add // 1')
BASE_ERR=$(echo "$BASELINE" | jq '[.TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[].ServiceSummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount] | add // 0')
BASE_RT=$(echo "$BASELINE" | jq '[.TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[] | .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalResponseTime / .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount] | add / length * 1000 // 0')

echo "Performance Comparison Report"
echo "============================="
echo ""
echo "                    Current    Baseline    Change"
echo "Requests:           $CURR_REQ        $BASE_REQ        $(echo "scale=0; ($CURR_REQ - $BASE_REQ) * 100 / $BASE_REQ" | bc)%"
echo "Faults:             $CURR_ERR        $BASE_ERR"
echo "Avg Response (ms):  ${CURR_RT%.*}        ${BASE_RT%.*}"
Output: Performance Comparison Report ============================= Current Baseline Change Requests: 15000 12000 25% Faults: 150 120 Avg Response (ms): 480 450
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Week-over-week comparison: # # Traffic: +25% (growth expected) # Errors: Proportional increase (1% stable) # Response time: +30ms (within tolerance) # # JobPost operations scaling well # Skills queries maintaining performance
Example 4: Export time series for visualization

Generate CSV data for external dashboards.

#!/bin/bash
OUTPUT_FILE="xray-timeseries-$(date +%Y%m%d).csv"

# Header
echo "timestamp,requests,faults,error_rate,avg_response_ms" > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

# Get 7 days of hourly data
aws xray get-time-series-service-statistics \
    --start-time $(date -d '7 days ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --period 3600 | jq -r '
    .TimeSeriesServiceStatistics[] |
    [
        .Timestamp,
        .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount,
        .ServiceSummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount,
        ((.ServiceSummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount / .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount * 100) | round),
        ((.ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalResponseTime / .ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount * 1000) | round)
    ] | @csv' >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Exported $(wc -l < "$OUTPUT_FILE") rows to $OUTPUT_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Sample data:"
head -5 "$OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Exported 169 rows to xray-timeseries-20250118.csv Sample data: timestamp,requests,faults,error_rate,avg_response_ms "2025-01-11T10:00:00Z",12000,120,1,450 "2025-01-11T11:00:00Z",14000,140,1,460 "2025-01-11T12:00:00Z",18000,360,2,520 "2025-01-11T13:00:00Z",20000,400,2,580
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 7-day export for anihpj visualization: # - 168 hourly data points # - Import to Grafana/Tableau # # Metrics captured: # - Total JobPost/Skills/Author requests # - Error counts by hour # - Response time trends
Example 5: Forecast future performance

Use forecasting to predict Django application behavior.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-time-series-service-statistics \
    --start-time $(date -d '24 hours ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --period 3600 \
    --forecast-statistics | jq -r '
    "Performance Forecast Analysis",
    "==============================",
    "",
    "Historical Data (last 24h):",
    (.TimeSeriesServiceStatistics | last(3)[] |
        "  \(.Timestamp | split("T")[1] | split("Z")[0]): \(.ServiceSummaryStatistics.TotalCount) requests, \(.ServiceSummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount) faults"
    ),
    "",
    "Forecasted (if available):",
    if .ForecastedStatistics then
        (.ForecastedStatistics[] |
            "  \(.Timestamp | split("T")[1] | split("Z")[0]): \(.PredictedCount) requests (predicted)"
        )
    else
        "  Forecasting requires more historical data"
    end'
Output: Performance Forecast Analysis ============================== Historical Data (last 24h): 12:00:00: 18000 requests, 180 faults 13:00:00: 19000 requests, 190 faults 14:00:00: 17000 requests, 170 faults Forecasted (if available): 15:00:00: 16000 requests (predicted) 16:00:00: 14000 requests (predicted) 17:00:00: 12000 requests (predicted)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Forecast for anihpj: # # Predicted traffic decline (evening hours): # - JobPost creation slowing # - Skills queries decreasing # # Use for capacity planning: # - Scale down instances after 17:00 # - Batch jobs can run during low traffic

get-trace-graph

Retrieves a service graph for one or more specific traces. Use this to visualize the service dependencies for a particular request through your Django application.

Syntax
aws xray get-trace-graph \
    --trace-ids <value> \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --trace-ids (list, required): One or more trace IDs to retrieve graphs for
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token
Return Value
  • Services (array): Service graph nodes with Name, Type, ResponseTimeHistogram, and Edges
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Get service graph for specific trace

Visualize all services involved in a specific Django request.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-trace-graph \
    --trace-ids "1-5f4b3c2a-abc123def456789012345678"
Output: { "Services": [ { "Name": "anihpj-django", "Type": "AWS::EC2::Instance", "ReferenceId": 0, "ResponseTimeHistogram": [ {"Value": 0.5, "Count": 1} ], "Edges": [ {"ReferenceId": 1}, {"ReferenceId": 2}, {"ReferenceId": 3} ] }, { "ReferenceId": 1, "Name": "PostgreSQL", "Type": "AWS::RDS::DBInstance" }, { "ReferenceId": 2, "Name": "Redis", "Type": "AWS::ElastiCache::Cluster" }, { "ReferenceId": 3, "Name": "S3", "Type": "AWS::S3::Bucket" } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Trace graph for single request: # Django App # ├── PostgreSQL (JobPost.objects.get()) # ├── Redis (cache.get('skills_list')) # └── S3 (static file serving) # # Total response time: 0.5s
Example 2: Compare trace graphs for multiple requests

Analyze service patterns across different Django request types.

#!/bin/bash
# Get graphs for a fast and slow trace
FAST_TRACE="1-5f4b3c2a-fast123"
SLOW_TRACE="1-5f4b3c2a-slow456"

echo "Comparing trace graphs..."
echo ""

for TRACE_ID in "$FAST_TRACE" "$SLOW_TRACE"; do
    echo "Trace: $TRACE_ID"
    echo "-------------------"
    
    aws xray get-trace-graph \
        --trace-ids "$TRACE_ID" | jq -r '
        "Services involved: \(.Services | length)",
        (.Services[] | 
            "  \(.Name) (\(.Type))" +
            if .ResponseTimeHistogram then
                " - \((.ResponseTimeHistogram | map(.Value * .Count) | add / (.ResponseTimeHistogram | map(.Count) | add) * 1000) | round)ms avg"
            else
                ""
            end
        ),
        ""'
done
Output: Comparing trace graphs... Trace: 1-5f4b3c2a-fast123 ------------------- Services involved: 3 anihpj-django (AWS::EC2::Instance) - 150ms avg PostgreSQL (AWS::RDS::DBInstance) - 50ms avg Redis (AWS::ElastiCache::Cluster) - 5ms avg Trace: 1-5f4b3c2a-slow456 ------------------- Services involved: 5 anihpj-django (AWS::EC2::Instance) - 2500ms avg PostgreSQL (AWS::RDS::DBInstance) - 1500ms avg Redis (AWS::ElastiCache::Cluster) - 5ms avg External-API (HTTP) - 800ms avg S3 (AWS::S3::Bucket) - 200ms avg
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Trace comparison: # # Fast trace (150ms): # - Simple JobPost.objects.get() # - Redis cache hit for Skills # # Slow trace (2500ms): # - Complex JobPost query with joins # - Cache miss, database query # - External Location API call # - S3 file upload
Example 3: Identify critical path in request

Find the slowest service in a trace graph.

#!/bin/bash
TRACE_ID="1-5f4b3c2a-abc123def456"

aws xray get-trace-graph \
    --trace-ids "$TRACE_ID" | jq -r '
    "Critical Path Analysis",
    "======================",
    "",
    # Find slowest service
    (.Services | 
        map(select(.ResponseTimeHistogram)) |
        sort_by(-(.ResponseTimeHistogram | map(.Value * .Count) | add / (map(.Count) | add))) |
        first
    ) as $slowest |
    
    "🔴 Bottleneck: \($slowest.Name)",
    "   Type: \($slowest.Type)",
    "   Response time: \(($slowest.ResponseTimeHistogram | map(.Value * .Count) | add / (map(.Count) | add) * 1000) | round)ms",
    "",
    "All services (sorted by latency):",
    (.Services | 
        map(select(.ResponseTimeHistogram)) |
        sort_by(-(.ResponseTimeHistogram | map(.Value * .Count) | add / (map(.Count) | add))) |
        .[] |
        "  \(.Name): \((.ResponseTimeHistogram | map(.Value * .Count) | add / (map(.Count) | add) * 1000) | round)ms"
    )'
Output: Critical Path Analysis ====================== 🔴 Bottleneck: PostgreSQL Type: AWS::RDS::DBInstance Response time: 1200ms All services (sorted by latency): PostgreSQL: 1200ms anihpj-django: 450ms External-API: 150ms Redis: 8ms S3: 5ms
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Critical path: PostgreSQL (1200ms) # # Bottleneck analysis: # - JobPost.objects.filter() with complex joins # - Skills.objects.prefetch_related() slow # # Recommendations: # - Add database indexes # - Use select_related() for Author # - Cache frequently accessed Skills
Example 4: Export trace graph for documentation

Generate a visual representation of service dependencies.

#!/bin/bash
TRACE_ID="1-5f4b3c2a-abc123"
OUTPUT_FILE="trace-graph-$TRACE_ID.json"

aws xray get-trace-graph \
    --trace-ids "$TRACE_ID" | jq '{
    traceId: "'"$TRACE_ID"'",
    exportTime: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    nodes: [.Services[] | {
        id: .ReferenceId,
        name: .Name,
        type: .Type,
        avgResponseMs: (if .ResponseTimeHistogram then
            (.ResponseTimeHistogram | map(.Value * .Count) | add / (map(.Count) | add) * 1000) | round
        else null end),
        dependencies: [.Edges[]?.ReferenceId]
    }],
    summary: {
        serviceCount: (.Services | length),
        hasDatabase: ([.Services[].Type] | any(. == "AWS::RDS::DBInstance")),
        hasCache: ([.Services[].Type] | any(. == "AWS::ElastiCache::Cluster")),
        hasS3: ([.Services[].Type] | any(. == "AWS::S3::Bucket"))
    }
}' > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Trace graph exported to: $OUTPUT_FILE"
jq '.summary' "$OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Trace graph exported to: trace-graph-1-5f4b3c2a-abc123.json { "serviceCount": 4, "hasDatabase": true, "hasCache": true, "hasS3": true }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Exported trace architecture: # - 4 services in request path # - PostgreSQL: JobPost, Skills storage # - Redis: Session and cache layer # - S3: Static files/uploads # # Use for documentation and reviews
Example 5: Aggregate graphs from multiple traces

Build a composite view from several request traces.

#!/bin/bash
# Get trace IDs from recent errors
TRACE_IDS=$(aws xray get-trace-summaries \
    --start-time $(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date +%s) \
    --filter-expression 'fault = true' \
    --query 'TraceSummaries[*].Id' \
    --output text | head -5)

if [ -z "$TRACE_IDS" ]; then
    echo "No error traces found"
    exit 0
fi

echo "Aggregating graphs from $(echo "$TRACE_IDS" | wc -w) error traces..."
echo ""

# Get graphs for all traces
aws xray get-trace-graph \
    --trace-ids $TRACE_IDS | jq -r '
    "Aggregated Error Trace Analysis",
    "================================",
    "",
    "Services appearing in errors:",
    ([.Services[].Name] | unique | sort | .[] | "  - \(.)"),
    "",
    "Service error frequency:",
    (
        [.Services[] | select(.SummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount > 0)] |
        group_by(.Name) |
        map({name: .[0].Name, faults: (map(.SummaryStatistics.FaultStatistics.TotalCount) | add)}) |
        sort_by(-.faults) |
        .[] | "  \(.name): \(.faults) faults"
    )'
Output: Aggregating graphs from 5 error traces... Aggregated Error Trace Analysis ================================ Services appearing in errors: - External-API - PostgreSQL - Redis - anihpj-django Service error frequency: PostgreSQL: 4 faults External-API: 2 faults anihpj-django: 1 fault
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Error pattern analysis: # # PostgreSQL (4 faults): # - JobPost.objects constraint violations # - Skills M2M relationship failures # # External-API (2 faults): # - Location geocoding timeouts # # Django app (1 fault): # - Request validation error

get-trace-segment-destination

Retrieves the destination configuration for trace segments. This shows where X-Ray trace segments from your Django application are being stored.

Syntax
aws xray get-trace-segment-destination
Parameters Description
  • No parameters required.
Return Value
  • Destination (string): The current destination for trace segments (XRay or CloudWatchLogs)
  • Status (string): Status of the destination configuration (PENDING, ACTIVE)
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Check trace destination

Verify where Django application traces are being stored.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray get-trace-segment-destination
Output: { "Destination": "XRay", "Status": "ACTIVE" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Trace storage destination: X-Ray # All JobPost, Skills, Author traces stored in X-Ray # Status: ACTIVE - currently receiving traces
Example 2: Verify destination with status check

Check the current destination and handle pending changes.

#!/bin/bash
RESULT=$(aws xray get-trace-segment-destination)

DESTINATION=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Destination')
STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Status')

echo "Trace Segment Destination Status"
echo "================================="
echo ""
echo "Destination: $DESTINATION"
echo "Status: $STATUS"
echo ""

case "$STATUS" in
    "ACTIVE")
        echo "✓ Destination is active and receiving traces"
        ;;
    "PENDING")
        echo "⏳ Destination change is pending..."
        echo "   This may take a few minutes to complete"
        ;;
    *)
        echo "⚠️  Unknown status: $STATUS"
        ;;
esac

case "$DESTINATION" in
    "XRay")
        echo ""
        echo "Traces stored in: AWS X-Ray service"
        echo "Retention: Standard X-Ray retention (30 days)"
        ;;
    "CloudWatchLogs")
        echo ""
        echo "Traces stored in: CloudWatch Logs"
        echo "Retention: Configurable via log group settings"
        ;;
esac
Output: Trace Segment Destination Status ================================= Destination: XRay Status: ACTIVE ✓ Destination is active and receiving traces Traces stored in: AWS X-Ray service Retention: Standard X-Ray retention (30 days)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # anihpj trace storage: # - Destination: X-Ray # - Status: ACTIVE # # All traces captured: # - JobPost.objects operations # - Skills.objects queries # - Author/Location lookups # # 30-day retention for debugging
Example 3: Monitor destination during migration

Poll destination status during a destination change.

#!/bin/bash
echo "Monitoring trace destination status..."
echo "Press Ctrl+C to stop"
echo ""

LAST_STATUS=""

while true; do
    RESULT=$(aws xray get-trace-segment-destination 2>/dev/null)
    
    DESTINATION=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Destination')
    STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Status')
    
    if [ "$STATUS" != "$LAST_STATUS" ]; then
        TIMESTAMP=$(date +%H:%M:%S)
        echo "[$TIMESTAMP] Destination: $DESTINATION, Status: $STATUS"
        
        if [ "$STATUS" == "ACTIVE" ] && [ "$LAST_STATUS" == "PENDING" ]; then
            echo "[$TIMESTAMP] ✓ Destination change completed!"
        fi
        
        LAST_STATUS=$STATUS
    fi
    
    sleep 10
done
Output: Monitoring trace destination status... Press Ctrl+C to stop [14:00:00] Destination: XRay, Status: ACTIVE [14:01:30] Destination: CloudWatchLogs, Status: PENDING [14:02:40] Destination: CloudWatchLogs, Status: PENDING [14:03:50] Destination: CloudWatchLogs, Status: ACTIVE [14:03:50] ✓ Destination change completed!
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Destination migration tracking: # # From: X-Ray (30-day retention) # To: CloudWatch Logs (custom retention) # # Migration duration: ~3-4 minutes # No trace data loss during transition # # Django traces affected: # - JobPost, Skills, Author operations
Example 4: Include destination in health check

Add trace destination to application health monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
HEALTH_FILE="xray-health-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M).json"

# Get trace destination
DEST_INFO=$(aws xray get-trace-segment-destination)

# Get encryption config
ENCRYPT_INFO=$(aws xray get-encryption-config)

# Get sampling rules count
RULES_COUNT=$(aws xray get-sampling-rules | jq '.SamplingRuleRecords | length')

# Generate health report
jq -n \
    --argjson dest "$DEST_INFO" \
    --argjson encrypt "$ENCRYPT_INFO" \
    --arg rules "$RULES_COUNT" \
    --arg timestamp "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" \
    '{
        timestamp: $timestamp,
        traceDestination: {
            destination: $dest.Destination,
            status: $dest.Status,
            healthy: ($dest.Status == "ACTIVE")
        },
        encryption: {
            keyId: $encrypt.EncryptionConfig.KeyId,
            type: $encrypt.EncryptionConfig.Type,
            status: $encrypt.EncryptionConfig.Status
        },
        samplingRules: ($rules | tonumber),
        overall: ($dest.Status == "ACTIVE" and $encrypt.EncryptionConfig.Status == "ACTIVE")
    }' > "$HEALTH_FILE"

echo "X-Ray Health Check"
echo "=================="
jq '.' "$HEALTH_FILE"
Output: X-Ray Health Check ================== { "timestamp": "2025-01-18T14:00:00Z", "traceDestination": { "destination": "XRay", "status": "ACTIVE", "healthy": true }, "encryption": { "keyId": "alias/aws/xray", "type": "KMS", "status": "ACTIVE" }, "samplingRules": 4, "overall": true }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # X-Ray health for anihpj: # # ✓ Trace destination: ACTIVE # ✓ Encryption: KMS enabled # ✓ 4 sampling rules configured # # All Django traces protected: # - JobPost operations encrypted # - Skills queries secured # - Author/Location data safe
Example 5: Compare destination costs

Analyze cost implications of different trace destinations.

#!/bin/bash
# Get current destination
CURRENT=$(aws xray get-trace-segment-destination | jq -r '.Destination')

# Get sampling statistics for volume estimate
STATS=$(aws xray get-sampling-statistic-summaries)
HOURLY_TRACES=$(echo "$STATS" | jq '[.SamplingStatisticSummaries[].SampledCount] | add')

# Monthly projections
MONTHLY_TRACES=$((HOURLY_TRACES * 24 * 30))
MONTHLY_GB=$(echo "scale=2; $MONTHLY_TRACES * 0.001 / 1000" | bc)  # ~1KB per trace

echo "Trace Destination Cost Analysis"
echo "================================"
echo ""
echo "Current destination: $CURRENT"
echo "Hourly trace volume: $HOURLY_TRACES"
echo "Monthly projection: $MONTHLY_TRACES traces (~${MONTHLY_GB}GB)"
echo ""
echo "Cost Comparison:"
echo ""
echo "X-Ray:"
echo "  Traces stored: \$5.00 per million"
echo "  Monthly est: \$(echo "scale=2; $MONTHLY_TRACES / 1000000 * 5" | bc)"
echo ""
echo "CloudWatch Logs:"
echo "  Ingestion: \$0.50 per GB"
echo "  Storage: \$0.03 per GB/month"
echo "  Monthly est: \$(echo "scale=2; $MONTHLY_GB * 0.53" | bc)"
echo ""
echo "Recommendation:"
if [ "$MONTHLY_TRACES" -gt 10000000 ]; then
    echo "  Consider CloudWatch Logs for high-volume cost savings"
else
    echo "  X-Ray recommended for standard tracing features"
fi
Output: Trace Destination Cost Analysis ================================ Current destination: XRay Hourly trace volume: 1500 Monthly projection: 1080000 traces (~1.08GB) Cost Comparison: X-Ray: Traces stored: $5.00 per million Monthly est: $5.40 CloudWatch Logs: Ingestion: $0.50 per GB Storage: $0.03 per GB/month Monthly est: $0.57 Recommendation: X-Ray recommended for standard tracing features
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cost analysis for anihpj: # # Current volume: ~1M traces/month # - JobPost operations: ~40% # - Skills queries: ~30% # - Other (Author, Location): ~30% # # X-Ray ($5.40/mo) recommended: # - Better trace analysis UI # - Service map visualization # - Insight anomaly detection

list-resource-policies

Lists all resource policies attached to your X-Ray resources. Resource policies control cross-account access to trace data.

Syntax
aws xray list-resource-policies \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token for additional results
Return Value
  • ResourcePolicies (array): List of policies with PolicyName, PolicyDocument, PolicyRevisionId, and LastUpdatedTime
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: List all resource policies

View cross-account access policies for Django application traces.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray list-resource-policies
Output: { "ResourcePolicies": [ { "PolicyName": "anihpj-cross-account-access", "PolicyDocument": "{\"Version\":\"2012-10-17\",\"Statement\":[{\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"AWS\":\"arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root\"},\"Action\":[\"xray:GetTraceSummaries\",\"xray:BatchGetTraces\"],\"Resource\":\"*\"}]}", "PolicyRevisionId": "rev-123", "LastUpdatedTime": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z" } ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cross-account access policy allows: # - External monitoring team to view traces # - Read access to JobPost, Skills operation traces # - No write/delete permissions
Example 2: Parse and display policy details

Extract and format resource policy permissions.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray list-resource-policies | jq -r '
    .ResourcePolicies[] |
    "Policy: \(.PolicyName)",
    "Last Updated: \(.LastUpdatedTime)",
    "Revision: \(.PolicyRevisionId)",
    "",
    "Permissions:",
    (.PolicyDocument | fromjson | .Statement[] |
        "  Principal: \(.Principal.AWS // .Principal)",
        "  Actions: \(.Action | if type == "array" then join(", ") else . end)",
        "  Effect: \(.Effect)",
        ""
    )'
Output: Policy: anihpj-cross-account-access Last Updated: 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z Revision: rev-123 Permissions: Principal: arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root Actions: xray:GetTraceSummaries, xray:BatchGetTraces Effect: Allow Policy: anihpj-monitoring-team Last Updated: 2025-01-10T14:30:00Z Revision: rev-456 Permissions: Principal: arn:aws:iam::111222333444:role/MonitoringRole Actions: xray:GetServiceGraph, xray:GetTraceSummaries Effect: Allow
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Resource policies for anihpj: # # Cross-account access: # - External ops team can read traces # - JobPost query traces visible # - Skills operation traces shared # # Monitoring team: # - Service graph access # - Read-only trace summaries
Example 3: Audit resource policy compliance

Check policies against security requirements.

#!/bin/bash
echo "X-Ray Resource Policy Audit"
echo "==========================="
echo ""

POLICIES=$(aws xray list-resource-policies)
POLICY_COUNT=$(echo "$POLICIES" | jq '.ResourcePolicies | length')

echo "Total policies: $POLICY_COUNT"
echo ""

# Check each policy for security issues
echo "$POLICIES" | jq -r '
    .ResourcePolicies[] |
    .PolicyName as $name |
    (.PolicyDocument | fromjson) as $doc |
    
    "Policy: \($name)",
    
    # Check for wildcards in principals
    (if ($doc.Statement[] | select(.Principal == "*")) then
        "  ⚠️  WARNING: Contains wildcard (*) principal"
    else
        "  ✓ No wildcard principals"
    end),
    
    # Check for overly permissive actions
    (if ($doc.Statement[] | select(.Action == "*" or .Action == "xray:*")) then
        "  ⚠️  WARNING: Contains wildcard actions"
    else
        "  ✓ Actions are scoped"
    end),
    
    # Check for conditions
    (if ($doc.Statement[] | select(.Condition)) then
        "  ✓ Has conditions for access control"
    else
        "  ℹ️  Consider adding conditions for stricter access"
    end),
    ""'
Output: X-Ray Resource Policy Audit =========================== Total policies: 2 Policy: anihpj-cross-account-access ✓ No wildcard principals ✓ Actions are scoped ℹ️ Consider adding conditions for stricter access Policy: anihpj-monitoring-team ✓ No wildcard principals ✓ Actions are scoped ✓ Has conditions for access control
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy audit for anihpj traces: # # Both policies pass basic security checks: # - No wildcard principals # - Scoped actions (read-only) # # Recommendation: # Add IP/VPC conditions to cross-account policy # for JobPost trace access control
Example 4: Export policies for backup

Save all resource policies to a backup file.

#!/bin/bash
BACKUP_FILE="xray-resource-policies-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

aws xray list-resource-policies | jq '{
    exportDate: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    accountId: env.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID,
    policies: [.ResourcePolicies[] | {
        policyName: .PolicyName,
        policyRevisionId: .PolicyRevisionId,
        lastUpdated: .LastUpdatedTime,
        policyDocument: (.PolicyDocument | fromjson)
    }]
}' > "$BACKUP_FILE"

echo "Resource policies backed up to: $BACKUP_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Policies exported:"
jq -r '.policies[].policyName' "$BACKUP_FILE"
echo ""
echo "To restore:"
echo "  aws xray put-resource-policy --policy-name  --policy-document file://policy.json"
Output: Resource policies backed up to: xray-resource-policies-20250118.json Policies exported: anihpj-cross-account-access anihpj-monitoring-team To restore: aws xray put-resource-policy --policy-name --policy-document file://policy.json
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backed up policies control access to: # - JobPost.objects trace data # - Skills.objects query traces # - Author/Location operation logs # # Restore needed after: # - Account migration # - Disaster recovery # - Policy corruption
Example 5: Generate policy documentation

Create markdown documentation of access policies.

#!/bin/bash
DOC_FILE="XRAY_ACCESS_POLICIES.md"

{
    echo "# X-Ray Resource Access Policies"
    echo ""
    echo "Generated: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
    echo ""
    echo "## Overview"
    echo ""
    
    POLICIES=$(aws xray list-resource-policies)
    POLICY_COUNT=$(echo "$POLICIES" | jq '.ResourcePolicies | length')
    
    echo "Total policies configured: $POLICY_COUNT"
    echo ""
    echo "## Policy Details"
    echo ""
    
    echo "$POLICIES" | jq -r '
        .ResourcePolicies[] |
        "### \(.PolicyName)",
        "",
        "- **Last Updated:** \(.LastUpdatedTime)",
        "- **Revision ID:** \(.PolicyRevisionId)",
        "",
        "#### Permissions",
        "",
        "| Principal | Actions | Effect |",
        "|-----------|---------|--------|",
        ((.PolicyDocument | fromjson).Statement[] |
            "| \(.Principal.AWS // .Principal) | \(.Action | if type == "array" then join(", ") else . end) | \(.Effect) |"
        ),
        ""'
    
    echo "## Security Notes"
    echo ""
    echo "- All policies grant read-only access to trace data"
    echo "- Cross-account access is restricted to specific AWS accounts"
    echo "- Review policies quarterly for compliance"
} > "$DOC_FILE"

echo "Documentation generated: $DOC_FILE"
head -40 "$DOC_FILE"
Output: Documentation generated: XRAY_ACCESS_POLICIES.md # X-Ray Resource Access Policies Generated: 2025-01-18T14:00:00Z ## Overview Total policies configured: 2 ## Policy Details ### anihpj-cross-account-access - **Last Updated:** 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z - **Revision ID:** rev-123 #### Permissions | Principal | Actions | Effect | |-----------|---------|--------| | arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root | xray:GetTraceSummaries, xray:BatchGetTraces | Allow | ### anihpj-monitoring-team - **Last Updated:** 2025-01-10T14:30:00Z - **Revision ID:** rev-456 #### Permissions | Principal | Actions | Effect | |-----------|---------|--------| | arn:aws:iam::111222333444:role/MonitoringRole | xray:GetServiceGraph, xray:GetTraceSummaries | Allow |
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy documentation for anihpj: # # Documented access to: # - JobPost trace data (read-only) # - Skills query traces (monitoring) # - Service graph visualization # # Use for: # - Security audits # - Compliance reviews # - Onboarding documentation

list-retrieved-traces

Lists traces that were retrieved using start-trace-retrieval. Use this to view archived traces from your Django application.

Syntax
aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
    --retrieval-token <value> \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --retrieval-token (string, required): Token from start-trace-retrieval
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token
Return Value
  • RetrievalStatus (string): Status of retrieval operation
  • TraceIds (array): List of retrieved trace IDs
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: List retrieved traces

View traces retrieved from archive for post-incident analysis.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
    --retrieval-token "retrieval-abc123"
Output: { "RetrievalStatus": "COMPLETE", "TraceIds": [ "1-5f4b3c2a-abc123def456789012345678", "1-5f4b3c2b-def456ghi789012345678901", "1-5f4b3c2c-ghi789jkl012345678901234" ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Retrieved 3 archived traces: # - Historical JobPost.objects queries # - Past Skills validation operations # - Useful for debugging resolved incidents
Example 2: Paginate through all retrieved traces

Iterate through large sets of retrieved traces.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-abc123"
ALL_TRACES=()
NEXT_TOKEN=""

echo "Collecting all retrieved traces..."

while true; do
    if [ -z "$NEXT_TOKEN" ]; then
        RESULT=$(aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
            --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN")
    else
        RESULT=$(aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
            --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" \
            --next-token "$NEXT_TOKEN")
    fi
    
    STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.RetrievalStatus')
    
    if [ "$STATUS" != "COMPLETE" ]; then
        echo "Retrieval not complete. Status: $STATUS"
        sleep 5
        continue
    fi
    
    TRACES=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.TraceIds[]')
    ALL_TRACES+=($TRACES)
    
    NEXT_TOKEN=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.NextToken // empty')
    
    if [ -z "$NEXT_TOKEN" ]; then
        break
    fi
    
    echo "Fetched ${#ALL_TRACES[@]} traces so far..."
done

echo "Total traces retrieved: ${#ALL_TRACES[@]}"
Output: Collecting all retrieved traces... Fetched 100 traces so far... Fetched 200 traces so far... Fetched 250 traces so far... Total traces retrieved: 250
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Archived traces from incident period: # - 250 traces retrieved # - Contains JobPost.objects operations # - Includes Skills relationship queries # - Author/Location lookups during outage
Example 3: Export retrieved trace IDs

Save trace IDs for further analysis.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-abc123"
OUTPUT_FILE="retrieved-traces-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

RESULT=$(aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN")

STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.RetrievalStatus')

if [ "$STATUS" != "COMPLETE" ]; then
    echo "Error: Retrieval status is $STATUS"
    exit 1
fi

echo "$RESULT" | jq '{
    exportDate: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    retrievalToken: "'"$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"'",
    status: .RetrievalStatus,
    traceCount: (.TraceIds | length),
    traceIds: .TraceIds
}' > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Exported to: $OUTPUT_FILE"
echo "Trace count: $(jq '.traceCount' "$OUTPUT_FILE")"
Output: Exported to: retrieved-traces-20250118.json Trace count: 50
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Exported archived traces for analysis: # - 50 traces from incident window # - Can be used with batch-get-traces # - Reference for post-mortem review # # Use: aws xray batch-get-traces --trace-ids
Example 4: Analyze retrieved traces by time

Parse trace IDs to understand time distribution.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-abc123"

aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" | jq -r '
if .RetrievalStatus != "COMPLETE" then
    "Retrieval not complete: \(.RetrievalStatus)"
else
    "Retrieved Trace Time Analysis",
    "==============================",
    "",
    "Total traces: \(.TraceIds | length)",
    "",
    "Time distribution:",
    (
        .TraceIds | 
        map(split("-")[1] | tonumber) |
        group_by(. / 3600 | floor) |
        map({
            hour: (.[0] | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:00")),
            count: length
        }) |
        sort_by(.hour) |
        .[] | "  \(.hour) UTC: \(.count) traces"
    )
end'
Output: Retrieved Trace Time Analysis ============================== Total traces: 50 Time distribution: 2025-01-15 08:00 UTC: 15 traces 2025-01-15 09:00 UTC: 25 traces 2025-01-15 10:00 UTC: 10 traces
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Time distribution shows incident impact: # - 08:00: Initial issues detected # - 09:00: Peak of incident (most traces) # - 10:00: Recovery phase # # Traces contain JobPost/Skills operations # during the outage window
Example 5: Batch process retrieved traces

Fetch full trace details for all retrieved traces.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-abc123"

# Get retrieved trace IDs
TRACE_IDS=$(aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" \
    --query 'TraceIds' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$TRACE_IDS" ]; then
    echo "No traces to process"
    exit 0
fi

# Process in batches of 5
echo "Processing retrieved traces..."
echo "$TRACE_IDS" | tr '\t' '\n' | \
    xargs -n5 | while read -r batch; do
    
    echo "Fetching batch: $(echo "$batch" | wc -w) traces"
    
    TRACES=$(aws xray batch-get-traces \
        --trace-ids $batch 2>/dev/null)
    
    # Extract error information
    echo "$TRACES" | jq -r '
        .Traces[] | 
        select(.Segments[].Document | fromjson | .fault == true) |
        "  Error in trace: \(.Id)"'
done

echo "Processing complete"
Output: Processing retrieved traces... Fetching batch: 5 traces Error in trace: 1-5f4b3c2a-abc123 Error in trace: 1-5f4b3c2a-def456 Fetching batch: 5 traces Error in trace: 1-5f4b3c2b-ghi789 Fetching batch: 5 traces Fetching batch: 5 traces Processing complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch processing found 3 error traces: # - abc123: JobPost.objects.create() failure # - def456: Skills constraint violation # - ghi789: Author lookup timeout # # Use for detailed error analysis

list-tags-for-resource

Lists tags associated with an X-Ray resource such as a group or sampling rule. Tags help organize and track costs for Django application tracing.

Syntax
aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn <value> \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string, required): The ARN of the X-Ray resource
  • --next-token (string): Pagination token
Return Value
  • Tags (array): List of key-value tag pairs
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: List tags for X-Ray group

View tags on a trace filter group for cost tracking.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-jobpost-errors"
Output: { "Tags": [ {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"}, {"Key": "Application", "Value": "anihpj"}, {"Key": "Team", "Value": "backend"}, {"Key": "CostCenter", "Value": "engineering"} ] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tags for JobPost error monitoring group: # - Environment: production # - Application: anihpj (Django project) # - Team: backend (responsible team) # - CostCenter: engineering (billing)
Example 2: List tags for sampling rule

View tags on a sampling rule for organization.

#!/bin/bash
RULE_ARN="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:sampling-rule/anihpj-api-sampling"

aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$RULE_ARN" | jq -r '
    "Tags for: '"$RULE_ARN"'",
    "==========================================",
    "",
    if .Tags | length == 0 then
        "No tags found"
    else
        (.Tags[] | "\(.Key): \(.Value)")
    end'
Output: Tags for: arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:sampling-rule/anihpj-api-sampling ========================================== Environment: production Application: anihpj Purpose: api-tracing RateType: high-volume
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tags for API sampling rule: # - Production environment # - anihpj Django application # - Used for tracing API endpoints: # * /api/jobposts/ # * /api/skills/ # * /api/users/
Example 3: Audit tags across all X-Ray groups

Check tagging compliance for all groups.

#!/bin/bash
echo "X-Ray Group Tagging Audit"
echo "========================="
echo ""

REQUIRED_TAGS=("Environment" "Application" "Team")

# Get all groups
GROUPS=$(aws xray get-groups --query 'Groups[].GroupARN' --output text)

for GROUP_ARN in $GROUPS; do
    GROUP_NAME=$(echo "$GROUP_ARN" | rev | cut -d'/' -f1 | rev)
    echo "Group: $GROUP_NAME"
    
    TAGS=$(aws xray list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN")
    
    MISSING=""
    for TAG in "${REQUIRED_TAGS[@]}"; do
        if ! echo "$TAGS" | jq -e ".Tags[] | select(.Key == \"$TAG\")" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
            MISSING="$MISSING $TAG"
        fi
    done
    
    if [ -z "$MISSING" ]; then
        echo "  ✓ All required tags present"
    else
        echo "  ⚠️  Missing tags:$MISSING"
    fi
    echo ""
done
Output: X-Ray Group Tagging Audit ========================= Group: anihpj-jobpost-errors ✓ All required tags present Group: anihpj-slow-queries ⚠️ Missing tags: Team Group: Default ⚠️ Missing tags: Environment Application Team
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tag compliance for anihpj groups: # # ✓ jobpost-errors: Fully tagged # - Tracks JobPost.objects failures # # ⚠️ slow-queries: Missing Team tag # - Tracks Skills, Author slow queries # # Action: Add missing tags for governance
Example 4: Generate tag inventory report

Create a CSV report of all X-Ray resource tags.

#!/bin/bash
REPORT_FILE="xray-tags-inventory-$(date +%Y%m%d).csv"

echo "ResourceType,ResourceName,ResourceARN,TagKey,TagValue" > "$REPORT_FILE"

# Get groups
GROUPS=$(aws xray get-groups --query 'Groups[].[GroupName,GroupARN]' --output text)

while IFS=$'\t' read -r name arn; do
    TAGS=$(aws xray list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$arn" 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ -n "$TAGS" ]; then
        echo "$TAGS" | jq -r --arg name "$name" --arg arn "$arn" '
            .Tags[] | "Group,\($name),\($arn),\(.Key),\(.Value)"' >> "$REPORT_FILE"
    fi
done <<< "$GROUPS"

# Get sampling rules
RULES=$(aws xray get-sampling-rules --query 'SamplingRuleRecords[].[SamplingRule.RuleName,SamplingRule.RuleARN]' --output text)

while IFS=$'\t' read -r name arn; do
    if [ -n "$arn" ] && [ "$arn" != "null" ]; then
