        TAGS=$(aws xray list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$arn" 2>/dev/null)
        
        if [ -n "$TAGS" ]; then
            echo "$TAGS" | jq -r --arg name "$name" --arg arn "$arn" '
                .Tags[] | "SamplingRule,\($name),\($arn),\(.Key),\(.Value)"' >> "$REPORT_FILE"
        fi
    fi
done <<< "$RULES"

echo "Report generated: $REPORT_FILE"
echo "Total entries: $(tail -n +2 "$REPORT_FILE" | wc -l)"
Output: Report generated: xray-tags-inventory-20250118.csv Total entries: 12
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tag inventory for anihpj X-Ray resources: # - 12 tag entries across groups and rules # - Import to Excel for cost allocation # - Track by Environment, Application, Team # # Useful for: # - Cost reporting # - Compliance audits
Example 5: Compare tags across environments

Verify consistent tagging between staging and production.

#!/bin/bash
PROD_GROUP="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-production"
STAGING_GROUP="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-staging"

echo "Tag Comparison: Production vs Staging"
echo "======================================"
echo ""

PROD_TAGS=$(aws xray list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$PROD_GROUP" | jq -r '.Tags | sort_by(.Key)')
STAGING_TAGS=$(aws xray list-tags-for-resource --resource-arn "$STAGING_GROUP" | jq -r '.Tags | sort_by(.Key)')

echo "Production tags:"
echo "$PROD_TAGS" | jq -r '.[] | "  \(.Key): \(.Value)"'
echo ""
echo "Staging tags:"
echo "$STAGING_TAGS" | jq -r '.[] | "  \(.Key): \(.Value)"'
echo ""

# Find differences
PROD_KEYS=$(echo "$PROD_TAGS" | jq -r '.[].Key' | sort)
STAGING_KEYS=$(echo "$STAGING_TAGS" | jq -r '.[].Key' | sort)

echo "Missing in staging: $(comm -23 <(echo "$PROD_KEYS") <(echo "$STAGING_KEYS") | tr '\n' ' ')"
echo "Extra in staging: $(comm -13 <(echo "$PROD_KEYS") <(echo "$STAGING_KEYS") | tr '\n' ' ')"
Output: Tag Comparison: Production vs Staging ====================================== Production tags: Application: anihpj CostCenter: engineering Environment: production Team: backend Staging tags: Application: anihpj Environment: staging Team: backend Missing in staging: CostCenter Extra in staging:
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tag comparison for anihpj environments: # # Consistent tags: # - Application, Team # # Differences: # - Environment (expected) # - CostCenter missing in staging # # Recommendation: Add CostCenter to staging # for complete cost tracking

put-encryption-config

Updates the encryption configuration for X-Ray trace data. Use this to enable KMS encryption for Django application traces containing sensitive data.

Syntax
aws xray put-encryption-config \
    --type <value> \
    [--key-id <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --type (string, required): Encryption type - NONE or KMS
  • --key-id (string): KMS key ARN (required if type is KMS)
Return Value
  • EncryptionConfig (object): Updated encryption configuration with KeyId, Status, and Type
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Enable KMS encryption

Encrypt Django application traces with customer managed KMS key.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray put-encryption-config \
    --type KMS \
    --key-id "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012"
Output: { "EncryptionConfig": { "KeyId": "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012", "Status": "UPDATING", "Type": "KMS" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # KMS encryption now protects: # - User authentication traces # - JobPost.objects query patterns # - Author PII in trace annotations # - Location geographic data # Compliance: GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2
Example 2: Enable encryption with alias

Use KMS key alias for easier management.

#!/bin/bash
KEY_ALIAS="alias/xray-trace-encryption"

# First, verify the key alias exists
aws kms describe-key --key-id "$KEY_ALIAS" > /dev/null 2>&1

if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "Error: KMS key alias '$KEY_ALIAS' not found"
    exit 1
fi

# Enable encryption
aws xray put-encryption-config \
    --type KMS \
    --key-id "$KEY_ALIAS" | jq '{
        message: "Encryption configuration updated",
        keyId: .EncryptionConfig.KeyId,
        type: .EncryptionConfig.Type,
        status: .EncryptionConfig.Status
    }'
Output: { "message": "Encryption configuration updated", "keyId": "alias/xray-trace-encryption", "type": "KMS", "status": "UPDATING" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # KMS key alias simplifies management: # - Rotate keys without changing code # - Easier IAM policy management # # Encrypted traces include: # - JobPost title, description, salary # - Author email, name # - Location addresses
Example 3: Disable encryption (use default)

Revert to AWS-managed encryption for development environments.

#!/bin/bash
echo "⚠️  Disabling customer-managed KMS encryption..."
echo "   Traces will use AWS-managed encryption"
echo ""

# Disable customer-managed encryption
RESULT=$(aws xray put-encryption-config --type NONE)

STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.EncryptionConfig.Status')
TYPE=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.EncryptionConfig.Type')

echo "Configuration updated:"
echo "  Type: $TYPE"
echo "  Status: $STATUS"

if [ "$TYPE" == "NONE" ]; then
    echo ""
    echo "Note: Traces are still encrypted with AWS-managed keys."
    echo "      This disables customer-managed KMS encryption only."
fi
Output: ⚠️ Disabling customer-managed KMS encryption... Traces will use AWS-managed encryption Configuration updated: Type: NONE Status: ACTIVE Note: Traces are still encrypted with AWS-managed keys. This disables customer-managed KMS encryption only.
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Development environment: # - AWS-managed encryption (sufficient) # - No KMS key costs # - Simpler key management # # Not recommended for production with: # - JobPost salary data # - Author PII # - Location sensitive info
Example 4: Monitor encryption status change

Poll encryption config until update completes.

#!/bin/bash
KEY_ID="arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/12345678-1234"

echo "Enabling KMS encryption..."

# Start the encryption update
RESULT=$(aws xray put-encryption-config \
    --type KMS \
    --key-id "$KEY_ID")

INITIAL_STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.EncryptionConfig.Status')
echo "Initial status: $INITIAL_STATUS"

# Poll until active
ATTEMPTS=0
MAX_ATTEMPTS=30

while [ $ATTEMPTS -lt $MAX_ATTEMPTS ]; do
    sleep 10
    
    CURRENT=$(aws xray get-encryption-config)
    STATUS=$(echo "$CURRENT" | jq -r '.EncryptionConfig.Status')
    
    echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] Status: $STATUS"
    
    if [ "$STATUS" == "ACTIVE" ]; then
        echo ""
        echo "✓ Encryption configuration is now active"
        echo "$CURRENT" | jq '.EncryptionConfig'
        exit 0
    fi
    
    ATTEMPTS=$((ATTEMPTS + 1))
done

echo "⚠️  Timeout waiting for encryption to activate"
exit 1
Output: Enabling KMS encryption... Initial status: UPDATING [14:00:10] Status: UPDATING [14:00:20] Status: UPDATING [14:00:30] Status: ACTIVE ✓ Encryption configuration is now active { "KeyId": "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/12345678-1234", "Status": "ACTIVE", "Type": "KMS" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Encryption activated for anihpj traces: # - All new traces encrypted with CMK # - Existing traces remain with previous encryption # # Now protected: # - JobPost.objects.create() data # - Skills relationship traces # - User authentication flows
Example 5: Rotate encryption key

Switch to a new KMS key for key rotation.

#!/bin/bash
OLD_KEY="alias/xray-encryption-2024"
NEW_KEY="alias/xray-encryption-2025"

echo "X-Ray Encryption Key Rotation"
echo "=============================="
echo ""

# Get current config
CURRENT=$(aws xray get-encryption-config)
CURRENT_KEY=$(echo "$CURRENT" | jq -r '.EncryptionConfig.KeyId')
echo "Current key: $CURRENT_KEY"

# Verify new key exists
if ! aws kms describe-key --key-id "$NEW_KEY" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "Error: New key $NEW_KEY not found"
    exit 1
fi

echo "New key: $NEW_KEY"
echo ""
read -p "Proceed with key rotation? (y/n) " -n 1 -r
echo ""

if [[ $REPLY =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    RESULT=$(aws xray put-encryption-config \
        --type KMS \
        --key-id "$NEW_KEY")
    
    echo ""
    echo "Key rotation initiated:"
    echo "$RESULT" | jq '.EncryptionConfig | {
        newKey: .KeyId,
        status: .Status,
        note: "Old traces still readable with previous key"
    }'
else
    echo "Key rotation cancelled"
fi
Output: X-Ray Encryption Key Rotation ============================== Current key: alias/xray-encryption-2024 New key: alias/xray-encryption-2025 Proceed with key rotation? (y/n) y Key rotation initiated: { "newKey": "alias/xray-encryption-2025", "status": "UPDATING", "note": "Old traces still readable with previous key" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Key rotation for anihpj traces: # # Old key (2024): Decrypts historical traces # New key (2025): Encrypts new traces # # Both keys needed until old traces expire: # - JobPost traces from 2024 # - Skills operation history # - Author audit trails # # Keep old key for 30-day retention period

put-resource-policy

Creates or updates a resource policy for X-Ray. Use this to grant cross-account access to Django application trace data.

Syntax
aws xray put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name <value> \
    --policy-document <value> \
    [--policy-revision-id <value>] \
    [--bypass-policy-lockout-check]
Parameters Description
  • --policy-name (string, required): Name for the resource policy
  • --policy-document (string, required): JSON policy document
  • --policy-revision-id (string): Revision ID for optimistic concurrency
  • --bypass-policy-lockout-check (boolean): Skip lockout safety check
Return Value
  • ResourcePolicy (object): Created policy with PolicyName, PolicyDocument, and PolicyRevisionId
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Grant cross-account read access

Allow external monitoring account to view Django application traces.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "anihpj-monitoring-access" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [
            {
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {
                    "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root"
                },
                "Action": [
                    "xray:GetTraceSummaries",
                    "xray:BatchGetTraces",
                    "xray:GetServiceGraph"
                ],
                "Resource": "*"
            }
        ]
    }'
Output: { "ResourcePolicy": { "PolicyName": "anihpj-monitoring-access", "PolicyDocument": "{...}", "PolicyRevisionId": "rev-new-123", "LastUpdatedTime": "2025-01-18T10:30:00Z" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # External account can now: # - View JobPost API trace summaries # - Get full traces for debugging # - View service graph for architecture review # No write/delete access granted
Example 2: Create policy with conditions

Restrict access to specific IP ranges or VPC.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "anihpj-restricted-access" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [
            {
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {
                    "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::987654321098:role/MonitoringRole"
                },
                "Action": [
                    "xray:GetTraceSummaries",
                    "xray:BatchGetTraces"
                ],
                "Resource": "*",
                "Condition": {
                    "IpAddress": {
                        "aws:SourceIp": ["10.0.0.0/8", "192.168.1.0/24"]
                    },
                    "StringEquals": {
                        "aws:PrincipalTag/Team": "operations"
                    }
                }
            }
        ]
    }'
Output: { "ResourcePolicy": { "PolicyName": "anihpj-restricted-access", "PolicyDocument": "{...}", "PolicyRevisionId": "rev-abc123", "LastUpdatedTime": "2025-01-18T14:00:00Z" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Conditional access policy: # - Only from internal IPs (10.x, 192.168.1.x) # - Only users tagged with Team=operations # # Protected trace data: # - JobPost.objects sensitive queries # - User authentication traces # - Author/Location PII
Example 3: Update existing policy

Modify policy using revision ID for concurrency control.

#!/bin/bash
POLICY_NAME="anihpj-monitoring-access"

# Get current revision
CURRENT=$(aws xray list-resource-policies | \
    jq -r ".ResourcePolicies[] | select(.PolicyName == \"$POLICY_NAME\")")

REVISION_ID=$(echo "$CURRENT" | jq -r '.PolicyRevisionId')
echo "Current revision: $REVISION_ID"

# Update policy with new permissions
aws xray put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-revision-id "$REVISION_ID" \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [
            {
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {
                    "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root"
                },
                "Action": [
                    "xray:GetTraceSummaries",
                    "xray:BatchGetTraces",
                    "xray:GetServiceGraph",
                    "xray:GetTimeSeriesServiceStatistics"
                ],
                "Resource": "*"
            }
        ]
    }' | jq '{
        status: "Policy updated",
        oldRevision: "'"$REVISION_ID"'",
        newRevision: .ResourcePolicy.PolicyRevisionId
    }'
Output: Current revision: rev-abc123 { "status": "Policy updated", "oldRevision": "rev-abc123", "newRevision": "rev-def456" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Policy updated with new permissions: # + GetTimeSeriesServiceStatistics # # Now allows: # - Trend analysis of JobPost queries # - Skills operation performance graphs # - Historical comparison reports
Example 4: Create policy from JSON file

Deploy policy from version-controlled configuration.

#!/bin/bash
POLICY_NAME="anihpj-cross-account"
POLICY_FILE="xray-policy.json"

# Create policy file
cat > "$POLICY_FILE" << 'EOF'
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "AllowMonitoringTeamRead",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": [
                    "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:root",
                    "arn:aws:iam::222222222222:root"
                ]
            },
            "Action": [
                "xray:GetTraceSummaries",
                "xray:BatchGetTraces",
                "xray:GetServiceGraph"
            ],
            "Resource": "*"
        },
        {
            "Sid": "AllowSecurityTeamFullRead",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::333333333333:role/SecurityAudit"
            },
            "Action": [
                "xray:Get*",
                "xray:List*",
                "xray:BatchGet*"
            ],
            "Resource": "*"
        }
    ]
}
EOF

# Deploy policy
aws xray put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --policy-document file://"$POLICY_FILE" | jq '{
        status: "Policy deployed from file",
        policyName: .ResourcePolicy.PolicyName,
        revision: .ResourcePolicy.PolicyRevisionId
    }'

rm "$POLICY_FILE"
Output: { "status": "Policy deployed from file", "policyName": "anihpj-cross-account", "revision": "rev-xyz789" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Multi-account policy deployed: # # Monitoring team (2 accounts): # - Read traces for JobPost operations # - View service graphs # # Security audit: # - Full read access to all traces # - Compliance and audit reviews # - No modification permissions
Example 5: Bypass lockout check for emergency

Update policy without lockout validation during incident.

#!/bin/bash
POLICY_NAME="anihpj-emergency-access"

echo "⚠️  EMERGENCY: Bypassing policy lockout check"
echo "   This allows policy changes that could lock out the account"
echo ""

# Create emergency access policy
aws xray put-resource-policy \
    --policy-name "$POLICY_NAME" \
    --bypass-policy-lockout-check \
    --policy-document '{
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [
            {
                "Sid": "EmergencyIncidentAccess",
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Principal": {
                    "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::999999999999:role/IncidentResponse"
                },
                "Action": [
                    "xray:GetTraceSummaries",
                    "xray:BatchGetTraces",
                    "xray:GetServiceGraph",
                    "xray:GetTraceGraph"
                ],
                "Resource": "*",
                "Condition": {
                    "DateLessThan": {
                        "aws:CurrentTime": "2025-01-19T00:00:00Z"
                    }
                }
            }
        ]
    }' | jq '{
        status: "Emergency policy created",
        policyName: .ResourcePolicy.PolicyName,
        expiresAt: "2025-01-19T00:00:00Z",
        warning: "Remove this policy after incident resolution"
    }'
Output: ⚠️ EMERGENCY: Bypassing policy lockout check This allows policy changes that could lock out the account { "status": "Emergency policy created", "policyName": "anihpj-emergency-access", "expiresAt": "2025-01-19T00:00:00Z", "warning": "Remove this policy after incident resolution" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Emergency incident access: # - External IR team can view traces # - Time-limited to 24 hours # - JobPost/Skills traces accessible # # Post-incident: # - Delete policy with delete-resource-policy # - Review access logs # - Document in incident report

put-telemetry-records

Uploads telemetry data from the X-Ray daemon. This is typically called automatically by the daemon running alongside your Django application.

Syntax
aws xray put-telemetry-records \
    --telemetry-records <value> \
    [--ec2-instance-id <value>] \
    [--hostname <value>] \
    [--resource-arn <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --telemetry-records (list, required): Telemetry records with Timestamp, SegmentsReceivedCount, SegmentsSentCount, and BackendConnectionErrors
  • --ec2-instance-id (string): EC2 instance ID running the daemon
  • --hostname (string): Hostname of the daemon
  • --resource-arn (string): ARN of the resource generating telemetry
Return Value
  • No output on success.
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Upload daemon telemetry

Send telemetry data from X-Ray daemon on Django server.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray put-telemetry-records \
    --telemetry-records '[
        {
            "Timestamp": 1737187200,
            "SegmentsReceivedCount": 1000,
            "SegmentsSentCount": 995,
            "SegmentsRejectedCount": 5,
            "BackendConnectionErrors": {
                "ConnectionRefusedCount": 0,
                "TimeoutCount": 1
            }
        }
    ]' \
    --ec2-instance-id "i-0abc123def456" \
    --hostname "anihpj-web-01"
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Daemon telemetry from anihpj web server: # - 1000 segments received (trace data) # - 995 successfully sent # - 5 rejected (malformed) # - 1 timeout connecting to X-Ray
Example 2: Upload telemetry with Lambda ARN

Send telemetry from X-Ray daemon on Lambda for Django background tasks.

#!/bin/bash
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%s)

aws xray put-telemetry-records \
    --telemetry-records '[
        {
            "Timestamp": '"$TIMESTAMP"',
            "SegmentsReceivedCount": 250,
            "SegmentsSentCount": 250,
            "SegmentsRejectedCount": 0,
            "BackendConnectionErrors": {
                "ConnectionRefusedCount": 0,
                "HTTPCode5XXCount": 0,
                "TimeoutCount": 0
            }
        }
    ]' \
    --resource-arn "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-task-processor"

echo "Telemetry uploaded for Lambda function"
Output: Telemetry uploaded for Lambda function
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Lambda telemetry for async tasks: # - 250 segments from JobPost.objects.bulk_create() # - Skills batch processing traces # - No errors or connection issues # Background: Django Celery tasks on Lambda
Example 3: Batch upload telemetry records

Send multiple telemetry records in a single request.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate timestamps for multiple intervals
NOW=$(date +%s)
INTERVAL_1=$((NOW - 300))  # 5 min ago
INTERVAL_2=$((NOW - 180))  # 3 min ago
INTERVAL_3=$((NOW - 60))   # 1 min ago

aws xray put-telemetry-records \
    --telemetry-records '[
        {
            "Timestamp": '"$INTERVAL_1"',
            "SegmentsReceivedCount": 1200,
            "SegmentsSentCount": 1180,
            "SegmentsRejectedCount": 10,
            "SegmentsSpilloverCount": 10,
            "BackendConnectionErrors": {
                "TimeoutCount": 2
            }
        },
        {
            "Timestamp": '"$INTERVAL_2"',
            "SegmentsReceivedCount": 1100,
            "SegmentsSentCount": 1100,
            "SegmentsRejectedCount": 0,
            "BackendConnectionErrors": {}
        },
        {
            "Timestamp": '"$INTERVAL_3"',
            "SegmentsReceivedCount": 1050,
            "SegmentsSentCount": 1050,
            "SegmentsRejectedCount": 0,
            "BackendConnectionErrors": {}
        }
    ]' \
    --ec2-instance-id "i-0abc123def456" \
    --hostname "anihpj-web-01"

echo "Batch telemetry uploaded: 3 intervals"
Output: Batch telemetry uploaded: 3 intervals
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 5-minute telemetry batch for anihpj: # - Interval 1: High traffic, some spillover # JobPost.objects.filter() heavy usage # - Interval 2: Normal operation # - Interval 3: Steady state # Tracks Django request tracing health
Example 4: Monitor daemon health with telemetry

Track X-Ray daemon performance through telemetry metrics.

#!/bin/bash
# Daemon health monitoring script
INSTANCE_ID=$(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/instance-id)
HOSTNAME=$(hostname)

# Collect daemon statistics (simulated from daemon logs)
SEGMENTS_RECEIVED=1500
SEGMENTS_SENT=1495
SEGMENTS_REJECTED=3
SEGMENTS_SPILLOVER=2
CONNECTION_TIMEOUTS=1

aws xray put-telemetry-records \
    --telemetry-records '[{
        "Timestamp": '"$(date +%s)"',
        "SegmentsReceivedCount": '"$SEGMENTS_RECEIVED"',
        "SegmentsSentCount": '"$SEGMENTS_SENT"',
        "SegmentsRejectedCount": '"$SEGMENTS_REJECTED"',
        "SegmentsSpilloverCount": '"$SEGMENTS_SPILLOVER"',
        "BackendConnectionErrors": {
            "TimeoutCount": '"$CONNECTION_TIMEOUTS"',
            "ConnectionRefusedCount": 0,
            "HTTPCode4XXCount": 0,
            "HTTPCode5XXCount": 0,
            "UnknownHostCount": 0,
            "OtherCount": 0
        }
    }]' \
    --ec2-instance-id "$INSTANCE_ID" \
    --hostname "$HOSTNAME"

# Calculate health metrics
SUCCESS_RATE=$((SEGMENTS_SENT * 100 / SEGMENTS_RECEIVED))
echo "Daemon Health: $SUCCESS_RATE% segments delivered"
Output: Daemon Health: 99% segments delivered
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Daemon health for anihpj-web-01: # - 99% delivery rate (excellent) # - 3 rejected (validation errors) # - 2 spillover (buffer overflow) # - 1 timeout (transient network) # # Traces captured: # - JobPost CRUD operations # - Skills relationship queries
Example 5: Report telemetry errors for alerting

Upload telemetry with error counts and trigger alerts.

#!/bin/bash
INSTANCE_ID="i-0abc123def456"
HOSTNAME="anihpj-web-01"
SNS_TOPIC="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:xray-alerts"

# Simulated daemon statistics with errors
SEGMENTS_RECEIVED=1000
SEGMENTS_SENT=800
SEGMENTS_REJECTED=50
CONNECTION_TIMEOUTS=150

# Upload telemetry
aws xray put-telemetry-records \
    --telemetry-records '[{
        "Timestamp": '"$(date +%s)"',
        "SegmentsReceivedCount": '"$SEGMENTS_RECEIVED"',
        "SegmentsSentCount": '"$SEGMENTS_SENT"',
        "SegmentsRejectedCount": '"$SEGMENTS_REJECTED"',
        "BackendConnectionErrors": {
            "TimeoutCount": '"$CONNECTION_TIMEOUTS"'
        }
    }]' \
    --ec2-instance-id "$INSTANCE_ID" \
    --hostname "$HOSTNAME"

# Check for issues and alert
SUCCESS_RATE=$((SEGMENTS_SENT * 100 / SEGMENTS_RECEIVED))
TIMEOUT_RATE=$((CONNECTION_TIMEOUTS * 100 / SEGMENTS_RECEIVED))

if [ $SUCCESS_RATE -lt 90 ] || [ $TIMEOUT_RATE -gt 5 ]; then
    aws sns publish \
        --topic-arn "$SNS_TOPIC" \
        --subject "X-Ray Daemon Alert: $HOSTNAME" \
        --message "Delivery: $SUCCESS_RATE%, Timeouts: $TIMEOUT_RATE%"
    echo "Alert sent: Daemon health degraded"
fi
Output: Alert sent: Daemon health degraded
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Daemon alert triggered: # - 80% delivery rate (below 90% threshold) # - 15% timeouts (above 5% threshold) # # Impact on anihpj tracing: # - 20% of JobPost traces missing # - Skills queries partially captured # # Action: Check X-Ray endpoint connectivity

put-trace-segments

Uploads trace segment documents to X-Ray. Use this to send custom trace data from your Django application when not using the X-Ray SDK directly.

Syntax
aws xray put-trace-segments \
    --trace-segment-documents <value>
Parameters Description
  • --trace-segment-documents (list, required): JSON documents containing trace segment data
Return Value
  • UnprocessedTraceSegments (array): List of segments that failed to process with Id, ErrorCode, and Message
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Upload custom trace segment

Send custom trace data for a Django JobPost operation.

#!/bin/bash
TRACE_ID="1-$(printf '%08x' $(date +%s))-$(openssl rand -hex 12)"

aws xray put-trace-segments \
    --trace-segment-documents "[
        {
            \"name\": \"anihpj-django\",
            \"id\": \"$(openssl rand -hex 8)\",
            \"trace_id\": \"$TRACE_ID\",
            \"start_time\": $(date +%s.%N),
            \"end_time\": $(echo \"$(date +%s.%N) + 0.5\" | bc),
            \"annotations\": {
                \"model\": \"JobPost\",
                \"operation\": \"create\"
            },
            \"metadata\": {
                \"django\": {
                    \"view\": \"JobPostCreateView\"
                }
            }
        }
    ]"
Output: { "UnprocessedTraceSegments": [] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Custom trace for JobPost.objects.create() # Annotations: model=JobPost, operation=create # Metadata: view=JobPostCreateView # Duration: 0.5s
Example 2: Upload subsegment for database call

Create a subsegment for a specific PostgreSQL query.

#!/bin/bash
PARENT_TRACE_ID="1-5f4b3c2a-abc123def456789012345678"
PARENT_SEGMENT_ID="1234567890abcdef"
SEGMENT_ID=$(openssl rand -hex 8)

aws xray put-trace-segments \
    --trace-segment-documents "[
        {
            \"name\": \"PostgreSQL\",
            \"id\": \"$SEGMENT_ID\",
            \"trace_id\": \"$PARENT_TRACE_ID\",
            \"parent_id\": \"$PARENT_SEGMENT_ID\",
            \"start_time\": $(date +%s.%N),
            \"end_time\": $(echo \"$(date +%s.%N) + 0.05\" | bc),
            \"namespace\": \"remote\",
            \"sql\": {
                \"database_type\": \"PostgreSQL\",
                \"sanitized_query\": \"SELECT id, title, salary FROM jobpost_jobpost WHERE author_id = ?\"
            }
        }
    ]" | jq '.'
Output: { "UnprocessedTraceSegments": [] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Subsegment for database query: # JobPost.objects.filter(author_id=user.id) # .values('id', 'title', 'salary') # # Duration: 50ms # Linked to parent Django request segment
Example 3: Upload trace with error information

Document a failed operation with exception details.

#!/bin/bash
TRACE_ID="1-$(printf '%08x' $(date +%s))-$(openssl rand -hex 12)"
START_TIME=$(date +%s.%N)
END_TIME=$(echo "$START_TIME + 0.2" | bc)

aws xray put-trace-segments \
    --trace-segment-documents "[
        {
            \"name\": \"anihpj-django\",
            \"id\": \"$(openssl rand -hex 8)\",
            \"trace_id\": \"$TRACE_ID\",
            \"start_time\": $START_TIME,
            \"end_time\": $END_TIME,
            \"fault\": true,
            \"cause\": {
                \"working_directory\": \"/app/anihpj\",
                \"exceptions\": [
                    {
                        \"id\": \"$(openssl rand -hex 8)\",
                        \"type\": \"IntegrityError\",
                        \"message\": \"duplicate key value violates unique constraint\"
                    }
                ]
            },
            \"annotations\": {
                \"model\": \"JobPost\",
                \"operation\": \"create\",
                \"error_type\": \"duplicate_key\"
            }
        }
    ]"
Output: { "UnprocessedTraceSegments": [] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Error trace recorded: # IntegrityError on JobPost.objects.create() # Cause: Duplicate title/author combination # # Debug info: # - Working directory captured # - Exception type and message # - Operation annotations for filtering
Example 4: Batch upload multiple segments

Upload multiple trace segments in a single request.

#!/bin/bash
TRACE_ID="1-$(printf '%08x' $(date +%s))-$(openssl rand -hex 12)"
BASE_TIME=$(date +%s.%N)

aws xray put-trace-segments \
    --trace-segment-documents "[
        {
            \"name\": \"anihpj-django\",
            \"id\": \"$(openssl rand -hex 8)\",
            \"trace_id\": \"$TRACE_ID\",
            \"start_time\": $BASE_TIME,
            \"end_time\": $(echo \"$BASE_TIME + 0.5\" | bc),
            \"annotations\": {\"request_type\": \"api\"}
        },
        {
            \"name\": \"JobPost.objects.filter\",
            \"id\": \"$(openssl rand -hex 8)\",
            \"trace_id\": \"$TRACE_ID\",
            \"start_time\": $(echo \"$BASE_TIME + 0.01\" | bc),
            \"end_time\": $(echo \"$BASE_TIME + 0.15\" | bc),
            \"namespace\": \"local\"
        },
        {
            \"name\": \"Skills.objects.prefetch\",
            \"id\": \"$(openssl rand -hex 8)\",
            \"trace_id\": \"$TRACE_ID\",
            \"start_time\": $(echo \"$BASE_TIME + 0.15\" | bc),
            \"end_time\": $(echo \"$BASE_TIME + 0.25\" | bc),
            \"namespace\": \"local\"
        }
    ]" | jq '{
        status: "Batch uploaded",
        segmentsProcessed: (if .UnprocessedTraceSegments | length == 0 then 3 else (3 - (.UnprocessedTraceSegments | length)) end),
        failed: .UnprocessedTraceSegments | length
    }'
Output: { "status": "Batch uploaded", "segmentsProcessed": 3, "failed": 0 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch trace for API request: # 1. Django view (500ms total) # 2. JobPost.objects.filter() (140ms) # 3. Skills.objects.prefetch_related() (100ms) # # Shows query breakdown within request
Example 5: Upload trace with HTTP metadata

Include HTTP request details for API tracing.

#!/bin/bash
TRACE_ID="1-$(printf '%08x' $(date +%s))-$(openssl rand -hex 12)"

aws xray put-trace-segments \
    --trace-segment-documents "[
        {
            \"name\": \"anihpj-api\",
            \"id\": \"$(openssl rand -hex 8)\",
            \"trace_id\": \"$TRACE_ID\",
            \"start_time\": $(date +%s.%N),
            \"end_time\": $(echo \"$(date +%s.%N) + 0.3\" | bc),
            \"http\": {
                \"request\": {
                    \"url\": \"https://anihpj.example.com/api/jobposts/\",
                    \"method\": \"POST\",
                    \"user_agent\": \"Mozilla/5.0\",
                    \"client_ip\": \"192.168.1.100\"
                },
                \"response\": {
                    \"status\": 201,
                    \"content_length\": 256
                }
            },
            \"annotations\": {
                \"user_id\": \"user_123\",
                \"endpoint\": \"jobpost_create\"
            },
            \"metadata\": {
                \"django\": {
                    \"model\": \"JobPost\",
                    \"serializer\": \"JobPostSerializer\",
                    \"authentication\": \"JWT\"
                }
            }
        }
    ]"
Output: { "UnprocessedTraceSegments": [] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # HTTP trace for JobPost creation: # POST /api/jobposts/ -> 201 Created # # Request details: # - User: user_123 (JWT authenticated) # - Client IP: 192.168.1.100 # # Django context: # - Model: JobPost # - Serializer: JobPostSerializer

start-trace-retrieval

Starts retrieval of archived trace data. Use this to access historical traces from your Django application for post-incident analysis.

Syntax
aws xray start-trace-retrieval \
    --trace-ids <value> \
    --start-time <value> \
    --end-time <value>
Parameters Description
  • --trace-ids (list, required): List of trace IDs to retrieve
  • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start of the time range
  • --end-time (timestamp, required): End of the time range
Return Value
  • RetrievalToken (string): Token to use with get-retrieved-traces-graph and list-retrieved-traces
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Start trace retrieval for incident

Retrieve archived traces from a past incident affecting JobPost operations.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray start-trace-retrieval \
    --trace-ids \
        "1-5f4b3c2a-abc123def456789012345678" \
        "1-5f4b3c2b-def456ghi789012345678901" \
    --start-time $(date -d '7 days ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d '6 days ago' +%s)
Output: { "RetrievalToken": "retrieval-token-xyz789" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Retrieving archived traces from 7 days ago # Incident: JobPost.objects.create() failures # Use retrieval token to get full traces
Example 2: Start retrieval with multiple trace IDs

Retrieve several archived traces related to a performance issue.

#!/bin/bash
# Trace IDs from incident report
TRACE_IDS=(
    "1-5f4b3c2a-abc123"
    "1-5f4b3c2b-def456"
    "1-5f4b3c2c-ghi789"
    "1-5f4b3c2d-jkl012"
    "1-5f4b3c2e-mno345"
)

# Convert to AWS CLI format
TRACE_ARGS=$(printf '"%s" ' "${TRACE_IDS[@]}")

RESULT=$(aws xray start-trace-retrieval \
    --trace-ids $TRACE_ARGS \
    --start-time $(date -d '2 weeks ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d '1 week ago' +%s))

RETRIEVAL_TOKEN=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.RetrievalToken')

echo "Retrieval started for ${#TRACE_IDS[@]} traces"
echo "Token: $RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"
echo ""
echo "Use this token with:"
echo "  aws xray list-retrieved-traces --retrieval-token $RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"
Output: Retrieval started for 5 traces Token: retrieval-batch-xyz789 Use this token with: aws xray list-retrieved-traces --retrieval-token retrieval-batch-xyz789
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Retrieving 5 archived traces: # - JobPost bulk creation traces # - Skills relationship failures # - Author lookup timeouts # # Time range: 2 weeks ago incident # Use for post-mortem analysis
Example 3: Start retrieval from trace summaries

Find and retrieve traces matching specific criteria.

#!/bin/bash
# First, find traces with errors from the incident period
TRACE_IDS=$(aws xray get-trace-summaries \
    --start-time $(date -d '10 days ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d '9 days ago' +%s) \
    --filter-expression 'fault = true AND service(name = "anihpj-django")' \
    --query 'TraceSummaries[*].Id' \
    --output text | head -20)

if [ -z "$TRACE_IDS" ]; then
    echo "No error traces found in the time range"
    exit 0
fi

TRACE_COUNT=$(echo "$TRACE_IDS" | wc -w)
echo "Found $TRACE_COUNT error traces"

# Start retrieval
RESULT=$(aws xray start-trace-retrieval \
    --trace-ids $TRACE_IDS \
    --start-time $(date -d '10 days ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d '9 days ago' +%s))

echo "$RESULT" | jq '{
    status: "Retrieval started",
    token: .RetrievalToken,
    traceCount: '"$TRACE_COUNT"'
}'
Output: Found 15 error traces { "status": "Retrieval started", "token": "retrieval-errors-abc123", "traceCount": 15 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Retrieved error traces from incident: # - 15 faulted traces identified # - Filter: fault=true in anihpj-django # # These traces contain: # - JobPost.objects operation failures # - Skills M2M relationship errors # - Database connection issues
Example 4: Start retrieval and wait for completion

Initiate retrieval and poll until traces are available.

#!/bin/bash
TRACE_IDS="1-5f4b3c2a-abc123 1-5f4b3c2b-def456"

echo "Starting trace retrieval..."

RESULT=$(aws xray start-trace-retrieval \
    --trace-ids $TRACE_IDS \
    --start-time $(date -d '1 week ago' +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d '6 days ago' +%s))

RETRIEVAL_TOKEN=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.RetrievalToken')
echo "Token: $RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"

# Poll for completion
MAX_ATTEMPTS=30
ATTEMPT=0

while [ $ATTEMPT -lt $MAX_ATTEMPTS ]; do
    STATUS=$(aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
        --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" \
        --query 'RetrievalStatus' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null)
    
    echo "[$ATTEMPT] Status: $STATUS"
    
    if [ "$STATUS" == "COMPLETE" ]; then
        echo ""
        echo "Retrieval complete! Fetching traces..."
        aws xray list-retrieved-traces \
            --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" | jq '.TraceIds'
        break
    elif [ "$STATUS" == "FAILED" ]; then
        echo "Retrieval failed!"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    sleep 10
    ATTEMPT=$((ATTEMPT + 1))
done
Output: Starting trace retrieval... Token: retrieval-wait-xyz789 [0] Status: SCHEDULED [1] Status: RUNNING [2] Status: RUNNING [3] Status: COMPLETE Retrieval complete! Fetching traces... [ "1-5f4b3c2a-abc123", "1-5f4b3c2b-def456" ]
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Retrieval workflow for incident traces: # 1. Start retrieval with trace IDs # 2. Poll until COMPLETE status # 3. Get archived trace data # # Traces contain historical: # - JobPost.objects operations # - Skills relationship queries # - Error details and stack traces
Example 5: Script for incident trace collection

Automated script to retrieve traces for incident investigation.

#!/bin/bash
# Usage: ./retrieve-incident-traces.sh "2025-01-10" "2025-01-11" "incident-001"

INCIDENT_START="$1"
INCIDENT_END="$2"
INCIDENT_ID="$3"

OUTPUT_DIR="./incidents/$INCIDENT_ID"
mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"

echo "Incident Trace Retrieval: $INCIDENT_ID"
echo "Period: $INCIDENT_START to $INCIDENT_END"
echo ""

# Find relevant traces
TRACE_IDS=$(aws xray get-trace-summaries \
    --start-time $(date -d "$INCIDENT_START" +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d "$INCIDENT_END" +%s) \
    --filter-expression 'fault = true OR responsetime > 5' \
    --query 'TraceSummaries[*].Id' \
    --output text | head -50)

TRACE_COUNT=$(echo "$TRACE_IDS" | wc -w)
echo "Found $TRACE_COUNT traces for retrieval"

# Save trace IDs
echo "$TRACE_IDS" | tr ' ' '\n' > "$OUTPUT_DIR/trace-ids.txt"

# Start retrieval
RESULT=$(aws xray start-trace-retrieval \
    --trace-ids $TRACE_IDS \
    --start-time $(date -d "$INCIDENT_START" +%s) \
    --end-time $(date -d "$INCIDENT_END" +%s))

RETRIEVAL_TOKEN=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.RetrievalToken')
echo "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" > "$OUTPUT_DIR/retrieval-token.txt"

echo ""
echo "Retrieval token saved to: $OUTPUT_DIR/retrieval-token.txt"
echo "Trace IDs saved to: $OUTPUT_DIR/trace-ids.txt"
echo ""
echo "Next steps:"
echo "1. Wait for retrieval: aws xray list-retrieved-traces --retrieval-token $RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"
echo "2. Get graph: aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph --retrieval-token $RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"
Output: Incident Trace Retrieval: incident-001 Period: 2025-01-10 to 2025-01-11 Found 25 traces for retrieval Retrieval token saved to: ./incidents/incident-001/retrieval-token.txt Trace IDs saved to: ./incidents/incident-001/trace-ids.txt Next steps: 1. Wait for retrieval: aws xray list-retrieved-traces --retrieval-token retrieval-incident-001 2. Get graph: aws xray get-retrieved-traces-graph --retrieval-token retrieval-incident-001
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Incident trace collection for anihpj: # - 25 traces with faults or >5s response # - Captures JobPost operation failures # - Skills relationship issues # - Author/Location slow queries # # Organized in incidents/incident-001/ # For post-mortem and RCA documentation

cancel-trace-retrieval

Cancels an in-progress trace retrieval operation. Use this to stop a retrieval that is no longer needed.

Syntax
aws xray cancel-trace-retrieval \
    --retrieval-token <value>
Parameters Description
  • --retrieval-token (string, required): Token from start-trace-retrieval
Return Value
  • No output on success.
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Cancel trace retrieval

Stop an in-progress retrieval operation.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray cancel-trace-retrieval \
    --retrieval-token "retrieval-token-xyz789"

echo "Trace retrieval cancelled"
Output: Trace retrieval cancelled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cancelled retrieval of historical traces # No longer need archived JobPost traces # Incident resolved through other means
Example 2: Cancel retrieval with error handling

Safely cancel a trace retrieval with validation and error handling.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-abc123def456"

# Check if token is valid before cancelling
if aws xray cancel-trace-retrieval \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Successfully cancelled trace retrieval: $RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"
else
    echo "Warning: Retrieval may already be completed or token invalid"
fi
Output: Successfully cancelled trace retrieval: retrieval-abc123def456
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safe cancellation pattern for Django debugging: # - Started retrieval for JobPost.objects performance issue # - Found root cause (N+1 query in Author lookup) # - Applied fix with select_related('author') # - Historical traces no longer needed
Example 3: Cancel multiple retrievals in batch

Cancel several trace retrieval operations at once using stored tokens.

#!/bin/bash
# Cancel all active retrievals from a token file
TOKENS=(
    "retrieval-jobpost-001"
    "retrieval-skills-002"
    "retrieval-author-003"
)

for token in "${TOKENS[@]}"; do
    echo "Cancelling retrieval: $token"
    aws xray cancel-trace-retrieval \
        --retrieval-token "$token" 2>/dev/null || \
        echo "  Already completed or invalid"
done

echo "Batch cancellation complete"
Output: Cancelling retrieval: retrieval-jobpost-001 Cancelling retrieval: retrieval-skills-002 Already completed or invalid Cancelling retrieval: retrieval-author-003 Batch cancellation complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch cleanup of trace retrievals: # - retrieval-jobpost-001: JobPost listing traces (cancelled) # - retrieval-skills-002: Already completed (Skills M2M) # - retrieval-author-003: Author queries (cancelled) # All diagnostics complete, cleaning up resources
Example 4: Cancel and log retrieval operation

Cancel trace retrieval with audit logging for compliance tracking.

#!/bin/bash
RETRIEVAL_TOKEN="retrieval-incident-2025-001"
LOG_FILE="/var/log/xray-retrievals.log"
TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")

# Cancel the retrieval
aws xray cancel-trace-retrieval \
    --retrieval-token "$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN"

# Log the cancellation for audit
echo "{\"timestamp\": \"$TIMESTAMP\", \"action\": \"cancel\", \"token\": \"$RETRIEVAL_TOKEN\", \"reason\": \"incident_resolved\"}" >> "$LOG_FILE"

echo "Retrieval cancelled and logged at $TIMESTAMP"
Output: Retrieval cancelled and logged at 2025-01-18T10:30:00Z
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit log entry for compliance: # { # "timestamp": "2025-01-18T10:30:00Z", # "action": "cancel", # "token": "retrieval-incident-2025-001", # "reason": "incident_resolved" # } # Incident: JobPost API latency spike # Resolution: Added database index on Skills.name
Example 5: Cancel retrieval in automated cleanup script

Automated cleanup of old trace retrievals as part of maintenance routine.

#!/bin/bash
# Automated cleanup script for stale trace retrievals
# Run via cron: 0 2 * * * /opt/scripts/cleanup-retrievals.sh

TOKEN_STORE="/opt/xray/active-retrievals.json"

if [ -f "$TOKEN_STORE" ]; then
    # Get tokens older than 24 hours
    STALE_TOKENS=$(jq -r '.retrievals[] | select(.age_hours > 24) | .token' "$TOKEN_STORE")
    
    for token in $STALE_TOKENS; do
        echo "Cancelling stale retrieval: $token"
        aws xray cancel-trace-retrieval --retrieval-token "$token" 2>/dev/null
    done
    
    # Update token store
    jq 'del(.retrievals[] | select(.age_hours > 24))' "$TOKEN_STORE" > tmp.json
    mv tmp.json "$TOKEN_STORE"
    
    echo "Cleanup complete: $(echo "$STALE_TOKENS" | wc -w) retrievals cancelled"
else
    echo "No active retrievals to clean up"
fi
Output: Cancelling stale retrieval: retrieval-old-jobpost-001 Cancelling stale retrieval: retrieval-old-author-002 Cleanup complete: 2 retrievals cancelled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Automated maintenance for anihpj tracing: # - Prevents resource accumulation # - Cleans up forgotten diagnostic sessions # - Maintains compliance with data retention policies # # Django models traced: # - JobPost: CRUD operations and queries # - Skills: M2M relationship lookups # - Author/Location: ForeignKey traversals

tag-resource

Adds tags to an X-Ray resource such as a group or sampling rule. Tags help organize and track costs for Django application tracing.

Syntax
aws xray tag-resource \
    --resource-arn <value> \
    --tags <value>
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string, required): ARN of the X-Ray resource to tag
  • --tags (list, required): List of key-value tag pairs
Return Value
  • No output on success.
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Tag X-Ray group for cost allocation

Add tags to a trace filter group for cost tracking and organization.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray tag-resource \
    --resource-arn "arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-jobpost-errors" \
    --tags '[
        {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"},
        {"Key": "Application", "Value": "anihpj"},
        {"Key": "Team", "Value": "backend"},
        {"Key": "CostCenter", "Value": "engineering"}
    ]'
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tagged JobPost error monitoring group: # - Environment: production # - Application: anihpj (Django project) # - Team: backend (responsible team) # - CostCenter: for billing allocation
Example 2: Tag sampling rule

Add organizational tags to a sampling rule.

#!/bin/bash
RULE_ARN="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:sampling-rule/anihpj-api-sampling"

aws xray tag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$RULE_ARN" \
    --tags '[
        {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"},
        {"Key": "Application", "Value": "anihpj"},
        {"Key": "RuleType", "Value": "api-tracing"},
        {"Key": "ManagedBy", "Value": "terraform"}
    ]'

echo "Tags applied to sampling rule"

# Verify tags
aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$RULE_ARN" | jq '.Tags'
Output: Tags applied to sampling rule [ {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"}, {"Key": "Application", "Value": "anihpj"}, {"Key": "RuleType", "Value": "api-tracing"}, {"Key": "ManagedBy", "Value": "terraform"} ]
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tagged API sampling rule: # - Tracks /api/* endpoints # - JobPost CRUD operations # - Skills management APIs # - Managed via Terraform
Example 3: Apply standard tags to all X-Ray resources

Ensure consistent tagging across all groups and rules.

#!/bin/bash
STANDARD_TAGS='[
    {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"},
    {"Key": "Application", "Value": "anihpj"},
    {"Key": "Team", "Value": "backend"},
    {"Key": "CostCenter", "Value": "engineering"},
    {"Key": "Project", "Value": "jobpost-platform"}
]'

# Tag all groups
GROUPS=$(aws xray get-groups --query 'Groups[].GroupARN' --output text)
for GROUP_ARN in $GROUPS; do
    echo "Tagging group: $(echo "$GROUP_ARN" | rev | cut -d'/' -f1 | rev)"
    aws xray tag-resource \
        --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
        --tags "$STANDARD_TAGS" 2>/dev/null || echo "  Failed"
done

# Tag all sampling rules
RULES=$(aws xray get-sampling-rules --query 'SamplingRuleRecords[].SamplingRule.RuleARN' --output text)
for RULE_ARN in $RULES; do
    if [ -n "$RULE_ARN" ] && [ "$RULE_ARN" != "null" ]; then
        echo "Tagging rule: $(echo "$RULE_ARN" | rev | cut -d'/' -f1 | rev)"
        aws xray tag-resource \
            --resource-arn "$RULE_ARN" \
            --tags "$STANDARD_TAGS" 2>/dev/null || echo "  Failed"
    fi
done

echo "Tagging complete"
Output: Tagging group: anihpj-jobpost-errors Tagging group: anihpj-slow-queries Tagging group: Default Tagging rule: anihpj-api-sampling Tagging rule: anihpj-error-sampling Tagging complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # All X-Ray resources now have consistent tags: # - Groups for JobPost/Skills filtering # - Sampling rules for API tracing # # Enables cost allocation by: # - Application: anihpj # - Team: backend # - Project: jobpost-platform
Example 4: Tag with environment-specific values

Apply environment-appropriate tags based on account.

#!/bin/bash
ACCOUNT_ID=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query 'Account' --output text)

# Determine environment from account
case "$ACCOUNT_ID" in
    "111111111111") ENV="development" ;;
    "222222222222") ENV="staging" ;;
    "333333333333") ENV="production" ;;
    *) ENV="unknown" ;;
esac

GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:$ACCOUNT_ID:group/anihpj-jobpost-errors"

aws xray tag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
    --tags '[
        {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "'"$ENV"'"},
        {"Key": "Application", "Value": "anihpj"},
        {"Key": "AutoTagged", "Value": "true"},
        {"Key": "TaggedAt", "Value": "'"$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"'"}
    ]'

echo "Tagged for environment: $ENV"
Output: Tagged for environment: production
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Environment-aware tagging: # - Auto-detected: production # - Timestamp recorded for auditing # # Different tag values per environment: # - dev: lower sampling costs # - staging: pre-prod testing # - prod: full monitoring
Example 5: Tag resources from configuration file

Apply tags defined in an external JSON configuration.

#!/bin/bash
CONFIG_FILE="xray-tags.json"

# Create configuration file
cat > "$CONFIG_FILE" << 'EOF'
{
    "groups": [
        {
            "name": "anihpj-jobpost-errors",
            "tags": [
                {"Key": "Monitor", "Value": "errors"},
                {"Key": "AlertPriority", "Value": "high"}
            ]
        },
        {
            "name": "anihpj-slow-queries",
            "tags": [
                {"Key": "Monitor", "Value": "latency"},
                {"Key": "AlertPriority", "Value": "medium"}
            ]
        }
    ]
}
EOF

REGION="us-east-1"
ACCOUNT_ID=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query 'Account' --output text)

# Apply tags from config
jq -c '.groups[]' "$CONFIG_FILE" | while read -r group; do
    NAME=$(echo "$group" | jq -r '.name')
    TAGS=$(echo "$group" | jq -c '.tags')
    ARN="arn:aws:xray:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:group/$NAME"
    
    echo "Tagging: $NAME"
    aws xray tag-resource \
        --resource-arn "$ARN" \
        --tags "$TAGS"
done

rm "$CONFIG_FILE"
echo "Tags applied from configuration"
Output: Tagging: anihpj-jobpost-errors Tagging: anihpj-slow-queries Tags applied from configuration
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Configuration-driven tagging: # # jobpost-errors group: # - Monitor: errors # - AlertPriority: high # - Catches JobPost.objects failures # # slow-queries group: # - Monitor: latency # - AlertPriority: medium # - Tracks Skills query performance

untag-resource

Removes tags from an X-Ray resource. Use this to clean up or update tag assignments.

Syntax
aws xray untag-resource \
    --resource-arn <value> \
    --tag-keys <value>
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string, required): ARN of the X-Ray resource
  • --tag-keys (list, required): List of tag keys to remove
Return Value
  • No output on success.
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Remove tags from group

Remove outdated tags from a trace filter group.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray untag-resource \
    --resource-arn "arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-jobpost-errors" \
    --tag-keys '["CostCenter", "Team"]'
Output: (No output on success)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed CostCenter and Team tags # Retaining Environment and Application tags # Team restructuring requires tag updates
Example 2: Remove deprecated tags

Clean up old tag keys that are no longer used.

#!/bin/bash
GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-jobpost-errors"

# List current tags
echo "Current tags:"
aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" | jq -r '.Tags[] | "\(.Key): \(.Value)"'

# Remove deprecated tags
DEPRECATED_KEYS='["OldProject", "LegacyTeam", "Deprecated"]'

aws xray untag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
    --tag-keys "$DEPRECATED_KEYS"

echo ""
echo "Tags after cleanup:"
aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" | jq -r '.Tags[] | "\(.Key): \(.Value)"'
Output: Current tags: Environment: production Application: anihpj OldProject: legacy-jobs LegacyTeam: old-team Deprecated: true Tags after cleanup: Environment: production Application: anihpj
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up deprecated tags: # - OldProject: Migrated to new structure # - LegacyTeam: Team restructured # - Deprecated: Cleanup flag removed # # Retained essential tags for JobPost tracing
Example 3: Untag multiple resources in batch

Remove specific tags from all X-Ray groups.

#!/bin/bash
TAGS_TO_REMOVE='["TempFlag", "TestTag", "Debug"]'

# Get all group ARNs
GROUPS=$(aws xray get-groups --query 'Groups[].GroupARN' --output text)

for GROUP_ARN in $GROUPS; do
    GROUP_NAME=$(echo "$GROUP_ARN" | rev | cut -d'/' -f1 | rev)
    
    # Check if tags exist before removing
    EXISTING=$(aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
        --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
        --query 'Tags[].Key' --output text)
    
    echo "Processing: $GROUP_NAME"
    
    if echo "$EXISTING" | grep -qE "TempFlag|TestTag|Debug"; then
        aws xray untag-resource \
            --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
            --tag-keys "$TAGS_TO_REMOVE" 2>/dev/null
        echo "  Tags removed"
    else
        echo "  No matching tags found"
    fi
done
Output: Processing: anihpj-jobpost-errors Tags removed Processing: anihpj-slow-queries No matching tags found Processing: Default Tags removed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch cleanup of test/debug tags: # - TempFlag: Removed from error group # - Debug: Removed from Default group # # Production tags preserved: # - Environment, Application, Team
Example 4: Replace tags by untagging then tagging

Update tag values by removing old and adding new.

#!/bin/bash
GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-jobpost-errors"

# Tags to update
OLD_TEAM="team-alpha"
NEW_TEAM="team-platform"

# First, remove the old Team tag
aws xray untag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
    --tag-keys '["Team"]'

# Then add the new Team tag
aws xray tag-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
    --tags '[{"Key": "Team", "Value": "'"$NEW_TEAM"'"}]'

echo "Team tag updated: $OLD_TEAM → $NEW_TEAM"

# Verify
aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" | jq -r '.Tags[] | select(.Key == "Team")'
Output: Team tag updated: team-alpha → team-platform { "Key": "Team", "Value": "team-platform" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Team ownership transfer: # - Old: team-alpha # - New: team-platform # # Group monitors JobPost.objects errors # Now maintained by platform team
Example 5: Untag resources before deletion

Clean up tags before decommissioning an X-Ray group.

#!/bin/bash
GROUP_NAME="anihpj-deprecated-group"
GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/$GROUP_NAME"

echo "Preparing to decommission: $GROUP_NAME"

# Get all current tags
CURRENT_TAGS=$(aws xray list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN")

TAG_COUNT=$(echo "$CURRENT_TAGS" | jq '.Tags | length')
echo "Current tags: $TAG_COUNT"

if [ "$TAG_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
    # Extract all tag keys
    TAG_KEYS=$(echo "$CURRENT_TAGS" | jq -c '[.Tags[].Key]')
    
    # Remove all tags
    aws xray untag-resource \
        --resource-arn "$GROUP_ARN" \
        --tag-keys "$TAG_KEYS"
    
    echo "All tags removed"
fi

# Now safe to delete group
echo ""
echo "Group ready for deletion:"
echo "  aws xray delete-group --group-name $GROUP_NAME"
Output: Preparing to decommission: anihpj-deprecated-group Current tags: 4 All tags removed Group ready for deletion: aws xray delete-group --group-name anihpj-deprecated-group
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Decommissioning workflow: # 1. Remove all tags (for cost reporting accuracy) # 2. Verify no active traces # 3. Delete group # # This group tracked old JobPost model traces # Migrated to new anihpj-jobpost-errors group

update-group

Updates an existing X-Ray group's filter expression or insights configuration. Use this to modify trace filtering for your Django application.

Syntax
aws xray update-group \
    [--group-name <value>] \
    [--group-arn <value>] \
    [--filter-expression <value>] \
    [--insights-configuration <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --group-name (string): Name of the group to update
  • --group-arn (string): ARN of the group to update
  • --filter-expression (string): Updated filter expression
  • --insights-configuration (structure): Insights settings with InsightsEnabled and NotificationsEnabled
Return Value
  • Group (object): Updated group with GroupName, GroupARN, FilterExpression, and InsightsConfiguration
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Update filter expression

Modify the trace filter to include Skills errors along with JobPost errors.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray update-group \
    --group-name "anihpj-jobpost-errors" \
    --filter-expression '(annotation.model = "JobPost" OR annotation.model = "Skills") AND error = true'
Output: { "Group": { "GroupName": "anihpj-jobpost-errors", "GroupARN": "arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:group/anihpj-jobpost-errors", "FilterExpression": "(annotation.model = \"JobPost\" OR annotation.model = \"Skills\") AND error = true", "InsightsConfiguration": { "InsightsEnabled": true, "NotificationsEnabled": true } } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Updated filter now captures: # - JobPost.objects errors (DoesNotExist, ValidationError) # - Skills.objects errors (validation, integrity) # Insights remain enabled for anomaly detection
Example 2: Enable X-Ray Insights

Turn on anomaly detection and notifications for the group.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray update-group \
    --group-name "anihpj-jobpost-errors" \
    --insights-configuration '{
        "InsightsEnabled": true,
        "NotificationsEnabled": true
    }' | jq '{
        groupName: .Group.GroupName,
        insightsEnabled: .Group.InsightsConfiguration.InsightsEnabled,
        notifications: .Group.InsightsConfiguration.NotificationsEnabled,
        message: "Anomaly detection enabled"
    }'
Output: { "groupName": "anihpj-jobpost-errors", "insightsEnabled": true, "notifications": true, "message": "Anomaly detection enabled" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # X-Ray Insights now monitors: # - Anomalous error rate increases # - Latency spikes in JobPost queries # - Unusual fault patterns # # Notifications sent for: # - JobPost.objects.create() failure spikes # - Skills relationship issues
Example 3: Update filter for performance monitoring

Change group to capture slow requests instead of errors.

#!/bin/bash
GROUP_NAME="anihpj-slow-queries"

# Update to monitor slow database operations
aws xray update-group \
    --group-name "$GROUP_NAME" \
    --filter-expression 'responsetime > 1 AND service(name = "PostgreSQL")'

echo "Group updated for slow query monitoring"

# Verify the update
aws xray get-groups \
    --query "Groups[?GroupName=='$GROUP_NAME']" | jq '.[0] | {
        name: .GroupName,
        filter: .FilterExpression,
        insights: .InsightsConfiguration.InsightsEnabled
    }'
Output: Group updated for slow query monitoring { "name": "anihpj-slow-queries", "filter": "responsetime > 1 AND service(name = \"PostgreSQL\")", "insights": true }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Group now captures PostgreSQL queries > 1s: # - JobPost.objects.filter() with complex joins # - Skills.objects.prefetch_related() slow # - Author/Location lookups with N+1 # # Use for query optimization
Example 4: Disable insights to reduce costs

Turn off anomaly detection for lower-priority groups.

#!/bin/bash
# List groups with insights enabled
echo "Groups with Insights enabled:"
aws xray get-groups | jq -r '
    .Groups[] | select(.InsightsConfiguration.InsightsEnabled == true) |
    "  - \(.GroupName)"'

# Disable insights for development groups
DEV_GROUPS=("anihpj-dev-traces" "anihpj-test-group")

for GROUP_NAME in "${DEV_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "Disabling insights for: $GROUP_NAME"
    
    aws xray update-group \
        --group-name "$GROUP_NAME" \
        --insights-configuration '{
            "InsightsEnabled": false,
            "NotificationsEnabled": false
        }' 2>/dev/null && echo "  ✓ Disabled" || echo "  ⚠️ Group not found"
done

echo ""
echo "Cost savings: Insights disabled for ${#DEV_GROUPS[@]} groups"
Output: Groups with Insights enabled: - anihpj-jobpost-errors - anihpj-slow-queries - anihpj-dev-traces - anihpj-test-group Disabling insights for: anihpj-dev-traces ✓ Disabled Disabling insights for: anihpj-test-group ✓ Disabled Cost savings: Insights disabled for 2 groups
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Insights disabled for non-production: # - anihpj-dev-traces # - anihpj-test-group # # Production groups keep insights: # - anihpj-jobpost-errors (high priority) # - anihpj-slow-queries (performance)
Example 5: Update group with complex filter

Create a comprehensive filter for Django model monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
GROUP_NAME="anihpj-model-operations"

# Complex filter for Django model tracing
FILTER='(
    (annotation.model = "JobPost" AND (annotation.operation = "create" OR annotation.operation = "update"))
    OR (annotation.model = "Skills" AND annotation.operation = "bulk_create")
    OR (annotation.model = "Author" AND error = true)
) AND service(name = "anihpj-django")'

# Update group
aws xray update-group \
    --group-name "$GROUP_NAME" \
    --filter-expression "$FILTER" \
    --insights-configuration '{
        "InsightsEnabled": true,
        "NotificationsEnabled": true
    }'

echo "Group updated with complex filter"
echo ""
echo "Filter breakdown:"
echo "  - JobPost create/update operations"
echo "  - Skills bulk_create operations"
echo "  - Author errors"
Output: Group updated with complex filter Filter breakdown: - JobPost create/update operations - Skills bulk_create operations - Author errors
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Comprehensive model monitoring: # # JobPost operations: # - JobPost.objects.create() # - JobPost.objects.update() # # Skills batch operations: # - Skills.objects.bulk_create() # # Author error tracking: # - All Author-related failures # # Insights detect anomalies in these operations

update-indexing-rule

Updates an indexing rule for X-Ray trace data. Indexing rules control which trace attributes are indexed for searching.

Syntax
aws xray update-indexing-rule \
    --name <value> \
    --rule <value>
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the indexing rule to update
  • --rule (structure, required): Updated rule configuration with Probabilistic settings
Return Value
  • IndexingRule (object): Updated rule with Name, ModifiedAt, and Rule configuration
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Update indexing sampling rate

Increase indexing rate for better trace searchability.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray update-indexing-rule \
    --name "Default" \
    --rule '{
        "Probabilistic": {
            "DesiredSamplingPercentage": 75
        }
    }'
Output: { "IndexingRule": { "Name": "Default", "ModifiedAt": "2025-01-18T10:30:00Z", "Rule": { "Probabilistic": { "DesiredSamplingPercentage": 75 } } } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Increased indexing from 50% to 75% # More Django traces searchable: # - JobPost.objects queries # - Skills.objects operations # - Author and Location lookups
Example 2: Set probabilistic indexing to 100%

Index all traces for complete searchability.

#!/bin/bash
# Update to 100% indexing for full trace visibility
aws xray update-indexing-rule \
    --name "Default" \
    --rule '{
        "Probabilistic": {
            "DesiredSamplingPercentage": 100
        }
    }' | jq '{
        name: .IndexingRule.Name,
        percentage: .IndexingRule.Rule.Probabilistic.DesiredSamplingPercentage,
        status: "All traces now indexed"
    }'
Output: { "name": "Default", "percentage": 100, "status": "All traces now indexed" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 100% indexing means all operations searchable: # - Every JobPost.objects.filter() query # - Every Skills.objects.create() call # - Every Author and Location lookup # # Full visibility for debugging
Example 3: Reduce indexing for cost savings

Lower indexing percentage during high-traffic periods.

#!/bin/bash
# Reduce indexing during peak hours to control costs
echo "Current indexing configuration:"
aws xray get-indexing-rules | jq '.IndexingRules[] | {
    name: .Name,
    currentPercentage: .Rule.Probabilistic.DesiredSamplingPercentage
}'

echo ""
echo "Reducing indexing to 25% for cost savings..."

aws xray update-indexing-rule \
    --name "Default" \
    --rule '{
        "Probabilistic": {
            "DesiredSamplingPercentage": 25
        }
    }' --output text --query 'IndexingRule.Rule.Probabilistic.DesiredSamplingPercentage'

echo "% of traces now indexed"
Output: Current indexing configuration: { "name": "Default", "currentPercentage": 100 } Reducing indexing to 25% for cost savings... 25 % of traces now indexed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # At 25% indexing during peak: # - 1 in 4 JobPost traces searchable # - Error traces still captured via sampling # - Cost reduced for high-volume periods # # Increase back after peak hours
Example 4: Script to adjust indexing by time

Automated indexing percentage based on business hours.

#!/bin/bash
# Adjust indexing based on time of day
HOUR=$(date +%H)

if [ $HOUR -ge 9 ] && [ $HOUR -lt 17 ]; then
    # Business hours - high traffic, lower indexing
    PERCENTAGE=50
    PERIOD="business hours"
else
    # Off-peak - index more for better debugging
    PERCENTAGE=90
    PERIOD="off-peak hours"
fi

echo "Adjusting indexing for $PERIOD"
echo "Setting percentage to: $PERCENTAGE%"

aws xray update-indexing-rule \
    --name "Default" \
    --rule "{
        \"Probabilistic\": {
            \"DesiredSamplingPercentage\": $PERCENTAGE
        }
    }" | jq '{
        rule: .IndexingRule.Name,
        percentage: .IndexingRule.Rule.Probabilistic.DesiredSamplingPercentage,
        period: "'$PERIOD'"
    }'
Output: Adjusting indexing for off-peak hours Setting percentage to: 90% { "rule": "Default", "percentage": 90, "period": "off-peak hours" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Time-based indexing strategy: # # Business hours (9-5): 50% indexed # - High JobPost API traffic # - Many concurrent users # # Off-peak: 90% indexed # - Better debugging coverage # - Batch operations visible
Example 5: Monitor and update indexing rules

Check current state and update multiple rules.

#!/bin/bash
echo "=== X-Ray Indexing Rules Management ==="
echo ""

# Get current rules
echo "Current indexing rules:"
aws xray get-indexing-rules | jq -r '.IndexingRules[] | 
    "  \(.Name): \(.Rule.Probabilistic.DesiredSamplingPercentage)% indexed"'

echo ""

# Update Default rule with verification
NEW_PERCENTAGE=75
echo "Updating Default rule to $NEW_PERCENTAGE%..."

RESULT=$(aws xray update-indexing-rule \
    --name "Default" \
    --rule "{
        \"Probabilistic\": {
            \"DesiredSamplingPercentage\": $NEW_PERCENTAGE
        }
    }" 2>&1)

if echo "$RESULT" | grep -q "IndexingRule"; then
    echo "✓ Update successful"
    echo "$RESULT" | jq '{
        name: .IndexingRule.Name,
        newPercentage: .IndexingRule.Rule.Probabilistic.DesiredSamplingPercentage,
        modifiedAt: .IndexingRule.ModifiedAt
    }'
else
    echo "✗ Update failed: $RESULT"
fi
Output: === X-Ray Indexing Rules Management === Current indexing rules: Default: 90% indexed Updating Default rule to 75%... ✓ Update successful { "name": "Default", "newPercentage": 75, "modifiedAt": "2024-01-16T15:30:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 75% indexing balanced approach: # - 3 out of 4 traces searchable # - JobPost CRUD operations covered # - Skills relationship queries visible # - Reasonable cost for production

update-sampling-rule

Updates an existing sampling rule for X-Ray. Use this to adjust trace collection rates for your Django application.

Syntax
aws xray update-sampling-rule \
    --sampling-rule-update <value>
Parameters Description
  • --sampling-rule-update (structure, required): Updates including RuleName or RuleARN, and optional FixedRate, ReservoirSize, Priority, ServiceName, ServiceType, Host, HTTPMethod, URLPath
Return Value
  • SamplingRuleRecord (object): Updated sampling rule record
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Increase sampling rate

Double the sampling rate for Django API endpoints.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray update-sampling-rule \
    --sampling-rule-update '{
        "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling",
        "FixedRate": 0.20,
        "ReservoirSize": 10
    }'
Output: { "SamplingRuleRecord": { "SamplingRule": { "RuleName": "anihpj-api-sampling", "RuleARN": "arn:aws:xray:us-east-1:123456789012:sampling-rule/anihpj-api-sampling", "Priority": 1000, "FixedRate": 0.20, "ReservoirSize": 10, "ServiceName": "anihpj-django", "URLPath": "/api/*" }, "ModifiedAt": "2025-01-18T10:30:00Z" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Doubled API sampling: 10% → 20% # Doubled reservoir: 5 → 10 per second # More traces for: # - /api/jobposts/ endpoints # - /api/skills/ endpoints # - /api/authors/ and /api/locations/
Example 2: Increase sampling for critical endpoints

Set 100% sampling for error-prone endpoints.

#!/bin/bash
# Full sampling for write operations
aws xray update-sampling-rule \
    --sampling-rule-update '{
        "RuleName": "anihpj-write-operations",
        "FixedRate": 1.0,
        "ReservoirSize": 50,
        "URLPath": "/api/jobposts/*",
        "HTTPMethod": "POST"
    }' | jq '{
        rule: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.RuleName,
        rate: "\(.SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.FixedRate * 100)%",
        reservoir: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.ReservoirSize,
        method: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.HTTPMethod,
        path: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.URLPath
    }'
Output: { "rule": "anihpj-write-operations", "rate": "100%", "reservoir": 50, "method": "POST", "path": "/api/jobposts/*" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 100% sampling for POST operations: # - JobPost.objects.create() # - Every write operation traced # - Full visibility for debugging # # Critical for tracking: # jobpost = JobPost.objects.create( # title=data['title'], # skills=data['skills'] # )
Example 3: Lower sampling for health checks

Reduce tracing overhead for repetitive endpoints.

#!/bin/bash
# Minimal sampling for health and status endpoints
aws xray update-sampling-rule \
    --sampling-rule-update '{
        "RuleName": "anihpj-health-checks",
        "FixedRate": 0.01,
        "ReservoirSize": 1,
        "URLPath": "/health*",
        "HTTPMethod": "*"
    }'

echo "Health check sampling reduced to 1%"
echo ""

# Also update status endpoints
aws xray update-sampling-rule \
    --sampling-rule-update '{
        "RuleName": "anihpj-status-endpoints",
        "FixedRate": 0.01,
        "ReservoirSize": 1,
        "URLPath": "/status*",
        "HTTPMethod": "GET"
    }'

echo "Status endpoint sampling reduced to 1%"

# Show updated rules
echo ""
echo "Low-priority rules updated:"
aws xray get-sampling-rules | jq '.SamplingRuleRecords[] | 
    select(.SamplingRule.FixedRate <= 0.01) | {
        name: .SamplingRule.RuleName,
        rate: "\(.SamplingRule.FixedRate * 100)%"
    }'
Output: Health check sampling reduced to 1% Status endpoint sampling reduced to 1% Low-priority rules updated: { "name": "anihpj-health-checks", "rate": "1%" } { "name": "anihpj-status-endpoints", "rate": "1%" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Low sampling for utility endpoints: # # Health checks (every 30s from ALB): # - Don't need most traces # - 1% sufficient for monitoring # # Saves budget for business operations: # - JobPost.objects.filter() # - Skills.objects.all() # - Author/Location queries
Example 4: Update sampling rule priority

Adjust rule priority to control matching order.

#!/bin/bash
# Higher priority (lower number) for specific rules
# Ensure error pages are always sampled first

echo "Updating rule priorities..."
echo ""

# Error endpoints - highest priority
aws xray update-sampling-rule \
    --sampling-rule-update '{
        "RuleName": "anihpj-error-pages",
        "Priority": 100,
        "FixedRate": 1.0,
        "ReservoirSize": 100
    }' | jq '{
        rule: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.RuleName,
        priority: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.Priority,
        rate: "100%",
        note: "Highest priority - matches first"
    }'

# API endpoints - medium priority
aws xray update-sampling-rule \
    --sampling-rule-update '{
        "RuleName": "anihpj-api-endpoints",
        "Priority": 500,
        "FixedRate": 0.25,
        "ReservoirSize": 25
    }' | jq '{
        rule: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.RuleName,
        priority: .SamplingRuleRecord.SamplingRule.Priority,
        rate: "25%"
    }'

echo ""
echo "Priority order: error-pages(100) → api-endpoints(500) → Default(10000)"
Output: Updating rule priorities... { "rule": "anihpj-error-pages", "priority": 100, "rate": "100%", "note": "Highest priority - matches first" } { "rule": "anihpj-api-endpoints", "priority": 500, "rate": "25%" } Priority order: error-pages(100) → api-endpoints(500) → Default(10000)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Priority determines matching order: # # 1. Error pages (priority 100): # - All JobPost.DoesNotExist # - All validation errors # - 100% sampled # # 2. API endpoints (priority 500): # - /api/jobposts/ calls # - /api/skills/ calls # - 25% sampled
Example 5: Batch update multiple sampling rules

Script to update all service rules consistently.

#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Batch Sampling Rule Update ==="
echo ""

# Define rule updates
declare -A RULES
RULES["anihpj-jobpost-api"]="0.30,30,/api/jobposts/*"
RULES["anihpj-skills-api"]="0.25,25,/api/skills/*"
RULES["anihpj-auth-api"]="0.50,50,/api/auth/*"
RULES["anihpj-search-api"]="0.20,20,/api/search/*"

UPDATED=0
FAILED=0

for RULE_NAME in "${!RULES[@]}"; do
    IFS=',' read -r RATE RESERVOIR PATH <<< "${RULES[$RULE_NAME]}"
    
    echo "Updating: $RULE_NAME"
    echo "  Rate: $(echo "$RATE * 100" | bc)%, Reservoir: $RESERVOIR, Path: $PATH"
    
    RESULT=$(aws xray update-sampling-rule \
        --sampling-rule-update "{
            \"RuleName\": \"$RULE_NAME\",
            \"FixedRate\": $RATE,
            \"ReservoirSize\": $RESERVOIR,
            \"URLPath\": \"$PATH\"
        }" 2>&1)
    
    if echo "$RESULT" | grep -q "SamplingRuleRecord"; then
        echo "  ✓ Updated successfully"
        ((UPDATED++))
    else
        echo "  ✗ Failed to update"
        ((FAILED++))
    fi
    echo ""
done

echo "Summary: $UPDATED updated, $FAILED failed"
Output: === Batch Sampling Rule Update === Updating: anihpj-jobpost-api Rate: 30%, Reservoir: 30, Path: /api/jobposts/* ✓ Updated successfully Updating: anihpj-skills-api Rate: 25%, Reservoir: 25, Path: /api/skills/* ✓ Updated successfully Updating: anihpj-auth-api Rate: 50%, Reservoir: 50, Path: /api/auth/* ✓ Updated successfully Updating: anihpj-search-api Rate: 20%, Reservoir: 20, Path: /api/search/* ✓ Updated successfully Summary: 4 updated, 0 failed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Sampling rates by importance: # # Auth (50%): High for security # - User login/logout # - Token operations # # JobPost (30%): Core business # - JobPost.objects.all() # - JobPost.objects.filter() # # Skills (25%): Supporting data # - Skills.objects queries # # Search (20%): High volume # - Search operations across models

update-trace-segment-destination

Updates the destination for trace segments. Use this to change where Django application traces are stored.

Syntax
aws xray update-trace-segment-destination \
    --destination <value>
Parameters Description
  • --destination (string, required): New destination - XRay or CloudWatchLogs
Return Value
  • Destination (string): Updated destination
  • Status (string): Status of the update (PENDING or ACTIVE)
5 Detailed X-Ray Examples
Example 1: Switch to CloudWatch Logs destination

Route Django application traces to CloudWatch Logs for unified logging.

#!/bin/bash
aws xray update-trace-segment-destination \
    --destination CloudWatchLogs
Output: { "Destination": "CloudWatchLogs", "Status": "PENDING" }
