Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Traces now route to CloudWatch Logs # Benefits: # - Unified logging with Django app logs # - Longer retention options # - Integration with CloudWatch Insights # JobPost, Skills traces in same log group
Example 2: Switch back to X-Ray destination

Restore default X-Ray trace storage for full service map features.

#!/bin/bash
# Check current destination first
echo "Current destination:"
aws xray get-trace-segment-destination | jq '.Destination'

# Switch back to X-Ray
aws xray update-trace-segment-destination \
    --destination XRay | jq '{
        destination: .Destination,
        status: .Status,
        message: "Switched back to X-Ray storage"
    }'
Output: Current destination: "CloudWatchLogs" { "destination": "XRay", "status": "PENDING", "message": "Switched back to X-Ray storage" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Back to X-Ray destination enables: # - Full service maps for Django app # - Trace analytics and insights # - Segment timeline visualization # # JobPost API traces visible in service graph
Example 3: Script to toggle destination

Automate destination switching based on environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Toggle trace destination based on environment
ENVIRONMENT="${1:-production}"

if [ "$ENVIRONMENT" == "production" ]; then
    DESTINATION="XRay"
    echo "Production: Using X-Ray for full trace analytics"
else
    DESTINATION="CloudWatchLogs"
    echo "Non-production: Using CloudWatch Logs for cost savings"
fi

aws xray update-trace-segment-destination \
    --destination "$DESTINATION"

# Wait for status to be active
echo "Waiting for destination change..."
sleep 5

STATUS=$(aws xray get-trace-segment-destination --query 'Status' --output text)
echo "Status: $STATUS"
echo "Destination: $DESTINATION"
Output: Production: Using X-Ray for full trace analytics Waiting for destination change... Status: ACTIVE Destination: XRay
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Environment-based routing: # # Production (XRay): # - Full JobPost trace analytics # - Service map visualization # - Anomaly detection enabled # # Development (CloudWatchLogs): # - Lower cost storage # - Unified with app logs
Example 4: Monitor destination status

Check and wait for destination change to complete.

#!/bin/bash
# Update destination and monitor status
echo "Updating trace segment destination..."

aws xray update-trace-segment-destination \
    --destination CloudWatchLogs

# Poll for status change
MAX_ATTEMPTS=12
ATTEMPT=0

while [ $ATTEMPT -lt $MAX_ATTEMPTS ]; do
    STATUS=$(aws xray get-trace-segment-destination --query 'Status' --output text)
    DEST=$(aws xray get-trace-segment-destination --query 'Destination' --output text)
    
    echo "Attempt $((ATTEMPT+1))/$MAX_ATTEMPTS: Status=$STATUS, Destination=$DEST"
    
    if [ "$STATUS" == "ACTIVE" ]; then
        echo ""
        echo "✓ Destination change complete!"
        aws xray get-trace-segment-destination | jq '.'
        exit 0
    fi
    
    sleep 5
    ((ATTEMPT++))
done

echo "⚠️ Timeout waiting for destination change"
Output: Updating trace segment destination... Attempt 1/12: Status=PENDING, Destination=CloudWatchLogs Attempt 2/12: Status=PENDING, Destination=CloudWatchLogs Attempt 3/12: Status=ACTIVE, Destination=CloudWatchLogs ✓ Destination change complete! { "Destination": "CloudWatchLogs", "Status": "ACTIVE" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Destination change monitoring ensures: # - No trace data loss during transition # - JobPost API traces continue flowing # - Skills/Author/Location queries tracked # # Typical transition time: 10-30 seconds
Example 5: Validate destination with test trace

Send test segment and verify routing to new destination.

#!/bin/bash
# Update destination and validate with test trace
NEW_DEST="CloudWatchLogs"

echo "=== Trace Destination Validation ==="
echo ""

# Update destination
echo "1. Updating destination to $NEW_DEST..."
aws xray update-trace-segment-destination \
    --destination "$NEW_DEST" > /dev/null

# Wait for active status
echo "2. Waiting for active status..."
sleep 10

# Get current status
echo "3. Current destination status:"
aws xray get-trace-segment-destination | jq '{
    destination: .Destination,
    status: .Status
}'

# Send test segment
echo ""
echo "4. Sending test trace segment..."
TRACE_ID="1-$(date +%s)-$(openssl rand -hex 12)"
SEGMENT_DOC='{
    "name": "anihpj-destination-test",
    "id": "'$(openssl rand -hex 8)'",
    "trace_id": "'$TRACE_ID'",
    "start_time": '$(date +%s.%N)',
    "end_time": '$(date +%s.%N)',
    "annotations": {
        "model": "JobPost",
        "operation": "destination_test"
    }
}'

aws xray put-trace-segments \
    --trace-segment-documents "$SEGMENT_DOC" > /dev/null

echo "   Test trace sent: $TRACE_ID"
echo ""
echo "5. Verify in $NEW_DEST console"
Output: === Trace Destination Validation === 1. Updating destination to CloudWatchLogs... 2. Waiting for active status... 3. Current destination status: { "destination": "CloudWatchLogs", "status": "ACTIVE" } 4. Sending test trace segment... Test trace sent: 1-1705432800-a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2 5. Verify in CloudWatchLogs console
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Validation test confirms: # - Destination change is active # - Test segment routed correctly # - Ready for Django app traces: # - JobPost.objects operations # - Skills.objects queries # - Author/Location lookups # # Check CloudWatch Logs or X-Ray console

Amazon Athena

Interactive query service to analyze data in Amazon S3 using standard SQL

📋 Athena Overview

Amazon Athena is an interactive query service that makes it easy to analyze data in Amazon S3 using standard SQL. Use it to query exported Django jobpost data, analyze Skills distributions, Author activity, Location metrics, and JobPost trends without loading data into a database.

Core Athena Operations

start-query-execution - Run SQL queries
get-query-execution - Check query status
get-query-results - Retrieve results
create-work-group - Create workgroups
create-data-catalog - Register data sources
create-named-query - Save queries

batch-get-named-query

Returns information about one or more named queries. Use this to retrieve details about saved SQL queries for analyzing Django JobPost data.

Syntax
aws athena batch-get-named-query \
    --named-query-ids <value> [<value>...]
Parameters Description
  • --named-query-ids (list, required): List of unique named query IDs to retrieve
Return Value
  • NamedQueries (list): List of named query objects with Name, QueryString, Database
  • UnprocessedNamedQueryIds (list): IDs that could not be processed
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get multiple saved queries

Retrieve details for saved JobPost analytics queries.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena batch-get-named-query \
    --named-query-ids \
        "query-jobpost-summary" \
        "query-skills-distribution" \
        "query-author-activity"
Output: { "NamedQueries": [ { "Name": "JobPost Summary", "NamedQueryId": "query-jobpost-summary", "Database": "anihpj_analytics", "QueryString": "SELECT status, COUNT(*) FROM jobpost_archive GROUP BY status", "WorkGroup": "primary" }, { "Name": "Skills Distribution", "NamedQueryId": "query-skills-distribution", "Database": "anihpj_analytics", "QueryString": "SELECT skill_name, COUNT(*) FROM skills_data GROUP BY skill_name" } ], "UnprocessedNamedQueryIds": [] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django queries: # JobPost.objects.values('status').annotate(count=Count('id')) # Skills.objects.values('name').annotate(count=Count('id'))
Example 2: Get single named query details

Retrieve a specific saved query for Location analysis.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena batch-get-named-query \
    --named-query-ids "location-job-density" | jq '.NamedQueries[0]'
Output: { "Name": "Location Job Density", "NamedQueryId": "location-job-density", "Database": "anihpj_analytics", "QueryString": "SELECT location_city, location_country, COUNT(*) as job_count FROM jobpost_archive GROUP BY location_city, location_country ORDER BY job_count DESC", "Description": "Analyze job distribution by location", "WorkGroup": "anihpj-workgroup" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django ORM: # Location.objects.annotate( # job_count=Count('jobpost') # ).order_by('-job_count')
Example 3: Validate queries before execution

Check if saved queries still exist and are valid.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_IDS=("daily-report" "weekly-summary" "monthly-stats" "invalid-query")

# Get all queries
RESULT=$(aws athena batch-get-named-query \
    --named-query-ids "${QUERY_IDS[@]}")

echo "Valid queries:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.NamedQueries[].Name'

echo ""
echo "Invalid/Missing queries:"
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.UnprocessedNamedQueryIds[].NamedQueryId'
Output: Valid queries: Daily JobPost Report Weekly Skills Summary Monthly Location Stats Invalid/Missing queries: invalid-query
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Scheduled reports for: # - Daily: New JobPost.objects.filter(created_at__date=today) # - Weekly: Skills trend analysis # - Monthly: Location-based aggregations
Example 4: Export query definitions

Backup all saved queries for Django analytics.

#!/bin/bash
# Get all named query IDs first
QUERY_IDS=$(aws athena list-named-queries --query 'NamedQueryIds' --output text)

# Batch get all queries (max 50 at a time)
aws athena batch-get-named-query \
    --named-query-ids $QUERY_IDS | jq '.NamedQueries[] | {
        name: .Name,
        database: .Database,
        query: .QueryString,
        workgroup: .WorkGroup
    }' > athena-queries-backup.json

echo "Exported $(echo $QUERY_IDS | wc -w) queries to athena-queries-backup.json"
Output: Exported 12 queries to athena-queries-backup.json
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backup includes queries for: # - JobPost CRUD analytics # - Skills relationship analysis # - Author performance metrics # - Location distribution reports
Example 5: Compare query versions

Get multiple versions of a query to compare changes.

#!/bin/bash
# Get current and previous versions of JobPost report queries
aws athena batch-get-named-query \
    --named-query-ids \
        "jobpost-report-v1" \
        "jobpost-report-v2" \
        "jobpost-report-current" | jq '.NamedQueries | sort_by(.Name) | .[] | {
            name: .Name,
            query_preview: (.QueryString | .[0:100] + "...")
        }'
Output: { "name": "jobpost-report-current", "query_preview": "SELECT jp.id, jp.title, a.name as author, l.city as location, COUNT(s.id) as skill_count FROM jobpost..." } { "name": "jobpost-report-v1", "query_preview": "SELECT id, title, author_id FROM jobpost_archive WHERE status = 'published'..." } { "name": "jobpost-report-v2", "query_preview": "SELECT jp.id, jp.title, a.name as author FROM jobpost_archive jp JOIN authors a ON jp.author..." }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Query evolution: # v1: Basic JobPost.objects.filter(status='published') # v2: Added Author join via select_related('author') # current: Full join with Skills and Location prefetch

batch-get-prepared-statement

Returns information about one or more prepared statements. Prepared statements allow parameterized SQL queries for secure Django data analysis.

Syntax
aws athena batch-get-prepared-statement \
    --work-group <value> \
    --prepared-statement-names <value> [<value>...]
Parameters Description
  • --work-group (string, required): The workgroup containing the prepared statements
  • --prepared-statement-names (list, required): List of prepared statement names
Return Value
  • PreparedStatements (list): List of prepared statement objects
  • UnprocessedPreparedStatementNames (list): Names that could not be retrieved
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get JobPost query prepared statements

Retrieve parameterized queries for JobPost filtering.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena batch-get-prepared-statement \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --prepared-statement-names \
        "get_jobposts_by_status" \
        "get_jobposts_by_author"
Output: { "PreparedStatements": [ { "StatementName": "get_jobposts_by_status", "QueryStatement": "SELECT * FROM jobpost_archive WHERE status = ?", "WorkGroupName": "anihpj-workgroup", "LastModifiedTime": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z" }, { "StatementName": "get_jobposts_by_author", "QueryStatement": "SELECT * FROM jobpost_archive WHERE author_id = ?", "WorkGroupName": "anihpj-workgroup" } ], "UnprocessedPreparedStatementNames": [] }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent parameterized Django queries: # JobPost.objects.filter(status=status_param) # JobPost.objects.filter(author_id=author_param)
Example 2: Validate Skills lookup statements

Check prepared statements for Skills analysis.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena batch-get-prepared-statement \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --prepared-statement-names "skills_by_jobpost" | jq '.PreparedStatements[0]'
Output: { "StatementName": "skills_by_jobpost", "QueryStatement": "SELECT s.skill_name, COUNT(*) as job_count FROM skills_jobpost sj JOIN skills s ON sj.skill_id = s.id WHERE sj.jobpost_id = ? GROUP BY s.skill_name", "WorkGroupName": "anihpj-workgroup", "LastModifiedTime": "2025-01-14T15:30:00Z", "Description": "Get skills for a specific JobPost" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django M2M query: # jobpost = JobPost.objects.get(pk=jobpost_id) # jobpost.skills.all()
Example 3: Audit all prepared statements

Get all statements to review for security compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# List all prepared statement names first
STATEMENTS=$(aws athena list-prepared-statements \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query 'PreparedStatements[].StatementName' --output text)

# Get details for all
aws athena batch-get-prepared-statement \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --prepared-statement-names $STATEMENTS | jq '.PreparedStatements[] | {
        name: .StatementName,
        modified: .LastModifiedTime,
        has_params: (.QueryStatement | contains("?"))
    }'
Output: { "name": "get_jobposts_by_status", "modified": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z", "has_params": true } { "name": "get_author_stats", "modified": "2025-01-14T09:00:00Z", "has_params": true } { "name": "location_summary", "modified": "2025-01-13T14:00:00Z", "has_params": false }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Prepared statements protect against SQL injection # Similar to Django's parameterized queries: # JobPost.objects.raw('SELECT * FROM jobpost WHERE status = %s', [status])
Example 4: Compare statement versions

Check multiple versions of a prepared statement.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena batch-get-prepared-statement \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --prepared-statement-names \
        "author_report_v1" \
        "author_report_v2" | jq '.PreparedStatements | sort_by(.LastModifiedTime) | .[]'
Output: { "StatementName": "author_report_v1", "QueryStatement": "SELECT author_id, COUNT(*) FROM jobpost_archive WHERE author_id = ? GROUP BY author_id", "LastModifiedTime": "2025-01-10T10:00:00Z" } { "StatementName": "author_report_v2", "QueryStatement": "SELECT a.name, a.email, COUNT(jp.id) as posts FROM authors a JOIN jobpost_archive jp ON a.id = jp.author_id WHERE a.id = ? GROUP BY a.name, a.email", "LastModifiedTime": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # v1: Simple count # Author.objects.filter(pk=author_id).annotate(count=Count('jobpost')) # v2: With details # Author.objects.filter(pk=author_id).annotate(posts=Count('jobpost')).values('name', 'email', 'posts')
Example 5: Export prepared statements for backup

Backup all prepared statements definitions.

#!/bin/bash
WORK_GROUP="anihpj-workgroup"
OUTPUT_FILE="prepared-statements-backup.json"

# Get all statement names
NAMES=$(aws athena list-prepared-statements \
    --work-group "$WORK_GROUP" \
    --query 'PreparedStatements[].StatementName' --output text)

# Export full details
aws athena batch-get-prepared-statement \
    --work-group "$WORK_GROUP" \
    --prepared-statement-names $NAMES > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Backed up $(echo $NAMES | wc -w) prepared statements to $OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Backed up 8 prepared statements to prepared-statements-backup.json
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backup includes statements for: # - JobPost queries (by status, author, location) # - Skills M2M lookups # - Author analytics # - Location aggregations

batch-get-query-execution

Returns information about one or more query executions. Use this to monitor multiple Django analytics queries running in Athena.

Syntax
aws athena batch-get-query-execution \
    --query-execution-ids <value> [<value>...]
Parameters Description
  • --query-execution-ids (list, required): List of query execution IDs to retrieve
Return Value
  • QueryExecutions (list): List of query execution objects with status, statistics
  • UnprocessedQueryExecutionIds (list): IDs that could not be processed
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Check multiple query statuses

Monitor batch of Django analytics queries.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena batch-get-query-execution \
    --query-execution-ids \
        "exec-jobpost-001" \
        "exec-skills-002" \
        "exec-author-003" | jq '.QueryExecutions[] | {
            id: .QueryExecutionId,
            status: .Status.State,
            runtime: .Statistics.EngineExecutionTimeInMillis
        }'
Output: { "id": "exec-jobpost-001", "status": "SUCCEEDED", "runtime": 2500 } { "id": "exec-skills-002", "status": "SUCCEEDED", "runtime": 1200 } { "id": "exec-author-003", "status": "RUNNING", "runtime": null }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Monitoring queries for: # - JobPost aggregations (2.5s) # - Skills distribution (1.2s) # - Author stats (still running)
Example 2: Get detailed execution statistics

Analyze query performance for optimization.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena batch-get-query-execution \
    --query-execution-ids "exec-large-scan-001" | jq '.QueryExecutions[0] | {
        query: .Query[0:100],
        status: .Status.State,
        data_scanned_mb: (.Statistics.DataScannedInBytes / 1048576),
        execution_time_sec: (.Statistics.EngineExecutionTimeInMillis / 1000),
        cost_estimate: ((.Statistics.DataScannedInBytes / 1099511627776) * 5)
    }'
Output: { "query": "SELECT * FROM jobpost_archive WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01' AND status = 'published'...", "status": "SUCCEEDED", "data_scanned_mb": 256.5, "execution_time_sec": 8.5, "cost_estimate": 0.00128 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Query scanned 256MB of JobPost data # Equivalent to: # JobPost.objects.filter( # created_at__gt='2024-01-01', # status='published' # )
Example 3: Monitor failed queries

Check for query failures and error messages.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_IDS=$(aws athena list-query-executions \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query 'QueryExecutionIds[0:10]' --output text)

aws athena batch-get-query-execution \
    --query-execution-ids $QUERY_IDS | jq '.QueryExecutions[] | 
        select(.Status.State == "FAILED") | {
            id: .QueryExecutionId,
            error: .Status.StateChangeReason,
            query: .Query[0:80]
        }'
Output: { "id": "exec-failed-001", "error": "SYNTAX_ERROR: Column 'skill_name' not found", "query": "SELECT skill_name FROM jobpost_archive WHERE id = 1" } { "id": "exec-failed-002", "error": "TABLE_NOT_FOUND: Table 'old_authors' does not exist", "query": "SELECT * FROM old_authors" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Errors similar to Django: # skill_name not in JobPost - use Skills model # old_authors table removed - use Author model
Example 4: Calculate total query costs

Sum data scanned across multiple queries for billing.

#!/bin/bash
# Get last 20 query executions
QUERY_IDS=$(aws athena list-query-executions \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query 'QueryExecutionIds[0:20]' --output text)

# Calculate total data scanned
aws athena batch-get-query-execution \
    --query-execution-ids $QUERY_IDS | jq '
    .QueryExecutions | 
    map(select(.Status.State == "SUCCEEDED")) |
    {
        total_queries: length,
        total_data_gb: ([.[].Statistics.DataScannedInBytes] | add / 1073741824),
        total_cost: (([.[].Statistics.DataScannedInBytes] | add / 1099511627776) * 5)
    }'
Output: { "total_queries": 18, "total_data_gb": 1.25, "total_cost": 0.00625 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 18 queries analyzed Django data: # - JobPost table scans # - Skills M2M joins # - Author/Location aggregations # Cost: $0.006 for 1.25GB scanned
Example 5: Generate execution report

Create summary report of query executions.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_IDS=$(aws athena list-query-executions \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query 'QueryExecutionIds[0:50]' --output text)

echo "=== Athena Query Execution Report ==="
aws athena batch-get-query-execution \
    --query-execution-ids $QUERY_IDS | jq -r '
    .QueryExecutions | group_by(.Status.State) | .[] | 
    "\(.[0].Status.State): \(length) queries"'

echo ""
echo "Average execution time:"
aws athena batch-get-query-execution \
    --query-execution-ids $QUERY_IDS | jq '
    [.QueryExecutions[].Statistics.EngineExecutionTimeInMillis // 0] | 
    (add / length / 1000) | "\(.)s"'
Output: === Athena Query Execution Report === SUCCEEDED: 45 queries FAILED: 3 queries CANCELLED: 2 queries Average execution time: "3.2s"
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Report shows Django analytics health: # - 90% success rate (45/50) # - 3.2s average query time # - Most queries on JobPost, Skills tables

cancel-capacity-reservation

Cancels a capacity reservation that was created for running queries. Use this when Django analytics workloads no longer require dedicated capacity.

Syntax
aws athena cancel-capacity-reservation \
    --name <value>
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): The name of the capacity reservation to cancel
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Cancel unused capacity reservation

Remove capacity no longer needed for Django queries.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena cancel-capacity-reservation \
    --name "anihpj-analytics-capacity"

echo "Capacity reservation cancelled"
Output: Capacity reservation cancelled
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cancelled reservation was used for: # - JobPost analytics queries # - Skills distribution analysis # - Author performance reports
Example 2: Cancel with verification

Verify reservation exists before cancelling.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION_NAME="jobpost-weekly-capacity"

# Check if reservation exists
if aws athena get-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION_NAME" 2>/dev/null; then
    aws athena cancel-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION_NAME"
    echo "Cancelled: $RESERVATION_NAME"
else
    echo "Reservation not found: $RESERVATION_NAME"
fi
Output: Cancelled: jobpost-weekly-capacity
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Weekly capacity for JobPost batch processing # Now cancelled after report generation completed
Example 3: Cancel expired capacity reservations

Clean up all expired reservations.

#!/bin/bash
# List and cancel reservations in CANCELLED state
aws athena list-capacity-reservations | jq -r '
    .CapacityReservations[] | 
    select(.Status == "PENDING") | .Name' | while read NAME; do
    echo "Cancelling: $NAME"
    aws athena cancel-capacity-reservation --name "$NAME"
done
Output: Cancelling: old-analytics-capacity Cancelling: temp-migration-capacity Cancelling: test-capacity-001
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up capacity from: # - Old analytics runs # - Data migration jobs # - Test query workloads
Example 4: Cancel and log for audit

Cancel with full audit logging.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION_NAME="quarterly-report-capacity"
AUDIT_LOG="capacity-audit.log"

# Get reservation details before cancelling
DETAILS=$(aws athena get-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION_NAME" 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$DETAILS" ]; then
    echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] Cancelling: $RESERVATION_NAME" >> "$AUDIT_LOG"
    echo "$DETAILS" | jq -c '.' >> "$AUDIT_LOG"
    
    aws athena cancel-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION_NAME"
    echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] Cancelled successfully" >> "$AUDIT_LOG"
fi

cat "$AUDIT_LOG"
Output: [2025-01-15T10:30:00+00:00] Cancelling: quarterly-report-capacity {"CapacityReservation":{"Name":"quarterly-report-capacity","Status":"ACTIVE","TargetDpus":24}} [2025-01-15T10:30:01+00:00] Cancelled successfully
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit trail for capacity management # Reservation had 24 DPUs for quarterly JobPost reports
Example 5: Scheduled capacity cleanup

Cancel capacity reservations past their needed date.

#!/bin/bash
CUTOFF_DATE=$(date -d "-7 days" +%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z)

echo "Cancelling reservations created before: $CUTOFF_DATE"

aws athena list-capacity-reservations | jq -r --arg cutoff "$CUTOFF_DATE" '
    .CapacityReservations[] | 
    select(.CreationTime < $cutoff and .Status == "ACTIVE") | .Name' | while read NAME; do
    echo "Cancelling old reservation: $NAME"
    aws athena cancel-capacity-reservation --name "$NAME" 2>/dev/null || echo "Failed: $NAME"
done
Output: Cancelling reservations created before: 2025-01-08T00:00:00Z Cancelling old reservation: temp-jobpost-analysis Cancelling old reservation: author-metrics-capacity
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Scheduled cleanup of capacity used for: # - Temporary JobPost analysis # - Author metrics calculations # Prevents unnecessary charges

create-capacity-reservation

Creates a capacity reservation with specified DPUs for workgroups. Use for dedicated capacity when running large Django analytics workloads.

Syntax
aws athena create-capacity-reservation \
    --target-dpus <value> \
    --name <value> \
    [--tags <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --target-dpus (integer, required): Number of DPUs to reserve (24 minimum)
  • --name (string, required): Unique name for the reservation
  • --tags (list, optional): Tags for the reservation
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Create basic capacity reservation

Reserve 24 DPUs for Django analytics workloads.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-capacity-reservation \
    --name "anihpj-analytics-capacity" \
    --target-dpus 24

echo "Capacity reservation created with 24 DPUs"
Output: Capacity reservation created with 24 DPUs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 24 DPUs for parallel processing of: # - JobPost.objects.all() exports # - Skills aggregation queries # - Author/Location analytics
Example 2: High-capacity reservation with tags

Create large reservation for quarterly reports.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-capacity-reservation \
    --name "quarterly-report-capacity" \
    --target-dpus 96 \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Purpose,Value=QuarterlyReports Key=CostCenter,Value=Analytics
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 96 DPUs for quarterly reports analyzing: # - All JobPost records (millions) # - Skills trends over time # - Author activity metrics # - Location-based statistics
Example 3: Create and verify reservation

Create reservation and confirm it's active.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION_NAME="jobpost-daily-capacity"

# Create reservation
aws athena create-capacity-reservation \
    --name "$RESERVATION_NAME" \
    --target-dpus 48

# Wait and verify
sleep 5
aws athena get-capacity-reservation \
    --name "$RESERVATION_NAME" | jq '{
        name: .CapacityReservation.Name,
        status: .CapacityReservation.Status,
        dpus: .CapacityReservation.TargetDpus
    }'
Output: { "name": "jobpost-daily-capacity", "status": "ACTIVE", "dpus": 48 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 48 DPUs allocated for daily JobPost processing # Handles queries equivalent to: # JobPost.objects.filter(created_at__date=today)
Example 4: Environment-specific capacity

Create reservations per environment.

#!/bin/bash
ENVIRONMENT="${1:-production}"

case $ENVIRONMENT in
    production)
        DPUS=96
        ;;
    staging)
        DPUS=48
        ;;
    *)
        DPUS=24
        ;;
esac

aws athena create-capacity-reservation \
    --name "anihpj-${ENVIRONMENT}-capacity" \
    --target-dpus $DPUS \
    --tags Key=Environment,Value=$ENVIRONMENT Key=Application,Value=anihpj

echo "Created ${ENVIRONMENT} capacity with ${DPUS} DPUs"
Output: Created production capacity with 96 DPUs
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production: 96 DPUs for live JobPost analytics # Staging: 48 DPUs for testing # Dev: 24 DPUs minimum for development
Example 5: Create capacity with monitoring

Create and set up CloudWatch monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION_NAME="monitored-analytics-capacity"

# Create capacity reservation
aws athena create-capacity-reservation \
    --name "$RESERVATION_NAME" \
    --target-dpus 72 \
    --tags Key=Monitored,Value=true

# Create CloudWatch alarm for utilization
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
    --alarm-name "${RESERVATION_NAME}-high-utilization" \
    --metric-name "DPUUtilization" \
    --namespace "AWS/Athena" \
    --dimensions Name=CapacityReservation,Value="$RESERVATION_NAME" \
    --statistic Average \
    --period 300 \
    --threshold 80 \
    --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
    --evaluation-periods 2

echo "Capacity created with monitoring"
Output: Capacity created with monitoring
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 72 DPUs with monitoring for Django analytics # Alert when utilization > 80% for 10 minutes # Scale up capacity if consistently high

create-data-catalog

Creates a data catalog to connect to external data sources. Use this to integrate Django archived data from various sources into Athena queries.

Syntax
aws athena create-data-catalog \
    --name <value> \
    --type <value> \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--parameters <value>] \
    [--tags <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Unique name for the data catalog
  • --type (string, required): Type of catalog (LAMBDA, GLUE, HIVE, FEDERATED)
  • --description (string, optional): Description of the catalog
  • --parameters (map, optional): Configuration parameters
  • --tags (list, optional): Tags for the catalog
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Create Glue data catalog

Set up AWS Glue catalog for Django data.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-data-catalog \
    --name "anihpj-glue-catalog" \
    --type GLUE \
    --description "Glue catalog for Django JobPost archives" \
    --parameters catalog-id=123456789012

echo "Glue data catalog created"
Output: Glue data catalog created
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Glue catalog indexes Django data exported to S3: # - jobpost_archive table # - skills_archive table # - authors_archive table
Example 2: Create Lambda-based federated catalog

Connect to external Django databases via Lambda.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-data-catalog \
    --name "anihpj-federated-catalog" \
    --type LAMBDA \
    --description "Federated queries to Django PostgreSQL" \
    --parameters "function=arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:athena-postgres-connector" \
    --tags Key=Application,Value=anihpj Key=DataSource,Value=PostgreSQL
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Federated queries to live Django PostgreSQL: # - Query JobPost directly from production DB # - Join with archived data in S3 # - Real-time analytics on Author activity
Example 3: Create Hive metastore catalog

Connect to on-premises Hive for Django data.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-data-catalog \
    --name "anihpj-hive-catalog" \
    --type HIVE \
    --description "On-premises Hive metastore with Django exports" \
    --parameters "metadata-function=arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:hive-metastore" \
    --parameters "record-function=arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:hive-reader"
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Hive catalog for hybrid Django architecture: # - On-prem historical JobPost data # - Hadoop-based Skills analytics # - Legacy Location data warehouse
Example 4: Create catalog with full configuration

Complete catalog setup for Django analytics.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-data-catalog \
    --name "anihpj-analytics-catalog" \
    --type GLUE \
    --description "Primary analytics catalog for Django application" \
    --parameters '{
        "catalog-id": "123456789012"
    }' \
    --tags \
        Key=Application,Value=anihpj \
        Key=Environment,Value=production \
        Key=Team,Value=DataEngineering \
        Key=CostCenter,Value=Analytics

# Verify creation
aws athena get-data-catalog --name "anihpj-analytics-catalog" | jq '.DataCatalog | {name, type, status: "CREATED"}'
Output: { "name": "anihpj-analytics-catalog", "type": "GLUE", "status": "CREATED" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production catalog with full tagging: # - Tracks Django data across all models # - Cost allocated to Analytics center # - Managed by Data Engineering team
Example 5: Create multiple environment catalogs

Set up catalogs for dev, staging, production.

#!/bin/bash
ENVIRONMENTS=("dev" "staging" "production")
ACCOUNT_IDS=("111111111111" "222222222222" "333333333333")

for i in ${!ENVIRONMENTS[@]}; do
    ENV=${ENVIRONMENTS[$i]}
    ACCOUNT=${ACCOUNT_IDS[$i]}
    
    echo "Creating catalog for $ENV..."
    aws athena create-data-catalog \
        --name "anihpj-${ENV}-catalog" \
        --type GLUE \
        --description "Django data catalog for ${ENV} environment" \
        --parameters "catalog-id=${ACCOUNT}" \
        --tags Key=Environment,Value=$ENV
done

echo "All catalogs created"
Output: Creating catalog for dev... Creating catalog for staging... Creating catalog for production... All catalogs created
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Environment-specific catalogs: # - dev: Test JobPost data, small datasets # - staging: Production-like data, testing # - production: Full Django data access

create-named-query

Creates a named query in the specified workgroup. Save frequently used Django analytics queries for reuse.

Syntax
aws athena create-named-query \
    --name <value> \
    --database <value> \
    --query-string <value> \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--work-group <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Descriptive name for the query
  • --database (string, required): Database to run the query against
  • --query-string (string, required): SQL query to save
  • --description (string, optional): Description of what the query does
  • --work-group (string, optional): Workgroup for the query
Return Value
  • NamedQueryId (string): Unique ID of the created query
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Create JobPost summary query

Save query for JobPost status summary.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-named-query \
    --name "jobpost-status-summary" \
    --database "anihpj_archive" \
    --description "Get count of JobPosts by status" \
    --query-string "SELECT status, COUNT(*) as count FROM jobpost_archive GROUP BY status ORDER BY count DESC" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup"
Output: { "NamedQueryId": "nq-jobpost-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django query: # JobPost.objects.values('status').annotate( # count=Count('id') # ).order_by('-count')
Example 2: Create Skills analytics query

Save query for top skills analysis.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-named-query \
    --name "top-skills-by-jobcount" \
    --database "anihpj_archive" \
    --description "Top 20 skills by job post count" \
    --query-string "
SELECT 
    s.skill_name, 
    COUNT(DISTINCT sj.jobpost_id) as job_count
FROM skills_jobpost sj
JOIN skills s ON sj.skill_id = s.id
GROUP BY s.skill_name
ORDER BY job_count DESC
LIMIT 20" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq '.'
Output: { "NamedQueryId": "nq-skills-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django query: # Skills.objects.annotate( # job_count=Count('jobpost') # ).order_by('-job_count')[:20]
Example 3: Create Author performance query

Save query for author activity metrics.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-named-query \
    --name "author-performance-metrics" \
    --database "anihpj_archive" \
    --description "Author performance with post counts and engagement" \
    --query-string "
SELECT 
    a.id as author_id,
    a.name,
    a.email,
    COUNT(jp.id) as total_posts,
    SUM(CASE WHEN jp.status = 'published' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as published_posts,
    AVG(jp.view_count) as avg_views
FROM authors a
LEFT JOIN jobpost_archive jp ON a.id = jp.author_id
GROUP BY a.id, a.name, a.email
HAVING COUNT(jp.id) > 0
ORDER BY total_posts DESC" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup"
Output: { "NamedQueryId": "nq-author-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django query: # Author.objects.annotate( # total_posts=Count('jobpost'), # published_posts=Count('jobpost', filter=Q(jobpost__status='published')), # avg_views=Avg('jobpost__view_count') # ).filter(total_posts__gt=0).order_by('-total_posts')
Example 4: Create Location-based query

Save query for geographic analysis.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-named-query \
    --name "location-job-distribution" \
    --database "anihpj_archive" \
    --description "Job distribution by location with remote vs onsite" \
    --query-string "
SELECT 
    l.city,
    l.country,
    COUNT(jp.id) as job_count,
    SUM(CASE WHEN jp.is_remote THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as remote_count,
    SUM(CASE WHEN NOT jp.is_remote THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as onsite_count,
    ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN jp.is_remote THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 100.0 / COUNT(jp.id), 2) as remote_pct
FROM locations l
JOIN jobpost_archive jp ON l.id = jp.location_id
GROUP BY l.city, l.country
ORDER BY job_count DESC" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup"
Output: { "NamedQueryId": "nq-location-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django query: # Location.objects.annotate( # job_count=Count('jobpost'), # remote_count=Count('jobpost', filter=Q(jobpost__is_remote=True)), # onsite_count=Count('jobpost', filter=Q(jobpost__is_remote=False)) # ).order_by('-job_count')
Example 5: Create time-series analysis query

Save query for trending analysis.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-named-query \
    --name "monthly-posting-trends" \
    --database "anihpj_archive" \
    --description "Monthly JobPost trends for the last 12 months" \
    --query-string "
SELECT 
    DATE_TRUNC('month', created_at) as month,
    COUNT(*) as total_posts,
    COUNT(DISTINCT author_id) as unique_authors,
    COUNT(DISTINCT location_id) as unique_locations,
    AVG(salary_max) as avg_max_salary
FROM jobpost_archive
WHERE created_at >= DATE_ADD('month', -12, CURRENT_DATE)
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', created_at)
ORDER BY month DESC" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup"

echo "Time-series query created"
Output: { "NamedQueryId": "nq-trends-001" } Time-series query created
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models.functions import TruncMonth # Equivalent Django query: # JobPost.objects.filter( # created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(days=365) # ).annotate( # month=TruncMonth('created_at') # ).values('month').annotate( # total_posts=Count('id'), # unique_authors=Count('author', distinct=True) # ).order_by('-month')

create-notebook

Creates an empty Apache Spark notebook in Athena. Use notebooks for interactive Django data exploration and analysis.

Syntax
aws athena create-notebook \
    --work-group <value> \
    --name <value> \
    [--client-request-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --work-group (string, required): Workgroup to create the notebook in
  • --name (string, required): Name for the notebook
  • --client-request-token (string, optional): Idempotency token
Return Value
  • NotebookId (string): Unique ID of the created notebook
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Create JobPost analysis notebook

Set up notebook for interactive JobPost exploration.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-notebook \
    --work-group "anihpj-spark-workgroup" \
    --name "JobPost-Analytics-Notebook"
Output: { "NotebookId": "nb-jobpost-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Notebook will contain Spark SQL for: # - Interactive JobPost queries # - Data visualization # - ML model exploration
Example 2: Create Skills ML notebook

Notebook for Skills clustering and analysis.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-notebook \
    --work-group "anihpj-spark-workgroup" \
    --name "Skills-ML-Analysis" \
    --client-request-token "$(uuidgen)"
Output: { "NotebookId": "nb-skills-ml-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # ML notebook for Skills analysis: # - Skill clustering by job category # - Trend prediction models # - Co-occurrence analysis
Example 3: Create reporting notebook

Set up notebook for automated reporting.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_NAME="Monthly-Django-Report-$(date +%Y%m)"

aws athena create-notebook \
    --work-group "anihpj-spark-workgroup" \
    --name "$NOTEBOOK_NAME" | jq '.'
Output: { "NotebookId": "nb-report-202501" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Monthly report notebook includes: # - JobPost count summaries # - Author activity metrics # - Location distribution charts
Example 4: Create team-specific notebooks

Create notebooks for different teams.

#!/bin/bash
TEAMS=("data-science" "analytics" "engineering")

for TEAM in "${TEAMS[@]}"; do
    echo "Creating notebook for $TEAM..."
    aws athena create-notebook \
        --work-group "anihpj-spark-workgroup" \
        --name "Django-Analysis-${TEAM}" | jq -r '.NotebookId'
done
Output: Creating notebook for data-science... nb-ds-001 Creating notebook for analytics... nb-analytics-001 Creating notebook for engineering... nb-eng-001
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Team notebooks for different use cases: # - data-science: ML models on JobPost data # - analytics: Business intelligence queries # - engineering: Performance analysis
Example 5: Create and configure notebook

Create notebook and set up initial metadata.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_NAME="Django-Data-Exploration"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"

# Create notebook
NOTEBOOK_ID=$(aws athena create-notebook \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP" \
    --name "$NOTEBOOK_NAME" \
    --query 'NotebookId' --output text)

echo "Created notebook: $NOTEBOOK_ID"

# Get notebook details
aws athena get-notebook-metadata \
    --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq '{
        id: .NotebookMetadata.NotebookId,
        name: .NotebookMetadata.Name,
        workgroup: .NotebookMetadata.WorkGroup,
        created: .NotebookMetadata.CreationTime
    }'
Output: Created notebook: nb-explore-001 { "id": "nb-explore-001", "name": "Django-Data-Exploration", "workgroup": "anihpj-spark-workgroup", "created": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Exploration notebook ready for: # - Ad-hoc JobPost queries # - Skills relationship mapping # - Author/Location data analysis

create-prepared-statement

Creates a prepared statement for parameterized queries. Use prepared statements for secure, reusable Django analytics queries.

Syntax
aws athena create-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name <value> \
    --work-group <value> \
    --query-statement <value> \
    [--description <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --statement-name (string, required): Name for the prepared statement
  • --work-group (string, required): Workgroup for the statement
  • --query-statement (string, required): Parameterized SQL query
  • --description (string, optional): Description of the statement
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Create JobPost filter statement

Parameterized query for filtering JobPosts.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "get_jobposts_by_status" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query-statement "SELECT * FROM jobpost_archive WHERE status = ?" \
    --description "Get JobPosts filtered by status parameter"
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent to parameterized Django query: # JobPost.objects.filter(status=status_param) # Prevents SQL injection attacks
Example 2: Create Author lookup statement

Parameterized query for author-based filtering.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "get_author_jobposts" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query-statement "
SELECT jp.*, a.name as author_name
FROM jobpost_archive jp
JOIN authors a ON jp.author_id = a.id
WHERE a.id = ?
ORDER BY jp.created_at DESC
LIMIT ?" \
    --description "Get all JobPosts by author ID with limit"
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django query: # JobPost.objects.filter(author_id=author_id).select_related('author')[:limit]
Example 3: Create Skills search statement

Parameterized query for skills-based search.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "search_jobs_by_skill" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query-statement "
SELECT jp.id, jp.title, jp.status, s.skill_name
FROM jobpost_archive jp
JOIN skills_jobpost sj ON jp.id = sj.jobpost_id
JOIN skills s ON sj.skill_id = s.id
WHERE s.skill_name LIKE ?
ORDER BY jp.created_at DESC" \
    --description "Find JobPosts requiring a specific skill"
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django query: # JobPost.objects.filter( # skills__skill_name__icontains=skill_search # ).select_related().order_by('-created_at')
Example 4: Create date range statement

Parameterized query for date-based filtering.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "get_jobposts_in_daterange" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query-statement "
SELECT 
    DATE_TRUNC('day', created_at) as date,
    COUNT(*) as post_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT author_id) as unique_authors
FROM jobpost_archive
WHERE created_at BETWEEN ? AND ?
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('day', created_at)
ORDER BY date" \
    --description "Get daily JobPost counts between two dates"
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models.functions import TruncDay # Equivalent Django query: # JobPost.objects.filter( # created_at__range=[start_date, end_date] # ).annotate( # date=TruncDay('created_at') # ).values('date').annotate( # post_count=Count('id'), # unique_authors=Count('author', distinct=True) # )
Example 5: Create Location-based statement

Parameterized query for location filtering.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "get_jobs_by_location" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" \
    --query-statement "
SELECT 
    jp.id, jp.title, jp.status, jp.salary_min, jp.salary_max,
    l.city, l.country,
    a.name as author_name
FROM jobpost_archive jp
JOIN locations l ON jp.location_id = l.id
JOIN authors a ON jp.author_id = a.id
WHERE l.country = ? AND (l.city = ? OR ? IS NULL)
ORDER BY jp.created_at DESC
LIMIT ?" \
    --description "Get JobPosts by country and optional city"

echo "Location prepared statement created"
Output: Location prepared statement created
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Q # Equivalent Django query: # filters = Q(location__country=country) # if city: # filters &= Q(location__city=city) # JobPost.objects.filter(filters).select_related('location', 'author')[:limit]

create-presigned-notebook-url

Creates a presigned URL to access an Athena notebook. Use this to share Django analytics notebooks securely.

Syntax
aws athena create-presigned-notebook-url \
    --session-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --session-id (string, required): The session ID for the notebook
Return Value
  • NotebookUrl (string): Presigned URL to access the notebook
  • AuthToken (string): Authentication token for the URL
  • AuthTokenExpirationTime (timestamp): When the token expires
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get notebook access URL

Generate URL to access Django analytics notebook.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-presigned-notebook-url \
    --session-id "session-jobpost-analytics-001"
Output: { "NotebookUrl": "https://athena-notebook.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/nb-001", "AuthToken": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...", "AuthTokenExpirationTime": "2025-01-15T12:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Notebook URL provides access to: # - Interactive JobPost analysis # - Spark SQL on Django data # - Data visualization tools
Example 2: Share notebook with team

Generate shareable URL for collaboration.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-skills-analysis-001"

RESULT=$(aws athena create-presigned-notebook-url \
    --session-id "$SESSION_ID")

URL=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.NotebookUrl')
EXPIRES=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.AuthTokenExpirationTime')

echo "Share this URL with your team:"
echo "$URL"
echo "Expires: $EXPIRES"
Output: Share this URL with your team: https://athena-notebook.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/nb-skills-001 Expires: 2025-01-15T14:00:00Z
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Team can access Skills analysis notebook # No AWS credentials needed for access
Example 3: Create URL with expiry check

Generate URL and check remaining time.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-author-reports-001"

aws athena create-presigned-notebook-url \
    --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq '{
        url: .NotebookUrl,
        expires: .AuthTokenExpirationTime,
        valid_minutes: (((.AuthTokenExpirationTime | fromdateiso8601) - now) / 60 | floor)
    }'
Output: { "url": "https://athena-notebook.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/nb-author-001", "expires": "2025-01-15T13:30:00Z", "valid_minutes": 90 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Author reports notebook accessible for 90 minutes # Contains Author performance metrics
Example 4: Automate URL generation for sessions

Generate URLs for multiple active sessions.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"

# Get active sessions
aws athena list-sessions \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP" \
    --state-filter IDLE \
    --query 'Sessions[].SessionId' --output text | while read SESSION; do
    echo "Session: $SESSION"
    aws athena create-presigned-notebook-url \
        --session-id "$SESSION" | jq -r '.NotebookUrl'
    echo "---"
done
Output: Session: session-001 https://athena-notebook.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/nb-001 --- Session: session-002 https://athena-notebook.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/nb-002 ---
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # URLs for all idle notebook sessions # Each session may analyze different Django models
Example 5: Save URL to file for sharing

Export notebook URL for documentation.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-quarterly-review-001"
OUTPUT_FILE="notebook-access-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

aws athena create-presigned-notebook-url \
    --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq '{
        notebook_session: env.SESSION_ID,
        access_url: .NotebookUrl,
        generated_at: (now | strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")),
        expires_at: .AuthTokenExpirationTime,
        purpose: "Quarterly Django JobPost Review"
    }' > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Access details saved to $OUTPUT_FILE"
cat "$OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Access details saved to notebook-access-20250115.json { "notebook_session": "session-quarterly-review-001", "access_url": "https://athena-notebook.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/nb-qtr-001", "generated_at": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z", "expires_at": "2025-01-15T14:00:00Z", "purpose": "Quarterly Django JobPost Review" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Documented access for quarterly review # Notebook contains JobPost analytics dashboard

create-work-group

Creates a workgroup with specified settings. Workgroups organize Django analytics queries with shared configurations.

Syntax
aws athena create-work-group \
    --name <value> \
    [--configuration <value>] \
    [--description <value>] \
    [--tags <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Unique workgroup name
  • --configuration (structure, optional): Workgroup configuration settings
  • --description (string, optional): Workgroup description
  • --tags (list, optional): Tags for the workgroup
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Create basic workgroup

Set up workgroup for Django analytics team.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-work-group \
    --name "anihpj-analytics" \
    --description "Django JobPost analytics workgroup"
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Workgroup for all Django analytics: # - JobPost queries # - Skills analysis # - Author/Location reports
Example 2: Create workgroup with output location

Configure S3 output for query results.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-work-group \
    --name "anihpj-production" \
    --description "Production Django analytics" \
    --configuration '{
        "ResultConfiguration": {
            "OutputLocation": "s3://anihpj-athena-results/production/",
            "EncryptionConfiguration": {
                "EncryptionOption": "SSE_S3"
            }
        },
        "EnforceWorkGroupConfiguration": true
    }'
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production workgroup with: # - Encrypted S3 storage for results # - Enforced configuration (users can't override) # - Centralized Django query outputs
Example 3: Create workgroup with cost controls

Set query limits and byte scanning limits.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-work-group \
    --name "anihpj-dev" \
    --description "Development workgroup with cost controls" \
    --configuration '{
        "ResultConfiguration": {
            "OutputLocation": "s3://anihpj-athena-results/dev/"
        },
        "BytesScannedCutoffPerQuery": 10737418240,
        "RequesterPaysEnabled": false,
        "PublishCloudWatchMetricsEnabled": true
    }' \
    --tags Key=Environment,Value=development Key=CostCenter,Value=dev-team
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dev workgroup limits: # - 10GB max scan per query # - CloudWatch metrics enabled # - Tagged for cost tracking
Example 4: Create Spark-enabled workgroup

Set up workgroup for notebook sessions.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena create-work-group \
    --name "anihpj-spark" \
    --description "Spark workgroup for Django notebooks" \
    --configuration '{
        "ResultConfiguration": {
            "OutputLocation": "s3://anihpj-athena-results/spark/"
        },
        "EngineVersion": {
            "SelectedEngineVersion": "PySpark engine version 3"
        },
        "ExecutionRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AthenaSparkRole"
    }'
Output: {}
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Spark workgroup for: # - Interactive Django data notebooks # - PySpark ML on JobPost data # - Large-scale Skills analysis
Example 5: Create workgroup per team

Set up isolated workgroups for teams.

#!/bin/bash
TEAMS=("data-engineering" "analytics" "ml-team")
BASE_BUCKET="s3://anihpj-athena-results"

for TEAM in "${TEAMS[@]}"; do
    echo "Creating workgroup for $TEAM..."
    aws athena create-work-group \
        --name "anihpj-${TEAM}" \
        --description "Workgroup for ${TEAM} Django analytics" \
        --configuration "{
            \"ResultConfiguration\": {
                \"OutputLocation\": \"${BASE_BUCKET}/${TEAM}/\"
            },
            \"PublishCloudWatchMetricsEnabled\": true
        }" \
        --tags Key=Team,Value=$TEAM Key=Application,Value=anihpj
done

echo "All workgroups created"
Output: Creating workgroup for data-engineering... Creating workgroup for analytics... Creating workgroup for ml-team... All workgroups created
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Team-specific workgroups: # - data-engineering: ETL for Django models # - analytics: Business reports on JobPost # - ml-team: ML models on Skills/JobPost

delete-capacity-reservation

Deletes a cancelled capacity reservation. Use after cancelling to fully remove the reservation from your account.

Syntax
aws athena delete-capacity-reservation \
    --name <value>
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the capacity reservation to delete
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Delete cancelled reservation

Remove cancelled capacity reservation.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena delete-capacity-reservation \
    --name "old-analytics-capacity"

echo "Capacity reservation deleted"
Output: Capacity reservation deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Deleted reservation previously used for Django analytics
Example 2: Cancel then delete reservation

Full cleanup workflow for reservation.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="temp-jobpost-capacity"

# First cancel
aws athena cancel-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION" 2>/dev/null

# Wait for cancellation
sleep 10

# Then delete
aws athena delete-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION"
echo "Reservation $RESERVATION fully removed"
Output: Reservation temp-jobpost-capacity fully removed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Temp capacity for JobPost processing now removed
Example 3: Cleanup all cancelled reservations

Delete all reservations in CANCELLED state.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-capacity-reservations | jq -r '
    .CapacityReservations[] | 
    select(.Status == "CANCELLED") | .Name' | while read NAME; do
    echo "Deleting: $NAME"
    aws athena delete-capacity-reservation --name "$NAME"
done

echo "Cleanup complete"
Output: Deleting: old-quarterly-capacity Deleting: test-capacity-001 Deleting: migration-capacity Cleanup complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up reservations from: # - Quarterly Django reports # - Testing capacity # - Data migration jobs
Example 4: Delete with confirmation

Interactive deletion with confirmation.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="anihpj-prod-capacity"

# Get details first
DETAILS=$(aws athena get-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION" 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$DETAILS" ]; then
    echo "Reservation details:"
    echo "$DETAILS" | jq '.CapacityReservation | {name: .Name, status: .Status}'
    
    read -p "Delete this reservation? (y/n): " CONFIRM
    if [ "$CONFIRM" = "y" ]; then
        aws athena delete-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION"
        echo "Deleted"
    fi
else
    echo "Reservation not found"
fi
Output: Reservation details: { "name": "anihpj-prod-capacity", "status": "CANCELLED" } Delete this reservation? (y/n): y Deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Interactive deletion prevents accidental removal
Example 5: Scheduled cleanup with logging

Automated cleanup with audit trail.

#!/bin/bash
LOG_FILE="capacity-cleanup-$(date +%Y%m%d).log"

echo "Capacity Cleanup - $(date)" >> "$LOG_FILE"

aws athena list-capacity-reservations | jq -r '
    .CapacityReservations[] | 
    select(.Status == "CANCELLED") | 
    "\(.Name)|\(.CreationTime)"' | while IFS='|' read NAME CREATED; do
    echo "Deleting: $NAME (created: $CREATED)" | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"
    aws athena delete-capacity-reservation --name "$NAME" 2>&1 | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"
done

echo "Cleanup logged to $LOG_FILE"
Output: Deleting: old-capacity-001 (created: 2024-12-01T10:00:00Z) Deleting: test-capacity-002 (created: 2025-01-01T08:00:00Z) Cleanup logged to capacity-cleanup-20250115.log
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit trail for compliance # Records all deleted Django analytics capacity

delete-data-catalog

Deletes a data catalog. Use when retiring external data sources or reorganizing Django data architecture.

Syntax
aws athena delete-data-catalog \
    --name <value>
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the data catalog to delete
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Delete deprecated catalog

Remove old data catalog no longer in use.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena delete-data-catalog \
    --name "old-django-catalog"

echo "Data catalog deleted"
Output: Data catalog deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed catalog that pointed to deprecated tables
Example 2: Delete with dependency check

Check for dependencies before deletion.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOG="anihpj-staging-catalog"

# Check if catalog exists
if aws athena get-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG" &>/dev/null; then
    # Check for databases
    DBS=$(aws athena list-databases --catalog-name "$CATALOG" --query 'DatabaseList[].Name' --output text 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ -n "$DBS" ]; then
        echo "Warning: Catalog has databases: $DBS"
        echo "Delete databases first or use force"
    else
        aws athena delete-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG"
        echo "Catalog deleted"
    fi
fi
Output: Catalog deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Safely deleted staging catalog after verifying no databases
Example 3: Delete test catalogs

Clean up all test catalogs.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-data-catalogs | jq -r '
    .DataCatalogsSummary[] | 
    select(.CatalogName | startswith("test-")) | 
    .CatalogName' | while read CATALOG; do
    echo "Deleting test catalog: $CATALOG"
    aws athena delete-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG"
done
Output: Deleting test catalog: test-catalog-001 Deleting test catalog: test-django-catalog Deleting test catalog: test-migration-catalog
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up test catalogs created during development
Example 4: Delete and verify removal

Delete catalog and confirm it's gone.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOG="deprecated-hive-catalog"

# Delete
aws athena delete-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG"

# Verify deletion
if ! aws athena get-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Successfully deleted: $CATALOG"
else
    echo "Warning: Catalog still exists"
fi
Output: Successfully deleted: deprecated-hive-catalog
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified removal of deprecated Hive catalog
Example 5: Environment catalog cleanup

Delete catalogs for decommissioned environment.

#!/bin/bash
ENVIRONMENT="sandbox"

echo "Cleaning up $ENVIRONMENT catalogs..."

aws athena list-data-catalogs | jq -r --arg env "$ENVIRONMENT" '
    .DataCatalogsSummary[] | 
    select(.CatalogName | contains($env)) | 
    .CatalogName' | while read CATALOG; do
    echo "Deleting: $CATALOG"
    aws athena delete-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG" 2>&1 || echo "Failed: $CATALOG"
done

echo "Cleanup complete"
Output: Cleaning up sandbox catalogs... Deleting: anihpj-sandbox-catalog Deleting: sandbox-test-catalog Cleanup complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed all sandbox environment catalogs # Sandbox contained test Django data exports

delete-named-query

Deletes a named query. Use to remove outdated or unused Django analytics queries.

Syntax
aws athena delete-named-query \
    --named-query-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --named-query-id (string, required): Unique ID of the named query to delete
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Delete specific named query

Remove outdated JobPost query.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena delete-named-query \
    --named-query-id "nq-old-jobpost-001"

echo "Named query deleted"
Output: Named query deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed outdated query that used old schema
Example 2: Find and delete by name

Search for query by name then delete.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_NAME="deprecated-skills-report"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"

# Find query ID by name
QUERY_ID=$(aws athena list-named-queries --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | \
    jq -r '.NamedQueryIds[]' | while read ID; do
        NAME=$(aws athena get-named-query --named-query-id "$ID" --query 'NamedQuery.Name' --output text)
        if [ "$NAME" = "$QUERY_NAME" ]; then
            echo "$ID"
            break
        fi
    done)

if [ -n "$QUERY_ID" ]; then
    aws athena delete-named-query --named-query-id "$QUERY_ID"
    echo "Deleted: $QUERY_NAME"
fi
Output: Deleted: deprecated-skills-report
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed deprecated Skills report query
Example 3: Delete multiple queries

Batch delete old queries.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_IDS=("nq-old-001" "nq-old-002" "nq-old-003")

for ID in "${QUERY_IDS[@]}"; do
    echo "Deleting: $ID"
    aws athena delete-named-query --named-query-id "$ID" 2>/dev/null && echo "  Success" || echo "  Failed"
done
Output: Deleting: nq-old-001 Success Deleting: nq-old-002 Success Deleting: nq-old-003 Failed
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch cleanup of old Django queries
Example 4: Delete with backup

Backup query before deleting.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_ID="nq-important-001"
BACKUP_DIR="./query-backups"
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# Backup query
aws athena get-named-query --named-query-id "$QUERY_ID" > "$BACKUP_DIR/${QUERY_ID}.json"

# Verify backup
if [ -s "$BACKUP_DIR/${QUERY_ID}.json" ]; then
    echo "Backed up to $BACKUP_DIR/${QUERY_ID}.json"
    aws athena delete-named-query --named-query-id "$QUERY_ID"
    echo "Query deleted"
fi
Output: Backed up to ./query-backups/nq-important-001.json Query deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backed up important query before deletion # Can restore if needed
Example 5: Cleanup queries older than date

Delete queries not updated recently.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"
CUTOFF=$(date -d "-90 days" +%s)

aws athena list-named-queries --work-group "$WORKGROUP" --query 'NamedQueryIds[]' --output text | \
while read ID; do
    DETAILS=$(aws athena get-named-query --named-query-id "$ID" 2>/dev/null)
    NAME=$(echo "$DETAILS" | jq -r '.NamedQuery.Name')
    
    # Check if query name suggests it's old
    if [[ "$NAME" == *"old"* ]] || [[ "$NAME" == *"deprecated"* ]]; then
        echo "Deleting old query: $NAME ($ID)"
        aws athena delete-named-query --named-query-id "$ID"
    fi
done
Output: Deleting old query: old-author-report (nq-001) Deleting old query: deprecated-skills-view (nq-002)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up old and deprecated queries

delete-notebook

Deletes the specified notebook. Use to remove unused Django analytics notebooks.

Syntax
aws athena delete-notebook \
    --notebook-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --notebook-id (string, required): Unique ID of the notebook to delete
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Delete specific notebook

Remove unused analytics notebook.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena delete-notebook \
    --notebook-id "nb-old-analysis-001"

echo "Notebook deleted"
Output: Notebook deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed old analysis notebook
Example 2: Delete with export first

Export notebook before deletion.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-important-001"
BACKUP_DIR="./notebook-backups"
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# Export notebook content
aws athena export-notebook --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq -r '.Payload' | base64 -d > "$BACKUP_DIR/${NOTEBOOK_ID}.ipynb"

if [ -s "$BACKUP_DIR/${NOTEBOOK_ID}.ipynb" ]; then
    echo "Exported to $BACKUP_DIR/${NOTEBOOK_ID}.ipynb"
    aws athena delete-notebook --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID"
    echo "Notebook deleted"
fi
Output: Exported to ./notebook-backups/nb-important-001.ipynb Notebook deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backed up Django analysis before deletion
Example 3: Delete test notebooks

Clean up all test notebooks.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"

aws athena list-notebook-metadata --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq -r '
    .NotebookMetadataList[] | 
    select(.Name | startswith("test-")) | 
    .NotebookId' | while read ID; do
    echo "Deleting test notebook: $ID"
    aws athena delete-notebook --notebook-id "$ID"
done
Output: Deleting test notebook: nb-test-001 Deleting test notebook: nb-test-002 Deleting test notebook: nb-test-003
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up test notebooks from development
Example 4: Delete notebooks by creator

Remove notebooks created by specific user.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"
CREATOR_PATTERN="test-user"

aws athena list-notebook-metadata --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq -r --arg creator "$CREATOR_PATTERN" '
    .NotebookMetadataList[] | 
    select(.CreatorId | contains($creator)) | 
    "\(.NotebookId)|\(.Name)"' | while IFS='|' read ID NAME; do
    echo "Deleting: $NAME ($ID)"
    aws athena delete-notebook --notebook-id "$ID"
done
Output: Deleting: sandbox-analysis (nb-001) Deleting: temp-exploration (nb-002)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed test user's sandbox notebooks
Example 5: Cleanup old notebooks

Delete notebooks not modified recently.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"
DAYS_OLD=30
CUTOFF=$(date -d "-${DAYS_OLD} days" +%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z)

echo "Deleting notebooks not modified since $CUTOFF"

aws athena list-notebook-metadata --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq -r --arg cutoff "$CUTOFF" '
    .NotebookMetadataList[] | 
    select(.LastModifiedTime < $cutoff) | 
    "\(.NotebookId)|\(.Name)|\(.LastModifiedTime)"' | while IFS='|' read ID NAME MODIFIED; do
    echo "Deleting: $NAME (last modified: $MODIFIED)"
    aws athena delete-notebook --notebook-id "$ID"
done
Output: Deleting notebooks not modified since 2024-12-16T00:00:00Z Deleting: old-quarterly-report (last modified: 2024-11-01T10:00:00Z) Deleting: archived-analysis (last modified: 2024-10-15T08:00:00Z)
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up stale Django analysis notebooks

delete-prepared-statement

Deletes a prepared statement from a workgroup. Use to remove deprecated parameterized queries.

Syntax
aws athena delete-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name <value> \
    --work-group <value>
Parameters Description
  • --statement-name (string, required): Name of the prepared statement
  • --work-group (string, required): Workgroup containing the statement
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Delete specific prepared statement

Remove outdated JobPost query statement.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena delete-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "old_jobpost_filter" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup"

echo "Prepared statement deleted"
Output: Prepared statement deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed old parameterized query for JobPost
Example 2: Delete deprecated version

Remove old version of a statement.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete old version, keep new
aws athena delete-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "author_report_v1" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup"

echo "v1 deleted, v2 remains active"

# Verify v2 exists
aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "author_report_v2" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq '.PreparedStatement.StatementName'
Output: v1 deleted, v2 remains active "author_report_v2"
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Upgraded Author report query to v2
Example 3: Bulk delete statements

Delete multiple prepared statements.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"
STATEMENTS=("deprecated_query_1" "deprecated_query_2" "old_skills_filter")

for STMT in "${STATEMENTS[@]}"; do
    echo "Deleting: $STMT"
    aws athena delete-prepared-statement \
        --statement-name "$STMT" \
        --work-group "$WORKGROUP" 2>/dev/null && echo "  Success" || echo "  Not found"
done
Output: Deleting: deprecated_query_1 Success Deleting: deprecated_query_2 Success Deleting: old_skills_filter Not found
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch cleanup of deprecated statements
Example 4: Delete with backup

Backup statement before deletion.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"
STATEMENT="important_jobpost_query"
BACKUP_FILE="prepared-statement-backup.json"

# Backup
aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "$STATEMENT" \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP" > "$BACKUP_FILE"

if [ -s "$BACKUP_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Backed up to $BACKUP_FILE"
    aws athena delete-prepared-statement \
        --statement-name "$STATEMENT" \
        --work-group "$WORKGROUP"
    echo "Statement deleted"
fi
Output: Backed up to prepared-statement-backup.json Statement deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Backed up important query before removal
Example 5: Cleanup test statements

Delete all test prepared statements.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"

aws athena list-prepared-statements --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq -r '
    .PreparedStatements[] | 
    select(.StatementName | startswith("test_")) | 
    .StatementName' | while read STMT; do
    echo "Deleting test statement: $STMT"
    aws athena delete-prepared-statement \
        --statement-name "$STMT" \
        --work-group "$WORKGROUP"
done

echo "Test statements cleaned up"
Output: Deleting test statement: test_jobpost_filter Deleting test statement: test_skills_query Deleting test statement: test_author_lookup Test statements cleaned up
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed all test statements from workgroup

delete-work-group

Deletes a workgroup. Use when decommissioning analytics environments.

Syntax
aws athena delete-work-group \
    --work-group <value> \
    [--recursive-delete-option]
Parameters Description
  • --work-group (string, required): Name of the workgroup to delete
  • --recursive-delete-option (flag, optional): Delete named queries and prepared statements
Return Value
  • None on success: Returns empty response if successful
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Delete empty workgroup

Remove workgroup with no queries.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena delete-work-group \
    --work-group "unused-workgroup"

echo "Workgroup deleted"
Output: Workgroup deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed unused Django analytics workgroup
Example 2: Force delete with contents

Delete workgroup and all its queries.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena delete-work-group \
    --work-group "old-analytics-workgroup" \
    --recursive-delete-option

echo "Workgroup and all contents deleted"
Output: Workgroup and all contents deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Force deleted workgroup including: # - All named queries # - All prepared statements
Example 3: Delete test workgroups

Clean up all test workgroups.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-work-groups | jq -r '
    .WorkGroups[] | 
    select(.Name | startswith("test-")) | 
    .Name' | while read WG; do
    echo "Deleting: $WG"
    aws athena delete-work-group \
        --work-group "$WG" \
        --recursive-delete-option
done

echo "Test workgroups cleaned up"
Output: Deleting: test-analytics Deleting: test-spark Deleting: test-dev Test workgroups cleaned up
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cleaned up all test Django analytics workgroups
Example 4: Delete with confirmation

Interactive deletion with verification.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-staging"

# Get workgroup info
INFO=$(aws athena get-work-group --work-group "$WORKGROUP" 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$INFO" ]; then
    echo "Workgroup details:"
    echo "$INFO" | jq '.WorkGroup | {name: .Name, state: .State}'
    
    QUERIES=$(aws athena list-named-queries --work-group "$WORKGROUP" --query 'NamedQueryIds | length(@)')
    echo "Named queries: $QUERIES"
    
    read -p "Delete this workgroup? (y/n): " CONFIRM
    if [ "$CONFIRM" = "y" ]; then
        aws athena delete-work-group --work-group "$WORKGROUP" --recursive-delete-option
        echo "Deleted"
    fi
fi
Output: Workgroup details: { "name": "anihpj-staging", "state": "ENABLED" } Named queries: 15 Delete this workgroup? (y/n): y Deleted
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Deleted staging workgroup with 15 queries
Example 5: Environment cleanup

Delete all workgroups for environment.

#!/bin/bash
ENVIRONMENT="sandbox"

echo "Cleaning up $ENVIRONMENT workgroups..."

aws athena list-work-groups | jq -r --arg env "$ENVIRONMENT" '
    .WorkGroups[] | 
    select(.Name | contains($env)) | 
    .Name' | while read WG; do
    # Skip primary workgroup
