    if [ "$WG" != "primary" ]; then
        echo "Deleting: $WG"
        aws athena delete-work-group \
            --work-group "$WG" \
            --recursive-delete-option 2>&1 || echo "  Failed"
    fi
done

echo "Environment cleanup complete"
Output: Cleaning up sandbox workgroups... Deleting: anihpj-sandbox Deleting: sandbox-test Environment cleanup complete
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Removed all sandbox Django analytics workgroups

export-notebook

Exports a notebook as an ipynb file. Use to backup or share Django analytics notebooks.

Syntax
aws athena export-notebook \
    --notebook-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --notebook-id (string, required): ID of the notebook to export
Return Value
  • NotebookMetadata (object): Metadata about the notebook
  • Payload (string): Base64-encoded notebook content
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Export notebook to file

Save notebook as ipynb file.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena export-notebook \
    --notebook-id "nb-jobpost-001" | jq -r '.Payload' | base64 -d > jobpost-analysis.ipynb

echo "Exported to jobpost-analysis.ipynb"
Output: Exported to jobpost-analysis.ipynb
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Exported JobPost analysis notebook
Example 2: Export with metadata

Export notebook with full details.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-skills-ml-001"
OUTPUT_DIR="./exported-notebooks"
mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"

RESULT=$(aws athena export-notebook --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID")

# Save metadata
echo "$RESULT" | jq '.NotebookMetadata' > "$OUTPUT_DIR/${NOTEBOOK_ID}-metadata.json"

# Save notebook
echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Payload' | base64 -d > "$OUTPUT_DIR/${NOTEBOOK_ID}.ipynb"

echo "Exported notebook and metadata to $OUTPUT_DIR"
Output: Exported notebook and metadata to ./exported-notebooks
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Exported Skills ML notebook with metadata
Example 3: Batch export all notebooks

Export all notebooks in workgroup.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"
OUTPUT_DIR="./notebook-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"

aws athena list-notebook-metadata --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq -r '
    .NotebookMetadataList[] | "\(.NotebookId)|\(.Name)"' | while IFS='|' read ID NAME; do
    SAFE_NAME=$(echo "$NAME" | tr ' ' '_')
    echo "Exporting: $NAME"
    aws athena export-notebook --notebook-id "$ID" | jq -r '.Payload' | base64 -d > "$OUTPUT_DIR/${SAFE_NAME}.ipynb"
done

echo "All notebooks exported to $OUTPUT_DIR"
ls -la "$OUTPUT_DIR"
Output: Exporting: JobPost-Analytics Exporting: Skills-ML-Analysis Exporting: Author-Reports All notebooks exported to ./notebook-backup-20250115 -rw-r--r-- 1 user user 15234 JobPost-Analytics.ipynb -rw-r--r-- 1 user user 28456 Skills-ML-Analysis.ipynb -rw-r--r-- 1 user user 12890 Author-Reports.ipynb
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Full backup of all Django analytics notebooks
Example 4: Export to S3

Export notebook directly to S3.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-quarterly-001"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-notebook-backups"
S3_KEY="exports/$(date +%Y/%m)/notebook-${NOTEBOOK_ID}.ipynb"

aws athena export-notebook --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | \
    jq -r '.Payload' | base64 -d | \
    aws s3 cp - "s3://${S3_BUCKET}/${S3_KEY}"

echo "Exported to s3://${S3_BUCKET}/${S3_KEY}"
Output: Exported to s3://anihpj-notebook-backups/exports/2025/01/notebook-nb-quarterly-001.ipynb
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Quarterly Django report exported to S3
Example 5: Export for sharing

Export and prepare for external sharing.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-demo-001"
OUTPUT_FILE="django-analytics-demo.ipynb"

# Export
aws athena export-notebook --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq -r '.Payload' | base64 -d > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

# Get file info
SIZE=$(wc -c < "$OUTPUT_FILE")
CELLS=$(jq '.cells | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")

echo "Exported: $OUTPUT_FILE"
echo "Size: $SIZE bytes"
echo "Cells: $CELLS"
echo "Ready for sharing"
Output: Exported: django-analytics-demo.ipynb Size: 45678 bytes Cells: 24 Ready for sharing
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Demo notebook with 24 cells showing: # - JobPost analysis # - Skills visualization # - Author metrics

get-calculation-execution

Gets information about a calculation execution. Use to monitor Spark calculations on Django data.

Syntax
aws athena get-calculation-execution \
    --calculation-execution-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --calculation-execution-id (string, required): ID of the calculation
Return Value
  • CalculationExecutionId (string): The calculation ID
  • SessionId (string): The session ID
  • Status (object): Current status and timing
  • Statistics (object): Execution statistics
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get calculation status

Check status of Spark calculation.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-calculation-execution \
    --calculation-execution-id "calc-jobpost-001" | jq '{
        id: .CalculationExecutionId,
        status: .Status.State,
        started: .Status.SubmissionDateTime,
        completed: .Status.CompletionDateTime
    }'
Output: { "id": "calc-jobpost-001", "status": "COMPLETED", "started": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z", "completed": "2025-01-15T10:05:30Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Spark calculation on JobPost data completed in 5.5 minutes
Example 2: Get detailed statistics

View execution statistics.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-calculation-execution \
    --calculation-execution-id "calc-skills-ml-001" | jq '{
        status: .Status.State,
        dpu_seconds: .Statistics.DpuExecutionInMillis / 1000,
        progress: .Statistics.Progress
    }'
Output: { "status": "COMPLETED", "dpu_seconds": 1250.5, "progress": "100%" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Skills ML calculation used 1250 DPU-seconds
Example 3: Monitor running calculation

Poll calculation until complete.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_ID="calc-large-analysis-001"

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws athena get-calculation-execution \
        --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID" | jq -r '.Status.State')
    
    echo "$(date +%H:%M:%S) - Status: $STATUS"
    
    case $STATUS in
        COMPLETED|FAILED|CANCELLED)
            break
            ;;
    esac
    sleep 10
done

echo "Calculation finished with status: $STATUS"
Output: 10:00:00 - Status: RUNNING 10:00:10 - Status: RUNNING 10:00:20 - Status: RUNNING 10:00:30 - Status: COMPLETED Calculation finished with status: COMPLETED
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Monitored large Django data analysis to completion
Example 4: Check for errors

Get error details from failed calculation.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-calculation-execution \
    --calculation-execution-id "calc-failed-001" | jq '{
        status: .Status.State,
        error: .Status.StateChangeReason,
        code_snippet: .Description[0:200]
    }'
Output: { "status": "FAILED", "error": "AnalysisException: Table or view not found: old_jobpost_table", "code_snippet": "SELECT * FROM old_jobpost_table WHERE..." }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Calculation failed - table renamed # Use jobpost_archive instead
Example 5: Generate execution report

Create summary report of calculation.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_ID="calc-quarterly-001"

aws athena get-calculation-execution \
    --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID" | jq '{
        report: {
            calculation_id: .CalculationExecutionId,
            session_id: .SessionId,
            workgroup: .WorkingDirectory,
            status: .Status.State,
            submitted: .Status.SubmissionDateTime,
            completed: .Status.CompletionDateTime,
            runtime_seconds: ((.Status.CompletionDateTime | fromdateiso8601) - (.Status.SubmissionDateTime | fromdateiso8601)),
            dpu_cost: (.Statistics.DpuExecutionInMillis / 3600000 * 0.35)
        }
    }'
Output: { "report": { "calculation_id": "calc-quarterly-001", "session_id": "session-001", "workgroup": "anihpj-spark", "status": "COMPLETED", "submitted": "2025-01-15T08:00:00Z", "completed": "2025-01-15T08:45:00Z", "runtime_seconds": 2700, "dpu_cost": 0.875 } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Quarterly Django report calculation: # - 45 minute runtime # - $0.875 DPU cost

get-calculation-execution-code

Gets the code executed in a calculation. Use to review Spark code run against Django data.

Syntax
aws athena get-calculation-execution-code \
    --calculation-execution-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --calculation-execution-id (string, required): ID of the calculation
Return Value
  • CodeBlock (string): The code that was executed
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get calculation code

Retrieve code from completed calculation.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-calculation-execution-code \
    --calculation-execution-id "calc-jobpost-001" | jq -r '.CodeBlock'
Output: df = spark.sql(""" SELECT status, COUNT(*) as count FROM jobpost_archive GROUP BY status """) df.show()
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Spark SQL equivalent to: # JobPost.objects.values('status').annotate(count=Count('id'))
Example 2: Save code to file

Export calculation code for review.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_ID="calc-skills-analysis-001"
OUTPUT_FILE="calculation-${CALC_ID}.py"

aws athena get-calculation-execution-code \
    --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID" | jq -r '.CodeBlock' > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Code saved to $OUTPUT_FILE"
head -20 "$OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Code saved to calculation-calc-skills-analysis-001.py # Skills Analysis Calculation from pyspark.sql import functions as F skills_df = spark.sql(""" SELECT s.skill_name, COUNT(*) as job_count FROM skills_jobpost sj JOIN skills s ON sj.skill_id = s.id GROUP BY s.skill_name ORDER BY job_count DESC """) top_skills = skills_df.limit(20) top_skills.show()
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Skills analysis using Spark # Similar to Skills.objects.annotate(job_count=Count('jobpost'))
Example 3: Review failed calculation code

Debug failed calculation.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_ID="calc-failed-001"

echo "=== Calculation Code ==="
aws athena get-calculation-execution-code \
    --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID" | jq -r '.CodeBlock'

echo ""
echo "=== Error Details ==="
aws athena get-calculation-execution \
    --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID" | jq -r '.Status.StateChangeReason'
Output: === Calculation Code === df = spark.sql("SELECT * FROM old_jobpost") df.show() === Error Details === AnalysisException: Table or view not found: old_jobpost
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Error: Table renamed, use jobpost_archive instead
Example 4: Audit multiple calculations

Review code from multiple calculations.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_IDS=("calc-001" "calc-002" "calc-003")

for ID in "${CALC_IDS[@]}"; do
    echo "=== $ID ==="
    aws athena get-calculation-execution-code \
        --calculation-execution-id "$ID" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.CodeBlock' | head -10
    echo "..."
    echo ""
done
Output: === calc-001 === # JobPost Summary df = spark.sql("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM jobpost_archive") ... === calc-002 === # Skills Distribution skills = spark.sql("SELECT skill_name, COUNT(*) as cnt FROM skills") ... === calc-003 === # Author Metrics authors = spark.sql("SELECT author_id, COUNT(*) FROM jobpost_archive") ...
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Audit trail of calculations on Django data
Example 5: Extract and rerun code

Get code for rerunning calculation.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_ID="calc-monthly-report-001"
RERUN_FILE="rerun-monthly-report.py"

# Extract code
aws athena get-calculation-execution-code \
    --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID" | jq -r '.CodeBlock' > "$RERUN_FILE"

# Add timestamp
echo "# Extracted from $CALC_ID on $(date)" | cat - "$RERUN_FILE" > temp && mv temp "$RERUN_FILE"

echo "Code ready for rerun: $RERUN_FILE"
wc -l "$RERUN_FILE"
Output: Code ready for rerun: rerun-monthly-report.py 45 rerun-monthly-report.py
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Monthly report code extracted for reuse

get-calculation-execution-status

Gets the status of a calculation execution. Use for lightweight status checks.

Syntax
aws athena get-calculation-execution-status \
    --calculation-execution-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --calculation-execution-id (string, required): ID of the calculation
Return Value
  • Status (object): State, submission time, completion time
  • Statistics (object): Execution statistics
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Quick status check

Get calculation status efficiently.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-calculation-execution-status \
    --calculation-execution-id "calc-001" | jq '.Status.State'
Output: "COMPLETED"
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Quick check - calculation complete
Example 2: Check completion time

Get timing information.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-calculation-execution-status \
    --calculation-execution-id "calc-jobpost-001" | jq '{
        state: .Status.State,
        submitted: .Status.SubmissionDateTime,
        completed: .Status.CompletionDateTime
    }'
Output: { "state": "COMPLETED", "submitted": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z", "completed": "2025-01-15T10:05:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # JobPost calculation took 5 minutes
Example 3: Monitor with progress

Poll status with progress display.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_ID="calc-large-001"

while true; do
    RESULT=$(aws athena get-calculation-execution-status \
        --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID")
    
    STATE=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Status.State')
    PROGRESS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Statistics.Progress // "N/A"')
    
    echo "$(date +%H:%M:%S) - $STATE - Progress: $PROGRESS"
    
    [[ "$STATE" =~ ^(COMPLETED|FAILED|CANCELLED)$ ]] && break
    sleep 5
done
Output: 10:00:00 - RUNNING - Progress: 25% 10:00:05 - RUNNING - Progress: 50% 10:00:10 - RUNNING - Progress: 75% 10:00:15 - COMPLETED - Progress: 100%
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tracked calculation progress to completion
Example 4: Batch status check

Check multiple calculations.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_IDS=("calc-001" "calc-002" "calc-003" "calc-004")

echo "Calculation Status Summary"
echo "=========================="
for ID in "${CALC_IDS[@]}"; do
    STATUS=$(aws athena get-calculation-execution-status \
        --calculation-execution-id "$ID" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.Status.State // "NOT_FOUND"')
    printf "%-20s : %s\n" "$ID" "$STATUS"
done
Output: Calculation Status Summary ========================== calc-001 : COMPLETED calc-002 : COMPLETED calc-003 : RUNNING calc-004 : FAILED
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dashboard view of Django calculation statuses
Example 5: Wait for completion

Script that waits for calculation.

#!/bin/bash
CALC_ID="calc-critical-001"
TIMEOUT=600
ELAPSED=0

echo "Waiting for calculation $CALC_ID..."

while [ $ELAPSED -lt $TIMEOUT ]; do
    STATE=$(aws athena get-calculation-execution-status \
        --calculation-execution-id "$CALC_ID" | jq -r '.Status.State')
    
    case $STATE in
        COMPLETED)
            echo "Calculation completed successfully"
            exit 0
            ;;
        FAILED|CANCELLED)
            echo "Calculation failed: $STATE"
            exit 1
            ;;
    esac
    
    sleep 10
    ELAPSED=$((ELAPSED + 10))
done

echo "Timeout waiting for calculation"
exit 2
Output: Waiting for calculation calc-critical-001... Calculation completed successfully
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Script waits for critical Django data calculation

get-capacity-assignment-configuration

Gets the capacity assignment configuration for a reservation. Shows which workgroups use reserved capacity.

Syntax
aws athena get-capacity-assignment-configuration \
    --capacity-reservation-name <value>
Parameters Description
  • --capacity-reservation-name (string, required): Name of the capacity reservation
Return Value
  • CapacityAssignmentConfiguration (object): Assignment configuration with workgroups
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get capacity assignments

View workgroups using capacity.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-capacity-assignment-configuration \
    --capacity-reservation-name "anihpj-capacity" | jq '.'
Output: { "CapacityAssignmentConfiguration": { "CapacityReservationName": "anihpj-capacity", "CapacityAssignments": [ { "WorkGroupNames": ["anihpj-production", "anihpj-analytics"] } ] } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Capacity shared between production and analytics workgroups
Example 2: List assigned workgroups

Extract workgroup names.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-capacity-assignment-configuration \
    --capacity-reservation-name "anihpj-capacity" | jq -r '
    .CapacityAssignmentConfiguration.CapacityAssignments[].WorkGroupNames[]'
Output: anihpj-production anihpj-analytics anihpj-reporting
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Three workgroups share Django analytics capacity
Example 3: Verify workgroup assignment

Check if workgroup uses capacity.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="anihpj-capacity"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-production"

ASSIGNED=$(aws athena get-capacity-assignment-configuration \
    --capacity-reservation-name "$RESERVATION" | jq -r --arg wg "$WORKGROUP" '
    .CapacityAssignmentConfiguration.CapacityAssignments[].WorkGroupNames[] | 
    select(. == $wg)')

if [ -n "$ASSIGNED" ]; then
    echo "$WORKGROUP is assigned to $RESERVATION"
else
    echo "$WORKGROUP is NOT assigned to $RESERVATION"
fi
Output: anihpj-production is assigned to anihpj-capacity
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production workgroup confirmed using reserved capacity
Example 4: Count assigned workgroups

Get number of workgroups using reservation.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="anihpj-capacity"

COUNT=$(aws athena get-capacity-assignment-configuration \
    --capacity-reservation-name "$RESERVATION" | jq '
    [.CapacityAssignmentConfiguration.CapacityAssignments[].WorkGroupNames[]] | length')

echo "Reservation: $RESERVATION"
echo "Assigned workgroups: $COUNT"
Output: Reservation: anihpj-capacity Assigned workgroups: 4
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 4 workgroups share Django analytics capacity
Example 5: Generate capacity report

Create full capacity assignment report.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="anihpj-capacity"

echo "=== Capacity Assignment Report ==="
echo "Reservation: $RESERVATION"
echo ""

# Get reservation details
aws athena get-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION" | jq '{
    target_dpus: .CapacityReservation.TargetDpus,
    allocated_dpus: .CapacityReservation.AllocatedDpus,
    status: .CapacityReservation.Status
}'

echo ""
echo "Assigned Workgroups:"
aws athena get-capacity-assignment-configuration \
    --capacity-reservation-name "$RESERVATION" | jq -r '
    .CapacityAssignmentConfiguration.CapacityAssignments[].WorkGroupNames[] | "  - \(.)"'
Output: === Capacity Assignment Report === Reservation: anihpj-capacity { "target_dpus": 48, "allocated_dpus": 48, "status": "ACTIVE" } Assigned Workgroups: - anihpj-production - anihpj-analytics - anihpj-reporting - anihpj-ml
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 48 DPUs shared across Django analytics workgroups

get-capacity-reservation

Gets information about a capacity reservation. Monitor DPU allocation for Django analytics.

Syntax
aws athena get-capacity-reservation \
    --name <value>
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the capacity reservation
Return Value
  • CapacityReservation (object): Reservation details including DPUs and status
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get reservation details

View capacity reservation info.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-capacity-reservation \
    --name "anihpj-analytics-capacity" | jq '.CapacityReservation'
Output: { "Name": "anihpj-analytics-capacity", "Status": "ACTIVE", "TargetDpus": 48, "AllocatedDpus": 48, "CreationTime": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 48 DPUs allocated for Django analytics
Example 2: Check utilization

Calculate capacity utilization.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-capacity-reservation \
    --name "anihpj-capacity" | jq '{
        name: .CapacityReservation.Name,
        target: .CapacityReservation.TargetDpus,
        allocated: .CapacityReservation.AllocatedDpus,
        utilization_pct: ((.CapacityReservation.AllocatedDpus / .CapacityReservation.TargetDpus) * 100)
    }'
Output: { "name": "anihpj-capacity", "target": 48, "allocated": 48, "utilization_pct": 100 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Capacity fully allocated for Django analytics
Example 3: Monitor reservation status

Check if reservation is active.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="anihpj-production-capacity"

STATUS=$(aws athena get-capacity-reservation \
    --name "$RESERVATION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.CapacityReservation.Status')

case $STATUS in
    ACTIVE)
        echo "✓ $RESERVATION is active"
        ;;
    PENDING)
        echo "⏳ $RESERVATION is pending activation"
        ;;
    CANCELLED)
        echo "✗ $RESERVATION is cancelled"
        ;;
    *)
        echo "? $RESERVATION status unknown or not found"
        ;;
esac
Output: ✓ anihpj-production-capacity is active
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production capacity active for Django queries
Example 4: Get reservation age

Calculate how long reservation has been active.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="anihpj-capacity"

CREATED=$(aws athena get-capacity-reservation \
    --name "$RESERVATION" | jq -r '.CapacityReservation.CreationTime')

CREATED_TS=$(date -d "$CREATED" +%s)
NOW_TS=$(date +%s)
AGE_DAYS=$(( (NOW_TS - CREATED_TS) / 86400 ))

echo "Reservation: $RESERVATION"
echo "Created: $CREATED"
echo "Age: $AGE_DAYS days"
Output: Reservation: anihpj-capacity Created: 2025-01-01T00:00:00Z Age: 14 days
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django analytics capacity running for 14 days
Example 5: Estimate capacity cost

Calculate estimated reservation cost.

#!/bin/bash
RESERVATION="anihpj-capacity"
HOURLY_RATE=0.35  # per DPU-hour

INFO=$(aws athena get-capacity-reservation --name "$RESERVATION")

DPUS=$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.CapacityReservation.TargetDpus')
CREATED=$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.CapacityReservation.CreationTime')

CREATED_TS=$(date -d "$CREATED" +%s)
NOW_TS=$(date +%s)
HOURS=$(( (NOW_TS - CREATED_TS) / 3600 ))

COST=$(echo "$DPUS * $HOURS * $HOURLY_RATE" | bc)

echo "Reservation: $RESERVATION"
echo "DPUs: $DPUS"
echo "Hours active: $HOURS"
echo "Estimated cost: \$$COST"
Output: Reservation: anihpj-capacity DPUs: 48 Hours active: 336 Estimated cost: $5644.80
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django analytics capacity cost estimation

get-data-catalog

Gets information about a data catalog. View catalog configuration for Django data sources.

Syntax
aws athena get-data-catalog \
    --name <value> \
    [--work-group <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the data catalog
  • --work-group (string, optional): Workgroup context for the catalog
Return Value
  • DataCatalog (object): Catalog details including type and parameters
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get catalog details

View data catalog configuration.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-data-catalog \
    --name "anihpj-glue-catalog" | jq '.DataCatalog'
Output: { "Name": "anihpj-glue-catalog", "Description": "Glue catalog for Django data", "Type": "GLUE", "Parameters": { "catalog-id": "123456789012" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Glue catalog connects to Django archived data
Example 2: Check catalog type

Verify catalog type for connection.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOG="anihpj-federated-catalog"

TYPE=$(aws athena get-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG" | jq -r '.DataCatalog.Type')

echo "Catalog: $CATALOG"
echo "Type: $TYPE"

case $TYPE in
    GLUE)
        echo "Connection: AWS Glue Data Catalog"
        ;;
    LAMBDA)
        echo "Connection: Federated via Lambda"
        ;;
    HIVE)
        echo "Connection: External Hive Metastore"
        ;;
esac
Output: Catalog: anihpj-federated-catalog Type: LAMBDA Connection: Federated via Lambda
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Federated catalog for Django PostgreSQL access
Example 3: Get Lambda connector ARN

Extract Lambda function for federated catalog.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOG="anihpj-postgres-catalog"

aws athena get-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG" | jq -r '
    .DataCatalog | {
        name: .Name,
        type: .Type,
        lambda_arn: .Parameters.function
    }'
Output: { "name": "anihpj-postgres-catalog", "type": "LAMBDA", "lambda_arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:athena-postgres-connector" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Lambda connector queries Django PostgreSQL database
Example 4: Verify catalog exists

Check if catalog is available.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOG="anihpj-catalog"

if aws athena get-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "✓ Catalog '$CATALOG' exists"
    aws athena get-data-catalog --name "$CATALOG" | jq '{
        name: .DataCatalog.Name,
        type: .DataCatalog.Type,
        description: .DataCatalog.Description
    }'
else
    echo "✗ Catalog '$CATALOG' not found"
fi
Output: ✓ Catalog 'anihpj-catalog' exists { "name": "anihpj-catalog", "type": "GLUE", "description": "Main Django data catalog" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified main Django catalog availability
Example 5: Compare catalog configurations

Compare multiple catalogs.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOGS=("anihpj-dev-catalog" "anihpj-prod-catalog")

echo "Catalog Comparison"
echo "=================="

for CAT in "${CATALOGS[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "--- $CAT ---"
    aws athena get-data-catalog --name "$CAT" 2>/dev/null | jq '{
        type: .DataCatalog.Type,
        params: .DataCatalog.Parameters
    }' || echo "Not found"
done
Output: Catalog Comparison ================== --- anihpj-dev-catalog --- { "type": "GLUE", "params": { "catalog-id": "111111111111" } } --- anihpj-prod-catalog --- { "type": "GLUE", "params": { "catalog-id": "222222222222" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dev and prod catalogs use different AWS accounts

get-database

Gets database information from a catalog. View Django analytics database details.

Syntax
aws athena get-database \
    --catalog-name <value> \
    --database-name <value> \
    [--work-group <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --catalog-name (string, required): Name of the data catalog
  • --database-name (string, required): Name of the database
  • --work-group (string, optional): Workgroup for the request
Return Value
  • Database (object): Database name, description, and parameters
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get database info

View Django analytics database.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-database \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" | jq '.Database'
Output: { "Name": "anihpj_archive", "Description": "Django JobPost archived data", "Parameters": { "created_by": "data_engineering" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Archive database contains exported Django data
Example 2: Get database location

Find S3 location for database.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-database \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" | jq -r '
    .Database | {
        name: .Name,
        location: .Parameters.location
    }'
Output: { "name": "anihpj_archive", "location": "s3://anihpj-data-lake/archive/" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django data stored in S3 data lake
Example 3: Verify database exists

Check database availability.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOG="AwsDataCatalog"
DATABASE="anihpj_archive"

if aws athena get-database \
    --catalog-name "$CATALOG" \
    --database-name "$DATABASE" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "✓ Database '$DATABASE' exists in $CATALOG"
else
    echo "✗ Database '$DATABASE' not found"
fi
Output: ✓ Database 'anihpj_archive' exists in AwsDataCatalog
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Confirmed Django archive database exists
Example 4: Get database from federated catalog

Access database via federated catalog.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-database \
    --catalog-name "anihpj-postgres-catalog" \
    --database-name "django_production" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq '.'
Output: { "Database": { "Name": "django_production", "Description": "Live Django PostgreSQL database", "Parameters": {} } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Federated access to live Django PostgreSQL
Example 5: Compare environment databases

Check databases across environments.

#!/bin/bash
CATALOG="AwsDataCatalog"
DATABASES=("anihpj_dev" "anihpj_staging" "anihpj_production")

echo "Database Status Check"
echo "===================="

for DB in "${DATABASES[@]}"; do
    INFO=$(aws athena get-database \
        --catalog-name "$CATALOG" \
        --database-name "$DB" 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ -n "$INFO" ]; then
        DESC=$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.Database.Description // "No description"')
        echo "✓ $DB: $DESC"
    else
        echo "✗ $DB: Not found"
    fi
done
Output: Database Status Check ==================== ✓ anihpj_dev: Development Django data ✓ anihpj_staging: Staging environment data ✓ anihpj_production: Production Django archive
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # All environment databases available

get-named-query

Gets information about a named query. Retrieve saved Django analytics queries.

Syntax
aws athena get-named-query \
    --named-query-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --named-query-id (string, required): Unique ID of the named query
Return Value
  • NamedQuery (object): Query details including name, SQL, database
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get named query details

View saved query information.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-named-query \
    --named-query-id "nq-jobpost-001" | jq '.NamedQuery'
Output: { "Name": "jobpost-status-summary", "Description": "Get JobPost counts by status", "Database": "anihpj_archive", "QueryString": "SELECT status, COUNT(*) as count FROM jobpost_archive GROUP BY status", "NamedQueryId": "nq-jobpost-001", "WorkGroup": "anihpj-workgroup" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Equivalent Django: # JobPost.objects.values('status').annotate(count=Count('id'))
Example 2: Extract just the SQL

Get query string for execution.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-named-query \
    --named-query-id "nq-skills-001" | jq -r '.NamedQuery.QueryString'
Output: SELECT s.skill_name, COUNT(DISTINCT sj.jobpost_id) as job_count FROM skills_jobpost sj JOIN skills s ON sj.skill_id = s.id GROUP BY s.skill_name ORDER BY job_count DESC LIMIT 20
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Top 20 Skills by job count
Example 3: Get query metadata

View query info without SQL.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-named-query \
    --named-query-id "nq-author-001" | jq '.NamedQuery | {
        name: .Name,
        description: .Description,
        database: .Database,
        workgroup: .WorkGroup
    }'
Output: { "name": "author-performance-report", "description": "Monthly author metrics and post counts", "database": "anihpj_archive", "workgroup": "anihpj-workgroup" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Author performance report query
Example 4: Save query to file

Export named query for backup.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_ID="nq-important-001"
OUTPUT_FILE="saved-query-${QUERY_ID}.sql"

aws athena get-named-query --named-query-id "$QUERY_ID" | jq -r '
    "-- Name: \(.NamedQuery.Name)\n-- Description: \(.NamedQuery.Description)\n-- Database: \(.NamedQuery.Database)\n\n\(.NamedQuery.QueryString)"
' > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Query saved to $OUTPUT_FILE"
cat "$OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Query saved to saved-query-nq-important-001.sql -- Name: location-job-distribution -- Description: Jobs by city and country -- Database: anihpj_archive SELECT l.city, l.country, COUNT(jp.id) as job_count FROM locations l JOIN jobpost_archive jp ON l.id = jp.location_id GROUP BY l.city, l.country ORDER BY job_count DESC
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Location distribution query exported
Example 5: Execute named query

Get and run a saved query.

#!/bin/bash
QUERY_ID="nq-daily-summary-001"

# Get query details
QUERY_INFO=$(aws athena get-named-query --named-query-id "$QUERY_ID")
SQL=$(echo "$QUERY_INFO" | jq -r '.NamedQuery.QueryString')
DB=$(echo "$QUERY_INFO" | jq -r '.NamedQuery.Database')
WG=$(echo "$QUERY_INFO" | jq -r '.NamedQuery.WorkGroup')

echo "Executing: $(echo "$QUERY_INFO" | jq -r '.NamedQuery.Name')"

# Run the query
EXEC_ID=$(aws athena start-query-execution \
    --query-string "$SQL" \
    --query-execution-context Database="$DB" \
    --work-group "$WG" \
    --query 'QueryExecutionId' --output text)

echo "Execution started: $EXEC_ID"
Output: Executing: daily-jobpost-summary Execution started: exec-20250115-001
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Executed saved daily summary query

get-notebook-metadata

Gets metadata about a notebook. View Django analytics notebook information.

Syntax
aws athena get-notebook-metadata \
    --notebook-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --notebook-id (string, required): ID of the notebook
Return Value
  • NotebookMetadata (object): Notebook details including name, workgroup, timestamps
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get notebook info

View notebook metadata.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-notebook-metadata \
    --notebook-id "nb-jobpost-001" | jq '.NotebookMetadata'
Output: { "NotebookId": "nb-jobpost-001", "Name": "JobPost-Analytics", "WorkGroup": "anihpj-spark-workgroup", "CreationTime": "2025-01-01T10:00:00Z", "LastModifiedTime": "2025-01-15T14:30:00Z", "Type": "IPYNB" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # JobPost analytics notebook info
Example 2: Check last modified

See when notebook was updated.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-skills-ml-001"

aws athena get-notebook-metadata \
    --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq '{
        name: .NotebookMetadata.Name,
        created: .NotebookMetadata.CreationTime,
        modified: .NotebookMetadata.LastModifiedTime,
        age_days: ((now - (.NotebookMetadata.LastModifiedTime | fromdateiso8601)) / 86400 | floor)
    }'
Output: { "name": "Skills-ML-Analysis", "created": "2025-01-05T08:00:00Z", "modified": "2025-01-14T16:00:00Z", "age_days": 1 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Skills ML notebook updated yesterday
Example 3: Get workgroup info

Find notebook's workgroup.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-quarterly-001"

WORKGROUP=$(aws athena get-notebook-metadata \
    --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq -r '.NotebookMetadata.WorkGroup')

echo "Notebook: $NOTEBOOK_ID"
echo "Workgroup: $WORKGROUP"

# Get workgroup details
aws athena get-work-group --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq '.WorkGroup.Configuration'
Output: Notebook: nb-quarterly-001 Workgroup: anihpj-spark-workgroup { "EngineVersion": { "SelectedEngineVersion": "PySpark engine version 3" }, "ResultConfiguration": { "OutputLocation": "s3://anihpj-athena-results/spark/" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Notebook runs in Spark workgroup
Example 4: Verify notebook exists

Check notebook availability.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-test-001"

if aws athena get-notebook-metadata --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" &>/dev/null; then
    NAME=$(aws athena get-notebook-metadata --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq -r '.NotebookMetadata.Name')
    echo "✓ Notebook exists: $NAME"
else
    echo "✗ Notebook not found: $NOTEBOOK_ID"
fi
Output: ✓ Notebook exists: Test-Django-Analysis
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Verified test notebook exists
Example 5: Generate notebook report

Create summary of notebook.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_ID="nb-production-001"

echo "=== Notebook Report ==="
aws athena get-notebook-metadata --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq '
    .NotebookMetadata | {
        id: .NotebookId,
        name: .Name,
        workgroup: .WorkGroup,
        type: .Type,
        created: .CreationTime,
        modified: .LastModifiedTime,
        days_since_modified: ((now - (.LastModifiedTime | fromdateiso8601)) / 86400 | floor)
    }'
Output: === Notebook Report === { "id": "nb-production-001", "name": "Production-Django-Analytics", "workgroup": "anihpj-spark-workgroup", "type": "IPYNB", "created": "2024-12-01T00:00:00Z", "modified": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z", "days_since_modified": 0 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production notebook recently updated

get-prepared-statement

Gets information about a prepared statement. View parameterized Django query details.

Syntax
aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name <value> \
    --work-group <value>
Parameters Description
  • --statement-name (string, required): Name of the prepared statement
  • --work-group (string, required): Workgroup containing the statement
Return Value
  • PreparedStatement (object): Statement details including SQL and timestamps
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get prepared statement

View parameterized query details.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "get_jobposts_by_status" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq '.PreparedStatement'
Output: { "StatementName": "get_jobposts_by_status", "QueryStatement": "SELECT * FROM jobpost_archive WHERE status = ?", "WorkGroupName": "anihpj-workgroup", "Description": "Filter JobPosts by status parameter", "LastModifiedTime": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Parameterized equivalent: # JobPost.objects.filter(status=status_param)
Example 2: Extract query SQL

Get just the parameterized SQL.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "get_author_jobposts" \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq -r '.PreparedStatement.QueryStatement'
Output: SELECT jp.*, a.name as author_name FROM jobpost_archive jp JOIN authors a ON jp.author_id = a.id WHERE a.id = ? ORDER BY jp.created_at DESC LIMIT ?
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Parameterized author lookup query
Example 3: Count parameters

Check number of parameters in statement.

#!/bin/bash
STATEMENT="search_jobs_by_criteria"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"

SQL=$(aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "$STATEMENT" \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq -r '.PreparedStatement.QueryStatement')

PARAM_COUNT=$(echo "$SQL" | grep -o '?' | wc -l)

echo "Statement: $STATEMENT"
echo "Parameters: $PARAM_COUNT"
echo "SQL: $SQL"
Output: Statement: search_jobs_by_criteria Parameters: 4 SQL: SELECT * FROM jobpost_archive WHERE status = ? AND location_id = ? AND created_at > ? LIMIT ?
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 4 parameters: status, location_id, created_at, limit
Example 4: Verify statement exists

Check if prepared statement is available.

#!/bin/bash
STATEMENT="skills_filter_query"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"

if aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "$STATEMENT" \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "✓ Statement '$STATEMENT' exists"
    aws athena get-prepared-statement \
        --statement-name "$STATEMENT" \
        --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | jq '{
            description: .PreparedStatement.Description,
            modified: .PreparedStatement.LastModifiedTime
        }'
else
    echo "✗ Statement not found"
fi
Output: ✓ Statement 'skills_filter_query' exists { "description": "Filter jobs by skill name", "modified": "2025-01-10T08:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Skills filter statement available
Example 5: Document prepared statements

Export statement for documentation.

#!/bin/bash
STATEMENT="location_report"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"
OUTPUT_FILE="statement-${STATEMENT}.md"

INFO=$(aws athena get-prepared-statement \
    --statement-name "$STATEMENT" \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP")

cat > "$OUTPUT_FILE" << EOF
# Prepared Statement: $STATEMENT

## Description
$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.PreparedStatement.Description // "No description"')

## Workgroup
$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.PreparedStatement.WorkGroupName')

## Last Modified
$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.PreparedStatement.LastModifiedTime')

## Query
\`\`\`sql
$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.PreparedStatement.QueryStatement')
\`\`\`
EOF

echo "Documentation saved to $OUTPUT_FILE"
Output: Documentation saved to statement-location_report.md
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Documented Location report statement

get-query-runtime-statistics

Gets runtime statistics for a query execution. Analyze Django query performance in detail.

Syntax
aws athena get-query-runtime-statistics \
    --query-execution-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --query-execution-id (string, required): ID of the query execution
Return Value
  • QueryRuntimeStatistics (object): Detailed runtime metrics and stage info
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get runtime statistics

View query performance metrics.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-query-runtime-statistics \
    --query-execution-id "exec-jobpost-001" | jq '.QueryRuntimeStatistics'
Output: { "Timeline": { "QueryQueueTimeInMillis": 50, "QueryPlanningTimeInMillis": 200, "EngineExecutionTimeInMillis": 2500, "ServiceProcessingTimeInMillis": 100 }, "Rows": { "InputRows": 1000000, "OutputRows": 1500 } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Query processed 1M JobPost rows, returned 1500
Example 2: Analyze query timeline

Break down execution phases.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-query-runtime-statistics \
    --query-execution-id "exec-large-001" | jq '.QueryRuntimeStatistics.Timeline | {
        queue_ms: .QueryQueueTimeInMillis,
        planning_ms: .QueryPlanningTimeInMillis,
        execution_ms: .EngineExecutionTimeInMillis,
        processing_ms: .ServiceProcessingTimeInMillis,
        total_ms: (.QueryQueueTimeInMillis + .QueryPlanningTimeInMillis + .EngineExecutionTimeInMillis + .ServiceProcessingTimeInMillis)
    }'
Output: { "queue_ms": 100, "planning_ms": 500, "execution_ms": 8500, "processing_ms": 200, "total_ms": 9300 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Total 9.3 seconds, 91% in execution
Example 3: Check row processing

Analyze input/output row ratios.

#!/bin/bash
EXEC_ID="exec-filter-001"

aws athena get-query-runtime-statistics \
    --query-execution-id "$EXEC_ID" | jq '.QueryRuntimeStatistics.Rows | {
        input: .InputRows,
        output: .OutputRows,
        filter_ratio: ((.InputRows - .OutputRows) / .InputRows * 100 | floor | tostring + "%")
    }'
Output: { "input": 5000000, "output": 25000, "filter_ratio": "99%" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Query filtered 99% of rows (highly selective WHERE clause)
Example 4: Compare query performance

Compare two query executions.

#!/bin/bash
EXEC_IDS=("exec-v1-001" "exec-v2-001")

echo "Query Performance Comparison"
echo "============================"

for ID in "${EXEC_IDS[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "Execution: $ID"
    aws athena get-query-runtime-statistics \
        --query-execution-id "$ID" 2>/dev/null | jq '{
            execution_sec: (.QueryRuntimeStatistics.Timeline.EngineExecutionTimeInMillis / 1000),
            rows_in: .QueryRuntimeStatistics.Rows.InputRows,
            rows_out: .QueryRuntimeStatistics.Rows.OutputRows
        }' || echo "  Not found"
done
Output: Query Performance Comparison ============================ Execution: exec-v1-001 { "execution_sec": 8.5, "rows_in": 5000000, "rows_out": 25000 } Execution: exec-v2-001 { "execution_sec": 2.1, "rows_in": 5000000, "rows_out": 25000 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # v2 query 4x faster with same results
Example 5: Generate performance report

Create detailed performance analysis.

#!/bin/bash
EXEC_ID="exec-quarterly-001"

echo "=== Query Performance Report ==="
echo "Execution ID: $EXEC_ID"
echo ""

STATS=$(aws athena get-query-runtime-statistics \
    --query-execution-id "$EXEC_ID")

echo "$STATS" | jq '
    .QueryRuntimeStatistics | {
        "Timeline (seconds)": {
            queue: (.Timeline.QueryQueueTimeInMillis / 1000),
            planning: (.Timeline.QueryPlanningTimeInMillis / 1000),
            execution: (.Timeline.EngineExecutionTimeInMillis / 1000),
            processing: (.Timeline.ServiceProcessingTimeInMillis / 1000)
        },
        "Row Statistics": {
            input_rows: .Rows.InputRows,
            output_rows: .Rows.OutputRows,
            rows_per_second: (.Rows.InputRows / (.Timeline.EngineExecutionTimeInMillis / 1000) | floor)
        }
    }'
Output: === Query Performance Report === Execution ID: exec-quarterly-001 { "Timeline (seconds)": { "queue": 0.1, "planning": 0.8, "execution": 45.2, "processing": 0.3 }, "Row Statistics": { "input_rows": 50000000, "output_rows": 150000, "rows_per_second": 1106194 } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Quarterly report processed 50M rows at 1.1M rows/sec

get-resource-dashboard

Gets resource usage dashboard. Monitor Django analytics resource consumption.

Syntax
aws athena get-resource-dashboard \
    [--work-group <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --work-group (string, optional): Workgroup to get dashboard for
Return Value
  • ResourceDashboard (object): Resource usage metrics and statistics
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get resource dashboard

View workgroup resource usage.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-resource-dashboard \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq '.'
Output: { "ResourceDashboard": { "QueriesCompleted": 1250, "DataScannedBytes": 52428800000, "TotalExecutionTimeMillis": 3600000, "AvgQueryDurationMillis": 2880 } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django workgroup processed 1250 queries
Example 2: Check data scanned

Monitor data consumption.

#!/bin/bash
DASHBOARD=$(aws athena get-resource-dashboard --work-group "anihpj-workgroup")

BYTES=$(echo "$DASHBOARD" | jq '.ResourceDashboard.DataScannedBytes')
GB=$(echo "scale=2; $BYTES / 1073741824" | bc)

echo "Data scanned: $GB GB"
Output: Data scanned: 48.83 GB
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django analytics scanned ~49 GB of data
Example 3: Calculate costs

Estimate query costs.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"
COST_PER_TB=5.00  # USD per TB scanned

DASHBOARD=$(aws athena get-resource-dashboard --work-group "$WORKGROUP")
BYTES=$(echo "$DASHBOARD" | jq '.ResourceDashboard.DataScannedBytes')

# Calculate cost
TB=$(echo "scale=6; $BYTES / 1099511627776" | bc)
COST=$(echo "scale=2; $TB * $COST_PER_TB" | bc)

echo "Data scanned: $TB TB"
echo "Estimated cost: \$$COST"
Output: Data scanned: 0.047684 TB Estimated cost: $0.24
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django analytics cost ~$0.24
Example 4: Performance metrics

Analyze query performance.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"

DASHBOARD=$(aws athena get-resource-dashboard --work-group "$WORKGROUP")

echo "=== Performance Metrics ==="
echo "$DASHBOARD" | jq -r '
    .ResourceDashboard | 
    "Queries: \(.QueriesCompleted)",
    "Avg Duration: \(.AvgQueryDurationMillis)ms",
    "Total Time: \(.TotalExecutionTimeMillis / 1000)s"'
Output: === Performance Metrics === Queries: 1250 Avg Duration: 2880ms Total Time: 3600s
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django queries average ~3 seconds
Example 5: Generate usage report

Create comprehensive report.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUP="anihpj-workgroup"
REPORT_FILE="athena-usage-$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

echo "=== Athena Resource Dashboard ==="
echo "Workgroup: $WORKGROUP"
echo "Date: $(date)"
echo ""

aws athena get-resource-dashboard --work-group "$WORKGROUP" | tee "$REPORT_FILE" | jq '
    .ResourceDashboard | {
        queries: .QueriesCompleted,
        data_scanned_gb: (.DataScannedBytes / 1073741824 | floor),
        total_time_hours: (.TotalExecutionTimeMillis / 3600000),
        avg_query_seconds: (.AvgQueryDurationMillis / 1000)
    }'

echo ""
echo "Report saved to: $REPORT_FILE"
Output: === Athena Resource Dashboard === Workgroup: anihpj-workgroup Date: Sat Jan 18 10:00:00 UTC 2026 { "queries": 1250, "data_scanned_gb": 48, "total_time_hours": 1, "avg_query_seconds": 2.88 } Report saved to: athena-usage-20260118.json
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Complete Django analytics usage report

get-session

Gets session information. Monitor Spark session for Django notebook analytics.

Syntax
aws athena get-session \
    --session-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --session-id (string, required): ID of the session
Return Value
  • SessionId (string): The session ID
  • Description (string): Session description
  • WorkGroup (string): Workgroup name
  • Status (object): Session state and timing
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get session details

View Spark session information.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-session \
    --session-id "session-jobpost-001" | jq '{
        id: .SessionId,
        workgroup: .WorkGroup,
        state: .Status.State,
        created: .Status.StartDateTime
    }'
Output: { "id": "session-jobpost-001", "workgroup": "anihpj-spark-workgroup", "state": "IDLE", "created": "2025-01-15T08:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Spark session ready for Django queries
Example 2: Check session state

Monitor session status.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-analytics-001"

STATE=$(aws athena get-session --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq -r '.Status.State')

case $STATE in
    IDLE)
        echo "✓ Session ready for queries"
        ;;
    BUSY)
        echo "⏳ Session executing calculation"
        ;;
    TERMINATED)
        echo "✗ Session terminated"
        ;;
    DEGRADED)
        echo "⚠ Session degraded"
        ;;
esac
Output: ✓ Session ready for queries
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Session ready for Django analytics
Example 3: Get session runtime

Calculate session duration.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-ml-001"

INFO=$(aws athena get-session --session-id "$SESSION_ID")

START=$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.Status.StartDateTime')
START_TS=$(date -d "$START" +%s)
NOW_TS=$(date +%s)
DURATION_MIN=$(( (NOW_TS - START_TS) / 60 ))

echo "Session: $SESSION_ID"
echo "Started: $START"
echo "Duration: $DURATION_MIN minutes"
Output: Session: session-ml-001 Started: 2025-01-15T08:00:00Z Duration: 150 minutes
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # ML session running for 2.5 hours
Example 4: Get session configuration

View session settings.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-session \
    --session-id "session-large-001" | jq '{
        id: .SessionId,
        workgroup: .WorkGroup,
        engine_version: .EngineVersion,
        coordinator_dpu: .SessionConfiguration.CoordinatorDpuSize,
        max_concurrent: .SessionConfiguration.MaxConcurrentDpus,
        idle_timeout: .SessionConfiguration.IdleTimeoutSeconds
    }'
Output: { "id": "session-large-001", "workgroup": "anihpj-spark-workgroup", "engine_version": "PySpark engine version 3", "coordinator_dpu": 1, "max_concurrent": 20, "idle_timeout": 600 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Large session with 20 concurrent DPUs for Django analytics
Example 5: Generate session report

Create detailed session summary.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-production-001"

echo "=== Session Report ==="
aws athena get-session --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq '
{
    "Session Info": {
        id: .SessionId,
        description: .Description,
        workgroup: .WorkGroup
    },
    "Status": {
        state: .Status.State,
        started: .Status.StartDateTime,
        last_modified: .Status.LastModifiedDateTime
    },
    "Configuration": {
        engine: .EngineVersion,
        notebook_version: .NotebookVersion
    }
}'
Output: === Session Report === { "Session Info": { "id": "session-production-001", "description": "Production Django analytics", "workgroup": "anihpj-spark-workgroup" }, "Status": { "state": "IDLE", "started": "2025-01-15T06:00:00Z", "last_modified": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z" }, "Configuration": { "engine": "PySpark engine version 3", "notebook_version": "Athena notebook version 1" } }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production session for Django analytics

get-session-endpoint

Gets the session endpoint for connecting to a session. Get connection details for Django notebooks.

Syntax
aws athena get-session-endpoint \
    --session-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --session-id (string, required): ID of the session
Return Value
  • SessionEndpoint (string): Endpoint URL for session connection
  • SessionId (string): The session ID
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get session endpoint

Retrieve connection endpoint.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-session-endpoint \
    --session-id "session-jobpost-001" | jq '.'
Output: { "SessionEndpoint": "https://session-jobpost-001.athena.us-east-1.amazonaws.com", "SessionId": "session-jobpost-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django notebook session endpoint
Example 2: Extract endpoint URL

Get just the endpoint URL.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-analytics-001"

ENDPOINT=$(aws athena get-session-endpoint \
    --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq -r '.SessionEndpoint')

echo "Connect to: $ENDPOINT"
Output: Connect to: https://session-analytics-001.athena.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django analytics session URL
Example 3: Check endpoint availability

Verify session is connectable.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-001"

RESULT=$(aws athena get-session-endpoint --session-id "$SESSION_ID" 2>&1)

if echo "$RESULT" | jq -e '.SessionEndpoint' > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    ENDPOINT=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.SessionEndpoint')
    echo "Session available at: $ENDPOINT"
else
    echo "Session not available or not ready"
fi
Output: Session available at: https://session-001.athena.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Session ready for Django queries
Example 4: Connect with endpoint

Use endpoint for API calls.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-spark-001"

# Get endpoint
ENDPOINT=$(aws athena get-session-endpoint \
    --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq -r '.SessionEndpoint')

echo "Session: $SESSION_ID"
echo "Endpoint: $ENDPOINT"
echo ""
echo "Use this endpoint for direct session API calls"

# Start a calculation using the session
aws athena start-calculation-execution \
    --session-id "$SESSION_ID" \
    --code-block "spark.sql('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM jobpost').show()"
Output: Session: session-spark-001 Endpoint: https://session-spark-001.athena.us-east-1.amazonaws.com Use this endpoint for direct session API calls { "CalculationExecutionId": "calc-001", "State": "CREATING" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Execute Django query via session endpoint
Example 5: Session connection report

Generate connection details.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-production-001"

echo "=== Session Connection Details ==="
echo "Session ID: $SESSION_ID"
echo ""

# Get endpoint
ENDPOINT_INFO=$(aws athena get-session-endpoint --session-id "$SESSION_ID")
ENDPOINT=$(echo "$ENDPOINT_INFO" | jq -r '.SessionEndpoint')

# Get status
STATUS=$(aws athena get-session-status --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq -r '.Status.State')

echo "Endpoint: $ENDPOINT"
echo "Status: $STATUS"
echo ""

if [ "$STATUS" = "IDLE" ] || [ "$STATUS" = "BUSY" ]; then
    echo "✓ Session is available for connections"
else
    echo "✗ Session not ready (status: $STATUS)"
fi
Output: === Session Connection Details === Session ID: session-production-001 Endpoint: https://session-production-001.athena.us-east-1.amazonaws.com Status: IDLE ✓ Session is available for connections
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django production session ready

get-session-status

Gets the current status of a session. Quick status check for Django notebook sessions.

Syntax
aws athena get-session-status \
    --session-id <value>
Parameters Description
  • --session-id (string, required): ID of the session
Return Value
  • SessionId (string): The session ID
  • Status (object): State and timing information
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Quick status check

Get session state efficiently.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-session-status \
    --session-id "session-001" | jq '.Status.State'
Output: "IDLE"
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Session ready for Django queries
Example 2: Check multiple sessions

Monitor all active sessions.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_IDS=("session-001" "session-002" "session-003")

echo "Session Status Dashboard"
echo "========================"
for ID in "${SESSION_IDS[@]}"; do
    STATUS=$(aws athena get-session-status \
        --session-id "$ID" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.Status.State // "NOT_FOUND"')
    printf "%-20s : %s\n" "$ID" "$STATUS"
done
Output: Session Status Dashboard ======================== session-001 : IDLE session-002 : BUSY session-003 : TERMINATED
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dashboard of Django analytics sessions
Example 3: Wait for session ready

Poll until session is idle.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-new-001"
TIMEOUT=120
ELAPSED=0

echo "Waiting for session $SESSION_ID..."

while [ $ELAPSED -lt $TIMEOUT ]; do
    STATE=$(aws athena get-session-status \
        --session-id "$SESSION_ID" | jq -r '.Status.State')
    
    if [ "$STATE" = "IDLE" ]; then
        echo "Session ready!"
        exit 0
    elif [ "$STATE" = "TERMINATED" ] || [ "$STATE" = "FAILED" ]; then
        echo "Session failed: $STATE"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo "Status: $STATE (${ELAPSED}s)"
    sleep 10
    ELAPSED=$((ELAPSED + 10))
done

echo "Timeout waiting for session"
Output: Waiting for session session-new-001... Status: CREATING (0s) Status: CREATING (10s) Status: IDLE (20s) Session ready!
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Session initialized for Django analytics
Example 4: Get session timing

Check session timestamps.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-session-status \
    --session-id "session-analytics-001" | jq '.Status | {
        state: .State,
        started: .StartDateTime,
        idle_since: .IdleSinceDateTime,
        last_modified: .LastModifiedDateTime
    }'
Output: { "state": "IDLE", "started": "2025-01-15T08:00:00Z", "idle_since": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "last_modified": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Session idle for 30 minutes
Example 5: Session health check script

Automated health monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="${1:-session-production-001}"
IDLE_THRESHOLD=3600  # 1 hour in seconds

STATUS=$(aws athena get-session-status --session-id "$SESSION_ID")

STATE=$(echo "$STATUS" | jq -r '.Status.State')
IDLE_SINCE=$(echo "$STATUS" | jq -r '.Status.IdleSinceDateTime // empty')

echo "Session Health Check"
echo "===================="
echo "Session: $SESSION_ID"
echo "State: $STATE"

if [ -n "$IDLE_SINCE" ]; then
    IDLE_TS=$(date -d "$IDLE_SINCE" +%s)
    NOW_TS=$(date +%s)
    IDLE_SEC=$((NOW_TS - IDLE_TS))
    
    echo "Idle time: $((IDLE_SEC / 60)) minutes"
    
    if [ $IDLE_SEC -gt $IDLE_THRESHOLD ]; then
        echo "⚠ WARNING: Session idle > 1 hour"
    fi
fi
Output: Session Health Check ==================== Session: session-production-001 State: IDLE Idle time: 45 minutes
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production session health check

get-table-metadata

Gets table metadata from a catalog. View Django archived table schemas in Athena.

Syntax
aws athena get-table-metadata \
    --catalog-name <value> \
    --database-name <value> \
    --table-name <value> \
    [--work-group <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --catalog-name (string, required): Data catalog name
  • --database-name (string, required): Database name
  • --table-name (string, required): Table name
  • --work-group (string, optional): Workgroup for the request
Return Value
  • TableMetadata (object): Table details including columns and properties
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get table schema

View JobPost archive table structure.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-table-metadata \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" \
    --table-name "jobpost_archive" | jq '.TableMetadata.Columns'
Output: [ {"Name": "id", "Type": "bigint"}, {"Name": "title", "Type": "string"}, {"Name": "description", "Type": "string"}, {"Name": "status", "Type": "string"}, {"Name": "author_id", "Type": "bigint"}, {"Name": "location_id", "Type": "bigint"}, {"Name": "salary_min", "Type": "int"}, {"Name": "salary_max", "Type": "int"}, {"Name": "created_at", "Type": "timestamp"}, {"Name": "updated_at", "Type": "timestamp"} ]
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Maps to Django JobPost model fields
Example 2: Get table properties

View table configuration.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-table-metadata \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" \
    --table-name "jobpost_archive" | jq '{
        name: .TableMetadata.Name,
        type: .TableMetadata.TableType,
        columns: (.TableMetadata.Columns | length),
        partition_keys: (.TableMetadata.PartitionKeys | length)
    }'
Output: { "name": "jobpost_archive", "type": "EXTERNAL_TABLE", "columns": 10, "partition_keys": 2 }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # JobPost archive partitioned by year/month
Example 3: Check partition keys

View table partitioning.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-table-metadata \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" \
    --table-name "jobpost_archive" | jq '.TableMetadata.PartitionKeys'
Output: [ {"Name": "year", "Type": "string"}, {"Name": "month", "Type": "string"} ]
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Data partitioned by year/month for efficient queries
Example 4: Compare Django models to Athena

Map table columns to Django fields.

#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Skills Table Schema ==="
aws athena get-table-metadata \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" \
    --table-name "skills" | jq '.TableMetadata.Columns[] | "\(.Name): \(.Type)"' -r

echo ""
echo "=== Skills-JobPost M2M Table ==="
aws athena get-table-metadata \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" \
    --table-name "skills_jobpost" | jq '.TableMetadata.Columns[] | "\(.Name): \(.Type)"' -r
Output: === Skills Table Schema === id: bigint skill_name: string category: string created_at: timestamp === Skills-JobPost M2M Table === id: bigint skill_id: bigint jobpost_id: bigint
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Skills M2M relationship mapped in Athena # skills_jobpost is the M2M through table
Example 5: Generate table documentation

Export table metadata as documentation.

#!/bin/bash
TABLE="jobpost_archive"
OUTPUT="table-${TABLE}-schema.md"

META=$(aws athena get-table-metadata \
    --catalog-name "AwsDataCatalog" \
    --database-name "anihpj_archive" \
    --table-name "$TABLE")

cat > "$OUTPUT" << EOF
# Table: $TABLE

## Overview
- **Type**: $(echo "$META" | jq -r '.TableMetadata.TableType')
- **Created**: $(echo "$META" | jq -r '.TableMetadata.CreateTime')

## Columns
| Name | Type | Comment |
|------|------|---------|
$(echo "$META" | jq -r '.TableMetadata.Columns[] | "| \(.Name) | \(.Type) | \(.Comment // "-") |"')

## Partition Keys
$(echo "$META" | jq -r '.TableMetadata.PartitionKeys[] | "- \(.Name) (\(.Type))"')
EOF

echo "Documentation saved to $OUTPUT"
Output: Documentation saved to table-jobpost_archive-schema.md
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Generated schema documentation for Django archive

get-work-group

Gets workgroup information. View Django analytics workgroup configuration.

Syntax
aws athena get-work-group \
    --work-group <value>
Parameters Description
  • --work-group (string, required): Name of the workgroup
Return Value
  • WorkGroup (object): Workgroup details including configuration and state
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Get workgroup details

View workgroup configuration.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-work-group \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq '.WorkGroup'
Output: { "Name": "anihpj-workgroup", "State": "ENABLED", "Configuration": { "ResultConfiguration": { "OutputLocation": "s3://anihpj-athena-results/" }, "EnforceWorkGroupConfiguration": true, "PublishCloudWatchMetricsEnabled": true }, "Description": "Django analytics workgroup" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Production workgroup for Django analytics
Example 2: Check output location

Get query results S3 path.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-work-group \
    --work-group "anihpj-workgroup" | jq -r '
    .WorkGroup.Configuration.ResultConfiguration.OutputLocation'
Output: s3://anihpj-athena-results/
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Django query results stored in S3
Example 3: Check cost controls

View byte scan limits.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-work-group \
    --work-group "anihpj-dev" | jq '{
        name: .WorkGroup.Name,
        bytes_limit: .WorkGroup.Configuration.BytesScannedCutoffPerQuery,
        bytes_limit_gb: (.WorkGroup.Configuration.BytesScannedCutoffPerQuery / 1073741824),
        enforced: .WorkGroup.Configuration.EnforceWorkGroupConfiguration
    }'
Output: { "name": "anihpj-dev", "bytes_limit": 10737418240, "bytes_limit_gb": 10, "enforced": true }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dev workgroup limited to 10GB scan per query
Example 4: Get engine version

Check Athena/Spark engine.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena get-work-group \
    --work-group "anihpj-spark-workgroup" | jq '{
        name: .WorkGroup.Name,
        engine: .WorkGroup.Configuration.EngineVersion.SelectedEngineVersion,
        effective_engine: .WorkGroup.Configuration.EngineVersion.EffectiveEngineVersion
    }'
Output: { "name": "anihpj-spark-workgroup", "engine": "PySpark engine version 3", "effective_engine": "PySpark engine version 3" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Spark workgroup for Django notebooks
Example 5: Compare workgroup configs

Compare dev and prod settings.

#!/bin/bash
WORKGROUPS=("anihpj-dev" "anihpj-production")

echo "Workgroup Comparison"
echo "===================="

for WG in "${WORKGROUPS[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "--- $WG ---"
    aws athena get-work-group --work-group "$WG" | jq '{
        state: .WorkGroup.State,
        output: .WorkGroup.Configuration.ResultConfiguration.OutputLocation,
        bytes_limit_gb: ((.WorkGroup.Configuration.BytesScannedCutoffPerQuery // 0) / 1073741824),
        metrics: .WorkGroup.Configuration.PublishCloudWatchMetricsEnabled
    }'
done
Output: Workgroup Comparison ==================== --- anihpj-dev --- { "state": "ENABLED", "output": "s3://anihpj-athena-results/dev/", "bytes_limit_gb": 10, "metrics": true } --- anihpj-production --- { "state": "ENABLED", "output": "s3://anihpj-athena-results/prod/", "bytes_limit_gb": 0, "metrics": true }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Dev limited to 10GB, prod unlimited

import-notebook

Imports a notebook into Athena. Upload Django analytics notebooks for execution.

Syntax
aws athena import-notebook \
    --work-group <value> \
    --name <value> \
    --payload <value> \
    --type <value> \
    [--notebook-s3-location-uri <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --work-group (string, required): Target workgroup
  • --name (string, required): Name for the notebook
  • --payload (string, optional): Base64-encoded notebook content
  • --type (string, required): Notebook type (IPYNB)
  • --notebook-s3-location-uri (string, optional): S3 URI of notebook
Return Value
  • NotebookId (string): ID of the imported notebook
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: Import from local file

Upload notebook from local filesystem.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_FILE="django-analysis.ipynb"
PAYLOAD=$(base64 -w 0 "$NOTEBOOK_FILE")

aws athena import-notebook \
    --work-group "anihpj-spark-workgroup" \
    --name "Django-Analysis-Imported" \
    --payload "$PAYLOAD" \
    --type IPYNB
Output: { "NotebookId": "nb-imported-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Imported Django analysis notebook
Example 2: Import from S3

Import notebook stored in S3.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena import-notebook \
    --work-group "anihpj-spark-workgroup" \
    --name "S3-Imported-Notebook" \
    --notebook-s3-location-uri "s3://anihpj-notebooks/analytics/jobpost-ml.ipynb" \
    --type IPYNB
Output: { "NotebookId": "nb-s3-001" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Imported notebook from S3 storage
Example 3: Batch import notebooks

Import multiple notebooks from directory.

#!/bin/bash
NOTEBOOK_DIR="./notebooks"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"

for FILE in "$NOTEBOOK_DIR"/*.ipynb; do
    NAME=$(basename "$FILE" .ipynb)
    echo "Importing: $NAME"
    
    PAYLOAD=$(base64 -w 0 "$FILE")
    
    aws athena import-notebook \
        --work-group "$WORKGROUP" \
        --name "$NAME" \
        --payload "$PAYLOAD" \
        --type IPYNB | jq -r '.NotebookId'
done
Output: Importing: jobpost-analysis nb-001 Importing: skills-ml nb-002 Importing: author-metrics nb-003
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Batch imported Django analytics notebooks
Example 4: Import and verify

Import notebook and confirm success.

#!/bin/bash
FILE="quarterly-report.ipynb"
NAME="Quarterly-Report-Q1-2025"
WORKGROUP="anihpj-spark-workgroup"

PAYLOAD=$(base64 -w 0 "$FILE")

NOTEBOOK_ID=$(aws athena import-notebook \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP" \
    --name "$NAME" \
    --payload "$PAYLOAD" \
    --type IPYNB \
    --query 'NotebookId' --output text)

echo "Imported: $NOTEBOOK_ID"

# Verify
aws athena get-notebook-metadata \
    --notebook-id "$NOTEBOOK_ID" | jq '{
        id: .NotebookMetadata.NotebookId,
        name: .NotebookMetadata.Name,
        created: .NotebookMetadata.CreationTime
    }'
Output: Imported: nb-qtr-001 { "id": "nb-qtr-001", "name": "Quarterly-Report-Q1-2025", "created": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Quarterly Django report notebook imported
Example 5: Import with environment suffix

Import notebook with environment naming.

#!/bin/bash
FILE="analysis-template.ipynb"
ENVIRONMENT="${1:-dev}"

case $ENVIRONMENT in
    dev)
        WORKGROUP="anihpj-dev-spark"
        ;;
    staging)
        WORKGROUP="anihpj-staging-spark"
        ;;
    production)
        WORKGROUP="anihpj-prod-spark"
        ;;
esac

NAME="Django-Analysis-${ENVIRONMENT}-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
PAYLOAD=$(base64 -w 0 "$FILE")

aws athena import-notebook \
    --work-group "$WORKGROUP" \
    --name "$NAME" \
    --payload "$PAYLOAD" \
    --type IPYNB

echo "Imported to $ENVIRONMENT: $NAME"
Output: { "NotebookId": "nb-dev-20250115" } Imported to dev: Django-Analysis-dev-20250115
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Notebook imported to dev environment

list-application-dpu-sizes

Lists available DPU sizes for Spark applications. Check capacity options for Django analytics.

Syntax
aws athena list-application-dpu-sizes \
    [--max-results <value>] \
    [--next-token <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum results to return
  • --next-token (string, optional): Pagination token
Return Value
  • ApplicationDPUSizes (list): Available DPU size options
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: List available DPU sizes

View all DPU options.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-application-dpu-sizes | jq '.ApplicationDPUSizes'
Output: [ { "ApplicationRuntimeId": "Athena notebook version 1", "SupportedDPUSizes": [4, 8, 16, 32, 64] }, { "ApplicationRuntimeId": "PySpark engine version 3", "SupportedDPUSizes": [4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128] } ]
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # DPU options for Django analytics workloads
Example 2: Get PySpark DPU sizes

Filter for Spark engine options.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-application-dpu-sizes | jq -r '
    .ApplicationDPUSizes[] | 
    select(.ApplicationRuntimeId | contains("PySpark")) | 
    .SupportedDPUSizes[]'
Output: 4 8 16 32 64 128
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # PySpark DPU options: 4 to 128
Example 3: Find minimum DPU size

Get smallest available size.

#!/bin/bash
MIN_DPU=$(aws athena list-application-dpu-sizes | jq '
    [.ApplicationDPUSizes[].SupportedDPUSizes[]] | min')

echo "Minimum DPU size available: $MIN_DPU"
Output: Minimum DPU size available: 4
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Minimum 4 DPUs for small Django queries
Example 4: Recommend DPU size

Suggest size based on data volume.

#!/bin/bash
DATA_SIZE_GB="${1:-10}"

SIZES=$(aws athena list-application-dpu-sizes | jq -r '
    .ApplicationDPUSizes[] | 
    select(.ApplicationRuntimeId | contains("PySpark")) | 
    .SupportedDPUSizes[]')

# Recommend based on data size
if [ "$DATA_SIZE_GB" -lt 10 ]; then
    RECOMMENDED=4
elif [ "$DATA_SIZE_GB" -lt 50 ]; then
    RECOMMENDED=16
elif [ "$DATA_SIZE_GB" -lt 100 ]; then
    RECOMMENDED=32
else
    RECOMMENDED=64
fi

echo "Data size: ${DATA_SIZE_GB}GB"
echo "Recommended DPU size: $RECOMMENDED"
Output: Data size: 10GB Recommended DPU size: 16
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # 16 DPUs recommended for 10GB Django data
Example 5: Cost estimation by DPU

Estimate costs for each DPU size.

#!/bin/bash
HOURLY_RATE=0.35
HOURS=8

echo "DPU Cost Estimation (${HOURS} hours)"
echo "====================================="

aws athena list-application-dpu-sizes | jq -r '
    .ApplicationDPUSizes[] | 
    select(.ApplicationRuntimeId | contains("PySpark")) | 
    .SupportedDPUSizes[]' | while read DPU; do
    COST=$(echo "$DPU * $HOURLY_RATE * $HOURS" | bc)
    printf "%-8s DPUs: \$%.2f\n" "$DPU" "$COST"
done
Output: DPU Cost Estimation (8 hours) ===================================== 4 DPUs: $11.20 8 DPUs: $22.40 16 DPUs: $44.80 32 DPUs: $89.60 64 DPUs: $179.20 128 DPUs: $358.40
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cost planning for Django analytics workloads

list-calculation-executions

Lists calculation executions in a session. View Spark calculations on Django data.

Syntax
aws athena list-calculation-executions \
    --session-id <value> \
    [--state-filter <value>] \
    [--max-results <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --session-id (string, required): Session to list calculations for
  • --state-filter (string, optional): Filter by state (CREATING, CREATED, QUEUED, RUNNING, etc.)
  • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum results
Return Value
  • Calculations (list): List of calculation summaries
5 Detailed Athena Examples
Example 1: List all calculations

View calculations in session.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-calculation-executions \
    --session-id "session-jobpost-001" | jq '.Calculations[]'
Output: { "CalculationExecutionId": "calc-001", "Status": {"State": "COMPLETED"}, "Description": "JobPost count by status" } { "CalculationExecutionId": "calc-002", "Status": {"State": "COMPLETED"}, "Description": "Skills distribution" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Calculations on Django data in session
Example 2: Filter by running state

Find currently running calculations.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-calculation-executions \
    --session-id "session-analytics-001" \
    --state-filter RUNNING | jq '.Calculations[] | {
        id: .CalculationExecutionId,
        description: .Description
    }'
Output: { "id": "calc-active-001", "description": "Large JobPost aggregation" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # One calculation running on Django data
Example 3: Get completed calculations

List successfully completed calculations.

#!/bin/bash
aws athena list-calculation-executions \
    --session-id "session-ml-001" \
    --state-filter COMPLETED \
    --max-results 10 | jq '.Calculations[] | {
        id: .CalculationExecutionId,
        description: .Description,
        submitted: .Status.SubmissionDateTime
    }'
Output: { "id": "calc-001", "description": "Load JobPost data", "submitted": "2025-01-15T08:00:00Z" } { "id": "calc-002", "description": "Train Skills model", "submitted": "2025-01-15T08:30:00Z" }
Django Context: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # ML session calculations completed
Example 4: Count calculations by state

Get calculation statistics.

#!/bin/bash
SESSION_ID="session-production-001"
